topics.title: Introduction to Rapid Response
topics.subtitle: Acute deterioration, bedside assessment, vitals, first pass stabilization

sections.title: Overview
items: Airway: check patency and protect. Breathing: check WOB, oxygenation, and ventilation. Circulation: check pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
A rapid response is called for acute clinical deterioration before full cardiopulmonary arrest.
The goal is early recognition, immediate stabilization, escalation of care, & prevention of ICU transfer or code.
Approach bedside with a script: stabilize first, gather key context fast, run a stripped-down ABCDE, & escalate early when trajectory looks bad.

sections.title: Before You Arrive
items: On phone, first ask: current vitals, O2 need, mentation change, urine output, & any new chest pain, dyspnea, or bleeding.
If unstable, tell nursing to call rapid now, get full vitals, place oxygen, & confirm IV access while you head in.
If not crashing, give immediate low-risk orders: repeat vitals, fingerstick glucose, fix bad pulse ox waveform, ECG if needed, & stay at bedside if pt worsens.

sections.title: Immediate Bedside Priorities
items: Start with a doorway 10-second scan: are they talking, working to breathe, pale/diaphoretic/cyanotic, & what oxygen, monitors, drips, or pressors are already running?
If they look like they may crash, grab help now & start ABCs from room.
check airway, breathing, circulation, & mentation on arrival.
Get your own full vitals; place on telemetry & pulse oximetry if not already monitored.
Get EKG when chest pain, arrhythmia, syncope, unexplained ↓ BP, or ischemia is suspected
Confirm code status, allergies, IV access, oxygen delivery device, & whether pt is worsening or transiently abnormal.
Start time-sensitive treatment now for obvious instability while another team member gathers chart history & recent data

pairs.value: Know why pt is in hospital & what problems have already been identified.
Ask what triggered call now, what nurse thinks problem is, & what change happened over last few hours.
Look for baseline cardiopulmonary disease, renal failure, liver disease, endocrine disease, anticoagulation use, seizure history, & prior ICU admissions.
Review CBC, BMP/CMP, lactate, blood gas, troponin, BNP, cultures, glucose, & any changing values.
Identify what pt is actively receiving including fluids, antibiotics, anticoagulants, insulin, sedatives, opioids, & vasoactive medications.
Ask which important medications have been held such as beta blockers, antihypertensives, anticoagulants, steroids, diuretics, antiseizure drugs, or home oxygen support.
Clarify what changed today: procedure, dialysis, new medication, bleeding, aspiration, fever, rhythm change, seizure, fall, or worsening urine output.
Mentally upgrade pts who are recent ICU downgrades, septic, neutropenic, high risk postop, or recently bleeding.
Talking normally suggests a patent airway. Hoarseness, gurgling, stridor, snoring, or inability to clear secretions means airway threat. Use jaw thrust, suction, sit upright, & escalate early.
Count RR yourself, check WOB, wheeze/crackles/absent sounds, verify SpO2 waveform, & check oxygen setup. Escalate for marked tachypnea, hypoxemia despite support, or fatigue.
Feel pulse, repeat BP if it does not fit picture, check perfusion, review urine output, & look at access. Consider ECG, fingerstick glucose, & targeted labs early.
Check AVPU, orientation, pupils, speech, & gross motor function. Review for opioids, sedatives, insulin, seizure risk, or stroke symptoms.
Look for bleeding, rash, swelling, distended abdomen, surgical complications, line problems, or obvious bedside clues.

sections.title: Focused Exam / ROS
items: Do a focused physical exam directed by instability: mentation, speech, WOB, lung exam, cardiac rhythm, perfusion, abdomen, edema, bleeding, & skin findings.
If pt can answer questions, get brief ROS for chest pain, dyspnea, palpitations, dizziness, headache, focal deficit, fever, abdominal pain, & bleeding symptoms.
Use bedside nurse's observations when pt cannot provide history: last known well, urine output, mentation trend, O2 need, & symptom progression.

sections.title: Typical Team Composition
items: Bedside nurse & charge nurse
Primary team clinician or covering provider
Rapid response nurse
Respiratory therapist when respiratory distress, hypoxemia, or airway concern
Pharmacist when medication support, pressors, reversal agents, or emergent dosing is needed
More support as needed: ICU team, anesthesia, security, or procedural staff

sections.title: Why Rapid Response Is Called
items: Acute AMS, WOB, oxygenation, heart rate, BP, or perfusion
Concern from bedside staff that pt is decompensating, even if trigger does not fit strict threshold
New chest pain, syncope, seizure, severe agitation, suspected stroke, sepsis, or unexpected bleeding
Any situation where early team-based assessment may prevent cardiac arrest or unplanned ICU transfer

sections.title: Escalation Lane
items: Wardmanageable: problem identified, initial treatment started, repeat vitals improving, & monitoring plan is clear.
Urgent help: call senior, ICU, specialty, or procedural support early if pt is high risk or may need a higher level of care.
Rapid / code now: severe airway threat, major respiratory distress, shock, severe dysrhythmia,worsening mentation, or any pt who looks close to arrest.

sections.title: Assessment & Initial Workup
items: Generate practical differential diagnosis based on first minute of information,then narrow it with the exam, vitals, ECG, & targeted testing.
Order appropriate labs & imaging early when they will change management: CBC, CMP, VBG/ABG, lactate, troponin, BNP, cultures, chest xray, CT, or POCUS as needed.

Treat now reversible problems without waiting for every result: oxygen, suction, bronchodilators, fluids, vasopressors, glucose, naloxone, antibiotics, blood products, or antiarrhythmics as clinically indicated.
Escalate to ICU, anesthesia, cath lab, stroke pathway, code team, or surgical consultation when bedside trajectory suggests pt may not remain stable on floor.

pairs.key: ICU / immediate higher level of care if
pairs.value: persistent respiratory distress, high-flow or NIV need, hemodynamic instability, vasopressor need, severe arrhythmia, airway risk, neurologic decline, or a trajectory suggesting pt may crash on the floor.

topics.title: Cardiac Arrest
topics.subtitle: Pulselessness, unresponsiveness, apnea, shockable vs nonshockable rhythms

sections.title: Immediate Steps
items: No pulse: start CPR NOW, get backboard under pt.
!!Circulation!!: check pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
!!Airway!!: check patency, look for obstructions.
!!Breathing!!: check ventilation efficiency.
CONFIRM CODE STATUS while compressions are starting.
TASK: Call code: dial !!2-3333!!
Put pads on ASAP for rhythm checks & defibrillation; shave or rip old pads off if needed.
BVM ventilate 30:2 with suction ready before anesthesia comes.

sections.title: Initial Steps & Delegation
items: Line up compressor, backup compressor #1, & backup compressor #2.
Assign (1) airway, (2) timekeeper/recorder, (3) pharmacy/meds, (4) IV access, (5) CPR coach/code whisperer: one-liner, day events, access, & recent labs.
Grab Stool
Get 2 PIVs; if difficult, place IO early.
prep meds: prep Epi Amps, get Epi or Levo Gtt ready.
Get Doppler for pulse checks & US for POCUS.
Call family.

sections.title: Chart Check
items: Why pt is in hospital.
Relevant comorbidities/causes of Code.
Day events: meds, procedures?, labs.
Current access, code status, & decisionmakers.
ECMO candidate? If arrest may reversible with ECMO as bridge, discuss ecmo within 10 minutes.

sections.title: Rhythm Based ACLS
items: Start CPR, give oxygen, and attach monitor/defibrillator.
Check rhythm q2 min.
If VF / pulseless VT:
- Shock, resume CPR for 2 min, and get IV/IO access.
- If still shockable, shock again and resume CPR.
- Give epinephrine 1 mg IV/IO q3-5 min during ongoing CPR.
- If still shockable after next cycle, shock again, resume CPR, then give amiodarone 300 mg IV/IO or lidocaine.
- Continue shock -> CPR -> meds -> treat reversible causes.
If asystole / PEA:
- Resume CPR for 2 min, get IV/IO access, and give epinephrine 1 mg IV/IO q3-5 min.
- Recheck rhythm q2 min and continue CPR.
- Treat reversible causes throughout.
Airway / monitoring:
- prepare advanced airway when it will not interrupt compressions.
- Use waveform capnography to confirm tube placement and monitor CPR quality.
CPR:
- Rate 100-120/min, depth at least 2 inches, full recoil, minimal pauses, avoid overventilation.
- 30:2 if no advanced airway; if advanced airway is in place, give 1 breath q6 sec with continuous compressions.

pairs.value: Give 1000 mL LR (if not overloaded); if bleeding, start blood products & MTP.
Check glucose early; give D50 if low.
Confirm O2 delivery, vent effectively, & intubate; check gas & pulse ox.
Txt cause; bicarbonate 1 amp q4min, optimize ventilation, & support shock.
Give 1 aml CaCl, insulin plus dextrose, & confirm on blood gas or labs.
Actively rewarm.
Use exam or POCUS for absent lung sliding & decompress now.
Use POCUS; if present, get ROSC, then pericardiocentesis.
If ROSC & ACS is likely, get ECG & move toward cath lab.
If RV strain & PE is likely, consider alteplase 100 mg IV & continue CPR for 6090 minutes afterward.
Review MAR & exposures; give antidotes (naloxone, glucagon) when indicated.

sections.title: Post-ROSC
items: Treat most likely cause of arrest.
Get Labs
Get EKG,
Full Pocus
EKG: cath lab early if ACS.
Aggressive hemodynamic support
Support perfusion with norepinephrine or epinephrine infusion & more fluid as needed.
Target SpO2 9298% & ETCO2 3540.
If not following commands, consider targeted temperature management & CT head.
Transformation to PEA = Good sign https://www.resuscitationjournal.com/article/S0300-9572(22)00142-3/fulltext.

drugs.name: Epinephrine
drugs.notes: 1 mg every 4 mins (35 minutes).

drugs.name: Amiodarone
drugs.notes: For refractory VF/pVT after defibrillation.

drugs.name: Lidocaine 2%
drugs.notes: Alternative to amiodarone for refractory VF/pVT.

drugs.name: Magnesium sulfate
drugs.notes: Use if torsades is suspected.

drugs.name: Sodium bicarb
drugs.notes: For hyperkalemia, severe acidosis, or toxinmed. arrest.

drugs.name: Alteplase
drugs.notes: Consider for arrest strongly suspected from massive PE (refer to TUHS policy on alteplase during code); continue CPR 6090 min after admin.

topics.title: ACS
topics.subtitle: Chest pain, ischemic ECG changes, rising troponin, diaphoresis

sections.title: First 60 Seconds
items:
Circulation: check pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
Airway: check patency and protect.
Breathing: check WOB, oxygenation, and ventilation.
EKG ASAP
- STEMI = STE >1 mm in 2 contiguous leads. In V2V3: ≥2 mm in men ≥40 years, ≥2.5 mm in men <40 years, or ≥1.5 mm in women regardless of age. Activate cath lab ASAP and do NOT wait for troponin.
- Posterior MI: ST depression V1V3 + dominant R waves: get posterior leads V7V9
- Right ventricular MI: inferior STEMI + ↓ BP: get rightsided leads (V4R)
Aspirin 325 mg PO/PR ASAP
IV access, telemetry, pulse oximetry

sections.title: STEMI vs NSTEMI vs Unstable Angina

pairs.value: STE in 2+ contiguous leads or STEMI equivalent. Do not wait for troponin. Activate cath lab now.
Troponin elevated without ST elevation. May have ST depression or T-wave inversion. Urgent, not immediate cath.
Ischemic symptoms with normal troponin. Still ACS: admit and risk-stratify.
Wellens, de Winter, posterior MI, or Sgarbossa-positive LBBB/paced rhythm.

images.caption: Anterior STEMI (LAD occlusion)
images.description: STE in V1V4. Reciprocal ST depression in inferior leads (II, III, aVF). Large territory: risk of cardiogenic shock & arrhythmia.

images.caption: Inferior STEMI (RCA occlusion)
images.description: ST elevation in II, III, aVF with reciprocal ST depression in I & aVL. Always get V4R to check for RV involvement & V7V9 for posterior extension.

images.caption: Wellens Syndrome (critical proximal LAD stenosis)
images.description: Deep, symmetric Twave inversions in V2V3 (Type A) or biphasic T waves in V2 (Type B). Patient is pain-free when this pattern appears. Do NOT stress test: go directly to cath. Represents critical proximal LAD stenosis with risk of anterior wall MI.

images.caption: de Winter T Waves (LAD occlusion equivalent)
images.description: Upsloping ST depression >1mm at Jpoint in V1V6 with tall, prominent, symmetric T waves. No ST elevation. This is STEMI equivalent: activate cath lab. Present in 2% of LAD occlusions.

images.caption: Sgarbossa Criteria (STEMI in LBBB)
images.description: In LBBB or paced rhythm: (1) concordant STE ≥1 mm in any lead = 5 pts, (2) concordant ST depression ≥1 mm in V1V3 = 3 pts, (3) discordant STE ≥5 mm (modified: ratio >0.25 of preceding S wave) = 2 pts. Score ≥3 is highly specific for MI.

images.caption: Posterior STEMI
images.description: ST depression in V1V3 with tall, broad R waves & upright T waves: mirror image of posterior STE. Confirm with posterior leads V7V9 showing ST elevation ≥0.5 mm. Often accompanies inferior STEMI.

pairs.value: STE in V1-V4. Large territory: risk of cardiogenic shock, CHF, arrhythmia.
STE in I, aVL, V5-V6. Often smaller infarct but can extend.
STE in II, III, aVF. Check RV involvement (V4R) & posterior (V7-V9). High vagal tone: ↓ HR common.
ST depression V1-V3, tall R waves, upright T waves in V1-V3. Confirm with V7-V9 showing STE.
Inferior STEMI + ↓ BP + clear lungs + JVD. Get V4R. NO nitrates & diuretics: preload dependent. Treat with IV fluids.
ST depression in leads opposite to STE: ↑ specificity for true STEMI. Absence does not rule it out.
[MD CALC Heart Score](https://www.mdcalc.com/calc/1752/heartscoremajorcardiacevents) Score 03: low risk, consider discharge. 4-6: moderate, admit. 7-10: high risk, early invasive.
Predicts inhospital & 6month mortality. High risk (>140): early invasive strategy within 24h. Intermediate (109140): invasive within 72h. Low (<109): ischemiaguided.
[MD CALC TIMI Score](https://www.mdcalc.com/calc/111/timiriskscoreuanstemi) Higher score = more benefit from early invasive.

sections.title: Management
items: Aspirin 325mg PO Load NOW
EKG: if STEMI, then cath lab now
hstroponin: now & at +1 hour or +3 hours
Delta 15 ng/L (up OR down) – approx 20%. Delta can down as troponin will ↓ after ACS event. +ACS if >15ng/L change
IV access, telemetry, pulse ox
O2 only if SpO2 <90%: routine O2 may ↑ mortality
Nitroglycerin 0.4 mg SL q5min x3 for pain (STOP if SBP <100, RV/inferior MI, or PDE5 inhibitor use)
Heparin: UFH bolus + gtt
BB: metoprolol tartrate 12.525 mg PO (HR >60, SBP >100, no shock): avoid IV BB
Statin: atorvastatin 80 mg PO or Rosuvastatin 40mg PO
P2Y12i (HOLD if CABG possible): ticagrelor 180 mg load > clopidogrel 600 mg (c/s Cards first)

sections.title: STEMI Reperfusion Strategy
items: Primary PCI (preferred): door-balloon <90 minutes
Fibrinolytics (if PCI not available within 120 min): tenecteplase (weight based IV bolus)
Fibrinolytic CI: active bleeding, prior hemorrhagic stroke, ischemic stroke <3 months, aortic dissection, intracranial neoplasm, known bleeding diasis
After fibrinolytics: transfer for angiography within 324 hours regardless of reperfusion success
Failed reperfusion (persistent STE + pain 60-90 min post-lytic): rescue PCI ASAP

drugs.name: Aspirin
drugs.notes: Nonenteric coated for loading. Give now to all ACS. Lifelong therapy.

drugs.name: Ticagrelor
drugs.notes: Preferred P2Y12 inhibitor in most ACS (PLATO trial). Hold 5 days before CABG.Don't use with ASA >100 mg/day.

drugs.name: Clopidogrel
drugs.notes: Alternative if ticagrelor not tolerated. Hold 5 days before CABG. CYP2C19 poor metabolizers may not respond.

drugs.name: Heparin (UFH)
drugs.notes: Target aPTT 1.52.5x control. Preferred if cath lab activation likely (shorter halflife, reverse with protamine).

drugs.name: Enoxaparin
drugs.notes: Alternative to UFH in NSTEMI. Reduce to 1 mg/kg daily if CrCl <30. Avoid if CABG planned within 24h.

drugs.name: Nitroglycerin
drugs.dose: 0.4 mg SL q5min x3,thenIV drip 5200 mcg/min

drugs.name: Metoprolol Tartrate
drugs.dose: 12.525 mg PO q6h,, then 200 mg daily
drugs.notes: Start within 24h if stable. Avoid if HR <60, SBP <100, PR >0.24s, severe bronchospasm.

drugs.name: Atorvastatin
drugs.notes: Highintensity statin for all ACS. Start regardless of baseline LDL. Check LFTs, CK at baseline.

drugs.name: Tenecteplase
drugs.notes: Fibrinolytic for STEMI when PCI not available within 120 min. <60 kg: 30 mg, 6070: 35 mg, 7080: 40 mg, 8090: 45 mg, >90: 50 mg.

pairs.value: SBP <90 after fluids, s/s endorgan damage. Large anterior MI. Inotropes (dobutamine), vasopressors (norepinephrine), mechanical support (IABP, Impella). Emergent cath.
VT/VF MCC of early death ACLS. Reperfusion arrhythmias (accelerated idioventricular rhythm) = benign. Complete heart block in inferior MI= temporary pacing.
Papillary muscle rupture (usually posteromedial, 27 days postMI). New loud murmur + flash pulmonary edema. Emergent surgery.
New harsh holosystolic murmur + hemodynamic collapse, 35 days postMI. Confirm with echo + stepup in O2 sat from RA to RV. Emergent surgery.
Sudden PEA arrest 35 days postMI. Pericardial tamponade on POCUS. Usually fatal: emergent surgery if caught.
Days to weeks postMI. Pleuritic chest pain, friction rub, diffuse ST elevation. NSAIDs + colchicine. Avoid anticoagulation if large effusion.

sections.title: Pearls & Pitfalls
items: Women, diabetics, & elderly with atypical s/s: dyspnea, nausea, fatigue, or epigastric pain without classic chest pain
nl. initial troponin does NOT r/o ACS: serial troponins are essential (0/1h or 0/3h protocol)
Type 2 MI (supplydemand mismatch, anemia, sepsis, etc.) is NOT same as Type 1 MI (plaque rupture): tx differs significantly
Cocaine chest pain: Use benzodiazepines, nitroglycerin, CCBs
RV infarct triad: ↓ BP + clear lungs + JVD: give fluids, avoid nitrates & diuretics
Wellens syndrome (deep symmetric TWI in V2V3) = critical proximal LAD stenosis: do NOT stress test, go to cath

topics.title: AFib / Flutter with RVR
topics.subtitle: Palpitations, tachycardia, dyspnea, irregular or flutter rhythm

sections.title: ABCs: Is Patient Unstable?
items: Circulation: check pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
Airway + Breathing: check WOB, oxygenation, and ventilation.
Unstable = ↓ BP, AMS, active ischemia, acute pulmonary edema: synchronized cardioversion
If mentating well, even with borderline BP, consider trial of rate control before shocking
telemetry, EKG, IV access, O2

sections.title: Chart Check
items: Precipitants: recent surgery, sepsis, ischemia, PE, EtOH/withdrawal, thyrotoxicosis
Prior EKGs, TTE (LA size, LV function, valve Dz, pulmHTN, LV thrombus)
Prior cardioversions, ablation history
Anticoagulation: agent, adherence, duration
Time of symptom onset: if<48h, cardioversion is safer without TEE
Hyperthyroid symptoms, recent TSH/T4
Caffeine & EtOH use, sympathomimetic Rx
Hx: HTN, DM, CHF, valve disease, OSA, angina, prior stroke/TIA

sections.title: EKG Interpretation
items: **AFib**: No P waves, irregularly irregular RR intervals
**AFL with RVR**: Sawtooth flutter waves (best seen in II, III, aVF, V1), regular or regularly irregular rate —150 bpm (2:1 block)
**If regular**: Consider escape rhythm vs complete heart block (CHB): not true AFib
**DDx**: MAT (3+ distinct Pwave morphologies, irregular), sinus tach with frequent PACs (look for P waves before each QRS)

sections.title: Etiology / Precipitants
items: **Cardiac**: ACS/ischemia, heart failure (too much or too little fluid: Starling curve), valvular disease, hypertensive heart disease
**Pulmonary**: PE, COPD exacerbation, hypoxia, pneumonia
**Stressinduced / sympathetic surge**: Postop, sepsis, pain, hyperthyroidism, cocaine/stimulants, pheochromocytoma
**Metabolic**: Low Mg, low K, thyrotoxicosis, severe anemia
**Toxic**: Acute alcohol, alcohol withdrawal, caffeine excess, sympathomimetic drugs

sections.title: Management
items: check hemodynamic stability
Unstable > immediate synchronized cardioversion; Biphasic waveform, 200 J initial energy
If stable: IV rate control
- Firstline: Beta blocker OR diltiazem (if EF >40%)
- Adjunct: IV magnesium
- Alternative: Digoxin or IV amiodarone (if HFrEF or other agents contraindicated)
- Avoid: Calcium channel blockers in moderatesevere LV dysfunction
- Avoid: AV nodal blockers in preexcitation (WPW)
Consider rhythm control (cardioversion)
- Electrical or pharmacological (ibutilide, amiodarone, flecainide/propafenone)
Anticoagulation check
- AF >48 hours: Requires 3 weeks anticoagulation OR TEE before cardioversion
- Continue anticoagulation >=4 weeks postcardioversion
Target heart rate
- Lenient: <110 bpm at rest (asymptomatic, preserved LV function)
- Strict: <=80 bpm at rest (symptomatic pts)
Evaluate triggers and long term strategy
check precipitants (infection, electrolytes, thyroid, alcohol)
Determine CHA2DS2VASc score for long term anticoagulation

sections.title: Stable: Rate Control Protocol
items: Trial IVF 500mL-1L NS if c/f hypovolemia
Metoprolol 5mg IV q5min x3 (if HR >130 or symptomatic & sBP >90): PO metoprolol 25mg PO q6h (max 400mg/day)
Diltiazem 0.25 mg/kg IV bolus (max 25mg): can rebolus after 15 min: drip 2.55 mg/hr: PO ER dose = 10 x [3(infusion rate mg/hr) + 3], max 360mg/day
AVOID diltiazem/verapamil in decompCHF (EF <40%): use amiodarone or digoxin
Digoxin: load 250 mcg q26h, then 125375 mcg daily. Onset 1h, peak 16h. Good for CHF + AFib. Avoid in CKD/ Abn [K]
Abx if c/f sepsis
Amiodarone if rate control fails or rhythm control is desired (see rhythm section)

sections.title: Rhythm Control: Amiodarone
items: On AC for 3 weeks OR 100% confirmed new AFib <48h
IV load: 150mg bolus over 10 min: can repeat
IV drip: 1 mg/min x 6 hours: then 0.5 mg/min x 18 hours (full IV load = 10g)
Transition to PO: 400mg PO TID, then 10g then 200mg PO BID maintenance
Watch for: ↓BP with rapid IV bolus, ↓HR, phlebitis, long term thyroid/pulm/hepatic tox
Can cause stroke if pt converts to sinus rhythm & is not anticoagulated: ensure AC on board

sections.title: Unstable: Synchronized Cardioversion
items: Indications: hemodynamic instability (↓ BP, shock, AMS, active ischemia, pulmonary edema)
Give 2 mg midazolam + 25 mcg fentanyl (will need more if tolerant)
Apply pads: anteriorposterior preferred
Select SYNC mode on defibrillator: confirm Synch marker on R waves
AFib: 200J biphasic; AFL: 50100J biphasic
If unsuccessful: reconfirm SYNC mode is on
WARNING: if pt not anticoagulated & sx onset >48h or unknown. cardiovert risk of stroke

sections.title: AC Strategy
items: **CHA2DS2VASc**: 1 point each for CHF, HTN, age 6574, DM, female sex, vascular disease; 2 points for age >75 & prior stroke/TIA
**Score 0 (male)**: No AC needed
**Score 1 (male)**: Consider AC
**Score >2**: Anticoagulation suggested (DOAC > warfarin for nonsevere MS AFib)
**Precardioversion**: If <48h onset, cardiovert without TEE & continue AC 4 weeks after; if >48h or unknown, get TEE or thetherapeutic AC for 3 weeks first
**Postcardioversion**: Continue AC for 4 weeks regardless of CHA2DS2VASc

sections.title: Initial Workup
items: EKG ASAP
Labs: CBC, BMP, Mg/Phos, TSH, LFTs, coagulation studies, troponin, NTproBNP
Consider UDS (cocaine, stimulants)
CXR: pulmonary edema, vascular congestion
POCUS: LV function, LA/RA size, valve disease, pulmonary HTN, LV thrombus

drugs.name: Metoprolol
drugs.notes: Firstline rate control if no decompensated CHF. Max 400mg/day PO. Avoid if HR <60, SBP <90, active bronchospasm, 2nd/3rd degree AV block.

drugs.name: Diltiazem
drugs.dose: 0.25 mg/kg IV bolus (max 25mg), rebolus x1 after 15 min, then 2.55 mg/hr drip
drugs.notes: PO ER dose = 10 x [3(infusion rate) + 3], max 360mg/day.

drugs.name: Digoxin
drugs.notes: Good for CHF + AFib (no negative inotropy). Onset 1h, peak 16h. Narrow thetherapeutic index: monitor levels & renal function.

drugs.name: Amiodarone
drugs.dose: 150mg IV over 10 min (x13),then1 mg/min x6h,then0.5 mg/min
drugs.notes: Rate & rhythm control. Use when BB/CCBs contraindicated (CHF). PO transition: 400mg BID: 200mg BID. Full load = 10g.

drugs.name: Synchronized cardioversion
drugs.notes: Always confirm SYNC mode ON. Sedate before if time allows. Firstline for hemodynamic instability/AMS .

sections.title: Pearls & Pitfalls
items: AFib with RVR alone rarely causes ↓ BP unless HR >150: AFib dec. preload (loss of atrial kick)
AFib ↓ preload through loss of atrial kick (up to 25% of cardiac output): esp important in diastolic dysfunction, HOCM, MS
NEVER give digoxin, diltiazem, or verapamil in WPW with AFib: can accelerate conduction down accessory pathway & cause VF
AVOID diltiazem & verapamil in decompensated CHF or EF <40%
Flutter is 2:1: if ventricular rate is exactly 150, always suspect flutter with 2:1 block
New AFib: always look for & treat precipitant: rate may resolve once sepsis, PE, hyperthyroidism treated

Van Gelder IC, Rienstra M, Bunting KV, et al. 2024 ESC Guidelines for the Management of Atrial Fibrillation. European Heart Journal. 2024;45(36):33143414. https://doi.org/10.1093/eurheartj/ehae176
January CT, Wann LS, Calkins H, et al. 2019 AHA/ACC/HRS Focused Update on Atrial Fibrillation. Journal of the American College of Cardiology. 2019;74(1):104132.

topics.title: Sick Sinus / Tachy-Brady Syndrome
topics.subtitle: Sinus node dysfunction causing pauses, bradycardia, atrial tachyarrhythmias, or alternating slow and fast rhythms

sections.title: Acute Priorities
items:
Circulation: check pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
Airway: check patency and protect.
Breathing: check WOB, oxygenation, and ventilation.
Place on telemetry, get ECG, and correlate symptoms with the rhythm if possible.
If unstable bradycardia is present, follow symptomatic bradycardia management: atropine first, then transcutaneous pacing or vasoactive support if needed.
If pt is currently in a tachyarrhythmia, treat rhythm based on stability while keeping sinus node dysfunction in mind.
Look immediately for reversible contributors such as medication effect, ischemia, electrolyte disturbance, hypothyroidism, sleep apnea, or autonomic triggers.

sections.title: Background
items: Sick sinus syndrome: dysfunction of SA node function and may also be described as sinus node dysfunction or sinoatrial node disease.
Presentations vary: persistent sinus bradycardia, sinus pauses or arrest, chronotropic incompetence, or periods of atrial tachyarrhythmia alternating with slow sinus recovery.
The tachy-brady form is common and often appears as atrial fibrillation or flutter followed by prolonged pauses when the fast rhythm terminates.
Symptoms come from low cardiac output(loose atrial kick), transient cerebral hypoperfusion, or rapid atrial arrhythmias rather than from one single ECG pattern.

pairs.value: Age-related fibrosis of the sinus node and atrial tissue, ischemic injury, infiltrative disease, remodeling after atrial arrhythmia, or less commonly ion channel disease.
Beta blockers, non-DHP calcium channel blockers, digoxin, amiodarone, other antiarrhythmics, clonidine, and similar nodal suppressing agents.
Hyperkalemia, other electrolyte disturbance, hypothyroidism, hypothermia, sleep apnea, increased vagal tone, and autonomic dysfunction.
Atrial fibrillation or flutter commonly coexists, especially in the tachy-brady phenotype.

sections.title: Typical Clinical Features
items: Syncope or near-syncope
Palpitations or an intermittent sensation of racing followed by pauses
Fatigue, exertional intolerance, or generalized weakness
Dyspnea or chest discomfort
Sleep disruption, confusion, or episodic dizziness

pairs.value: Sinus bradycardia, sinus pauses, sinoatrial exit block, sinus arrest, or failure to mount an appropriate rate with exertion.
AFib, atrial flutter, or atrial tachycardia that is followed by a prolonged pause or marked bradycardia after conversion.
A single ECG may be normal. Diagnosis often requires telemetry, Holter monitoring, event monitoring, or loop recording tied to symptoms.
Sinus tachycardia, focal atrial tachycardia, AVNRT, AVRT, atrial flutter with fixed block, or other PSVT.
AFib, atrial flutter with variable conduction, multifocal atrial tachycardia, or sinus rhythm with frequent ectopy.
Inferior MI, med toxicity, hyperkalemia, hypothyroidism, hypothermia, vasovagal episodes, increased ICP, Lyme disease, or BRASH syndrome.

sections.title: Evaluation
items: ECG now Check old ekgs for chronic sinus bradycardia, pauses, or atrial arrhythmias.
BMP, magnesium, calcium, TSH, troponin, and med review including digoxin or AV nodal blockers.
Consider ambulatory Holter, event monitor, or implantable loop recorder when episodes are intermittent.
Evaluate for reversible causes before labeling the problem as intrinsic sinus node disease.

sections.title: Management
items: check rhythm and stability: bradycardia vs tachycardia, vitals, mentation, shock, ischemic pain, and acute heart failure.
Treat reversible causes: review nodal blocking meds, correct hypoxemia, ischemia, electrolyte problems, hypothyroidism, and metabolic derangements.
If symptomatic bradycardia or hemodynamic compromise is present:
- Atropine 0.5-1 mg IV q3-5 min up to 2 mg total; avoid in heart transplant pts without autonomic reinnervation.
- If atropine fails or is contraindicated: epinephrine 2-10 mcg/min IV, dopamine 5-20 mcg/kg/min IV, or cautious isoproterenol.
- Transcutaneous pacing is reasonable; place pads early and confirm electrical plus mechanical capture.
Use temporary transvenous pacing only for persistent unstable bradycardia refractory to medical therapy while awaiting resolution or permanent pacing.
If unstable tachyarrhythmia is present: synchronized cardioversion.
If stable narrow-complex tachycardia is present:
- Start with vagal maneuvers.
- Then consider adenosine for SVT, or diltiazem/verapamil or IV beta-blocker for rate control depending on rhythm and EF.
- In AFib/flutter, use nondihydropyridine CCBs or beta-blockers first-line; consider amiodarone if HF limits those agents.
Plan for permanent pacemaker if symptomatic bradycardia persists or recurs.
Avoid antiarrhythmics for tachycardia control until pacing is secured if bradycardia risk is high.

sections.title: Pearls & Pitfalls
items: Do not diagnose sick sinus syndrome from one isolated heart rate alone; the key issue is symptom-producing sinus node dysfunction.
Post-conversion pauses after AFib or flutter are a major clue to tachy-brady syndrome.
!!Before escalating nodal blockers for a fast atrial rhythm, ask whether pt may already have sinus node disease underneath.
Always review reversible causes before committing a pt to a permanent device!!.

pairs.value: Syncope, prolonged pauses, recurrent symptomatic bradycardia, hemodynamic instability, need for pacing, concern for ischemia, or active tachy-brady cycling is present.
Pt is stable after reversal of an obvious trigger, symptoms are improving, and telemetry is available while the workup continues.

sections.title: Complications
items: Recurrent syncope or injury from pauses
Atrial fibrillation or flutter
Progressive need for permanent pacing
Associated conduction disease such as AV block in some pts

sections.title: References & Resources
items: Kusumoto FM, Schoenfeld MH, Barrett C, et al. 2018 ACC/AHA/HRS Guideline on the Evaluation and Management of Patients With Bradycardia and Cardiac Conduction Delay. Circulation. 2019;140(8):e382e482. https://doi.org/10.1161/CIR.0000000000000628
Semelka M, Gera J, Usman S. Sick sinus syndrome: a review. American Family Physician. 2013;87(10):691-696.

topics.title: SVT (Supraventricular Tachycardia)
topics.subtitle: Sudden regular tachycardia, palpitations, chest pressure, lightheadedness

sections.title: Acute Management
items: Airway: patency? protect? Breathing: check WOB, oxygenation, and ventilation.
Circulation: check pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
Hemodynamically stable?
- If unstable (↓ BP, AMS, shock, ischemic chest pain, ac. HF/pulm edema): synch cardioversion 200J: 25mcg fent + 2 versed
- If sync failing & pt crashing: proceed to unsynchronized shocks
Call cardiology Early for wide-complex, rec. episodes, or dx uncertainty

sections.title: Chart Check / Hx.
items: Prior ECGs: baseline QRS, BBB, delta wave/preexcitation, prior AF/flutter/SVT
Cardiac hx: CAD, HFrEF, valvular dz, congenital heart dz, prior ablation
Current meds: beta agonists, decongestants, stimulants, vasodilators, catecholamines, loop diuretics, antipsychotics, inotropes
Missed home BB or CCB
Recent triggers: infxn, fever, bleed, dehydration, pain, ETOH, caffeine, hyperthyroid sx
Lytes, Hgb, troponin, TSH
Known WPW/accessory pathway or hx suggesting it

sections.title: Exam
items: First decision: stable vs unstable
Treat as unstable if tachycardia is causing: ↓ BP, acute AMS, shock, ischemic chest discomfort, acute HF/pulm edema
HR & whether rhythm feels regular
BP trend (not one value)
Mentation assessment
Chest pain, dyspnea, crackles, JVP, edema
Perfusion: diaphoresis, cool extremities, delayed cap refill
Signs of volume depletion, infection, bleeding, withdrawal, stimulant use
Tachyarrhythmia clinically important when rate ≥150/min, though mgmt depends on sx & perfusion, not rate alone

pairs.value: Regular narrowcomplex tachycardia: most commonly AVNRT, AVRT, or focal atrial tachycardia
AV nodal reentrant tachycardia: most common SVT subtype overall
AV reentrant tachycardia: involves accessory pathway (WPW if overt preexcitation)
Dangerous: AV nodal blockers (adenosine, BB, CCB, digoxin) contraindicated in preexcited AF/flutter. Can ↑ accessory pathway conduction: VF
Unstable = tachycardia causing ↓ BP, AMS, shock, ischemic chest pain, or acute HF. Unstable: synchronized cardioversion

images.caption: AVNRT
images.description: Reference ECG/image for AV nodal reentrant tachycardia.

pairs.value: AVNRT, orthodromic AVRT, focal atrial tachycardia, atrial flutter with fixed block, sinus tachycardia
AFib, atrial flutter with variable block, multifocal atrial tachycardia (MAT)
VT (strongly consider), SVT with aberrancy, antidromic AVRT / preexcited rhythm
Infection/fever, hypovolemia, anemia, lyte abnormalities, hyperthyroidism, ETOH, caffeine, stimulants, Meds exposure
Determine regular vs irregular, narrow vs wide QRS —don't call everything 'SVT'

sections.title: Management
items: Check hemodynamic stability
Unstable (hypotension, AMS, shock, chest pain, acute HF): immediate synchronized cardioversion
Stable: try vagal maneuvers first
- Modified Valsalva: strain, then passive leg raise supine
- Carotid sinus massage only after confirming no bruit
If vagal maneuvers fail: give IV adenosine
Keep pt on a cardiac monitor with pads available
Avoid adenosine in preexcited AF, severe asthma, or post-heart transplant pts
If adenosine fails or is contraindicated: use IV diltiazem, verapamil, or beta blocker when appropriate
Avoid AV nodal blockers if preexcitation or VT is suspected
If refractory: consider cardioversion or antiarrhythmic therapy

sections.title: Practical Modified Valsalva
items: For cooperative stable pt only
Sit pt semirecumbent
Have pt blow forcefully into syringe for ~15 seconds
Immediately lay pt flat & raise legs to ~45° for ~15 seconds
Return upright & recheck rhythm
Modified Valsalva has better conversion rates than standard Valsalva (AAFP evidence review)

sections.title: Cardioversion: Step by Step
items: Confirm pt has pulse & tachycardia is causing instability
Call for more support, place pt on monitor/defib with pads attached
Ensure suction, O2, IV access, & airway equipment at bedside
Turn on SYNC mode: verify sync markers tracking each QRS. Resync after each shock
Set 100J synchronized for narrowcomplex tachycardia
Sedate if feasible (diazepam ± fentanyl)
Clear pt & deliver synchronized shock
Recheck rhythm, pulse, BP, mentation, sx NOW. Get postconversion ECG
If in tachycardia: reenable SYNC, escalate energy, consider antiarrhythmic, consult cardiology/ICU
If sync delayed & pt crashing: proceed to unsynchronized shocks

pairs.value: during tachycardia & after conversion: compare to prior
Lyte abnormalities (K, Mg, Ca) can precipitate or sustain SVT
Check for anemia as a trigger
If chest pain, ischemic concern, or prolonged tachycardia
If unexplained/recurrent SVT or hyperthyroid features
CXR, infectious workup, VBG/ABG, lactate, tox screen: only if suggested by presentation
telemetry, pulse ox, frequent BP. Defib pads early if sx or marginal

drugs.name: Adenosine
drugs.dose: attach pads: 6mg rapid IVP, then 12mg rapid IVP if no conversion, then may repeat 12mg x1
drugs.notes: Attach to monitor/ECG before dosing. 1/2life ~10s.

drugs.name: Metoprolol tartrate
drugs.dose: 5mg IV over 12 min; may repeat q5min up to 15mg total
drugs.contraindications: ↓ BP, decomp HFrEF, severe ↓ HR, preexcitation, 2nd/3rd degree AVB
drugs.notes: Only if pt is truly stable. Avoid if rhythm dx uncertain

drugs.name: Diltiazem
drugs.dose: 0.25 mg/kg (~10mg) IV over 2 min; if needed 0.35 mg/kg after 15 min
drugs.contraindications: ↓ BP, decomp HFrEF, preexcitation, WPW, widecomplex tachy
drugs.notes: NonDHP CCB. Don't use with IV beta-blocker concurrently.

drugs.name: Verapamil
drugs.dose: 2.55mg IV slow push; may repeat carefully if needed
drugs.contraindications: ↓ BP, decomp HFrEF, preexcitation, WPW, widecomplex tachy of uncertain origin
drugs.notes: NonDHP CCB. Use cautiously: more negative inotropy than diltiazem

sections.title: References & Resources
items: Page RL, Joglar JA, Caldwell MA, et al. 2015 ACC/AHA/HRS Guideline for the Management of Adult Patients With Supraventricular Tachycardia. Circulation. 2016;133(14):e506e574. https://doi.org/10.1161/CIR.0000000000000311

Brugada J, Katritsis DG, Arbelo E, et al. 2019 ESC Guidelines for the Management of Patients with Supraventricular Tachycardia. European Heart Journal. 2020;41(5):655720. https://doi.org/10.1093/eurheartj/ehz467

topics.title: Ventricular Tachycardia
topics.subtitle: Widecomplex tachycardia, palpitations, syncope, hypotension, arrest risk

sections.title: Acute Management
items: Circulation: check pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
Airway: check patency and protect.
Breathing: check WOB, oxygenation, and ventilation.
Pulseless VT: defibrillation: unsynchronized 200J biphasic; begin CPR per ACLS
Unstable with pulse: synchronized cardioversion: 200J; sedate if conscious & time permits
If stable monomorphic VT: procainamide 2050 mg/min IV (max 17 mg/kg or 1 g): first line (PROCAMIO trial)
Alternative for stable VT (esp with acute MI or ↓ EF): amiodarone 150 mg IV over 10 min
If polymorphic VT / torsades: Mg 2 g IV + unsynchronized defibrillation if deteriorating + stop ALL QTprolonging drugs
If irregular WCT (c.f AFib with WPW): do NOT give AV nodal blockers: procainamide or cardioversion
EKG (compare to prior); troponin, BMP, digoxin level
If wide QRS (>200 ms) with rate <120: think ↑K: calcium, insulin/D50, bicarb

sections.title: Chart Check / Hx.
items: Cardiac hx: prior MI (scarmed. VT), cardiomyopathy, CHF, EF, valvular disease
Prior arrhythmia hx: VT episodes, ICD, ablation
ICD present? (shocks or failed shocks: interrogate device)
meds: QT-prolonging drugs, digoxin, antiarrhythmics, BB
Electrolyte hx: renal disease, diuretics (hypoK, hypoMg)
Ischemia risk factors: known CAD, recent chest pain, prior PCI/CABG
Toxicologic: TCA, cocaine, digoxin
Family hx: sudden cardiac death, long QT, Brugada, HCM, ARVC
Symptoms: palpitations, syncope/presyncope, chest pain, SOB, AMS

pairs.value: HR>120 bpm; BP may nl. (stable VT) or ↓↓ (unstable); if HR <120 with wide QRS: think ↑K or AIVR
↓ BP, AMS, chest pain, signs of shock, acute HF: unstable = immediate cardioversion
Regular tachycardia; cannon A waves in JVP (AV dissociation: pathognomonic for VT); variable S1 intensity
Crackles if acute pulmonary edema from ↓ cardiac output
AMS from hypoperfusion; syncope hx
Cool, mottled if cardiogenic shock; check for ICD pocket (left anterior chest)
≥3 consecutive wide QRS complexes (>120 ms) originating from ventricles at rate >120 bpm
Uniform QRS morphology: single reentrant circuit (often scar-related postMI)
Varying QRS morphology: includes torsades de pointes (twisting of points around baseline)
Polymorphic VT in setting of prolonged QT: classic sinusoidal twisting pattern; tx: Mg, overdrive pacing, stop QTprolonging drugs
Nonsustained VT: <30 sec duration; still requires workup for structural heart disease
>30 sec duration or causes hemodynamic compromise before 30 sec
≥3 episodes of sustained VT within 24 hrs: requires escalated therapy, EP consult, consider overdrive pacing or ablation
QRS ≥120 ms during tachycardia; 80% is VT (>90% if known cardiac disease): assume VT until proven otherwise

sections.title: EKG Features Favoring VT > SVT w/ Abberancy

pairs.value: P waves & QRS complexes march independently: pathognomonic for VT
Hybrid morphology (simultaneous ventricular + SVT activation): strongly favors VT
Narrow QRS complex intermixed: atrial impulse "captures" ventricle during VT: strongly favors VT
All precordial leads (V1-V6) showing same QRS direction (all positive or all negative): favors VT
"Northwest axis" (negative in I & aVF): VT
>160 ms = more likely VT; >200 ms = ↑K or Na channel blocker toxicity
Absence of RS complex in all precordial leads; RS interval >100 ms; AV dissociation; morphology criteria in V1/V6
Treating VT as SVT can fatal; treating SVT as VT is rarely harmful
Prior MI with scar (most common), dilated cardiomyopathy, HCM, ARVC, valvular disease
Acute MI or unstable angina: VT may presenting rhythm
↑K (very wide, rate <120), hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia
Digoxin toxicity (bidirectional VT is classic), TCA OD, cocaine, QT-prolonging drugs: torsades
SVT with aberrancy (pre-existing or related BBB), aflutter with BBB, sinus tach with BBB, antidromic AVRT (WPW)
AFib with BBB, AFib with WPW ( avoid AV nodal blockers), polymorphic VT / torsades
Severe acidosis, acute HF, Brugada syndrome, long QT syndrome, catecholaminergic polymorphic VT

sections.title: Management & Pearls
items: check hemodynamic stability
- Unstable (hypotension, AMS, shock, chest pain, acute HF) > immediate cardioversion
- Stable > proceed to evaluation and reversible causes
Monomorphic VT: synchronized cardioversion
Polymorphic VT: unsynchronized highenergy defibrillation
If stable: get ECG and check reversible causes
Evaluate for ischemia, electrolyte abnormalities, and drug induced proarrhythmia
Get echocardiography to check for structural heart disease
If stable: choose pharmacologic therapy based on structural heart disease
- With structural heart disease: IV procainamide preferred; amiodarone or sotalol are alternatives
- Without structural heart disease: consider verapamil or beta blockers for idiopathic VT
Avoid verapamil/diltiazem in widecomplex tachycardia of unknown origin or myocardial dysfunction
Avoid lidocaine except possibly for acute MIrelated VT
For polymorphic VT or torsades: give IV magnesium
Correct electrolytes, especially potassium
Use overdrive pacing or isoproterenol if QT prolongation is present
Secondary prevention: consider ICD implantation
ICD indicated after VF or hemodynamically unstable VT not due to reversible causes
ICD indicated for structural heart disease with spontaneous sustained VT
Stable monomorphic VT often still warrants ICD; ablation may substitute in select cases
Longterm VT suppression in ICD pts: beta blockers first line
Amiodarone plus beta blocker is common for recurrent VT or ICD shocks
Sotalol is an alternative if no severe LV dysfunction, renal impairment, or VT storm
Mexiletine is often added for breakthrough VT
Assume wide complex tachycardia is VT until proven otherwise: 80% of WCT is VT (>90% with known cardiac disease)
Treating VT as SVT can fatal; treating SVT as VT is rarely harmful: when in doubt, treat as VT
Do NOT give AV nodal blockers for irregular WCT (adenosine, diltiazem, digoxin, BB): causes VF in WPW
Procainamide > amiodarone for stable VT (PROCAMIO trial) unless acute MI or ↓ EF (n amiodarone preferred)
Very wide QRS (>200 ms) with rate <120 bpm: think ↑K before VT: give calcium first
Cannon A waves in JVP = AV dissociation = VT: quick bedside exam finding
Bidirectional VT (alternating QRS axis) is classic for digoxin toxicity: check level, give DigiFab
Electrical storm (≥3 VT episodes in 24 hrs): escalate to overdrive pacing, deep sedation, EP consult, consider ablation
Admit ALL VT pts: even if successfully cardioverted; high recurrence risk + for structural workup + ICD evaluation

sections.title: Orders
items: EKG: compare to prior; look for AV dissociation, concordance, fusion/capture beats
BMP: K+ & Mg2+ critically important; correct now if abnl.
Troponin: acute ischemia may trigger
Digoxin level if on digoxin (toxicity = bidirectional VT, accelerated junctional rhythm)
ABG/VBG: metabolic acidosis can perpetuate VT
CBC
Cardiac monitoring ( mandatory)
Toxicology screen if clinical suspicion (TCA, cocaine)
CXR: cardiomegaly, pulmonary edema
TTE: check EF, wall motion abnormalities, structural disease (often deferred until after acute stabilization)

drugs.name: Procainamide
drugs.dose: 2050 mg/min IV until suppressed; max 17 mg/kg or 1 g total; maintenance 14 mg/min
drugs.notes: Firstline for stable monomorphic VT (PROCAMIO trial: 67% termination vs 38% for amiodarone). Monitor QRS width & BP during infusion

drugs.name: Amiodarone
drugs.dose: 150 mg IV over 10 min, then 1 mg/min × 6 hrs (360 mg), then 0.5 mg/min × 18 hrs (540 mg)
drugs.contraindications: Severe sinus node disease, 2nd/3rd degree block without pacer, cardiogenic shock
drugs.notes: Preferred if acute MI or ↓ EF. Slower onset than procainamide. Total 24hr dose ~1050 mg

drugs.name: Lidocaine
drugs.dose: 11.5 mg/kg IV bolus; may repeat 0.50.75 mg/kg q5-10 min (max 3 mg/kg); infusion 14 mg/min
drugs.notes: Alternative for ischemiarelated VT; fewer hemodynamic effects than procainamide. ↓ dose in liver disease & elderly

drugs.name: Magnesium sulfate
drugs.dose: 2 g IV over 5-10 min; repeat 1x PRN
drugs.notes: First for torsades de pointes; replete if Mg <2.0 in any VT

drugs.name: Adenosine (diagnostic)
drugs.dose: 6 mg rapid IV push, then 12 mg if no response
drugs.contraindications: DONT use for irregular WCT (AFib with WPW: VF risk)
drugs.notes: Diagnostic trial for regular monomorphic WCT: will NOT terminate VT but may unmask underlying SVT. Safe to give in stable pt

drugs.name: Sodium bicarbonate
drugs.notes: For Na channel blocker toxicity (TCA, cocaine) w/ wide QRS; also for ↑K with VT

pairs.key: ICU / immediate higher level of care if
pairs.value: persistent respiratory distress, high-flow or NIV need, hemodynamic instability, vasopressor need, severe arrhythmia, airway risk, neurologic decline, or a trajectory suggesting pt may crash on the floor.

sections.title: References & Resources
items: AlKhatib SM, Stevenson WG, Ackerman MJ, et al. 2017 AHA/ACC/HRS Guideline for Management of Patients With Ventricular Arrhythmias and the Prevention of Sudden Cardiac Death. Circulation. 2018;138(13):e272e391. https://doi.org/10.1161/CIR.0000000000000549

Zeppenfeld K, TfeltHansen J, de Riva M, et al. 2022 ESC Guidelines for the Management of Patients with Ventricular Arrhythmias and the Prevention of Sudden Cardiac Death. European Heart Journal. 2022;43(40):39974126. https://doi.org/10.1093/eurheartj/ehac262

topics.title: Cardiogenic Shock / ADHF
topics.subtitle: Hypotension, dyspnea, pulmonary edema, cool extremities, elevated lactate

sections.title: ABCs
items:
Circulation: check pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
Airway: check patency and protect.
Breathing: check WOB, oxygenation, and ventilation.
Dyspnea, tachypnea, hypoxia: O2, then BiPAP early: AVOID intubation (removal of respiratory drive: cardiovascular collapse)
ICU if SBP <90 for >30 min or requiring pressor/inotrope
Monitoring: telemetry & pulse ox, arterial line, consider CVP/PA catheter
Target SpO2 >92%
Can't miss: new MI: get EKG & troponin now

sections.title: Chart Check & Exam
items: Dry weight, last TTE (EF, valvular disease, wall motion), prior cath
Precipitant: weight trend, med nonadherence, new meds, recent MI, infection
Exam: general appearance, AMS, warm vs cold extremities, cap refill, JVP, IVC on POCUS, LE edema, crackles, murmurs (new MR/TR = emergency)
POCUS: Blines (pulmonary edema), large chambers, poor contractility, pericardial effusion
Profile: Warm/Wet, Cold/Wet, Cold/Dry, Warm/Dry

pairs.value: Adequate perfusion, volume overloaded. MCC ADHF.: Diuresis (IV Lasix), afterload reduction if HTN.
Low output + volume overloaded. Worst prognosis.: Inotrope (dobutamine/milrinone) + diuresis if MAP allows. Consider MCS.
Low output, hypovolemic or severely ↓ preload.: Cautious fluid challenge 250500mL + inotrope.
Compensated. Optimize oral meds. No acute intervention needed.
nl. ~5 mmHg
nl. ~25/5 mmHg
nl. ~25/10 mmHg
nl. ~10 mmHg (goal 1418 in ADHF management)
nl. ~125/10 mmHg
CI >2.2 L/min/m2, central venous sat >65%, SVR 8001200, CVP 812
New MI (RCA: RV failure; LAD: LV failure), acute MR/TR (pap. rupture), aortic dissection, tamponade, high risk PE
Ischemia/MI, ADHF, myocarditis, severe AS, acute valvular regurgitation, arrhythmia (AFib with RVR, VT)
Cardiac tamponade, severe PE, tension PTX, severe AS
Med nonadherence, diet, Infection/sepsis, arrhythmia, renal failure, NSAIDs/steroids, Cardiotoxic drugs.

sections.title: Management
items: Classify clinical profile: warm/cold and wet/dry
Warm and wet > ADHF
Cold and wet > cardiogenic shock
Cold and dry > consider hypovolemia or RV failure
If cardiogenic shock: confirm diagnosis and stage severity
Look for SBP <90 plus endorgan hypoperfusion: lactate, oliguria, AMS
Use SCAI shock classification if avail
Activate shock team early if avail
Identify and treat precipitants: CHAMP
Acute coronary syndrome > urgent revascularization
Hypertensive emergency
Arrhythmias
Mechanical complications: papillary rupture, VSD, tamponade
Pulmonary embolism
Optimize volume status
ADHF: IV loop diuretics; start about 2.5x chronic oral dose
Cardiogenic shock: check volume cautiously and avoid overdiuresis
If diuretic resistant: add a thiazide
Vasopressor/inotrope selection in cardiogenic shock
Firstline vasopressor: norepinephrine
Firstline inotrope: dobutamine
Alternative inotrope: milrinone
Avoid epinephrine when possible
Consider invasive hemodynamic monitoring
PA catheter can define hemodynamic subset
Use PA catheter data to guide fluids, vasoactives, and MCS decisions
If refractory: consider mechanical circulatory support
IABP: modest support and bridge option
Impella: greater hemodynamic support
VAECMO: full cardiopulmonary support for biventricular failure

sections.title: Initial Management by Scenario
items: Pulmonary edema + ↑ WOB: BiPAP + IV Lasix 4080mg IV
HTN or normotensive + wet: afterload reduction (hydralazine, nitroprusside, nitroglycerin) + diuresis
↓ BP: passive leg raise or cautious 250500mL fluid bolus: check response
H↓ BP + no CHF: 1L NS pressurebagged, recheck
↓ BP persists: start Levo, then +inotrope (dobutamine>milrinone)
Wet + cold + SBP >90: diurese + inotrope
Severe effusion contributing to low output: consider thoracentesis
Refractory to medical therapy: mechanical circulatory support (IABP, Impella, ECMO): consult cardiology/CT surgery

drugs.name: Furosemide (Lasix)
drugs.dose: 4080mg IV (or 2.5x home PO dose if on chronic Lasix)
drugs.notes: Firstline for pulmonary edema & volume overload. Can convert to drip 540mg/hr for refractory overload.

drugs.name: Norepinephrine (Levophed)
drugs.notes: Firstline vasopressor in cardiogenic shock. Start BEFORE inotrope: giving inotrope first may drop BP furr.

drugs.name: Dobutamine
drugs.notes: Inotrope + mild vasodilator. Use for Cold/Wet profile. Avoid if on long term BB: milrinone preferred.

drugs.name: Milrinone
drugs.notes: Inodilator (↓ afterload). Preferred if on BB. Renally cleared: use caution in AKI. Will drop BP.

drugs.name: Nitroglycerin
drugs.notes: Preload & mild afterload reduction. Useful for HTN + pulmonary edema.

drugs.name: Nitroprusside
drugs.notes: Potent afterload reducer. Goal SVR 8001200, MAP >60. Use for severe HTN or high afterload state. Cyanide toxicity risk with prolonged use.

drugs.name: Hydralazine
drugs.notes: Oral afterload reduction. Paired with nitrates for ACE/ARBintolerant pts (BiDil regimen).

sections.title: Pearls & Pitfalls
items: AVOID intubation whenever possible in cardiogenic shock: removal of respiratory drive causes cardiovascular collapse
NEVER give inotrope before vasopressor in hypotensive cardiogenic shock: inodilators drop BP furr
For pts on long term BB: milrinone is preferred inotrope (beta receptor already blocked)
Positive pressure ventilation (BiPAP/intubation) can ↑ RV afterload: use with caution in RV failure
Severe AS + cardiogenic shock: AVOID high dose afterload reducers (ACE inhibitors, high dose nitrates): pt depends on elevated afterload for coronary perfusion
Rapidly decreasing afterload in AS can precipitate cardiovascular collapse
New MR or TR murmur in ACS context = papillary muscle rupture: emergent cardiac surgery consultation
Free wall rupture postMI: emergent surgical repair
Overshooting diuresis can reduce preload excessively: worsening low output: monitor closely

sections.title: References & Resources
items: Heidenreich PA, Bozkurt B, Aguilar D, et al. 2022 AHA/ACC/HFSA Guideline for the Management of Heart Failure. Circulation. 2022;145(18):e895e1032. https://doi.org/10.1161/CIR.0000000000001063

McDonagh TA, Metra M, Adamo M, et al. 2023 Focused Update of the 2021 ESC Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Treatment of Acute and Chronic Heart Failure. European Heart Journal. 2023;44(37):36273639. https://doi.org/10.1093/eurheartj/ehad195
van Diepen S, Katz JN, Albert NM, et al. Contemporary Management of Cardiogenic Shock: AHA Scientific Statement. Circulation. 2017;136(16):e52e68. https://doi.org/10.1161/CIR.0000000000000525

topics.title: Cardiac Tamponade
topics.subtitle: Hypotension, JVD, muffled heart sounds, pulsus paradoxus, effusion

sections.title: Acute Management
items:
Circulation: check pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
Airway: check patency and protect.
Breathing: check WOB, oxygenation, and ventilation.
ABCs: monitoring, pulse ox, largebore IV access × 2
**IV fluid bolus**: NS 500–1000 mL to augment preload & temporize hemodynamics
Avoid positive pressure vent: ↓venous return, worsens tamponade
Keep pt upright or legs elevated to optimize preload
Stat bedside TTE to confirm dx
Call cardiology / interventional cardiology for urgent pericardiocentesis
If periarrest: bedside emergency pericardiocentesis (subxiphoid approach)
Transfer to ICU

sections.title: Chart Check / Hx.
items: Known pericardial effusion: size trend on prior echos
Recent cardiac surgery, catheterization, or ablation (iatrogenic)
Malignancy (lung, breast, lymphoma, melanoma)
Anticoagulation status: hemorrhagic effusion risk
Autoimmune disease, renal failure (uremia), recent pericarditis
Trauma Hx.

sections.title: Exam
items: Beck's triad: ↓ BP + JVD + muffled heart sounds
Pulsus paradoxus >10 mmHg: measure with manual BP cuff
Tachycardia (compensatory), tachypnea
Cold extremities, delayed capillary refill, oliguria
AMS if severely hypoperfused

pairs.value: Pericardial fluid accumulation causing compression of cardiac chambers: impaired diastolic filling: ↓ cardiac output
↓ BP, JVD, muffled heart sounds: present in only ~10–40% of cases
SBP drop >10 mmHg during inspiration. Reflects exaggerated ventricular interdependence.
Rate of accumulation matters more than volume: rapid 150 mL causes tamponade; slow 1L may tolerated
MCC of large effusions. Lung, breast, lymphoma, melanoma. Often hemorrhagic.
Postcardiac cath, pacemaker placement, ablation, cardiac surgery. May present acutely.
Viral, uremic, autoimmune, postMI (Dressler). Effusion may progress to tamponade.
Penetrating or blunt chest trauma. Consider in all unstable trauma pts with JVD
Type A dissection with hemopericardium: surgical emergency
Tension pneumothorax, massive PE: all cause obstructive shock with JVD
Urgent pericardial drainage is definitive therapy;don't rely on temporizing measures alone.
Use cautious fluid boluses to augment preload & vasopressors if needed while arranging emergent pericardiocentesis or surgery.
Avoid positivepressure ventilation & unnecessary preload reduction because they can worsen obstructive shock.
Monitor for recurrence, send pericardial fluid studies when geted, & treat underlying etiology.
Postpericardiocentesis: leave drain in place (pigtail catheter) until output <25–50 mL/day
Serial TTE to monitor for reaccumulation
Treat underlying cause (chemo for malignant, dialysis for uremic, abx for purulent)
Recurrent effusions: consider pericardial window (CT surgery consult)
Monitor for constrictive pericarditis as late complication
Bedside TTE (RA/RV collapse, IVC plethora), ECG (low voltage, electrical alternans, sinus tach)
CBC, BMP, troponin, coags (PT/INR, PTT), type & screen
CXR: waterbottle silhouette if effusion >250 mL. CT if stable & etiology unclear.
Send for: cell count, protein, LDH, glucose, cytology, gram stain/culture, AFB, adenosine deaminase

drugs.name: nl. Saline
drugs.notes: Volume expansion to augment preload. Temporizing measure only.

drugs.name: Norepinephrine
drugs.notes: If hypotensive despite fluids. Bridge to pericardiocentesis.

drugs.name: Atropine
drugs.notes: For reflex ↓ HR. Avoid if tachycardic.

pairs.key: ICU / immediate higher level of care if
pairs.value: persistent respiratory distress, high-flow or NIV need, hemodynamic instability, vasopressor need, severe arrhythmia, airway risk, neurologic decline, or a trajectory suggesting pt may crash on the floor.

sections.title: References & Resources
items: Adler Y, Charron P, Imazio M, et al. 2015 ESC Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Management of Pericardial Diseases. European Heart Journal. 2015;36(42):29212964. https://doi.org/10.1093/eurheartj/ehv318
Defined Approach to Pericardiocentesis. Roberts & Hedges' Clinical Procedures in Emergency Medicine and Acute Care, 7th edition. 2019.

topics.title: Hypertensive Emergency
topics.subtitle: Severe hypertension with chest pain, neuro deficits, AKI, pulmonary edema

sections.title: ABCs & Initial Measures
items:
Circulation: check pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
Airway: check patency and protect.
Breathing: check WOB, oxygenation, and ventilation.
Pulse oximetry & O2 to maintain SpO2 >92%
cardiac monitor
Two pIVs: if IV meds
Foley catheter: urine output if renal damage

pairs.value: SBP ≥180 mmHg or DBP ≥120 mmHg with acute end organ damage. MCC: poorly controlled primary HTN.
H/A, n/v, AMS, lethargy, blurred vision, seizures, coma
AKI, hematuria, proteinuria, RBC casts
Ac CHF, pulmonary edema, chest pain, ACS
Hypertensive encephalopathy, PRES (posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome: MRI dx), ICH, stroke
Papilledema, retinal hemorrhages
Microangiopathic hemolytic anemia (MAHA): peripheral smear
Asymptomatic = severe HTN without endorgan damage —lower BP over 2448h with PO agents. Emergency = endorgan damage: IV therapy.
1st:HR control with esmolol (target HR <60), 2) add nicardipine or nitroprusside for SBP <120 within 20 min. NO pure vasodilators without betablockade (reflex tachycardia worsens shear stress).
Nicardipine or labetalol. Reduce MAP 2025% in 60 min. MRI brain for PRES. ↓BP: improves AMS fast(hours).
Target SBP <140 acutely (INTERACT2, ATACHII). Nicardipine or labetalol. Reverse AC (see below). Neurosurgery C/S.
Labetalol 20mg IV (can repeat), or hydralazine 5-10mg IV, or nicardipine gtt. Magnesium sulfate 4g IV load: 1g/hr gtt for seizure prophylaxis/tx. Definitive tx = delivery.
Nitroglycerin or nitroprusside (afterload reduction) + IV furosemide. NO labetalol (negative inotropy).
Phentolamine 5-10mg IV bolus (alpha blocker). NEVER give betablocker alone without alpha blockade first.

sections.title: Management
items: Distinguish hypertensive emergency from severe hypertension without target organ damage
Hypertensive emergency: SBP >180 and/or DBP >120 with acute target organ damage
Severe hypertension without target organ damage: intensify oral therapy and arrange follow up
check for target organ damage
Neurologic: encephalopathy, ICH, ischemic stroke
Cardiac: ACS, acute pulmonary edema
Vascular: aortic dissection
Renal: AKI
Obstetric: preeclampsia/eclampsia
Admit to ICU and start IV antihypertensive therapy
Use shortacting, titratable IV agents
Use continuous BP monitoring; consider arterial line with nitroprusside
Set BP target based on the clinical scenario
Aortic dissection: SBP <120 and HR <60 within 20 min
Acute pulmonary edema, ACS, or HF: SBP <140 within 1 hr
ICH: SBP 140150 within 1 hr; avoid <140
Ischemic stroke before thrombolysis: BP <185/110
Ischemic stroke without thrombolysis and BP <220/120: no acute reduction for 4872 hr
All other emergencies: lower BP by 25% in first hour, then to 160/100 over 26 hr
Select IV agent based on comorbidity
Aortic dissection: esmolol or labetalol first; betablock before vasodilator
Acute pulmonary edema: nitroglycerin, clevidipine, or nitroprusside; avoid beta blockers
ACS: esmolol or nitroglycerin; avoid hydralazine
AKI: clevidipine, fenoldopam, or nicardipine
Eclampsia/preeclampsia: labetalol, hydralazine, or nicardipine; avoid ACEi/ARB and nitroprusside
Pheochromocytoma/catecholamine excess: phentolamine, nicardipine, or clevidipine
General: nicardipine or labetalol; avoid hydralazine first line
Avoid excessive BP reduction
Overshoot to SBP <100120 is associated with increased mortality
If BP drops too far: stop IV agents and consider IV fluids with or without vasopressors

pairs.value: Treat HTN emergency only when severe BP elevation is accompanied by acute endorgan injury.
Lower MAP gradually,by no more than ~2025% in first hour unless specific emergency requires different target.
Use nicardipine, clevidipine, labetalol, or another appropriate titratable IV agent based on the organ system involved.
Aortic syndromes, stroke, pulmonary edema, eclampsia, & catecholamine excess each have different BP goals & drug preferences.

sections.title: Diagnostic Workup
items: Labs: CBC with peripheral smear (MAHA), BMP, Mg, UA (RBC/protein/casts)
Urine pregnancy test: preeclampsia/eclampsia
Urine Tox: cocaine, sympathomimetics
EKG: LVH, ischemia, strain pattern
Troponin & NTproBNP if c/f cards
Noncontrast head CT: r/o ICH or cerebral edema if neuro sx
MRI brain: PRES or ischemic lesions
CXR: pulmonary edema, wide mediastinum (dissection)
Renal US or TTE: if AKI or cardiac s/s

drugs.name: Nicardipine
drugs.notes: Max 15 mg/hr. CCB: titratable. Good for most hypertensive emergencies including neurologic.

drugs.name: Clevidipine
drugs.dose: Start 12 mg/hr, double q90sec initially; slow titration q5-10min as target approaches
drugs.notes: Max 21 mg/hr. Ultrashortacting CCB. Most titratable agent available. Lipid emulsion: contraindicated in egg/soy allergy, pancreatitis.

drugs.name: Labetalol
drugs.notes: Max 300mg/24h. Alpha + beta blocker. Good for aortic dissection (with esmolol).

drugs.name: Esmolol
drugs.dose: Load 500 mcg/kg over 1 min,then25 mcg/kg/min; titrate to max 200 mcg/kg/min
drugs.notes: Ultrashortacting beta1 blocker. Firstline for aortic dissection (HR control). Halflife ~9 min: easy to titrate.

drugs.name: Fenoldopam
drugs.dose: Start 0.10.3 mcg/kg/min, titrate by 0.1 mcg/kg/min q15min
drugs.notes: Max 1.6 mcg/kg/min. Dopamine1 agonist: enhances renal perfusion & natriuresis. Preferred if AKI present. May cause reflex tachycardia.

drugs.name: Nitroprusside
drugs.notes: Potent arterial + venous dilator. Use for severe HTN with high afterload (ADHF). Cyanide toxicity with prolonged use (>4872h) or renal failure. Lightsensitive: wrap tubing.

drugs.name: Hydralazine
drugs.notes: Onset 1020 min, duration unpredictable. Use for eclampsia/preeclampsia if labetalol/nicardipine unavailable. Avoid as first line: less titratable.

sections.title: References & Resources
items: Whelton PK, Carey RM, Aronow WS, et al. 2017 ACC/AHA Guideline for the Prevention, Detection, Evaluation, and Management of High Blood Pressure in Adults. Hypertension. 2018;71(6):e13e115. https://doi.org/10.1161/HYP.0000000000000065

Unger T, Borghi C, Charchar F, et al. 2020 International Society of Hypertension Global Hypertension Practice Guidelines. Hypertension. 2020;75(6):13341357. https://doi.org/10.1161/HYPERTENSIONAHA.120.15026
van den Born BJH, et al. ESC Council on Hypertension Position Document on the Management of Hypertensive Emergencies. European Heart Journal: Cardiovascular Pharmacotherapy. 2019;5(1):3746.

topics.title: Pericarditis
topics.subtitle: Pleuritic chest pain, friction rub, diffuse ST elevation, PR depression

sections.title: Acute Management
items:
Circulation: check pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
Airway: check patency and protect.
Breathing: check WOB, oxygenation, and ventilation.
ABCs: check hemodynamic stability: r/o tamponade (↓ BP, JVD, muffled heart sounds)
ECG: look for diffuse concave ST elevation + PR depression
Stat TTE if C/F effusion or tamponade
IV access, monitoring if hemodynamically unstable

sections.title: Chart Check / Hx.
items: Recent viral illness (MCC), fever, malaise
Postcardiac surgery (Dressler syndrome: weeks to months postMI or surgery)
Autoimmune disease: SLE, RA, sarcoidosis
meds: anticoagulants, hydralazine, isoniazid, procainamide
Radiation therapy to chest, malignancy Hx.
Renal function (uremic pericarditis if BUN >60)

sections.title: Exam
items: Pericardial friction rub: best heard with diaphragm at left sternal border, pt leaning forward
Tachycardia, lowgrade fever
Pulsus paradoxus >10 mmHg: c/f tamponade
JVD, muffled heart sounds: evolving tamponade
Peripheral edema, hepatomegaly if chronic constrictive process

pairs.value: Inflammation of pericardium. Dx requires ≥2 of: chest pain, friction rub, diffuse ST elevation/PR depression, newith worsening pericardial effusion
Pericarditis + troponin elevation suggesting myocardial involvement. Restrict activity until troponin returns to normal
Relapse after sxfree interval ≥4–6 weeks. Occurs in 15–30% without colchicine.
Chronic pericardial thickening/calcification: impaired diastolic filling. Late complication.
Diffuse concave ST elevation + PR depression in multiple leads; reciprocal PR elevation/ST depression in aVR & sometimes V1. No territorial pattern.
ST & PR abnormalities normalize. T waves may flatten.
Diffuse Twave inversions appear after ST segments return to baseline.
ECG returns to normal. Some pts stop at earlier stages or never show all four stages.

Spodick sign = downsloping TP segment,best in II or lateral leads. Early repolarization lacks PR depression &has stable, chronic ST elevation. STEMI has territorial changes with reciprocal ST depression.
Serotonin syndrome: Hours | Neuroleptic malignant syndrome: Days | Anticholinergic toxicity: Hours | Malignant hyperthermia: Minutes (OR)
Serotonin syndrome: Clonus/hyperreflexia | Neuroleptic malignant syndrome: Leadpipe rigidity | Anticholinergic toxicity: Mydriasis, dry | Malignant hyperthermia: Generalized rigidity
Serotonin syndrome: Hyperactive | Neuroleptic malignant syndrome: nl./decreased | Anticholinergic toxicity: Absent | Malignant hyperthermia: nl.
Serotonin syndrome: Diaphoresis | Neuroleptic malignant syndrome: Diaphoresis | Anticholinergic toxicity: Dry, flushed | Malignant hyperthermia: Mottled
Serotonin syndrome: Mydriasis | Neuroleptic malignant syndrome: nl. | Anticholinergic toxicity: Mydriasis | Malignant hyperthermia: nl.
Serotonin syndrome: Mildly elevated | Neuroleptic malignant syndrome: >1000 | Anticholinergic toxicity: nl. | Malignant hyperthermia: Markedly elevated
Most common (80–90%). Coxsackie, echovirus, adenovirus, EBV, CMV, HIV
Dressler syndrome (postMI, postsurgery, postablation). Onset 1–6 weeks after event.
SLE, RA, ankylosing spondylitis, sarcoidosis, vasculitis
BUN >60. Does not respond to NSAIDs: requires dialysis
Lung, breast, lymphoma, melanoma. Often hemorrhagic effusion.
Purulent pericarditis is surgical emergency. TB pericarditis in endemic areas.
Differentiate from STEMI: diffuse ST changes (not territorial), PR depression, no reciprocal changes, pain worse supine

sections.title: Management
items: Start first line dual antiinflammatory therapy
Use NSAID > aspirin, taper after s/s resolution and CRP wnl
colchicine 3 months in acute disease/ 6 months in recurrent disease
Add PPI for gastro protection
Restrict exercise for at least 1 month; target max HR about 100
Continue restriction until clinical remission
get baseline imaging
Get TTE to check effusion, tamponade, or constriction
Use CMR for recurrent or incessant pericarditis to check edema and inflammation
If refractory to first line therapy: determine phenotype
Inflammatory phenotype: elevated CRP, fever, or CMR inflammation
Inflammatory phenotype > prefer antiIL1 therapy (rilonacept or anakinra)
Non inflammatory phenotype: normal CRP and no CMR inflammation>lowdose steroids if therapy is refractory or not tolerated
Avoid steroids in a first episode when possible: inc. recurrence risk
For recurrent pericarditis: consider antiIL1 therapy after failure of first line therapy with or without steroids
Screen for hepatitis, HIV, and TB before starting antiIL1 therapy
Once stable on antiIL1 therapy, other antiinflammatories can often be weaned sequentially
Longer antiIL1 courses may reduce recurrence
Thirdline options: azathioprine, IVIG, or radical pericardiectomy at experienced centers
Admit high risk pts: fever, large effusion, tamponade, immunocompromise, anticoagulation, trauma, or suspected bacterial or malignant cause
Monitor CRP weekly: guide duration of therapy
Activity restriction until sx resolve & CRP returns to normal (athletes: ≥3 months)
Taper aspirin/NSAID over 2–4 weeks based on sx + CRP
Continue colchicine for 3 months (first episode) or 6 months (recurrent)
Repeat TTE if initially had effusion: ensure resolution
Watch for tamponade: worsening dyspnea, ↓ BP, JVD

pairs.value: EKG, troponin, CBC, BMP, ESR/CRP
CK, TSH, LFTs. If systemic dz: ANA, RF, complement
TTE: check effusion size & tamponade physiology. CXR: cardiomegaly if effusion >250 mL
BCxs, HIV, QuantiFERONTB
Cardiac MRI if dx unclear: detects inflammation, differentiates from myocarditis, measures pericardial thickness

drugs.name: Aspirin
drugs.notes: Firstline. Taper over 4 weeks. Always add PPI for GI protection.

drugs.name: Colchicine
drugs.notes: ↓ recurrence by ~50%. Duration: 3 months. Start with first episode.

drugs.name: Ibuprofen
drugs.notes: Alternative NSAID if aspirin not tolerated. Taper over 2–4 weeks.

drugs.name: Prednisone
drugs.notes: Last resort: save for uremic, autoimmune, or NSAIDrefractory. ↑s recurrence risk if used in viral

pairs.key: ICU / immediate higher level of care if
pairs.value: persistent respiratory distress, high-flow or NIV need, hemodynamic instability, vasopressor need, severe arrhythmia, airway risk, neurologic decline, or a trajectory suggesting pt may crash on the floor.

sections.title: References & Resources
items: Adler Y, Charron P, Imazio M, et al. 2015 ESC Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Management of Pericardial Diseases. European Heart Journal. 2015;36(42):29212964. https://doi.org/10.1093/eurheartj/ehv318

Chiabrando JG, Bonaventura A, Vecchié A, et al. Management of Acute and Recurrent Pericarditis: JACC StateoftheArt Review. Journal of the American College of Cardiology. 2020;75(1):7692. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jacc.2019.11.021

topics.title: LVAD Emergencies
topics.subtitle: Lowflow alarms, hypotension, hemolysis, shock, absent palpable pulse

sections.title: Acute Management
items: Airway: check patency; if GCS <8 or unable to protect airway: intubate
Breathing: SpO2, RR, auscultate; apply supplemental O2 if hypoxic
Circulation: DO NOT rely on NIBP or pulse ox: LVAD pts may have no palpable pulse & no SpO2 reading
Auscultate for LVAD hum: hum = device running; absent hum = pump failure: EMERGENCY
Check MAP via arterial line or manual Doppler BP: target MAP 7080 mmHg
If unresponsive with no LVAD hum: start CPR: chest compressions ARE safe in LVAD pts (risk of dislodgement is oretical)
IV access (avoid left arm if driveline on left); labs, ECG, CXR
Contact LVAD coordinator & CT surgery now —theyshould be involved in ALL LVAD emergencies

sections.title: LVAD assessment
items: Check LVAD controller for alarms & parameters (speed, power, pulsatility index, flow)
Auscultate: listen for LVAD hum over left chest
Doppler BP: place cuff, inflate above expected MAP, deflate slowly: first Doppler signal = MAP
Check driveline site for signs of infection, bleeding, or disconnection
Get labs: CBC, CMP, LDH, plasmafree Hgb, INR, fibrinogen
Get EKG (arrhythmias are common in LVAD pts)
If unstable: check connections, battery, controller: swap to backup controller if avail

sections.title: Key Principles
items: Do NOT use automated BP cuffs: LVADs produce flow; use Doppler for MAP
Do NOT do chest compressions unless confirmed cardiac arrest with no LVAD flow: risk of dislodging cannula
Target MAP by Doppler: **60–90 mmHg**
Always contact LVAD center / VAD coordinator early
Patients may lack palpable pulse: this can nl. with flow devices

pairs.value: ↑ Power / ↑ LDH / ↓ flow / dark urine. Heparinize, consider tPA. Contact VAD center urgently.
Low flow alarm, ↓ pulsatility. Volume deplete or RV failure. Give 500mL bolus, reduce pump speed.
MCC of readmission. Hold anticoagulation. Transfuse PRBCs. Avoid lowering MAP <60: may worsen pump function.
Eryma/drainage at exit site. BCx × 2, wound culture. Broadspectrum abx.Don't pull driveline.
JVD, hepatomegaly, peripheral edema, ↓ LVAD flow. Inotropes (milrinone), ↓LVAD speed, avoid volume overload.
VT/VF may tolerated with LVAD support. Treat per ACLS if hemodynamically unstable. Amiodarone 150mg IV.
Swap batteries now. If controller fails, switch to backup. If total power loss: h&crank pump per device protocol.
Both ischemic & hemorrhagic possible. CT head stat. Reverse anticoagulation for hemorrhagic. Contact VAD center before tPA.

sections.title: Cardiac Arrest in LVAD Patient
items: Confirm arrest: unresponsive + no Doppler signal + LVAD not running
Check LVAD controller & connections first: power failure is reversible cause
Swap to backup controller/batteries
If LVAD confirmed nonfunctional & no signs of life: start compressions cautiously (risk of cannula displacement)
Defibrillate VT/VF per standard ACLS: safe with LVAD in place
Pushdose epi 10–20 mcg IV for low MAP if LVAD running but hypotensive
Do NOT delay contacting LVAD center —theymay be able to troubleshoot remotely

sections.title: Management
items: check perfusion, not pulse
Check mentation, skin color and temperature, and cap refill
Auscultate for the LVAD hum to confirm pump function
get Doppler MAP; goal is >60 mmHg
Check controller and connections
Verify driveline connections, battery status, and alarms
Note power, flow, and pulsatility index
Contact the VAD center immediately
get baseline labs and imaging
Get CBC, BMP, LDH, plasmafree hemoglobin, INR, troponin, and lactate
Get ECG because arrhythmias are common
Get chest Xray for pump position and driveline integrity
Get bedside echocardiography
Pump thrombosis: suspect with power increase, high LDH, hemoglobinuria, or HF symptoms
Pump thrombosis: admit to ICU and start IV anticoagulation with UFH or bivalirudin
Pump thrombosis: give supportive care with inotropes and diuretics
Pump thrombosis: avoid thrombolytics first line and transfer for pump exchange if no improvement
Suction event or low flow alarm: check for LV underfilling
Hypovolemia > cautious fluid bolus
RV failure > milrinone or dobutamine
Tamponade > emergent pericardiocentesis or surgery
Arrhythmia > treat per ACLS
Suction event: decrease pump speed temporarily
Suction event: use bedside echo to check LV size, septum, and RV function
Suction event: correct the cause before increasing speed
Bleeding, often GI: check stability and transfuse as needed
Use leukocytedepleted products for bridge to transplant pts
Hold anticoagulation or antiplatelet therapy only after discussing with the VAD center
Start PPI therapy
Use endoscopy or colonoscopy to find the source; consider push enteroscopy if needed
Use reversal agents only for life-threatening bleeding after VAD team input
Hemolysis without thrombosis: check pump position with bedside echo
Optimize preload and reduce pump speed if positioning is adequate
Persistent hemolysis may require pump exchange
Give supportive care including transfusion and hemodiafiltration if needed
Ventricular arrhythmias may be tolerated; check perfusion rather than pulse alone
Get ECG and do not dismiss rhythm as device interference
Use echo to check for suction event or septal shift
If suctionrelated: decrease speed and give a gentle fluid bolus
Cardioversion and defibrillation are safe without disconnecting the device
Use amiodarone for stable VT and ACLS for unstable arrhythmias
Consider RV mechanical support for refractory arrhythmias
Cardiac arrest: standard ACLS still applies
Verify pump function by hum and controller check
If pump stops: check connections and replace batteries or controller
Chest compressions are controversial but reasonable if no pump function
Defibrillation is safe and should not be delayed
Consider ECMO as a bridge if refractory
Driveline infection: get wound and blood cultures
Start broad spectrum antibiotics and local wound care
Transfer to the VAD center for definitive management
Deep infection may require debridement or pump exchange
Right ventricular failure: look for high JVP, hepatomegaly, and low LVAD flow
Optimize preload and avoid overdiuresis
Reduce LVAD speed to allow RV filling
Use milrinone or dobutamine
Consider inhaled pulmonary vasodilators such as nitric oxide or epoprostenol
Consider temporary RVAD if refractory

drugs.name: Heparin
drugs.notes: For suspected pump thrombosis. Target aPTT 60–80s. Monitor closely.

drugs.name: Milrinone
drugs.notes: For RV failure. No loading dose in acute setting. Monitor for ↓ BP.

drugs.name: Amiodarone
drugs.notes: For VT/VF. Follow with 1mg/min × 6hthen0.5mg/min × 18h.

drugs.name: Phenylephrine
drugs.notes: For acute ↓ BP.

drugs.name: Vasopressin
drugs.notes: Alternative vasopressor. May be preferred over norepinephrine in LVAD pts.

items: Givertz MM, DeFilippis EM, Colvin M, et al. HFSA/SAEM/ISHLT Clinical Expert Consensus Document on the Emergency Management of Patients With Ventricular Assist Devices. Journal of Heart and Lung Transplantation. 2019.
