Molina EJ, Shah P, Kiernan MS, et al. The Society of Thoracic Surgeons Intermacs 2020 Annual Report. Annals of Thoracic Surgery. 2021;111(3):778792. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.athoracsur.2020.12.038

topics.title: ECMO Emergencies
topics.subtitle: Hypoxemia, shock, circuit alarms, bleeding, limb ischemia

sections.title: Acute Management
items:
Circulation: check pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
Airway: check patency and protect.
Breathing: check WOB, oxygenation, and ventilation.
CIRCUIT EMERGENCY: call ECMO specialist/perfusionist STAT
Lower pt's head (Trendelenburg) if air may have reached pt
Deair circuit with perfusionist help before restarting
Monitor postoxygenator blood gas: ↓ PaO2 or ↑ PaCO2 suggests failing membrane
Prepare for emergent oxygenator exchange (perfusionist)
Direct pressure on cannula site: do NOT remove partially dislodged cannula
Call surgery STAT for recannulation

sections.title: Chart Check / Hx.
items: ECMO type: VV (respiratory only) vs VA (cardiac + respiratory)
Cannulation sites & dates: femoral, IJ, central
ECMO flow rate (L/min), sweep gas (L/min), FiO2 on circuit
vent settings —"lung rest" on VVECMO (low TV, low rate, low FiO2)
Anticoagulation: current heparin dose, last aPTT/antiXa, recent dose changes
Indication: ARDS, cardiogenic shock, PE, bridge to transplant, postcardiotomy
Duration on ECMO: complications ↑ with time
Distal perfusion catheter present? (VAECMO: prevents limb ischemia)
Contact info for ECMO team/perfusionist: must be available 24/7

sections.title: Exam
items: Cannula sites: bleeding, hematoma, signs of infection, secure dressings
Distal limb perfusion (VAECMO): check pulses, color, temp, & SpO2 on cannulated extremity q1h: limb ischemia is an emergency
Circuit inspection: clots in tubing or oxygenator (dark spots), kinks, connections secure
Auscultate: heart sounds (LV distension: diminished), lungs (pulmonary edema if LV not ejecting on VAECMO)
NorthSouth syndrome (VAECMO with femoral cannulation): check SpO2 on RIGHT h&: if ↓ vs lower extremity, upper body is receiving poorly oxygenated blood from native heart
Neurologic: pupil reactivity, GCS: cerebral embolism risk on VAECMO
Abdomen: compartment syndrome risk, check for GI bleeding

pairs.value: Extracorporeal membrane oxygenation: an external circuit that oxygenates blood & removes CO2, functioning as an artificial lung ± heart
Venovenous: blood drained from vein: oxygenator: returned to vein near RA. Respiratory support only: requires native cardiac function
Venoarterial: blood drained from vein: oxygenator: returned to artery. Provides both cardiac & respiratory support
Femoral vein: IJ return, or dual lumen IJ catheter (Avalon)
Femoral vein drainage: femoral artery return (peripheral), or RA: aorta (central, surgical)
Lower anticoagulation, longer duration possible (days–weeks), partial vs full support

images.caption: ECMO Configurations: VV vs VA Cannulation
images.description: VVECMO (left): Deoxygenated blood drained from femoral vein, passed through oxygenator/heat exchanger, returned to IJ vein near RA. VAECMO (right): Blood drained from femoral vein (RA), oxygenated, returned to femoral artery (aorta). Note distal perfusion catheter on arterial cannula to prevent limb ischemia.

pairs.value: Severe ARDS (P/F <100), refractory hypoxemia, status asthmaticus, smoke inhalation, bridge to lung transplant, pulmonary contusion
Cardiogenic shock, massive PE, refractory cardiac arrest (eCPR), postcardiotomy failure, myocarditis, bridge to heart transplant/LVAD, drug OD with cardiac depression
MCC complication: surgical site, cannula site, GI, intracranial. Anticoagulation + consumptive coagulopathy
Circuit clots, oxygenator thrombus, DVT/PE, arterial emboli, stroke
Blood returning to aorta ↑ LV afterload: LV doesn't eject: distension: pulmonary edema: myocardial damage. Needs LV venting
Peripheral VAECMO: oxygenated blood from femoral artery mixes with deoxygenated blood from native heart. Upper body (brain, coronaries) may hypoxic. Check R h& SpO2
Femoral arterial cannula: distal ischemia. Prevented by distal perfusion catheter. Fasciotomy if compartment syndrome
Cannula site, blood stream infection, pneumonia. ↑ risk with duration
Mechanical destruction of RBCs by circuit: monitor plasmafree Hgb, LDH, haptoglobin

sections.title: Management
items: check perfusion and circuit function
Check mentation, skin perfusion, and cap refill
Confirm the circuit is running and check flows, RPM, and alarms
Inspect for clot, air, kinks, or disconnections
get baseline labs
Get CBC, BMP, LDH, plasmafree hemoglobin, and fibrinogen
Get ABG and lactate
Check ACT or aPTT and antiXa
Call the ECMO specialist or perfusionist immediately
Cardiac arrest on ECMO: verify circuit function first
If the circuit is running with adequate flow, look for other causes of collapse
If the circuit has stopped, check power and connections and swap controller or batteries
Increase ECMO flow to the maximum tolerated
Defibrillation is safe and should not be delayed
Chest compressions are controversial; consider if no circuit flow
Treat reversible causes such as hypovolemia, tamponade, tension pneumothorax, or arrhythmia
On VAECMO, consider LV distension and need for venting
Air embolism: clamp the circuit immediately and stop the pump
Find the air source and deair the circuit before restart
Use Trendelenburg; consider left lateral decubitus for arterial air
If unable to deair, perform full circuit change
Consider hyperbaric oxygen for major systemic air embolism
Oxygenator failure: suspect with rising premembrane pressure, low postmembrane PaO2, or visible clot
Increase FiO2 and sweep gas temporarily
Prepare for emergent oxygenator or circuit exchange
Use backup pump or handcrank if needed
Increase ventilator support as a bridge
Pump failure: clamp circuit to prevent backflow
Attempt controller or battery swap
Handcrank as a bridge if avail
Prepare for emergent circuit change
Maximize ventilator and inotropic support
Accidental decannulation: apply direct pressure and clamp the circuit
Call for emergent surgical support
Resuscitate with blood, fluids, and vasopressors
Prepare for emergent recannulation
Bleeding: find the source
Reduce or hold anticoagulation only with ECMO team input
Transfuse PRBCs, platelets, and cryoprecipitate as needed
Consider TXA or aminocaproic acid
Use reversal agents only for life-threatening bleeding
Use IR or surgery for uncontrolled bleeding
Intracranial hemorrhage: get urgent neurosurgical input and consider ECMO discontinuation
Circuit thrombosis: suspect with rising pressures, visible clot, hemolysis, or falling flows
Optimize anticoagulation
Use bivalirudin if heparin resistance or HIT is suspected
Prepare for circuit exchange if clot burden is significant
Monitor for embolic complications
Hemolysis: check plasmafree hemoglobin, LDH, and haptoglobin
Look for high RPM, kink, small cannula, or thrombus
Reduce pump speed if tolerated and optimize cannula position
Exchange the circuit if refractory
Give supportive care with transfusion and renal protection
Limb ischemia on VAECMO: monitor Doppler pulses, color, temperature, and compartment tension hourly
Check lactate and CPK
Place distal perfusion catheter early if ischemia develops
If DPC is present, confirm patency and position
Compartment syndrome requires emergent fasciotomy
Move cannula or use central cannulation if needed
Amputate only if the limb is unsalvageable
Differential hypoxemia on VAECMO: monitor right radial oxygenation
Increase ECMO flow and optimize ventilator settings
Consider beta blockade, VAVECMO, or central or axillary return if persistent
Target right radial SpO2 9498%
LV distension on VAECMO: suspect with pulmonary edema, closed aortic valve, or LV thrombus risk
Reduce afterload and add inotropy
Consider IABP, Impella, surgical vent, or atrial septostomy
Infection: get blood and cannula cultures
Start broad spectrum antibiotics
Inspect cannulation sites daily
Consider circuit change for persistent bacteremia
Get source control if possible
Daily goals with ECMO team: target flows, sweep, anticoagulation, weaning plan
Anticoagulation: titrate heparin to target aPTT/antiXa. Hold for active bleeding, resume cautiously
vent: "lung rest" on VVECMO (TV 34 mL/kg, PEEP 1015, rate 10, FiO2 0.3). Avoid ventinduced lung injury
VAECMO: ensure LV is ejecting: check aortic valve opening on echo. If LV distended: inotropes, IABP, or LV vent (Impella)
Transfuse: Hgb >78, plt >5080k (>100k if bleeding), fibrinogen >150. Minimize volume: circuit dilution is common
Nutrition: start enteral feeds within 2448h if hemodynamically stable
Monitor cannulated limb hourly (VAECMO): pulse, color, temp, compartment checks
DVT prophylaxis:held d/t systemic anticoagulation: discuss with team
Weaning trials: ↓ ECMO flows & check native heart/lung function. VV: ↓ sweep gas. VA: ↓ flows to 12 L/min under echo guidance

pairs.value: Anticoagulation monitoring: goal aPTT 4060 sec or antiXa 0.30.7 (institutional protocol)
Preoxygenator (venous) & postoxygenator (arterial) gases: check circuit & native lung function
Monitor for bleeding, consumptive coagulopathy, DIC. Transfuse plt >5080k, fibrinogen >150
Hemolysis markers: ↑ suggests circuit/pump issue
Endorgan perfusion marker: ↓ on adequate ECMO support
check lung recovery (VV), LV distension/pulmonary edema (VA), cannula position
check LV function, aortic valve opening (VAECMO: must open to prevent stasis), RV function, pericardial effusion
Monitor ly: detects NorthSouth syndrome. If <90%: ↑ vent support or convert to VAV/central

drugs.name: Heparin (UFH)
drugs.dose: Bolus: 50100 units/kg at cannulation; Infusion: 1020 units/kg/h
drugs.notes: standard anticoagulation for ECMO. Goal aPTT 4060 sec (or per institutional protocol). AntiXa 0.30.7 units/mL. Monitor q46h.

drugs.name: Bivalirudin
drugs.notes: Alternative for HIT. Direct thrombin inhibitor. Titrate to aPTT 4060 sec. Shorter halflife than heparin.

drugs.name: Norepinephrine
drugs.notes: Firstline vasopressor on ECMO. Target MAP ≥65 mmHg. VAECMO ptsneed vasopressors to maintain native coronary/cerebral perfusion.

drugs.name: Epinephrine
drugs.notes: Inotrope for ↓ native cardiac function on VAECMO. Helps maintain LV ejection to prevent stasis & LV distension.

drugs.name: Milrinone
drugs.notes: Inodilator: ↓ LV afterload on VAECMO. Helps prevent LV distension. Avoid loading dose: causes ↓ BP .

drugs.name: Aminocaproic acid (Amicar)
drugs.notes: Antifibrinolytic for bleeding on ECMO. Use with caution: ↑ thrombotic risk in circuit.

pairs.key: ICU / immediate higher level of care if
pairs.value: persistent respiratory distress, high-flow or NIV need, hemodynamic instability, vasopressor need, severe arrhythmia, airway risk, neurologic decline, or a trajectory suggesting pt may crash on the floor.

sections.title: References & Resources
items: Extracorporeal Life Support Organization (ELSO). ELSO Guidelines for Adult Respiratory Failure. 2021. https://www.elso.org/ecmo-resources/elso-ecmo-guidelines.aspx
Badulak J, Antonini MV, Stead CM, et al. ELSO COVID19 Working Group Guideline. ASAIO Journal. 2021;67(5):472481.

Combes A, Hajage D, Capellier G, et al. Extracorporeal Membrane Oxygenation for Severe Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome (EOLIA trial). New England Journal of Medicine. 2018;378(21):19651975. https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1800385

topics.title: Acute Respiratory Failure
topics.subtitle: Dyspnea, hypoxemia, hypercapnia, increased WOB, abnormal gas

sections.title: Acute Management
items: Airway: airway patent? Look, listen; suction if secretions/blood; jaw thrust if obstructed; call for airway help early
-If Pt looks terrible, can't protect airway: call ICU + anesthesia, then get ready to intubate
Breathing: RR, SpO2(good pleth?), WOB, accessory muscle use, paradoxical breathing, speaking in full sentences?
•Start O2 now: NRB 15 L/min if in distress
•Rapid O2 escalation if NRB insufficient: HFNC (up to 60 L/min, FiO2 up to 100%) OR BiPAP: intubation
Circulation: HR, BP, perfusion; ↓BP, then tension PTX, PE, or cardiogenic shock
•Put on cardiac monitor

sections.title: Chart Check / Hx.
items: Code status clarify before intubation
Onset: sudden (PE, PTX, mucus plug,aspiration, flash pulm edema) vs gradual (COPD, CHF, pneumonia)
Baseline lung Dz.: COPD, asthma, ILD, OSA; Home O2? Home BiPAP/CPAP?
Cardiac hx: CHF (EF?), valvular Dz., recent MI, orthopnea, PND
atelectasis, pneumonia, & PE,
Recent intubation: laryngeal edema
Infection s/s— fever, cough, sputum (pneumonia, COVID, Flu)
VTE risk: immobility, surgery, cancer, DVT hx, pregnancy, OCP (PE)
Meds: sedatives, opioids, BB, neuromuscular agents
Neuromuscular disease: MG, GBS, ALS, spinal cord injury (diaphragm weakness)
Trauma: Rib Frx (flail chest), PTX, hemothorax, pulmonary contusion
Allergies / anaphylaxis: bronchospasm, angioedema
Smoking History

pairs.value: SpO2 <90% on RA or PaO2 <60 mmHg = hypoxia; RR >30 or <8; ↓ BP (tension PTX, PE, shock)
Tripoding, acc. musc. use, nasal flaring, diaphoresis = severe; can't speak in full sentences = impending failure
Stridor (upper obst.: croup, angioedema, FB, postextubation), drooling, muffled voice
Pulmonary edema (CHF), pneumonia, ARDS, aspiration
Asthma, COPD, bronchospasm, anaphylaxis; silent chest in asthma = BAD
Pneumothorax (± tracheal deviation), large pleural effusion, mainstem intubation
PE (clear lungs + hypoxia), neuromuscular weakness, metabolic acidosis (Kussmaul breathing)
JVD + crackles = CHF; JVD + ↓ BS + tracheal deviation = tension PTX; JVD + clear lungs = PE or tamponade
Unilateral leg swelling (DVT: PE), b/l edema (CHF), cyanosis (severe hypoxia), cold: hemorrhage
AMS from ↓ PaO2 or ↑CO2 (CO2 narcosis); asterixis (CO2 retention); ↓ NIF in NM disease
Failure of oxygenation; ↓ PaO2 with nl/↓ PaCO2 (initially); ↑A gradient
Cardiogenic (CHF, flash pulm edema, valvular) or noncardiogenic (ARDS, neurogenic)
CAP, HAP, VAP, aspiration; viral (COVID, influenza); opportunistic if immunocompromised
Clear lungs + hypoxia + tachycardia + risk factors; massive PE = ↓ BP
Spontaneous, traumatic, iatrogenic; tension PTX = ↓ BP + tracheal deviation: needle decompression
B/l infiltrates, PaO2/FiO2 <300, not fully explained by cardiac failure; common triggers: sepsis, aspiration, pneumonia, pancreatitis, transfusion (TRALI)
Failure of ventilation; ↑ PaCO2 ± ↓ PaO2; pH ↓ if acute
MCC of hypercapnic failure;wheezing, prolonged expiration, ↑ WOB
Status asthmaticus: wheezing: silent chest = ominous; ↑ PaCO2 in asthma = impending arrest
MG crisis, GBS, ALS, spinal cord injury, OD (opioids, benzos): ↓ respiratory drive or muscle weakness
BMI >30 + daytime hypercapnia;overlaps with OSA; worsens during sleep/sedation
Angioedema, anaphylaxis, FB aspiration, croup, epiglottitis, Ludwig angina, postextubation laryngeal edema

sections.title: Management
items: Sit the pt up, give supplemental O2 if needed, confirm good O2 pleth, & escalate to HFNC or NIPPV based on WOB & oxygenation.
- Do NOT withhold O2 in COPD: !!hypoxia kills faster than CO2 retention!!; target SpO2 >88% in known CO2 retainers, but treat hypoxia first
Use BiPAP early for hypercapnic failure & for CHF+pulmonary edema if pt is awake & protecting airway.
Intubate if refractory hypoxemia, worsening hypercapnia/acidemia, cant protect airway, severe fatigue, or hemodynamic instability.
-Lung protective ventilation in ARDS: TV 6 mL/kg IBW, Pplat<30 cmH2O, PEEP per ARDSNet table, permissive hypercapnia
-For asthma/COPD intubation: low RR (812), long expiratory time (I:E 1:45), watch for autoPEEP (breath stacking); disconnect vent if hemodynamic collapse
Treat cause: bronchodilators/steroids for COPD, nitrates/diuresis/NIPPV for pulmonary edema, abx for PNA, AC for PE, & decompression for tension PTX.
Silent chest in asthma = BAD: no air movement; if PaCO2 is nl. or rising in acute asthma = impending respiratory arrest
Clear lungs + hypoxia = think PE, NM weakness, or metabolic acidosis (Kussmaul breathing)
BiPAP for COPD, CPAP for CHF: BiPAP: pressure support to ↓ WOB & blow off CO2₂O
HFNC:~1cmH2O PEEP per 10 L/min: bridge thetherapy; better tolerated than NIPPV
Postintubation ↓ BP : c/f tension PTX (b/l breath sounds?), autoPEEP (disconnect vent briefly), ↓preload from positive pressure, sedationinduced vasodilation
POCUS is faster than CXR: Blines (edema), lung sliding (PTX), IVC (volume), EF (cardiac function); can guide management in realtime
Clarify code status / GOC before intubation: this conversation is essential

pairs.value: <6 L/min (FiO2 ~2444%); for mild hypoxia; allows eating/talking
1015 L/min (FiO2 ~7090%); reservoir bag must stay inflated; first line for acute distress
Up to 60 L/min, FiO2 up to 100%; provides PEEP effect (~1 cmH2O per 10 L/min); humidified; tolerated well; first line for hypoxic failure if NRB insufficient
IPAP + EPAP; first line for COPD exacerbation with hypercapnia; typical start: !!IPAP 10-12, EPAP 5!!; titrate IPAP for ventilation (CO2), EPAP for oxygenation
GCS <8, unable to protect airway, SpO2 <88% despite max noninvasive support, pH <7.25 with rising PaCO2 despite BiPAP, respiratory fatigue, hemodynamic instability
AC/VC mode; TV 8 mL/kg IBW; RR 1418; FiO2 100% initially: titrate to SpO2 9296%; PEEP 5: titrate using ARDSNet table if ARDS

sections.title: Orders
items: Portable CXR: Infiltrates (pneumonia, edema, ARDS), PTX, effusion, cardiomegaly
EKG: RV strain (PE), ischemia (ACS: flash pulm edema), dysrhythmia
BNP/NTproBNP: differentiates cardiogenic (CHF) from pulmonary cause (BNP >500 pg/mL suggests CHF)
CBC: ↑ WBC (infection), anemia (↓ O2 carrying capacity)
BMP: Lytes, renal function, anion gap (metabolic acidosis)
Troponin: if ACS or massive PE suspected
Lactate: tissue hypoperfusion, shock
Ddimer: if PE considered, though in acute decompensation, favor CTA (ageadjusted: age × 10 for pts >50)
POCUS: Blines (pulmonary edema), lung sliding (PTX), IVC (volume status), cardiac function (EF, RV dilation)
CTA chest: if PE suspected & pt can tolerate (ignore Cr in an emergency)
Sputum culture: if pneumonia, intubated, or immunocompromised

drugs.name: Albuterol
drugs.dose: 2.5 mg neb q20 min × 3 or neb; MDI 48 puffs q20 min
drugs.notes: For bronchospasm (asthma, COPD, anaphylaxis). nebs for severe. Add ipratropium 0.5 mg for COPD

drugs.name: Methylprednisolone/pred
drugs.dose: 125 mg IV (asthma/COPD); or prednisone 4060 mg PO
drugs.notes: For asthma/COPD exacerbation; onset 46 hrs; give early. 5days

drugs.name: Furosemide
drugs.dose: 4080 mg IV (or 2.5× home PO dose); may repeat or start gtt 520 mg/hr
drugs.notes: For cardiogenic pulmonary edema; onset 515 min IV. Monitor K+, Mg2+, UOP

drugs.name: Nitroglycerin
drugs.dose: 0.4 mg SL q5 min × 3; or gtt 5200 mcg/min IV
drugs.contraindications: SBP <100, PDE5 inhibitor use, severe aortic stenosis
drugs.notes: For flash pulmonary edema with HTN; ↓ preload + afterload;effective in hypertensive pulm edema

drugs.name: Epinephrine
drugs.dose: 0.30.5 mg IM (1:1000) q515 min for anaphylaxis; neb 2.25% racemic epi for stridor
drugs.notes: Firstline for anaphylaxis with bronchospasm; racemic epi for croup/postextubation stridor

drugs.name: Magnesium sulfate
drugs.notes: Adjunct for severe asthma refractory to albuterol + steroids; bronchodilator via smooth muscle relaxation

sections.title: References & Resources
items: HighFlow Nasal Oxygen vs Noninvasive Ventilation in Patients With Acute Respiratory Failure: The RENOVATE Randomized Clinical Trial. The Journal of the American Medical Association. 2025. RENOVATE Investigators & the BRICNet Authors, Maia IS, KawanoDourado L, et al.

Baldomero AK, Melzer AC, Greer N, et al. **Effectiveness & Harms of HighFlow Nasal Oxygen for Acute Respiratory Failure: An Evidence Report for Clinical Guideline From American College of Physicians.** Annals of Internal Medicine. 2021.

topics.title: Flash Pulmonary Edema/SCAPE
topics.subtitle: Sudden dyspnea, severe hypertension, crackles, hypoxemia, frothy sputum

sections.title: Acute Management
items: Airway: patency and protect.
Breathing: check WOB, oxygenation, and ventilation.
Circulation: pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
Sit pt up, put on monitor, IV access, Get ECG
Optimize O2; inc WOB: BiPAP
- NIPPV first line if awake & protecting airway: ↓ preload/afterload & improves oxygenation rapidly
If SBP is OK: start nitroglycerin; in severe HTN + pulmonary edema-->high dose nitrates
Diurese if volume overloaded or known HF, !!but do NOT delay nitrates/NIPPV while waiting for diuresis!!
If worsening fatigue, AMS, refractory hypoxemia, or cant tolerate NIPPV: prep intubation
Treat cause: ACS, hypertensive emergency, dysrhythmia, acute valvular Dz, missed dialysis, med nonadherence

sections.title: Chart Check / Hx.
items: Minutes-hours onset: flash edema/SCAPE > ADHF.
Common background: poorly controlled HTN, prior HF, CAD, valvular disease, or prior episodes.
riggers: missed antihypertensives, missed dialysis, ischemia, dysrhythmia, abrupt volume shift, or med nonadherence.
Ask about chest pain, palpitations, orthopnea, sudden nocturnal dyspnea, and recent urine output.
PDE5 inhibitor use before giving nitrates

pairs.value: Abrupt severe dyspnea, diaphoresis, tachypnea, diffuse crackles, and often frothy sputum.
Air hunger, accessory muscle use, inability to lie flat, and speaking in short phrases.
SBP is often >180 with tachycardia, marked JVD, and sometimes cool extremities from poor forward flow.
Lungs are wet, but pt may be intravascularly depleted; do not assume immediate diuresis is the first move.
↓ BP, AMS, mottling, low urine output, or rising lactate suggest cardiogenic shock rather than isolated hypertensive flash edema.
Sympathetic crashing acute pulmonary edema (SCAPE).
acute ↑ afterload --> pulmonary edema and ↓ forward flow-->sympathetic surge -->↑↑ afterload.
Unlike slower CHF exacerbation or hypotensive cardiogenic shock, SCAPE is usually very hypertensive and can worsen over minutes.
Rapid NIPPV and afterload reduction matter most; diuretics less important.
Marked afterload excess with sympathetic overdrive causing abrupt cardiogenic pulmonary edema.
More gradual decompensation from HFrEF, HFpEF, missed diuretics, or missed dialysis.
Acute ischemia, papillary muscle dysfunction, or acute MR can present with sudden pulmonary edema.
Acute MR, severe AS, or dysrhythmia-related decompensation can mimic or trigger the presentation.
ARDS, aspiration, pneumonia, diffuse alveolar hemorrhage, inhalation injury, or re-expansion edema.
PE, COPD/asthma, upper airway obstruction, negative-pressure edema, immersion edema, naloxone-associated edema, or neurogenic pulmonary edema.

sections.title: Management
items: Sit pt upright, start BiPAP early if awake and protecting airway.
rapid afterload reduction: Start nitroglycerin ASAP. If taking too long to give to pt, bridge with SL nitroglycerin.
Dont reflexively give diuretics. Recheck volume after NIPPV and nitrates control the hypertension; add loop diuretic later if true overload is present.
If pt is tiring or may need intubation, --> improve hemodynamics first(consider placing a line) if rapidly titrating nitroglycerin.

sections.title: Orders
items: CXR: may be normal very early; later look for cephalization, interstitial or alveolar edema, Kerley B lines, small bilateral pleural effusions, and cardiomegaly.
ECG: look for ischemia, infarction, tachydysrhythmia, or LVH strain.
CBC, BMP, Mg, troponin, and BNP or NT-proBNP.
POCUS: diffuse bilateral B-lines, global systolic function, IVC assessment, and pleural effusions; A-line predominance argues against pulmonary edema.
Formal TTE if valvular disease, new murmur, cardiogenic shock, or diagnosis remains unclear.
If presentation is atypical, keep PE, pneumonia, COPD/asthma, & ARDS in differential

sections.title: Characteristic X-Ray
items: Typical film shows bilateral pulmonary vascular congestion with interstitial or alveolar edema in a central bat-wing distribution.
Look for Kerley B lines, cephalization, cardiomegaly, and small bilateral pleural effusions, though the film can lag behind the clinical picture early.

drugs.name: Nitroglycerin
drugs.dose: 0.4 mg SL q5 min while setting up IV; IV infusion 100 mcg/min and titrate rapidly upward as needed
drugs.contraindications: SBP <100, PDE5 inhibitor use, severe aortic stenosis
drugs.notes: First-line therapy in SCAPE. High doses provide arterial vasodilation and afterload reduction; use BP and WOB to guide titration.

drugs.name: Furosemide
drugs.dose: 4080 mg IV, or ~22.5x home oral dose
drugs.notes: Use after BP and respiratory distress are controlled if true volume overload is present. It is slower and less helpful than early NIPPV plus nitrates in SCAPE.

drugs.name: Nitroprusside
drugs.dose: 0.5 mcg/kg/min IV, titrate to BP; avoid prolonged high-dose use
drugs.contraindications: Hypotension; caution with renal failure and prolonged infusion
drugs.notes: Alternative vasodilator when severe afterload excess persists despite nitroglycerin.

sections.title: Pearls & Pitfalls
items: Do NOT reflexively give large IV fluid boluses to crashing dyspneic pt with diffuse crackles & severe hypertension
NIPPVimproves symptoms within minutes & can avert intubation
nl. BNP does not fully exclude flash edema early in course
A new murmur, ischemic ECG changes, or shock raise C/F acute MR, papillary muscle dysfunction, or large MI
Persistent hypoxemia despite appropriate edema treatment prompt rethought of diagnosis

pairs.key: ICU / immediate higher level of care if
pairs.value: persistent respiratory distress, high-flow or NIV need, hemodynamic instability, vasopressor need, severe arrhythmia, airway risk, neurologic decline, or a trajectory suggesting pt may crash on the floor.

sections.title: References & Resources
items: Heidenreich PA, Bozkurt B, Aguilar D, et al. 2022 AHA/ACC/HFSA Guideline for the Management of Heart Failure. Circulation. 2022;145(18):e895e1032. https://doi.org/10.1161/CIR.0000000000001063

Gray A, Goodacre S, Newby DE, et al. Noninvasive Ventilation in Acute Cardiogenic Pulmonary Edema (3CPO trial). New England Journal of Medicine. 2008;359(2):142151. https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa0707992

Mebazaa A, Yilmaz MB, Levy P, et al. Recommendations on PreHospital and Early Hospital Management of Acute Heart Failure: ESC Working Group Consensus. European Journal of Heart Failure. 2015;17(6):544558.

topics.title: Pulmonary Embolism
topics.subtitle: Acute dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, tachycardia, hypoxemia, RV strain

sections.title: Acute Management
items: How do they look? Distressed, hypoxemic, hypotensive, or periarrest?
Airway: if crashing, obtunded, or unable to protect airway, call for help early & prepare to intubate
Breathing: check SpO2, WOB, & for O2 or ventilation
Circulation: check SBP, MAP, HR, mentation, perfusion, & lactate
Place on monitor, get IV access, & call PERT early if unstable or high risk

sections.title: Chart Check / Hx.
items: VTE risk factors: recent surgery/immobility, cancer (active), prior VTE, pregnancy/postpartum, OCP/HRT, thrombophilia
Onset & progression of sx: sudden dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, ↑HR, syncope, hemoptysis
Hemodynamic status: SBP, HR, any hypotensive episodes, syncope
Bleeding risk assessment: recent surgery (<3 wk), active bleed, CVA hx, thrombocytopenia, anticoagulant use
Renal function: determines anticoagulant choice (UFH vs LMWH vs DOAC)
Cancer hx: may affect anticoagulation duration (consider indefinite tx)
Prior PE/DVT: recurrence risk, prior tx
Pregnancy status: CTPA preferred over V/Q; LMWH is anticoagulant of choice

sections.title: Exam
items: Hemodynamics: SBP <90 or MAP <60 = Category DE: activate PERT
Tachycardia, tachypnea, hypoxia: apply R modifier if supplemental O2 needed
JVD, loud P2, RV heave: signs of RV strain/pressure overload
Unilateral leg swelling, calf tenderness, Homan sign (unreliable): concurrent DVT
Mentation: AMS, obtundation (endorgan hypoperfusion: Category D2 or E)
Skin: cool, mottled arms/legs (cardiogenic shock)
Auscultation: clear lungs (PEhas clear lungs), new TR murmur

pairs.value: Replaces massive/submassive/low-risk with Categories A–E based on clinical severity, biomarkers, RV imaging, hemodynamics, & respiratory status
Cardiopulmonary failure: systemic thrombolysis is first line; consider ↓dose alteplase in selected pts & activate PERT now
Incipient failure: consider thrombolysis if bleeding risk is acceptable; catheter directed therapy may reduce bleeding risk; activate PERT
C3 needs monitored admission, anticoagulation, & close reassessment for 72h; C1C2 need admission, anticoagulation, & serial assessment
Lowrisk ptsget anticoagulation alone; LMWH is preferred initially, & DOACs are preferred over warfarin when appropriate
Subclinical: aS/S PE, incidental finding. ok to DC
Symptomatic, low clinical severity (PESI≤85,sPESI=0, Bova≤4). B1 = subseg, B2 =nonsubseg. Early DC
Symptomatic, elevated clinical severity (PESI >85, sPESI ≥1, Bova >4). C1 = nl RV + nl biomarkers. C2 = abn RV OR ↑ biomarkers. C3 = abn RV & ↑ biomarkers (highest intermediate risk)
Incipient cardiopulm failure. D1 = transient ↓ BP (SBP <90 for <15min or fluid resp). D2 = normotensive shock (lactate >2, AKI, UOP <0.5 mL/kg/h, AMS, CI <2.2, MAP <60)
Cardiopulm failure: persistent ↓ BP /shock. E1 = HD collapse with cardiogenic shock (SCAI C). E2 = refractory cardiogenic shock (SCAI DE) or cardiac arrest
Added when hypoxemia, ↑HR, or needs O2/ventilation (e.g., C3R, D2R)
Major surgery, trauma, immobilization, cancer, pregnancy/OCP: tx duration36 months
No identifiable risk factor: consider extended anticoagulation (>36 mo), ageappropriate cancer screening
Chest pain, ↑ troponin, ST changes: EKG & echo help differentiate. PE causes RV ischemia with troponin rise
Tearing chest/back pain, BP differential, wide mediastinum. Thrombolysis is lethal if dissection
Sudden dyspnea, unilateral absent breath sounds, ↓ BP if tension
JVD, muffled heart sounds, ↓ BP: Beck's triad. Echo differentiates
Wheezing, prolonged expiration, hx of obstructive disease: but PE can coexist
Fever, productive cough, consolidation on CXR: PE can present with fever

sections.title: Management
items: Confirm diagnosis
Use CTPA; use V/Q scan if contrast is contraindicated
check clot burden and RV size on CT
check hemodynamic stability
Hemodynamically unstable > Category E
Transient or recurrent hypotension responsive to fluids > Category D
Normotensive > proceed to risk stratification
Risk stratify with sPESI, PESI, or Hestia
Lowrisk > Category B
Elevated risk > Category C
For Category C: check RV function and biomarkers
RV dysfunction plus elevated troponin > Category C3
RV dysfunction or elevated troponin alone > Category C2
Neither > Category C1
Add R modifier if hypoxemic or requiring supplemental O2
Start anticoagulation immediately
Category AC: DOAC preferred, or LMWH then DOAC
Category DE: UFH or LMWH; UFH preferred if advanced therapy may be needed
For most pts, LMWH is preferred over UFH
Category C3: watch 72h: PEITHO shock occurs @ ~1.51.8 days. Serial vitals, trop, lactate q612h
Cat DE post thrombolysis: restart heparin when aPTT <80. Watch for bleeding (esp ICH). Repeat TTE at 2448h to check RV recovery
Transition to DOAC when stable: apixaban 10mg BID x7d> 5mg BID or rivaroxaban 15mg BID x21d> 20mg daily
Duration of AC: provoked with major reversible RF: 36 mo. Unprovoked: consider indefinite. Cancer: indefinite (DOAC or LMWH)
IVC filter: If Abs CI to AC, & acute proximal DVT. Remove when anticoagulation can started/4 weeks. NOT suggested routinely
Followup within 1 week of discharge (Class I): check adherence, bleeding, sx
3 month visit: recheck anticoagulation duration, screen for persistent sx/dyspnea (CTEPD risk)

pairs.value: Best PE dx. RV/LV ratio >0.9 on CT = RV dysfunction (helps categorize C vs D)
For lowith intermediate pretest probability only (PERC negative + Wells ≤4). Ageadjusted: < age × 10 ng/mL IF >50
↑ troponin = myocardial injury from RV strain. differentiates C1 vs C2/C3
↑ = RV strain. Comb with trop & RV imaging for risk strat
↑ lactate >2 mmol/L = tissue hypoperfusion (Cat D2)
C/D. check RV/LV ratio, RV diameter, TAPSE, RVSP, McConnell sign, septal bowing, IVC collapse
Sinus tach (most common), S1Q3T3, RBBB, RV strain (TWI V1V4), right axis
Hypoxemia, resp. alkalosis, ↑ A gradient. nl. ABG does not r/o PE
If CTPA unavailable or contraindicated. DVT found in ~50% of PE pts

[Wells PE](https://www.mdcalc.com/calc/115/wellscriteriapulmonaryembolism) for pretest probability. [sPESI](https://www.mdcalc.com/calc/1247/simplified-pesi-pulmonary-embolism-severity-index) for severity: 0 = low risk (Cat B); ≥1 = elevated (Cat C+)

drugs.name: Enoxaparin/Lovenox (LMWH)
drugs.dose: 1 mg/kg SC q12h or 1.5 mg/kg SC daily
drugs.notes: Preferred over UFH for initial parenteral tx (Class I, 2026 AHA/ACC). Predictable pharmacokinetics, no monitoring.

drugs.name: Heparin (UFH)
drugs.notes: Use for Category DE (may need thrombolysis: UFH is titratable & shortacting), CrCl <30, obesity, or when rapid reversal may needed. Goal aPTT 6080 sec.

drugs.name: Norepinephrine +/ Vasopressin
drugs.dose: Norepinephrine 0.050.5 mcg/kg/min IV; add vasopressin 0.04 U/min
drugs.contraindications: No absolute CI in shock; use caution with severe ischemia or extravasation risk
drugs.notes: Preferred vasopressor approach for hypotensive PE with RV failure. Norepinephrine supports MAP with less tachycardia than epinephrine; add vasopressin (decreases PVR) when escalating pressor support or when catecholamine burden is high.

drugs.name: Epinephrine
drugs.contraindications: Relative: marked tachyarrhythmia or severe myocardial ischemia
drugs.notes: Reasonable if profound shock or periarrest & you need both inotropy & vasopressor effect quickly, but higher arrhythmia & lactate burden than levo +/ vaso.

drugs.name: Alteplase (tPA): full dose
drugs.contraindications: Active hemorrhage, CVA <3 mo, intracranial neoplasm, recent surgery <3 wk, aortic dissection
drugs.notes: Hold heparin during infusion, restart when aPTT <80. ~10% risk of severe bleeding, 1.7% risk of ICH.

drugs.name: Alteplase: ↓ dose
drugs.notes: Trending toward similar efficacy with less bleeding. Option in pts with relative CI.

drugs.name: Alteplase: catheter directed (CDL)
drugs.contraindications: Same as systemic, plus vascular access contraindications
drugs.notes: For Cat DE when considering lysis. May reduce major bleeding vs systemic.<5mg per PA NOT suggested over 5-10mg (Class 3, no benefit). Not suggested for Cat AC1.

drugs.name: Apixaban
drugs.dose: 10mg PO BID x 7 days,then5mg PO BID
drugs.notes: DOAC alternative. Lowest bleeding risk in trials. Can use with CrCl 1530 (advantage over rivaroxaban). No bridging needed.

drugs.name: Rivaroxaban
drugs.dose: 15mg PO BID x 21 days,then20mg PO daily
drugs.contraindications: CrCl <30, severe hepatic dz, triplepositive APS
drugs.notes: DOACs preferred over warfarin (Class I, 2026 AHA/ACC). No bridging needed: start directly. Take with food for absorption.

drugs.name: Tenecteplase
drugs.notes: Single IV bolus: easier administration than alteplase infusion. Studied in PEITHO trial (halfdose). Not FDAapproved for PE but used offlabel.

pairs.key: ICU / immediate higher level of care if
pairs.value: persistent respiratory distress, high-flow or NIV need, hemodynamic instability, vasopressor need, severe arrhythmia, airway risk, neurologic decline, or a trajectory suggesting pt may crash on the floor.

sections.title: References & Resources
items: Konstantinides SV, Meyer G, Becattini C, et al. 2019 ESC Guidelines for the Diagnosis & Management of Acute Pulmonary Embolism. European Heart Journal. 2020;41(4):543603. https://doi.org/10.1093/eurheartj/ehz405
Stevens SM, Woller SC, Kreuziger LB, et al. Antithrombotic Therapy for VTE Disease: 2nd Update of the CHEST Guideline. Chest. 2021;160(6):e545e608. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.chest.2021.07.055
Defined Approach to Pulmonary Embolism Response Teams (PERT Consortium). PE Response Team Management Algorithm. Journal of the American College of Cardiology. 2019.

topics.title: COPD Exacerbation
topics.subtitle: Dyspnea, wheeze, increased sputum, hypercapnia, prolonged expiration

sections.title: Acute Management
items: Airway: can the pt talk, protect the airway, & handle secretions? Worsening somnolence, inability to speak, or silent chest means impending failure
Breathing: check RR, WOB, accessory muscle use, air movement, wheeze, SpO2, & whether the pt needs controlled O2, NIV, or immediate intubation
Circulation: check HR, BP, mentation; severe tachycardia, hypotension, or diaphoresis push you to look for fatigue, sepsis, PE, or another cause of decompensation
Start controlled O2 now & target SpO2 8892%; avoid prolonged hyperoxia, especially if CO2 retention is likely

sections.title: Chart Check / Hx.
items: Baseline COPD severity, home O2?, prior ICU/intubation,home CPAP? prior PFTs if known
Trigger: infection, aspiration, HF, PE, med nonadherence, sedatives/opioids, recent procedure
Change from baseline: dyspnea, sputum volume, sputum purulence, fever, chest pain
Current inhalers, systemic steroids, recent antibiotics, anticoagulation, diuretics
Check recent gas, BMP, CBC, viral testing, CXR, & prior culture data if repeatedly admitted

sections.title: Exam
items: Respiratory distress, accessory muscle use, tripod posture, pursedlip breathing, prolonged expiration, wheeze
Silent chest, poor air mvmt, somnolence, or diaphoresis suggest impending failure
Look for trigger or alternate dx: fever, focal crackles, JVD, edema, unilateral breath sounds, pleuritic pain

pairs.value: Often viral or bacterial; symptoms are dyspnea, wheeze, sputum change, & hyperinflation
Fever, focal infiltrate, asymmetric exam, higher inflammatory burden
Orthopnea, crackles, edema, elevated BNP, vascular congestion
Pleuritic chest pain, tachycardia, hypoxemia out of proportion, RV strain
Sudden unilateral pain, asymmetric breath sounds, abrupt decompensation
Prominent bronchospasm, atopy, younger onset, stronger bronchodilator response

sections.title: Management
items: Start bronchodilators early: albuterol + ipratropium by neb, recheck air movement & WOB after each round, & keep treatments close together up front if the pt is still tight
steroids: prednisone 40 mg PO daily x5 days if able to take PO, or IV methylprednisolone if vomiting, severe distress, or poor absorption is likely
Use NIV: hypercapnic or tiring: BiPAP for acute hypercapnic respiratory failure, if pH <7.35 or CO2 is rising.
Oxygen: Titrate O2 to SpO2 8892%; recheck gas for hypercarbia
Bronchodilators: SABA is first line; combine albuterol with ipratropium early in moderatesevere exacerbations
Steroids: Prednisone 40 mg PO daily x5 days; use IV steroids if unable to take PO or absorption unremliable
Antibiotics: sputum purulence + inc. sputum and/or dyspnea, or if intubted; for 5 days
NIV: BiPAP is first line for acute hypercapnic respiratory failure, especially pH <7.35 & PaCO2 >45; lowers intubation & mortality
Intubation: Use for NIV failure, inprotect airway, severe encephalopathy, refractory hypoxemia, or hemodynamic collapse

sections.title: Orders
items: pulse ox, telemetry, frequent reassessment of WOB & mentation
VBG or ABG if moderatesevere distress, altered mentation, or suspected hypercapnia
CBC, BMP, Mg; add troponin/BNP if cardiac trigger is possible
CXR; viral testing & sputum culture only when clinically useful
ECG if tachyarrhythmia, chest pain, or possible ischemia
RT consult for nebulizers & NIV setup

drugs.name: Albuterol
drugs.dose: 2.55 mg neb q20 min x3, then q14h PRN or neb if severe
drugs.notes: Firstline bronchodilator; watch for tachycardia & hypokalemia

drugs.name: Ipratropium
drugs.dose: 0.5 mg neb q20 min x3, then q46h
drugs.notes: Add to albuterol early in moderatesevere exacerbation

drugs.name: Prednisone
drugs.notes: Preferred steroid if GI absorption is intact; longer coursesnot needed

drugs.name: Methylprednisolone
drugs.dose: 4060 mg IV daily or divided; 125 mg IV once if critically ill
drugs.notes: Use if unable to take PO, vomiting, or unreliable absorption

drugs.name: Azithromycin
drugs.dose: 500 mg daily x3 days or 500 mg day 1 then 250 mg daily
drugs.notes: Use when antibiotics are indicated; consider QT risk

drugs.name: Doxycycline
drugs.notes: Reasonable antibiotic option; avoids QT prolongation

drugs.name: Amoxicillinclavulanate
drugs.notes: Reasonable option if bacterial trigger is suspected

drugs.name: Ceftriaxone
drugs.notes: Use if severe illness, unable to take PO, or concern for pneumonia

drugs.name: Magnesium sulfate
drugs.notes: Not routine; consider as adjunct in severe bronchospasm

drugs.name: Epinephrine
drugs.notes: Not routine COPD thetherapy; use if anaphylaxis or severe upperairway component is suspected

pairs.key: ICU / immediate higher level of care if
pairs.value: persistent respiratory distress, high-flow or NIV need, hemodynamic instability, vasopressor need, severe arrhythmia, airway risk, neurologic decline, or a trajectory suggesting pt may crash on the floor.

sections.title: References & Resources
items: Global Initiative for Chronic Obstructive Lung Disease (GOLD). 2025 GOLD Report: Global Strategy for the Diagnosis, Management, and Prevention of COPD. 2025. https://goldcopd.org/2025-gold-report/
Wedzicha JA, Calverley PM, Albert RK, et al. Prevention of COPD Exacerbations: ERS/ATS Guideline. European Respiratory Journal. 2017;50(3):1602265. https://doi.org/10.1183/13993003.02265-2016

topics.title: Tracheostomy Emergencies
topics.subtitle: Obstruction, dislodgement, bleeding, secretions, loss of airway

sections.title: Acute Management
items: Airway: check patency and protect. Breathing: check WOB, oxygenation, and ventilation. Circulation: check pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
ABCs: Check air mvmt through trach, auscultate lungs, check SpO2
Suction trach tube with saline lavage (3–5 cc NS): MCC of Obst. is mucus plugging: watch fatal respiratory compromise if not cleared
If suction catheter does not pass: tube is obstructed: remove inner cannula
Give O2 via face mask over stoma while prepping
If unable to reinsert & mature trach: place suction catheter through stoma as guide & feed smaller trach or 6.0 ETT over it
If unable to reinsert & fresh trach (<7 days): Do NOT blindly reinsert: call surgery. Orotracheal intubation from above & occlude stoma
If complete airway failure: BVM over stoma (cover mouth/nose) or oral intubation (+ cover stoma)
After successful recannulation of fresh trach: keep pt under observation & notify surgical team
Prevention: suction q2h for first 24h posttrach, then q4h for 24–48h; humidified air; inner cannula cleaning q4–8h

sections.title: Chart Check / Hx.
items: Tracheostomy vs laryngectomy: distinction for airway management
Age of trach: fresh (<7 days) vs mature (>7 days)
Trach size & type (cuffed vs uncuffed, fenestrated vs nonfenestrated)
Reason for trach (prolonged intubation, tumor, subglottic stenosis, b/l vocal cord paralysis)
Baseline vent settings or trach collar status
Last trach change date, Hx. of difficult changes

sections.title: Exam
items: Air mvmt through trach: place hand over trach opening to feel airflow
Stomal site: eryma, purulence, crepitus, granulation tissue, bleeding
Auscultate lungs b/l: ↓ breath sounds: Obst., dislodgement, or pneumothorax
Subcutaneous emphysema around stoma (false passage or pneumomediastinum)
Trach positioning: is flange flush against skin? Any visible displacement?

pairs.value: Surgical opening in trachea with tube in place; pt still has an intact upper airway: can intubated orally if needed
Surgical removal of larynx; NO connection between pharynx & trachea: cannot be intubated orally. Stoma is ONLY airway
Stoma tract is immature: do NOT remove tube without surgical backup. High risk of false passage if dislodged
Established tract. Can be safely exchanged at bedside if dislodged
Mucus plugging (most common), blood clot, granulation tissue, false passage, tube malposition. Bronchoscopy may needed for granulation tissue or malposition dx
Accidental decannulation, pt selfremoval, loose trach ties, coughing, obesity (deep stoma tract)
Granulation tissue at stoma, suctioning trauma, peristomal skin breakdown. Usually selflimited.

Tracheoinnominate fistula (TIF): hemorrhage from erosion into brachiocephalic artery. ~3 days–6 weeks post trach. ~100% Mortality w/o intervention, 64.5% overall. Mild bleed> massive hemorrhage in 44% of cases (typically within 1 week)
Stomal cellulitis, tracheitis, abscess, VAP/tracheostomyassociated pneumonia

sections.title: Management
items: If still Obst after inner cannula removal: deflate cuff & remove entire trach tube
Try to reinsert trach tube (use one size smaller if difficulty). Use obturator for insertion.
Tube obstruction/dislodgement: Remove inner cannula, suction aggressively, provide oxygen at face & stoma, & replace tracheostomy if trained personnel & equipment are available.
Call airway/ENT/anesthesia early. If trach replacement fails, attempt oral intubation if anatomy permits; if not, ventilate through stoma.
Bleeding: Sentinel bleeding can precede tracheoinnominate fistula. Overinflate cuff, try to manually compress, & move now toward operative control.
Infection / local issues: Treat stomal cellulitis, tracheitis, mucus plugging, cuff problems, or false passage based on cause while maintaining airway patency throughout.
Bedside equipment: Always have at bedside: trach tube one size smaller, obturator, ETTs (6.0–7.0), BVM, suction with catheters, spare inner cannula, 10 mL syringe for cuff
Predischarge: All pts & caregivers receive emergency trach management education & maintain emergency supplies (spare trach, suction, BVM) at all times

sections.title: TracheoInnominate Fistula (TIF) Protocol
items: initial bleed: scant BRB: massive hemorrhage —> treat as TIF until proven otherwise
Activate MTP: anticipate massive hemorrhage
Call ENT/surgery STAT: tx is emergent OR
Hyperinflate cuff: inflate with 50 cc air to tamponade bleeding vessel against sternum (controls hemorrhage success ~70%)
If fails: remove trach, intubate orally (advance ETT distal to fistula site) & inflate ETT cuff past bleeding point
Digital innominate artery compression: insert finger through stoma, compress artery anteriorly against posterior sternum
Maintain digital pressure during transport to OR
Sentinel bleeding (minor hemoptysis) precedes massive hemorrhage in 44% of TIF cases,within 1 week: warrants urgent bronchoscopy & imaging
Txt: open surgical ligation of innominate artery or endovascular occlusion (comparable success rates)
Mortality ~64.5% overall, ~100% without intervention: rapid recognition & temporization are critical

pairs.value: SpO2, VBG/ABG, CBC, BMP, type & screen (if bleeding)
CXR: tube position, pneumothorax, pneumomediastinum, subcutaneous emphysema
Type & crossmatch, coags (PT/INR, PTT), fibrinogen. CT angiography if TIF suspected (if stable enough)
Sputum culture, BCxs, wound culture if purulent stomal drainage

drugs.name: NS(trach)
drugs.notes: Loosens secretions before suctioning. Use before routine suctioning.

drugs.name: Albuterol
drugs.notes: For bronchospasm or peristomal wheezing.

drugs.name: Vancomycin
drugs.notes: For stomal cellulitis/abscess with MRSA risk. Adjust for renal function.

drugs.name: PiperacillinTazobactam
drugs.notes: Broadspectrum for tracheostomyrelated pneumonia or deep stomal infection.

pairs.key: ICU / immediate higher level of care if
pairs.value: persistent respiratory distress, high-flow or NIV need, hemodynamic instability, vasopressor need, severe arrhythmia, airway risk, neurologic decline, or a trajectory suggesting pt may crash on the floor.

sections.title: References & Resources
items: Long B, Koyfman A. Resuscitating the Tracheostomy Patient in ED. The American Journal of Emergency Medicine. 2016.
Joshi KD, Singh A, Singh DK, et al. TracheoInnominate Artery Fistula: A Systematic Review of Diagnostic & Management Strategies. Otolaryngology Head & Neck Surgery. 2025.

McGrath BA, Bates L, Atkinson D, Moore JA. Multidisciplinary Guidelines for the Management of Tracheostomy and Laryngectomy Airway Emergencies (NTSP). Anaesthesia. 2012;67(9):1025-1041. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1365-2044.2012.07217.x
Mitchell RB, Hussey HM, Setzen G, et al. Clinical Consensus Statement: Tracheostomy Care. Otolaryngology — Head & Neck Surgery. 2013;148(1):620. https://doi.org/10.1177/0194599812460376

topics.title: Angioedema
topics.subtitle: Lip or tongue swelling, stridor, drooling, airway threat, urticaria variable

sections.title: Acute Management
items: How do they look? Voice change, stridor, drooling, dyspnea, tongue swelling, or bottom of mouth swelling = high risk
Airway: priority. Hoarseness, muffled voice, poolimg secretions, or progressive tongue/floor of mouth/laryngeal edema
Breathing: check SpO2, stridor, & WOB; worsening effort> airway is narrowing
Circulation: check BP, HR, & for flushing, wheeze, or collapse> anaphylaxis> isolated angioedema
Prepare for difficult airway early: fiberoptic setup, surgical airway kit, & anesthesia/ENT at bedside if airway symptoms are present

sections.title: Chart Check / Hx.
items: ACEi use Can cause angioedema !!at any point!! during therapy
Known hereditary angioedema (HAE): prior attacks, family Hx., home meds (C1 inhibitor, icatibant)
Recent allergen exposure: foods, insect stings, meds, contrast dye
urticaria/hives: histamine mediated (allergic)
No urticaria:c/f bradykinin mediated (HAE or ACEI)
Prior intubations or difficult airways
BB use: blunt epinephrine response?
GI sx: Abd angioedema can mimic SBO (nausea, vomiting, cramping)

sections.title: Exam
items: Airway: voice changes, muffled voice, stridor, drooling = impending airway loss
Face/lips: asymmetric,edema:
Tongue: protrusion or floor of mouth swelling = high risk
Oropharynx: uvular edema (Quincke disease), soft palate swelling
Larynx: direct or fiberoptic visualization if any voice changes (24% need surgical airway)
Hands, feet, genitals: other common sites of involvement
Abdomen: tender, distension: intestinal angioedema can mimic surgical abdomen

pairs.value: Paroxysmal, nonpitting, nondemarcated swelling of dermal or submucosal layers —asymmetric & nonpruritic
IgE mast cell degranulation (allergic). w/ urticaria. Responds to epinephrine, antihistamines, steroids
Excessive bradykinin from unregulated complement pathway. Does NOT respond to standard anaphylaxis meds
C1 esterase inhibitor deficiency (autosomal dominant). 75% onset before age 15. Recurrent attacks
Incidence 0.12.2% (higher in Black pts). Can occur months to years after starting Meds
Isolated uvular angioedema: specific presentation
Foods, insect stings, meds, latex: with urticaria & pruritus
Most common drug cause. 0.12.2% incidence. Higher risk: Black pts, females, age >65
C1 esterase inh. deficiency: Type I (85%, low level) or Type II (15%, dysfunctional). AD inheritance
Associated with lymphoproliferative disorders, autoimmune disease
Angioedema + ↓ BP + bronchospasm + urticaria: treat as anaphylaxis
Localized, pruritic,vesicular: clear exposure Hx.
Warm, tender, erymatous: unilateral, fever, leukocytosis
After eating fish: facial flushing, urticaria, histamine mediated

sections.title: Management
items: Airway first: if progressive tongue, floor of mouth, or laryngeal edema>early & secure the airway before complete obstruction develops. call ent and ANES
Cause: urticaria/pruritus (histamine); no urticaria with ACEI use or HAE history (bradykinin).
Histamine mediated: IM epinephrine 0.3 mg, diphenhydramine 50 mg IV, methylprednisolone 125 mg IV, bronchodilators if needed.
Bradykinin mediated: ACEi or hereditary angioedema> mild response to epinephrine, steroids, & antihistamines. Use C1 esterase inhibitor, icatibant, or ecallantide; FFP if C1 inhibitor unavailable.
ACEi angioedema: stop ACE inhibitor permanently. Consider icatibant 30 mg SC & tranexamic acid 1 g IV bradykinin mediated disease is suspected.
If refractory or on a BB, consider glucagon 5 mg IV. Observe if mild isolated lip/facial swelling closely.
airway assessments q12h: angioedema can progress unpredictably
ACEi angioedema: Stop permanently. Do NOT switch to another ACEI. ARBs have low cross reactivity but use with caution
HAE: home tx plan: pt have icatibant or C1 inhibitor for selfadministration
HAE prophylaxis (hematology/allergy): lanadelumab SC q2wks, oral berotralstat, or C1 inhibitor SC/IV twice weekly
Allergic angioedema: give epinephrine autoinjector, allergy referral, identify & avoid triggers
Avoid estrogen containing meds in HAE (oral contraceptives, HRT: can trigger attacks)

pairs.value: Best screening test for HAE: low during & between attacks. nl. C4 rules out HAE
Low level (Type I) or low function with nl. level (Type II) confirms HAE
Elevated in anaphylaxis/mast cell activation: helps distinguish histaminemed.
Baseline labs; eosinophilia may suggest allergic etiology
ACEI, ARBs (rare), NSAIDs, estrogencontaining meds (can trigger HAE)
If any voice changes: directly check laryngeal edema & airway patency
If unable to do laryngoscopy: check for airway narrowing

drugs.name: Epinephrine
drugs.notes: Firstline for allergic/histaminemed. angioedema. Less effective for bradykininmed. but still give if etiology uncertain.

drugs.name: C1 inhibitor (Berinert/Cinryze)
drugs.notes: Firstline for hereditary angioedema. Replaces deficient C1 esterase inhibitor. Rapid onset.

drugs.name: Icatibant (Firazyr)
drugs.notes: Bradykinin B2 receptor antagonist. Effective for both HAE & ACEi angioedema. Decreases resolution time from 27h to 8h in ACEi angioedema.

drugs.name: Ecallantide (Kalbitor)
drugs.dose: 10mg SC x 3 injections at different sites (30mg total)
drugs.contraindications: Risk of anaphylaxis: must be administered by healthcare provider
drugs.notes: Kallikrein inhibitor. For HAE attacks. Nonsignificant trend toward benefit in ACEI angioedema.

drugs.name: Tranexamic acid
drugs.notes: Antifibrinolytic: adjunct for bradykininmed. angioedema. Inhibits plasmin activation of complement.

drugs.name: Diphenhydramine
drugs.notes: H1 blocker. Effective for histamine angioedema. Ineffective for bradykinin mediated.

drugs.name: Methylprednisolone
drugs.notes: For histaminemed. angioedema. No role in bradykininmed. angioedema.

drugs.name: Glucagon
drugs.notes: For pts on BB who are not responding to epinephrine. Bypasses betareceptor blockade.

drugs.name: FFP
drugs.notes: Contains C1 esterase inhibitor. Use if C1 inhibitor concentrate unavailable. oretical risk of worsening HAE by providing substrate
