items: Depetri F, Tedeschi A, Cugno M. Angioedema & Emergency Medicine: From Pathophysiology to Diagnosis & Treatment. European Journal of Internal Medicine. 2019.
Moellman JJ, Bernstein JA, Lindsell C, et al. A Consensus Parameter for the Evaluation & Management of Angioedema in the Emergency Department. Academic Emergency Medicine. 2014. Guideline.

Zuraw BL, Bernstein JA, Lang DM, et al. A Focused Parameter Update: Hereditary Angioedema, Acquired C1 Inhibitor Deficiency, & AngiotensinConverting Enzyme InhibitorAssociated Angioedema. The Journal of Allergy & Clinical Immunology. 2013. Guideline.
New Thetherapies for Type 1 & Type 2 Hereditary Angioedema.

Maurer M, Magerl M, Betschel S, et al. The International WAO/EAACI Guideline for the Management of Hereditary Angioedema — The 2021 Revision and Update. Allergy. 2022;77(7):19611990. https://doi.org/10.1111/all.15214

Bernstein JA, Moellman JJ. Emerging Concepts in the Diagnosis and Treatment of Patients with Undifferentiated Angioedema. International Journal of Emergency Medicine. 2012;5:39. https://doi.org/10.1186/1865-1380-5-39
Yang WH, Banerji A. Angioedema. Allergy, Asthma & Clinical Immunology. 2024;20:41. https://aacijournal.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13223-024-00934-3

topics.title: Severe Asthma
topics.subtitle: Wheeze, dyspnea, silent chest, hypoxemia, rising CO2, fatigue

sections.title: Acute Management
items: Airway: check patency and protect. Breathing: check WOB, oxygenation, and ventilation. Circulation: check pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
How do they look? mentation, ability to speak, air mvmt, RR, accessory muscle use, SpO2, BP, & hemodynamics
Call RT early. pulse ox, cardiac monitor, frequent BP, & EtCO2 if avail
Give O2 to keep SpO2 >92%; NRB first if severely hypoxemic
Ensure IV access & watch for fatigue, silent chest, worsening Co2, or impending arrest
If not crashing: NIPPV;don't delay intubation if worsening
Prepare early for advanced airway management if exhaustion, declining mentation, poor air mvmt, or refractory hypoxemia

sections.title: Chart Check / Hx.
items: Asthma severity: prior intubations (#1 predictor of fatal asthma),ICU admit, recent ED visits/hospitalizations
Controller meds: ICS, LABA, LAMA, biologics?
Recent PO/IV steroid use (severe baseline)
triggers: URI, allergen, med nonadherence, recent intubation/extubation, BB, NSAIDs, environmental irritants
Home PEF baseline if known
Comorbidities: GERD, OSA, allergic rhinitis, ABPA, anxiety
Smoking hx, occupational exposures
Allergies: esp aspirin/NSAID sensitivity (Samter triad)

sections.title: Exam
items: Concerning findings:
Severe WOB, unable to speak full sentences
Silent chest or markedly ↓ air movement: ominous, suggests critical Obst.
AMS, exhaustion, accessory muscle fatigue
Cyanosis
Pulsus paradoxus >10 mmHg (>1520 mmHg =severe)
Rising EtCO2: esp if previously low, normalizing in tiring pt (fatigue, NOT improvement)
check: air entry bilaterally, wheezing (diffuse vs focal), RR, HR, accessory muscle use, diaphoresis, ability to speak, tripod positioning

pairs.value: Deadly asthma exacerbation with severe airflow Obst. & bronchospasm not responding to initial therapy. risk of resp failure, arrest, barotrauma, & hemodynamic collapse
Or <40% predicted: suggests severe exacerbation
Very high risk: consider early intubation
Air trapping from incomplete exhalation: ↑ intrathoracic pressure: ↓ venous return: ↓ BP. Key concern during mechanical ventilation
Intrinsic PEEP from air trapping. Worse by high RR, short expiratory time, or breath stacking
Accepting ↑ PaCO2 if pH ≥~7.15 & hemodynamics acceptable: avoids dangerous vent settings/barotrauma

sections.title: Red Flags for Imminent Respiratory Failure
items: Rising EtCO2 with worsening exam
Falling pH on ABG/VBG
AMS: confusion, somnolence, agitation
Silent chest: no wheezing = no air movement
Pulsus paradoxus >1520 mmHg
SpO2 <90% despite NRB or high flow O2
Accessory muscle fatigue: pt is tiring out
PEF <100 L/min or <25% predicted
Normalizing CO2 + pt in distress = fatigue, NOT improvement> intubation

pairs.value: URI (MCC), med nonadherence, BB, NSAIDs, recent intubation/extubation, environmental irritants
COPD exacerbation, anaphylaxis, angioedema, PE, pneumothorax, foreign body aspiration, vocal cord dysfunction, CHF (cardiac asthma), endobronchial lesion
Pneumothorax ( check CXR before positive pressure), PE, flash pulm edema, pneumonia, aspiration, pleural effusion
ACOS (asthmaCOPD overlap), eosinophilic pneumonia, ABPA, ChurgStrauss

sections.title: Management
items: Stabilize O2 & airflow now: give O2 to keep SpO2 >92%, albuterol Intermittent: 5mg q20min x3, then 2.5-10mg q14hr as needed OR Continuous 15mg/hr, Levalbuterol if significant tachycardia [5] Ipratropium 0.250.5mg q20min & recheck air movement/ WOB after every treatment cycle
methylprednisolone 60125 mg IV = prednisone 4060 mg PO early (IV/PO equally effective).
If still severe> add MGSO4 2g IV over ~20 min

Escalate before the crash: if pt tiring, CO2 is rising, or air movement getting worse> call ICU / airway help, NIPPV if possible: max insp support (insp pressure 8, Peep 03), & consider IM epinephrine or SC terbutaline for refractory severe bronchospasm while setting up the next step

Move to airway control & postintubation stabilization if failing: intubate for exhaustion, worsening AMS, silent chest, severe acidosis, refractory hypoxemia, or periarrest. After intubation, use low RR, long expiratory time, permissive hypercapnia, & watch closely for autoPEEP, hypotension, & pneumothorax

sections.title: Intubation & Vent Strategy
items: Indications for intubation: AMS, progressive fatigue, rising PaCO2/EtCO2 + distress, resp acidosis, silent chest, persistent hypoxemia, arrest/periarrest, NIPPV failure
RSI: ketamine (bronchodilatory) ispreferred induction agent. Avoid histaminereleasing agents
AC mode
RR 810/min; start slow; avoid air trapping
If Pplat >30 > lower RR
Low I:E >prolonged expiratory time
Low peak pressure> avoid breath stacking > correcting CO2
VT 68 mL/kg IBW
PEEP 05
Flow 80100 L/min
FiO2 100%; titrate down fast
If Pplat still >30 > think PTX, misplaced ETT, edema
Permissive hypoventilation / hypercapnia OK; goal pH >7.2, Pplat <30
Deep sedation required. NMB if severe dyssynchrony or marked autoPEEP
If postintubation ↓ BP: c/f autoPEEP: take off vent briefly to allow exhalation (can be lifesaving). Also r/o PTX

pairs.value: worsening status, or impending resp failure. Normalizing CO2 in distressed pt = danger sign
Lytes, renal function. ↓ K from albuterol. Ensure Mg repleted
Eosinophilia (allergic), ↑ WBC (infection vs steroid effect)
If pt can do it without delaying tx. <100 L/min or <40% predicted = severe. <25% predicted =high risk
Only if C/F pneumothorax, PNA, aspiration, edema, or alternative dx. Not routine
May be ↑ from WOB, ↑ catecholamines, or albuterol (betaagonist effect: not always tissue hypoperfusion)
If available. Rising EtCO2 = worsening Obst. or fatigue

drugs.name: Albuterol ( neb)
drugs.notes: Firstline. Some centers use higher doses in extreme cases. Monitor HR, K

drugs.name: Ipratropium
drugs.notes: Add to albuterol for severe exacerbations. Anticholinergic bronchodilator

drugs.name: Methylprednisolone
drugs.notes: Give early: takes 46h for full effect. Transition to PO prednisone 4060mg when able

drugs.name: Magnesium sulfate
drugs.notes: Smooth muscle relaxation. Single dose. Monitor for ↓ BP, flushing

drugs.name: Epinephrine (IM)
drugs.contraindications: Use cautiously in elderly, CAD, severe HTN
drugs.notes: For anaphylaxis, angioedema, or refractory severe bronchospasm. IV infusion in ICU for refractory cases

drugs.name: Terbutaline
drugs.notes: Beta2 agonist. Can consider infusion in refractory cases

drugs.name: Ketamine
drugs.dose: 0.52 mg/kg IV bolus; can follow with infusion
drugs.notes: Bronchodilatory properties. Useful for severe agitation, periintubation sedation, or dissociative dose for RSI

drugs.name: Heliox (80:20 or 70:30)
drugs.dose: inhalation via tightfitting mask or vent
drugs.contraindications: High FiO2 requirement (heliox requires low FiO2 blend to be effective)
drugs.notes: ↓ Airflow resistance via lower gas density. Most useful if FiO2 requirement is low. Not universally available

pairs.value: Normalizing/rising CO2 in distressed pt = impending failure. Postintubation ↓ BP: think autoPEEP, disconnect vent briefly. Avoid breath stacking. Watch for ↓ K from albuterol

sections.title: References & Resources
items: Garner O, Ramey JS, Hanania NA. Management of LifeThreatening Asthma: Severe Asthma Series. Chest. 2022.
Dabbs W, Bradley MH, Chamberlin SM. Acute Asthma Exacerbations: Management Strategies. American Family Physician. 2024.
Global Initiative for Asthma (GINA). 2024 GINA Report: Global Strategy for Asthma Management and Prevention. 2024. https://ginasthma.org/2024-report/

National Asthma Education and Prevention Program (NAEPP). 2020 Focused Updates to the Asthma Management Guidelines. NIH/NHLBI. 2020. https://www.nhlbi.nih.gov/health-topics/asthma-management-guidelines-2020-updates
Rowe BH, Bretzlaff JA, Bourdon C, et al. Magnesium Sulfate for Treating Exacerbations of Acute Asthma in the Emergency Department. Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews. 2000;(2):CD001490.

topics.title: Upper Airway Obst.
topics.subtitle: Stridor, hoarseness, drooling, muffled voice, distress

sections.title: Acute Management
items: Airway: check patency and protect. Breathing: check WOB, oxygenation, and ventilation. Circulation: check pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
Decide partial vs complete obstruction now
Complete obstruction: Heimlich/Abd thrusts for foreign body; chest thrusts if obese or pregnant; CPR if unconscious
Partial obstruction: keep pt calm, upright, and avoid agitation
Call anesthesia, ENT, surgery early; keep cric tray at bedside
Give O2; use heliox if FiO2 allows
If c/f Infxn: start IV abx now
If cant intubate & cant oxygenate: surgical cricothyrotomy without delay
Avoid blind nasotracheal intubation or aggressive oral exam in suspected epiglottitis/supraglottic disease

sections.title: Chart Check / Hx.
items: Onset: sudden (foreign body, anaphylaxis, laryngospasm) vs progressive (infection, tumor, hematoma, edema)
Recent intubation/extubation: postextubation stridor (laryngeal edema, subglottic stenosis). Was cuffleak test done?
Recent surgery: neck, thyroid, carotid ( expanding hematoma), cervical spine
Infection sx: fever, sore throat, odynophagia, trismus, dental pain (Ludwig angina), drooling
Allergic hx: anaphylaxis, angioedema, ACEI use (bradykininmed. angioedema: won't respond to epi/steroids/antihistamines)
Smoking, radiation to H&N, known laryngeal/tracheal tumor or stenosis
Anticoagulation: ↑ risk of neck/retropharyngeal hematoma
Prior difficult airway, h/o tracheostomy, known subglottic stenosis
Immunocompromised: deep space infections, fungal epiglottitis, necrotizing infections

sections.title: Exam
items: Stridor: inspiratory = supraglottic/glottic. Expiratory = subglottic/tracheal. Biphasic = fixed glottic/subglottic lesion
Voice changes: hotpotato voice (supraglottic: epiglottitis, peritonsillar abscess), hoarseness (glottic: vocal cord pathology)
Air movement: present (partial) vs absent (complete: emergency)
Drooling, inability to swallow secretions: suggests supraglottic edema or mass
Tripod positioning, neck extension: pt optimizing airway
Neck exam: swelling, tenderness, crepitus (subcutaneous emphysema), tracheal deviation, expanding hematoma
Floor of mouth: woody/brawny induration, tongue protrusion ( Ludwig angina)
Oropharynx: peritonsillar bulging (PTA), uvular deviation: avoid in suspected epiglottitis in children
Trismus: suggests deep space infection (Ludwig, parapharyngeal, masticator space)
Cyanosis, ↓ SpO2, accessory muscle use, paradoxical chest/Abd movement

pairs.value: Structures above the carina: nose, pharynx, larynx, extrathoracic trachea. Obstruction at any level can life-threatening
Some air movement present. Stridor, voice changes, ↑ WOB. Canprogress to complete. Keep pt calm, do NOT agitate
No air movement. Unable to speak, cough, or breathe. Silent. Cyanosis. Immediate intervention required
Highpitched sound from turbulent airflow through narrowed airway. Inspiratory = supraglottic. Expiratory = subglottic. Biphasic = fixed obstruction.
Can't Intubate, Can't Oxygenate: indication for surgical cricothyrotomy. Final common pathway in airway emergency
Located between thyroid & cricoid cartilage. 1 fingerbreadth below laryngeal prominence. Site for emergency surgical airway
Sudden onset choking, coughing, stridor. Food bolus most common in adults (esp elderly, intoxicated, dentures, neurologic dysphagia)
Rapidonset laryngeal/tongue/lip swelling. Histaminemed. (epi responsive) vs bradykininmed. (ACEI: does NOT respond to epi/steroids/antihistamines, needs icatibant or C1INH)
Severe sore throat out of proportion to exam, dysphagia, drooling, muffled/hotpotato voice, stridor, fever. Lateral neck XR: thumbprint sign. Do NOT agitate
Bilateral submandibular/sublingual/submental space infection. Woody floor of mouth, tongue protrusion, trismus. 85% odontogenic origin. Airway emergency
Unilateral tonsillar bulging, uvular deviation, trismus, hotpotato voice. Needle aspiration or I&D
Neck stiffness, dysphagia, drooling, fever. CT neck with contrast. ↑ risk of mediastinitis if ruptures
Laryngeal edema from intubation. Risk factors: prolonged intubation, large ETT, traumatic intubation, female. Cuffleak test can predict
Postsurgical (thyroid, carotid, spine), anticoagulated. Expanding hematoma = open neck at bedside if airway compromised
Sudden glottic closure from irritation (secretions, blood, extubation). Positive pressure, jaw thrust, small dose succinylcholine (0.10.3 mg/kg IV) or topical lidocaine
Progressive stridor, hoarseness, dysphagia. Laryngeal, thyroid, mediastinal masses. May need awake fiberoptic intubation or tracheostomy
Burns to face/oropharynx, singed nasal hairs, carbonaceous sputum, hoarseness. Intubate early: edema worsens over 1224h
Bilateral = stridor & obstruction. Postthyroidectomy (recurrent laryngeal nerve injury), intubation trauma, neurologic causes

sections.title: Management
items: Immediate assessment
Position pt upright (if tolerated)
check severity: stridor, voice changes, inability to lie supine, respiratory distress
Call for help early (anesthesia, ENT, surgery)
Keep pt calm; avoid agitation
Initial Interventions
Oxygen: Highflow oxygen (consider nasal high flow 160 L/min if avail)
Treat underlying cause:
Anaphylaxis: IM epinephrine 0.30.5 mg (1:1000)
Angioedema: Consider epinephrine, antihistamines, steroids
Infection: Antibiotics, steroids (if needed)
Foreign body: Heimlich maneuver or direct removal
Temporizing measures:
Heliox (80% helium/20% oxygen) if avail
Nasopharyngeal airway
Bagvalvemask ventilation (if needed)
Definitive Airway Management
Indications for intubation:
Inability to maintain oxygenation/ventilation
Progressive obstruction despite treatment
Stridor at rest, severe voice changes
Inability to handle secretions
Decreased level of consciousness
Extensive facial/neck burns or trauma
Intubation approach:
Preferred: Awake fiberoptic or video laryngoscopy (if difficult airway predicted)
Alternative: Rapid sequence intubation (if airway anatomy favorable)
Have rescue plan ready: LMA, cricothyroidotomy kit
Surgical Airway (if intubation fails)

pairs.value: Conscious: Abd thrusts (chest thrusts if obese/pregnant). Unconscious: CPR with airway checks. Direct laryngoscopy with Magill forceps for visible FB. If below cords: rigid bronchoscopy
IM epi first. If laryngeal edema progressing: early intubation (smaller ETT, video laryngoscopy). Surgical airway if CICO
Do NOT agitate. Double setup: OR with most experienced intubator + ENT for surgical airway backup. Awake fiberoptic preferred if avail. Smaller ETT (12 sizes down)
Awake fiberoptic intubation preferred (trismus limits DL). Have surgical airway ready. Do NOT paralyze: loss of muscle tone causes complete obstruction. ENT/OMFS for I&D
Awake intubation if cooperative. Risk of rupture & aspiration during intubation. CT neck first if stable. Surgical drainage by ENT
Neb racemic epi + dexamethasone. If refractory: reintubate (smaller ETT). Consider prophylactic steroids preextubation in high risk pts
Emergent bedside neck opening if airway threatened: cut sutures/staples, evacuate hematoma.thensecure airway. Do NOT wait for OR
Intubate early: edema worsens over 1224h. Once swollen, intubation becomes extremely difficult. Large ETT (edema will narrow around it)
Awake fiberoptic intubation or tracheostomy under local. May need rigid bronchoscopy. Avoid paralysis: tumor may cause complete obstruction.
If pt is in distress, secure airway first
Thumbprint sign (epiglottitis), prevertebral soft tissue widening (retropharyngeal abscess), steeple sign (subglottic stenosis)
Gold standard for deep space infections (Ludwig, retropharyngeal, parapharyngeal abscess), hematoma, tumor. Only if pt is stable enough
Direct visualization of supraglottic & glottic structures. Best diagnostic tool if avail & safe to do
Preextubation: deflate cuff, occlude ETT, check air leak around tube. No leak: ↑ risk of postextubation stridor
Infection workup, metabolic status
If infectious etiology suspected: before abx if feasible
If respiratory distress: check ventilation & oxygenation
If anaphylaxis suspected: send within 12h of onset

drugs.name: Racemic epinephrine (neb)
drugs.dose: 2.25% solution: 0.5mL in 3mL NS nebulized. Alt: Lepinephrine 5mL of 1mg/mL neb
drugs.notes: Mucosal vasoconstriction: ↓ airway edema. Effect in 1030 min. May rebound: observe ≥2h after. Can repeat

drugs.name: Dexamethasone
drugs.dose: 10mg IV (adults). Can give 0.6 mg/kg IV/PO in pediatrics
drugs.notes: Give early: onset 46h. ↓ Airway edema for inflammatory/infectious/postextubation causes. May give preextubation prophylactically in high risk pts

drugs.name: Epinephrine (IM)
drugs.notes: Firstline for anaphylaxis related airway obstruction. Does NOT work for bradykininmed. angioedema (ACEI). See Angioedema topic

drugs.name: Heliox (70:30 or 80:20)
drugs.dose: inhalation via tightfitting NRB or vent
drugs.contraindications: High FiO2 requirement (>40% limits heliox effectiveness)
drugs.notes: Heliumoxygen mix: lower density: ↓ turbulent flow: ↓ WOB in partial obstruction. Temporizing bridge while definitive tx is arranged

drugs.name: Ceftriaxone
drugs.notes: Empiric abx for epiglottitis, deep space infections. Add vancomycin if MRSA concern. Alt: ampicillinsulbactam 3g IV q6h

drugs.name: Ampicillinsulbactam
drugs.notes: Broadspectrum for Ludwig angina & deep space infections: covers oral flora incl anaerobes. Alt: penicillin G + metronidazole

pairs.key: ICU / immediate higher level of care if
pairs.value: persistent respiratory distress, high-flow or NIV need, hemodynamic instability, vasopressor need, severe arrhythmia, airway risk, neurologic decline, or a trajectory suggesting pt may crash on the floor.

sections.title: References & Resources
items: Duff JP, Topjian AA, Berg MD, et al. 2019 AHA Focused Update on Pediatric Advanced Life Support: Airway Management. Circulation. 2019;140(24):e904e914.
Pfleger A, Eber E. Management of Acute Severe Upper Airway Obstruction in Children. Paediatric Respiratory Reviews. 2013;14(2):7077. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.prrv.2013.02.003
Cook TM, Woodall N, Frerk C. Major Complications of Airway Management in the UK: Results of NAP4. British Journal of Anaesthesia. 2011;106(5):617631. https://doi.org/10.1093/bja/aer058

topics.title: Pneumothorax / Hemothorax
topics.subtitle: Sudden dyspnea, pleuritic pain, unilateral breath sounds, shock

sections.title: Acute Management
items: Airway: check patency and protect. Breathing: check WOB, oxygenation, and ventilation. Circulation: check pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
ABCs: check for tension physiology now: ↓ BP, tachycardia, JVD, tracheal deviation, no lung sliding
Tension pneumothorax = clinical dx: do NOT delay for CXR. Treat now
Needle decompression: 1416g needle, 2nd ICS midclavicular line or 4th5th ICS anterior axillary line, then place a definitive chest tube
For massive hemothorax with shock: activate MTP, decompress/drain, & call thoracic surgery early
If ventilated pt develops sudden ↑ PIP, ↓ BP, ↓ SpO2: suspect tension pneumo: needle decompress now

sections.title: Chart Check / Hx.
items: Mechanism: trauma (penetrating, blunt, rib fractures), iatrogenic (central line, thoracentesis, biopsy, mechanical ventilation/barotrauma, postCPR)
Primary spontaneous: tall, thin, young male, smoker. No underlying lung dz
Secondary spontaneous: COPD (most common), asthma, CF, PNA (necrotizing), PCP, TB, lung cancer, Marfan, LAM
On mechanical ventilation: barotrauma from high PEEP, high VT, air trapping (status asthmaticus)
Recent procedures: subclavian/IJ central line, thoracentesis, pacemaker, lung biopsy
Anticoagulation status (hemothorax risk)
Prior pneumo (recurrence rate ~30% for primary spontaneous)
Catamenial pneumothorax (recurrent pneumo with menses: endometriosis related)

sections.title: Exam
items: Tension pneumo signs: ↓ BP, tachycardia, JVD, tracheal deviation away from affected side,Hypoxia (50% nonintubted; 90% intubated) hyperresonance to percussion, cyanosis, PEA arrest
Simple pneumo: ↓ breath sounds unilaterally, ± pleuritic chest pain, dyspnea. May be subtle
Hemothorax: ↓ breath sounds, dullness to percussion (vs hyperresonance in pneumo), signs of hemorrhagic shock if massive
Subcutaneous emphysema: crepitus on palpation (air tracking through soft tissue)
Chest wall: tenderness, instability, flail segment (multiple rib fractures)
In ventilated pt: sudden ↑ PIP, ↓ compliance, ↓ SpO2, ↓ BP, difficulty bagging: think tension pneumo

pairs.value: Air in pleural space without mediastinal shift. May be small (<2cm at apex) or large
Oneway valve: air enters but can't escape. Progressive pressure: mediastinal shift: ↓ venous return: obstructive shock: PEA arrest. Clinical dx: treat before imaging
"Sucking chest wound": defect in chest wall communicating with pleural space. Cover with 3sided occlusive dressing,thenchest tube
Blood in pleural space. Massive = >1500mL initial or >200mL/h ongoing. Causes: trauma, iatrogenic, aortic rupture, tumor, coagulopathy
Air + blood: may need 2 chest tubes (apical for air, basilar for blood)
Seen on CT but not CXR. If pt being ventilated: still needs chest tube (risk of converting to tension)
Penetrating injury, blunt trauma with rib fractures, blast injury
Central line (subclavian > IJ), thoracentesis, lung biopsy, pacemaker, mechanical ventilation (barotrauma), postCPR
Rupture of apical subpleural blebs. Tall, thin, young male, smoker. Recurrence ~30%
COPD (most common), asthma, CF, PCP PNA, TB cavitary, lung cancer, Marfan/EhlersDanlos, LAM
Tension pneumo, PE, cardiac tamponade, MI, anaphylaxis, autoPEEP (air trapping in ventilated pt)
Pneumothorax, pleural effusion/hemothorax, mucus plugging/atelectasis, mainstem intubation
Immediate needle/finger decompression, then chest tube; 2nd ICS midclavicular or 5th ICS midaxillary; no imaging delay.

Stable + small: conservative mgmt, O2, analgesia, observe, repeat CXR; discharge if stable. Large/symptomatic: aspiration, pigtail, or chest tube. Small, stable PSP in a nonventilated pt may observed with O2 & serial CXR.
Low threshold for drainage; PPV or hemodynamic compromise = chest tube; traumatic tension = decompression first, then tube.

Smallbore or pigtail acceptable for pneumothorax; largebore tube for blood/clot; 4th6th ICS anterior or midaxillary line. Typical sizing: 2832 Fr for pneumo, 3236 Fr for hemothorax. Connect to underwater seal/Pleurevac with 20 cmH2O suction & get postprocedure CXR.
VATS for persistent air leak, recurrence, bilateral disease, hemopneumothorax, or high risk occupation; not routine for first PSP.
>=300500 mL or unstable: largebore chest tube; drain early, resuscitate, involve trauma/thoracic surgery.
>1500 mL initial or >200 mL/h x3h: decompress, restore blood, MTP, urgent VATS/thoracotomy evaluation; consider autotransfusion where available.
<300 mL + stable: conservative mgmt, repeat CXR up to 24h; avoid retained blood >300 mL.
VATS first line, ideally within 5 days; surgery for instability, retained hemothorax, persistent air leak, or diaphragmatic injury.
Warm saline irrigation may reduce reintervention; antibiotics for penetrating trauma, retained hemothorax drainage, or operative mgmt.
Do not delay decompression for imaging; chest tube output alone can mislead; fibrinolytics only if poor surgical candidate.

sections.title: Chest Tube Insertion
items: Position: supine or semireclined, arm abducted overhead on affected side
Site: 4th5th ICS, anterior axillary line (triangle of safety)
Prep, drape, local anesthesia (lidocaine down to pleura)
Make 23cm skin incision along rib (one interspace below target). Dissect bluntly with Kelly clamp over TOP of rib (avoid neurovascular bundle on inferior rib margin)
Puncture parietal pleura with clamp: expect rush of air (pneumo) or blood (hemothorax)
Finger sweep pleural space to confirm intrapleural position & clear adhesions
Direct chest tube (2832 Fr pneumo, 3236 Fr hemothorax) posteriorly & apically. Advance until all holes are within pleural space
Connect to underwater seal/Pleurevac, apply suction (20 cmH2O)
Secure with suture (0 silk), apply sterile dressing
CXR now: confirm placement, lung reexpansion, no residual pneumo/effusion
Document: tube size, position, initial output (volume & character), air leak presence

pairs.value: Visceral pleural line (pneumo), meniscus/opacification (effusion/hemothorax). Do NOT delay for tension pneumo: clinical dx
Most sensitive. Occult pneumo, sizing, bulla/bleb identification. Get if stable & dx uncertain
Fastest bedside dx. Absent lung sliding = pneumo. Lung point = specific finding. Free fluid in dependent thorax = hemothorax
Baseline Hgb for hemothorax
If hemothorax or surgical intervention anticipated
If respiratory distress
Mandatory after chest tube placement or needle decompression
Monitor hourly.>1500mL initial or >200mL/h x3h = massive hemothorax: thoracotomy

drugs.name: Lidocaine 1%
drugs.dose: 1020mL local infiltration at chest tube insertion site
drugs.notes: Infiltrate skin, subcutaneous tissue, periosteum of rib, intercostal muscles, parietal pleura

drugs.name: Procedural sedation
drugs.dose: Ketamine 12 mg/kg IV or fentanyl 12 mcg/kg + midazolam 12mg IV
drugs.notes: For awake chest tube placement if time permits. Do NOT delay for sedation in emergent cases

drugs.name: Ketorolac
drugs.notes: Rib fracture pain. Adequate analgesia prevents splinting: atelectasis: PNA

drugs.name: TXA (tranexamic acid)
drugs.dose: 1g IV over 10 min (within 3h of injury if traumatic)
drugs.notes: For massive hemothorax as part of MTP. Consider intrapleural TXA in select cases

pairs.key: ICU / immediate higher level of care if
pairs.value: persistent respiratory distress, high-flow or NIV need, hemodynamic instability, vasopressor need, severe arrhythmia, airway risk, neurologic decline, or a trajectory suggesting pt may crash on the floor.

sections.title: References & Resources
items: Tran J, Haussner W, Shah K. Traumatic Pneumothorax: A Review of Current Diagnostic Practices & Evolving Management. The Journal of Emergency Medicine. 2021.
Coccolini F, Cremonini C, Moore EE, et al. Thoracic Trauma WSESAAST Guidelines. World Journal of Emergency Surgery. 2025.
Anderson D, Chen SA, Godoy LA, Brown LM, Cooke DT. Comprehensive Review of Chest Tube Management: A Review. JAMA Surgery. 2022.
Roberts DJ, LeighSmith S, Faris PD, et al. Clinical Presentation of Patients With Tension Pneumothorax: A Systematic Review. Annals of Surgery. 2015;261(6):10681078.
Baumann MH, Strange C, Heffner JE, et al. Management of Spontaneous Pneumothorax: ACCP Delphi Consensus Statement. Chest. 2001;119(2):590602.

Hallifax RJ, McKeown E, Sivakumar P, et al. Ambulatory Management of Primary Spontaneous Pneumothorax (RAMPP trial). New England Journal of Medicine. 2020;382(5):405415. https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1916030

topics.title: Acute Ischemic Stroke
topics.subtitle: Focal deficits, aphasia, weakness, facial droop, lastknownwell

sections.title: Acute Management
items: Airway: check patency and protect. Breathing: check WOB, oxygenation, and ventilation. Circulation: check pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
How do they look? Focal deficit, aphasia, gaze deviation, neglect, or decreased responsiveness?
ABCs: protect airway if vomiting, obtunded, or unable to manage secretions; give O2 if hypoxemic
POC glucose ASAP
Call code stroke now & document last known nl., if on anticoagulation, & CI to TPA
Place on monitor, get IV access, & send pt to noncontrast head CT STAT
make NPO
Do not lower BP unless needed for thrombolysis/endovascular therapy or another hypertensive emergency

sections.title: Chart Check / Hx.
items: last known well + sym discovery time, symp improving or fluctuating
Baseline Fxn & prior stroke deficits
Anticoagulants, antiplatelets, recent surgery, recent bleeding, or prior ICH
Vascular risk factors: HTN, DM, AFib, smoking, CAD, prior stroke/TIA
Recent trauma, headache, seizure at onset, or mimic history
Pregnancy status, renal function, & contrast allergies if thrombectomy imaging is being considered

sections.title: Exam
items: Use NIHSS to quantify deficit & trend exam over time
Face, arm, leg weakness; aphasia, dysarthria, neglect, visual field loss, gaze deviation, ataxia
Check for cortical signs suggesting largevessel occlusion: aphasia, neglect, forced gaze deviation
check airway protection, aspiration risk, & BP
Look for mimics: seizure, ↓ Glucose, migraine, intoxication, syncope, functional deficit

pairs.value: Balance, Eyes, Face, Arm, Speech, Time: use for rapid recognition & immediate stroke activation
<25 minutes
<60 minutes
Up to 4.5 hours from symptom onset in selected pts
24 hours in pts with largevessel occlusion
If not receiving reperfusion therapy, BP is left untreated unless severe hypertension or another emergency is present

sections.title: Contraindications to Thrombolysis (Key)
items: Active internal bleeding
Active bleeding at noncompressible site
Intracranial surgery or head trauma (<3 months)
Ischemic Stroke or serious head injury in past 3 months
History of intracranial hemorrhage
BP >185/110 that cannot be controlled before treatment
Platelet count <100k or INR >1.7
Direct thrombin or factor Xa inhibitor use (unless not taken in 48 hours)
Glucose <50 or >400 mg/dL
Signs of subarachnoid hemorrhage
Extensive regions of clear hypoattenuation on CT

pairs.value: Usually severe cortical deficits; thrombectomy candidate if imaging & timing fit
AFib, LV thrombus, valve disease, endocarditis
Pure motor or sensory syn without cortical signs
↓ Glucose, seizure with Todd paralysis, migraine, ICH, mass lesion, functional disorder
Quickly check for IV thrombolysis &/or thrombectomy; se decisions are timecritical & involve neurology/stroke team now
Standard for LVO within 06h when imaging/severity fit (commonly NIHSS >6, ASPECTS >6); selected pts can treated out to 24h if perfusion imaging shows salvageable tissue.
If eligible for both within 4.5h, give IV thrombolysis & proceed to thrombectomy; thrombolysis not delay thrombectomy & not be skipped in eligible pts.

Prethrombolysis/thrombectomy: <185/110. First 24h after thrombolysis: <180/105. After thrombolysis: commonly maintain SBP 140180. After thrombectomy: avoidlow SBP (<120). If no reperfusion thetherapy, avoid routine BP lowering unless >=220/120, then reduce ~15% initially.
Treat fever, maintain euvolemia, correct glucose abnormalities, & avoid hypoxia & ↓ BP
If no thrombolysis, start antiplatelet therapy after hemorrhage is excluded; if thrombolysis is given, avoid antiplatelets/anticoagulants for 24 hours

If noncardioembolic minor stroke (NIHSS <=3) or high risk TIA (ABCD2 >=4) without thrombolysis/thrombectomy, start aspirin + clopidogrel within 24h & continue 2130 days, then transition to single antiplatelet thetherapy.
STAT Noncon head CT; CTA head/neck with perfusion imaging if considering thrombectomy
POC glucose, CBC, BMP/CMP, PT/INR, aPTT, troponin
ECG telemetry; look for AFib or concurrent ischemia
Swallow screen, lipid panel, A1c, TTE as part of inpt stroke workup

drugs.name: Alteplase (tPA)
drugs.notes: STEMI equivalent: time is brain. Call neurology now.

drugs.name: Labetalol
drugs.notes: If BP >185/110 before tPA. Target <185/110 before dosing.

drugs.name: Nicardipine
drugs.notes: Alternative for BP control pre/post tPA.

topics.title: Seizure / Status Epilepticus
topics.subtitle: Convulsions, gaze deviation, postictal state, persistent AMS, acidosis

sections.title: Acute Management
items: Airway: check patency and protect. Breathing: check WOB, oxygenation, and ventilation. Circulation: check pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
ABCs; lateral position; no restraint
Airway failure > intubate
Neuro call early

sections.title: Chart Check / Hx.
items: Prior seizures, epilepsy dx, AED regimen, adherence
Prodrome: palpitations, diaphoresis, N/V, HA, confusion, anxiety
Triggers: exertion, fatigue, pain, infection, stress, urination, defecation
Witnessed: lateral tongue bite, incontinence, focal signs
EtOH / benzo / barbiturate use, withdrawal risk
Lowered seizure threshold meds

sections.title: Exam
items: CN deficits, facial droop, hemiparesis
Dysphagia, dizziness, ataxia
Lateral tongue bite
Incontinence
Todd's paralysis / other focality

pairs.value: >=5 min, or recurrent seizures without baseline return
EEG seizure >=10 sec; clues: AMS, subtle twitching, eye deviation
Persists after 2 adequate benzo doses
Seizurelike events; no epileptiform activity; eye closure, fluctuating course, asynchronous movements, pelvic thrusting, crying, minimal postictal state
PNES and epilepsy can coexist
Epilepsy, stroke, hemorrhage, mass, trauma, migraine, PRES
Convulsive syncope: brief, rapid recovery, minimal postictal state
Hypoglycemia, hypercarbia, hypoxia, Na disorder, hypocalcemia
Bupropion, venlafaxine, buspirone, TCAs, tramadol, diphenhydramine, clozapine, fluoroquinolones, steroids, amphetamines
EtOH, benzos, barbiturates; SE risk
Sepsis, encephalitis, meningitis, HIV, syphilis, tetanus, fever
HTN emergency / PRES, ↓ BP, eclampsia, PNES
Forced eye closure, waxing/waning, nonrhythmic or outofphase movements, crying, minimal injury, rapid baseline return
Drooling, tongue bite, incontinence, grunting, apparent LOC still possible

sections.title: Management
items: If safe, video event for neuro review
**Lorazepam 4 mg IV (0.1 mg/kg) q5 min x23**
No IV > midazolam IM/IN/buccal or diazepam PR
05 min: ABCs, IV, POC glucose, O2, thiamine if at risk
RSI if needed; ketamine or propofol reasonable
Still seizing after 5-10 min > repeat benzo
1040 min > levetiracetam, fosphenytoin, valproate, or lacosamide
Refractory SE > intubate, ICU, EEG, anesthetic infusion
Superrefractory SE > neurocritical care; autoimmune / rescue options
Correct cause: glucose, Na, Ca, infection, withdrawal, toxin, structural lesion
After control > telemetry, pulse ox, neuro checks; EEG / MRI / LP as needed

pairs.value: POC glucose, IV access, iStat/VBG, BMP, Mg, Phos, CBC
LFTs, CK, lactate, INR, UA, ECG, telemetry
Early lactate supports recent generalized tonicclonic seizure; prolactin occasionally helpful
CXR, betahCG, tox studies, AED levels, CT head, MRI brain, urgent EEG

pairs.key: Image now if firsttime / high risk
pairs.value: First seizure + persistent AMS, focal deficit, trauma, anticoagulation, immunocompromise, malignancy, fever, severe HA, bleed / mass / stroke concern
LP, blood cultures, empiric CNS infection coverage
Exclude mimics; once stable, avoid repeated anticonvulsants or invasive escalation for atypical persistent events

drugs.name: Lorazepam
drugs.dose: 0.1 mg/kg IV (max 4 mg), may repeat once
drugs.notes: Preferred IV benzo

drugs.name: Diazepam
drugs.dose: 0.150.2 mg/kg IV (max 10 mg), may repeat once
drugs.notes: Alternative IV / PR agent

drugs.name: Midazolam
drugs.dose: 10 mg IM if >40 kg; 5 mg if 1340 kg; IN 5 mg per nostril; buccal 10 mg
drugs.notes: Best if no IV access

drugs.name: Levetiracetam
drugs.notes: Secondline; 1015 min infusion

drugs.name: Fosphenytoin
drugs.notes: Secondline; monitor rhythm, BP

drugs.name: Valproate
drugs.notes: Secondline; ~10 min infusion

drugs.name: Lacosamide
drugs.notes: Alternative secondline; ~15 min infusion

drugs.name: Phenobarbital
drugs.notes: More sedation, respiratory depression

drugs.name: Propofol
drugs.notes: Refractory SE; intubation, ICU; watch infusion syndrome

drugs.name: Midazolam gtt
drugs.notes: Refractory SE; ICU, EEG

drugs.name: Ketamine
drugs.dose: 0.52 mg/kg bolus or ICU infusion per protocol
drugs.notes: Refractory SE option; less respiratory / hemodynamic depression

drugs.name: Thiamine
drugs.notes: Before glucose if alcoholism / malnutrition

topics.title: Serotonin Syndrome
topics.subtitle: Clonus, hyperreflexia, fever, agitation, autonomic instability

sections.title: Acute Management
items: Airway: check patency and protect. Breathing: check WOB, oxygenation, and ventilation. Circulation: check pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
Stop ALL serotonergic meds: this is most important step
ABCs: check hemodynamic stability, airway protection if severely altered
IV fluid: aggressive hydration for hyperthermia & Rhabdo
Paralysis + intubation if temp >41°C refractory to benzos (use nondepolarizing agents)

sections.title: Chart Check / Hx.
items: Recent Meds change: new serotonergic drug added, dose ↑, or drug interaction
Home SSRI/SNRI: fluoxetine, sertraline, paroxetine, citalopram, venlafaxine, duloxetine
High-risk additions: tramadol, fentanyl, linezolid, ondansetron (zofran), metoclopramide, trazodone, dextromethorphan
MAOI use (phenelzine, tranylcypromine, selegiline): most dangerous combinations
St. John's wort, methylene blue (potent MAOI), recreational drugs (MDMA, cocaine)

sections.title: Exam
items: Clonus: spontaneous, inducible (ankle), or ocular (hallmark finding)
Hyperreflexia: esp lower extremities
Tremor, myoclonus, agitation
Diaphoresis, mydriasis (dilated pupils), flushing
Hypertonia/rigidity (more prominent in lower extremities)
Temp >38°C, tachycardia, HTN, tachypnea

pairs.value: Druginduced excess serotonergic activity: triad of neuromuscular excitation, autonomic dysfunction, & AMS

Requires serotonergic agent + ANY of: spontaneous clonus, inducible clonus + agitation/diaphoresis, ocular clonus + agitation/diaphoresis, tremor + hyperreflexia, hypertonia + temp >38°C + ocular/inducible clonus
Usually within 24h of drug change (often within 6h). Rapid onset distinguishes from NMS.
NMS: leadpipe rigidity, bradyreflexia, slow onset (days). SS: clonus, hyperreflexia, rapid onset (hours)
Leadpipe rigidity, bradyreflexia, slow onset (days–weeks), dopamine blocker exposure. CKmuch higher.
Volatile anesthetics or succinylcholine exposure. OR/PACU setting. Hypercarbia is earliest sign.
Dry skin (no diaphoresis), urinary retention, absent bowel sounds. SS has diaphoresis & hyperactive bowels
Cocaine, amphetamines. Similar autonomic findings but no clonus or hyperreflexia
Fever, AMS, nuchal rigidity. LP required to differentiate.

sections.title: Management
items: Agitation & tremors: **lorazepam 2 mg IV PRN** (benzos are first line)
Severe sx: **cyproheptadine 12 mg PO/NG load**: 4 mg PO q2h until improvement (max 32 mg/day)
Cooling measures for hyperthermia avoid antipyretics (not effective in drug induced hyperthermia)

Hunter Serotonin Toxicity Criteria: serotonergic agent + any one of following: spontaneous clonus; inducible clonus + agitation or diaphoresis; ocular clonus + agitation or diaphoresis; tremor + hyperreflexia; or hypertonia + temperature >38°C + ocular or inducible clonus
ICU admission for severe cases
Immediate: Stop all serotonergic agents & give supportive care. Most cases improve within 24h after stopping the offending drug, though long halflife agents may last longer.
Mild (tremor, hyperreflexia): Supportive care, remove precipitating drugs, & use benzodiazepines for agitation/tremor.
Moderate (autonomic instability, fever <41.1°C): Aggressively correct cardiorespiratory & thermal abnormalities, continue benzodiazepines, & add a 5HT2A antagonist such as cyproheptadine.
Severe (temp >41.1°C): Immediate sedation, nondepolarizing paralysis, orotracheal intubation, ventilation, & aggressive cooling; premature release of paralysis can allow hyperthermia to recur.
Agitation control: Benzodiazepines are central across all severities. Avoid physical restraints b/c isometric muscle activity worsens lactic acidosis & hyperthermia.
Autonomic instability: Hypotension: use directacting pressors such as norepinephrine, phenylephrine, or epinephrine. Hypertension/tachycardia: use shortacting agents such as esmolol or nitroprusside.
Hyperthermia: This is from muscle activity, not hypothalamic setpoint change; antipyretics have no role.
Avoid: Avoid succinylcholine, propranolol, bromocriptine, dantrolene, & prolonged physical restraints.
Disposition trigger: Moderatesevere illness, escalating autonomic instability, hyperthermia, for intubation, or rhabdomyolysis requires ICUlevel care.
Ongoing care: Trend CK, renal function, temperature, & urine output;don't restart serotonergic agents until fully recovered.
Hunter criteria: Use the syndromedefining exam findings to support diagnosis & avoid mislabeling NMS, anticholinergic toxicity, or sympathomimetic intoxication.
Cyproheptadine course: For moderatesevere disease: 12 mg PO/NG initially, then 2 mg q2h until improvement, then 8 mg q6h maintenance; total 1232 mg in 24h.
Parenteral 5HT2A antagonists: If enteral cyproheptadine cannot be used, chlorpromazine IM or olanzapine SL are possible alternatives, though evidence is limited.
Most cases resolve within 24–72 hours after stopping offending agent
Monitor CK q6–12h: trend for Rhabdo
Aggressive IV fluids to maintain UOP >1 mL/kg/h if CK elevated
Serial neuro checks: document clonus resolution
Do NOT restart serotonergic agents until fully resolved. Pharmacy consult for alternatives.
Watch for DIC if severe: serial coags, fibrinogen, Ddimer

pairs.value: Fingerstick glucose, BMP, CBC, CK (Rhabdo), lactate, VBG
LFTs, Mg, PO4, UA (myoglobinuria), PT/PTT/INR (DIC screen)
UTox, serum APAP, salicylate level, ethanol level
QTc prolongation (many serotonergic agents prolong QT)
Head CT if altered to r/o structural cause

drugs.name: Lorazepam
drugs.notes: Firstline for agitation, tremor, & the hyperadrenergic state. Repeat q1015 min PRN.

drugs.name: Cyproheptadine
drugs.dose: 12 mg PO/NG load, then 2 mg q2h until improved, then 8 mg q6h
drugs.notes: Suggested 5HT2A antagonist; only enteral formulation. Total 24h dose1232 mg.

drugs.name: Chlorpromazine
drugs.notes: Parenteral alternative 5HT2A antagonist in select severe cases.

drugs.name: Olanzapine
drugs.notes: Alternative 5HT2A antagonist option; evidence is limited.

drugs.name: Norepinephrine
drugs.notes: Preferred directacting pressor for hypotension, especially with MAOI interactions.

drugs.name: Phenylephrine / Epinephrine
drugs.notes: Other directacting pressor options for hypotension.

drugs.name: Esmolol
drugs.notes: Shortacting option for marked tachycardia or hypertension.

drugs.name: Nitroprusside
drugs.notes: Shortacting antihypertensive option for severe autonomic hypertension.

drugs.name: Vecuronium
drugs.notes: Use for severe hyperthermia with intubation; avoid succinylcholine.

pairs.key: ICU / immediate higher level of care if
pairs.value: persistent respiratory distress, high-flow or NIV need, hemodynamic instability, vasopressor need, severe arrhythmia, airway risk, neurologic decline, or a trajectory suggesting pt may crash on the floor.

topics.title: Acute Agitation
topics.subtitle: Agitation, aggression, delirium, intoxication, danger to staff or self

sections.title: Acute Management
items: Airway: check patency and protect. Breathing: check WOB, oxygenation, and ventilation. Circulation: check pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
Safety first: check whether the pt is an immediate danger to self, staff, or other pts
ABCs: check airway protection, SpO2, RR, BP, pulse, glucose, & obvious trauma
Look for now reversible threats: hypoxia, ↓ glucose, seizure, stroke, intoxication, withdrawal, head injury

sections.title: Chart Check / Hx.
items: Baseline mentation: ask nursing, family: is this new or chronic?
Recent Meds changes: new opioids, benzos, anticholinergics, steroids
Alcohol/substance use Hx.: withdrawal timeline
Dementia Hx. (Parkinson's, Lewy body: avoid haloperidol)
Recent surgery/anesthesia (postoperative delirium)
Infection signs: UTI, PNA, line infection (often trigger)

sections.title: Exam
items: Attention testing: serial 7s, spell WORLD backward, months of year backward
Signs of infection: fever, new crackles, suprapubic tenderness, line site eryma
Urinary retention (bladder scan), fecal impaction
Focal neurological deficits (stroke, mass lesion)
Pupil size (opioid toxicity: miotic; anticholinergic: mydriatic)
Tremor, diaphoresis, tachycardia (withdrawal)

pairs.value: Acute, fluctuating disturbance in attention & awareness. Always assume medical cause until proven otherwise
Agitation, combativeness, pulling at lines/tubes, attempted selfharm. ~25% of delirium cases
Lethargy, withdrawal, flat affect. Often missed: more common (~75%) & associated with worse outcomes
Acute onset + fluctuating course + inattention + EIR disorganized thinking OR altered LOC
UTI, PNA, line infection, C. diff, wound infection: most common precipitant
↓ Glucose, hypernatremia, hyponatremia, uremia, hepatic encephalopathy, hypercalcemia
Opioids, benzodiazepines, anticholinergics (diphenhydramine, scopolamine), steroids, fluoroquinolones
EtOH withdrawal (onset 6–24h), benzodiazepine withdrawal, opioid withdrawal
Stroke, ICH, seizure (postictal), meningitis/encephalitis
Urinary retention, constipation/impaction, pain, sleep deprivation, ICU delirium, postop

sections.title: Management
items: Call for help early if violence risk; clear the room of unnecessary staff & dangerous objects
Use verbal deescalation, simple commands, lowstimulation environment, & 1:1 observation if needed
If the pt isescalating or cannot be redirected, prepare for chemical sedation & higher level monitoring
Immediate goal: Protect the pt & staff while identifying a medical, neurologic, toxic, or withdrawal cause.
Safety risk: If the pt is attempting harm, pulling lines, eloping, or cannot be verbally redirected, escalate quickly to security, sitter, & chemical sedation.
Deescalation first: Use calm verbal redirection, one speaker, reduced stimulation, familiar staff/family if helpful, & avoid escalating confrontation.
Restraints: Use only if required for immediate safety & pair with rapid reassessment plus treatment of the underlying cause.
Medical causes first: Treat hypoxia, ↓ glucose, infection, pain, urinary retention, constipation, withdrawal, stroke, or seizure before labeling this primary psychiatric agitation.

Medication strategy: PO is preferred when feasible. For parenteral rapid tranquilization, droperidol or olanzapine are preferred first line options; avoid haloperidol in Parkinsonism or Lewy body disease.
Etiologyspecific sedation: Alcohol/sedative withdrawal or stimulant intoxication: benzodiazepines may preferred. Delirium & known psychiatric illness: antipsychotics are preferred.
Combination thetherapy: Antipsychotic + benzodiazepine is not routine b/c adverse effects increase without clear general benefit, though select severe cases may use a monitored combination strategy.
Ketamine: Reserve IM ketamine 25 mg/kg as secondline for extreme violence, failed first line sedation, or for immediate control.
Monitoring: After sedation, do close observation with frequent vitals, sedation level checks, pulse ox without routine supplemental O2 masking decline, & EtCO2 when available.
reorient, clock/calendar, familiar objects, maintain sleepwake cycle
access to glasses/hearing aids
Minimize room changes, reduce unnecessary stimuli & interruptions
Early mobilization when safe: PT/OT consult
Remove Foley, lines, restraints if clinically appropriate
Recheck QTc daily if on scheduled antipsychotics
Titrate down & Stop antipsychotics as delirium resolves

pairs.value: Fingerstick glucose, BMP, CBC, UA with culture, SpO2.
QTc measurement before antipsychotics; repeat after more doses.
Continuous pulse ox, frequent vitals, sedation scale reassessment, & EtCO2 if avail.
LFTs, ammonia, TSH, B12, CXR, blood cultures, UTox, serum tox studies, bladder scan.

pairs.key: If focal neuro signs / fever / trauma
pairs.value: Head CT & consider LP if febrile + AMS without a clear source.

drugs.name: Droperidol
drugs.notes: Preferred first line parenteral agent; strong evidence for effective rapid sedation with lower respiratory depression than lorazepam or ziprasidone.

drugs.name: Haloperidol
drugs.notes: Alternative antipsychotic; higher EPS & QTc concerns than droperidol or olanzapine.

drugs.name: Quetiapine
drugs.notes: Smallest QT ↑. Preferred for Parkinson's/Lewy body pts.

drugs.name: Olanzapine
drugs.notes: Preferred first line parenteral option; good SL option if refusing PO.Don't combine IM olanzapine with IM benzodiazepines.

drugs.name: Risperidone
drugs.notes: Alternative oral option.

drugs.name: Aripiprazole
drugs.notes: Use if QTc >500. Minimal QT prolongation.

drugs.name: Valproic acid
drugs.notes: If QTc >500 & parenteral option needed.

drugs.name: Lorazepam
drugs.notes: Preferred for alcohol/benzo withdrawal; worsens delirium in many other cases.

drugs.name: Midazolam
drugs.notes: Fastest sedation onset but higher respiratory depression risk; may worsen delirium.

drugs.name: Ketamine
drugs.notes: Secondline for extremely dangerous agitation requiring immediate control; lowerdose strategies may reduce airway compromise.

pairs.key: ICU / immediate higher level of care if
pairs.value: persistent respiratory distress, high-flow or NIV need, hemodynamic instability, vasopressor need, severe arrhythmia, airway risk, neurologic decline, or a trajectory suggesting pt may crash on the floor.

topics.title: Acute Dystonic Reaction
topics.subtitle: Torticollis, oculogyric crisis, jaw spasm, recent dopamine blockade

sections.title: Acute Management
items: Airway: check patency and protect. Breathing: check WOB, oxygenation, and ventilation. Circulation: check pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
Stop offending medication now: most commonly dopamineblocking antipsychotics or antiemetics
ABCs first: check for stridor, tongue/laryngeal involvement, inability to handle secretions, or respiratory distress
If airway/neck spasm is severe: call for help early, place on monitor, prepare for advanced airway management

sections.title: Chart Check / Hx.
items: Recent or newly increased dopamineblocking meds: haloperidol, droperidol, prochlorperazine, metoclopramide, promethazine, risperidone, aripiprazole
Time from med exposure: commonly within 24h; ~90% occur within 5 days of start or dose increase
Prior EPS or dystonic reaction history
Young age, male sex, boys/infants, cocaine use, & highpotency antipsychotics increase risk
Ask exactly what movement started first: neck twisting, jaw spasm, tongue protrusion, eye deviation, trouble speaking, trouble swallowing

pairs.value: Sudden, painful, sustained muscle contraction after dopamineblocking medication: pt isawake & frightened
Cervical dystonia/torticollis, tongue protrusion, trismus, dysarthria, dysphagia, blepharospasm, truncal dystonia, opisthotonos
Forced upward or lateral eye deviation: classic for acute dystonia &mistaken for seizure or psychosis
Laryngeal dystonia = stridor, dysphonia, inability to swallow, respiratory distress: this is rare but life-threatening
Usually nl. AMS suggests different or more process
Most common. Dopamine blockade in the nigrostriatal pathway after antipsychotics or antiemetics.
Firstgeneration antipsychotics, secondgeneration antipsychotics, metoclopramide, prochlorperazine; SSRIs, anticonvulsants, opioids, & methylphenidate are rarer causes.
Usually has impaired awareness, postictal phase, or rhythmic motor activity not sustained patterned spasm
Has clonus, hyperreflexia, diaphoresis, & autonomic instability not focal sustained dystonia
Consider if no dopamineblocking exposure or if re is diffuse rigidity, wound history, or electrolyte abnormality
Slower onset with generalized rigidity, fever, autonomic instability, & marked CK elevation not isolated focal spasm
Give diphenhydramine 2550 mg IV/IM or benztropine 12 mg IV/IM promptly.
Add lorazepam 0.52 mg IV/IM for severe anxiety, pain, or incomplete response.
Clinical improvement isdramatic & begins within minutes after IV/IM anticholinergic thetherapy; the dose can repeated if symptoms persist.
Continue oral anticholinergic thetherapy for 47 days after resolution b/c symptoms can recur hours after the initial injection.
Stop, switch, or lower the offending agent; if it must be continued, consider prophylactic anticholinergic thetherapy.
Counsel the pt that future exposure to dopamine receptor blockers increases recurrence risk.
Recheck 1015 min after treatment: persistent symptoms may need repeat anticholinergic dose
Document culprit medicationin chart & handoff
If offending drug must be continued, discuss dose reduction, agent change, or short prophylactic anticholinergic course
Counsel pt that recurrence can happen if same medication is given again
Consider pharmacy or psychiatry input if antipsychotic therapy is still required

sections.title: Orders
items: This is primarily a clinical Dx —don't delay treatment for testing
Check vitals & pulse oximetry if face/neck symptoms are prominent
Review MAR carefully for culprit medication & timing
Fingerstick glucose if atypical or altered
BMP/Ca/Mg if history is unclear, symptoms recur, or another neuromuscular cause is possible
ECG if significant tachycardia, QTprolonging comedications, or antipsychotic toxicity is a concern

drugs.name: Diphenhydramine
drugs.dose: 2550mg IV/IM, then 2550mg PO q6h x 47 days
drugs.contraindications: Delirium, severe anticholinergic burden, narrowangle glaucoma
drugs.notes: Firstline anticholinergic; relief israpid & dramatic.

drugs.name: Benztropine
drugs.dose: 12mg IV/IM, then 12mg PO BID x 47 days
drugs.notes: Alternative first line anticholinergic; useful if the pt remains at EPS risk.

drugs.name: Lorazepam
drugs.notes: Adjunct if severe anxiety, distress, or incomplete response to anticholinergic therapy. Not first line as monothetherapy.

pairs.key: ICU / immediate higher level of care if
pairs.value: persistent respiratory distress, high-flow or NIV need, hemodynamic instability, vasopressor need, severe arrhythmia, airway risk, neurologic decline, or a trajectory suggesting pt may crash on the floor.
