topics.title: GuillainBarré Syndrome (GBS)
topics.subtitle: Ascending weakness, areflexia, paresthesias, dysautonomia, low NIF

sections.title: Acute Management
items: Airway: check patency and protect. Breathing: check WOB, oxygenation, and ventilation. Circulation: check pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
ABCs: check respiratory status ASAP. Call RT early. pulse Ox, telemetry, frequent BP
check for autonomic instability: HR swings, BP lability, urinary retention, ileus
Avoid succinylcholine: risk severe ↑K from denervation
Monitor swallowing: if bulbar weakness, keep NPO & consider NG/OG feeding

sections.title: Chart Check / Hx.
items: Preceding illness 14 wks ago: URI, GI illness ( Campylobacter jejuni: most common trigger), CMV, EBV, Mycoplasma, Zika, COVID, influenza
Recent vaccination (rare association)
Symptom onset & progression: ascending weakness starting in legs, progressing over days to weeks. Peak sx at 24 wks
Sensory sx: paressias, numbness (often precede weakness), pain (esp back/thighs)
Cranial nerve involvement: facial weakness (bilateral), dysphagia, dysarthria, ophthalmoplegia
Autonomic sx: palpitations, urinary retention, constipation, diaphoresis, orthostasis
Prior episodes (recurrent GBS rare: if ≥3 relapses, consider CIDP)
Baseline ambulation status & rate of decline
Meds: BB (worsen ↓ HR in dysautonomia), neuromuscular blockers to avoid

sections.title: Exam
items: Symmetric ascending weakness: legs > arms. Grade strength (MRC scale 05) in all limbs
Areflexia or hyporeflexia: required finding. Check biceps, triceps, patellar, Achilles
Respiratory assessment: count to 20 in single breath (if can't reach 1012, concerning), singlebreath count, cough strength, accessory muscle use
Neck flexor weakness: correlates with diaphragmatic involvement & intubation risk
Cranial nerves: facial weakness (CN VII,bilateral), bulbar function (swallow, gag, tongue), extraocular movements (MillerFisher)
Sensory exam: glovestocking paressias, vibration & proprioception (often preserved early)
Autonomic signs: resting tachycardia, BP lability, pupillary dysfunction, ileus, urinary retention
check trajectory: is pt getting better or worse? Hourly trend matters more than single exam

pairs.value: Acute immunemed. polyneuropathy: postinfectious autoimmune attack on peripheral nerves. MCC of acute flaccid paralysis
Acute inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy: most common subtype (8590%). Demyelination of motor & sensory nerves
Acute motor axonal neuropathy: motoronly,postCampylobacter. Axonal subtype. Higher intubation risk & slower recovery
Acute motor & sensory axonal neuropathy: combined motor + sensory axonal involvement. More severe
Variant: ophthalmoplegia + ataxia + areflexia. Descending pattern. AntiGQ1b antibodies. Generally milder course
CSF: ↑ protein (>45 mg/dL) with nl. WBC (<10 cells/μL). Classic finding but may nl. in first week. >50 WBC suggests alternative dx
Improvementthendeterioration within 8 wks (up to 10% of pts). Retreat with original modality. ≥3 relapses: consider CIDP

sections.title: Autonomic Instability
items: Occurs in~50% of GBS pts: can life-threatening. Telemetry mandatory
Labile BP:severe HTN to ↓ BP. Avoid aggressive tx: episodestransient
Hypertensive episodes: txt stimuli (pain, full bladder, agitation). Shortacting IV vasodilators (nicardipine, clevidipine). Avoid BB (↑ ↓ HR risk)
↓ HR: can progress to asystole. Avoid vagal stimuli (suctioning). D/C negative chronotropes. IV atropine at bedside. External pacing if refractory
Tachycardia:sinus: may not need tx. Check for pain, fever, PE, hypovolemia first
Urinary retention: Foley catheter, bladder scans
Gastroparesis/ileus: promotility agents, consider postpyloric feeding. Aggressive bowel regimen
Acute colonic pseudoObst.: neostigmine (pretreat with glycopyrrolate 0.4mg: neostigmine 2mg IV over 5 min)
Complications: PRES (posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome) & Takotsubo cardiomyopathy from dysautonomic hypertensive episodes
PEARL: disease plateaus, then improves.don't retreat if improving

pairs.value: Campylobacter jejuni (most common), CMV, EBV, Mycoplasma, influenza, COVID19, Zika, hepatitis E, recent surgery, vaccination (rare)
Transverse myelitis, spinal cord compression, myasnia gravis, botulism, poliomyelitis, tick paralysis, critical illness polyneuropathy, hypokalemic periodic paralysis
Sensory level, bladder dysfunction early, UMN signs (hyperreflexia). GBS = LMN (areflexia)
Fatigable weakness, ptosis, diplopia. Reflexes preserved. No sensory involvement
Descending paralysis (opposite of GBS), dilated pupils, early bulbar involvement, GI prodrome
Back pain, sensory level, UMN signs, bowel/bladder dysfunction. MRI spine diagnostic
Ascending paralysis similar to GBS: search for embedded tick (scalp, hairline). Resolves with tick removal
Chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy: if sx progress >8 wks or ≥3 relapses, reclassify from GBS to CIDP

sections.title: Management
items: Measure FVC & NIF at bedside: repeat q46h (q2h if declining). Trend > single value
Step 1: prevent respiratory failure: Trend FVC & NIF closely, repeat airway exams, involve RT early, & move to ICU or intubation based on trajectory not waiting for a late gas change.

Step 2: start diseasedirected thetherapy: Start IVIG or PLEX early once GBS is strongly suspected;don't wait for LP/EMG. Choose one modality based on access, hemodynamics, renal function, & local resources; combined IVIG + PLEX is not routinely beneficial.

Step 3: supportive & autonomic care: Use telemetry & frequent BP monitoring, give DVT prophylaxis, manage neuropathic pain, monitor swallowing/nutrition, prevent pressure injuries, & avoid succinylcholine or delays in thetherapy.

pairs.value: Most important test. Measure q46h (q2h if declining). Trend > single value. <20 mL/kg = ICU. <15 mL/kg = consider intubation. <10 mL/kg = strong indication for intubation
< −30 cmH2O = concerning. < −20 cmH2O = intubation likely. Trend alongside FVC
Albuminocytologic dissociation: ↑ protein (>45), WBC <10. May be nl. in first week (8090% sens after wk 1). >50 WBC: consider HIV, Lyme, sarcoid, lymphoma
Confirms dx & subtype (demyelinating vs axonal). May be nl. <12 wks after onset. nl. study >12 wks postonset argues against GBS
Selective enhancement of anterior spinal nerve roots is suggestive. Also r/o cord compression
AntiGQ1b (MillerFisher), antiGM1 (AMAN). Not required for dx but can help with subtyping
BMP (lytes, Cr), CBC, LFTs, CK, ESR/CRP, TSH. Stool culture for Campylobacter. HIV, Lyme if risk factors
If respiratory distress or declining FVC: rising PaCO2 is late finding, do NOT rely on gas alone
cardiac monitoring: dysautonomia causes ↓ HR, tachycardia, asystole
If available: rising trend with declining FVC suggests impending resp failure

sections.title: Respiratory Monitoring & Intubation
items: FVC & NIF are key bedside tests: measure on admission,thenq46h (q2h if declining). ABG/VBG is late indicator
Rough thresholds (integrate with clinical picture: se are NOT absolute cutoffs):
FVC <20 mL/kg: ICU admission
FVC <15 mL/kg: prepare for intubation
FVC <10 mL/kg: strong indication for intubation
NIF weaker than −30 cmH2O: concerning. Weaker than −20 cmH2O: intubation likely
>30% decline in FVC or NIF from baseline: ICU transfer
Clinical signs matter most: ↑ WOB, inability to cough, secretion management difficulty, ↓ singlebreath count, neck flexor weakness, bulbar dysfunction
Do NOT rely on ABG alone: rising PaCO2 is LATE finding. By n, pt may crash during intubation
Preintubation: avoid succinylcholine (↑K). Use rocuronium. Have pushdose epinephrine ready for vagal episodes
Consider early elective intubation not emergent: GBS pts decompensate rapidly
Consider early tracheostomy (~1 wk) in pts with severe weakness, AMAN subtype, or rapid progression: improves comfort, mobilization, oral hygiene

drugs.name: IVIG
drugs.contraindications: IgA deficiency (anaphylaxis risk: check IgA level), severe renal insufficiency
drugs.notes: Firstline: start on clinical dx,don't wait for LP/EMG. Risks: thromboembolism, aseptic meningitis, renal injury, hemolysis. Repeat course within 12 wks not beneficial

drugs.name: Plasmapheresis (PLEX)
drugs.dose: 5 exchanges over 12 wks (typically 200250 mL/kg total plasma volume)
drugs.contraindications: Hemodynamic instability (relative), sepsis, active bleeding, poor vascular access
drugs.notes: best if started within 7 days s/s. Equal efficacy to IVIG. More hemodynamic instability but ↓ relapse rate. Preferred in some centers. Requires central line. Combined IVIG + PLEX NOT superior

drugs.name: Gabapentin
drugs.dose: 300mg PO daily, titrate up to 1200mg TID as tolerated
drugs.notes: Firstline for neuropathic pain (very common in GBS). Can also use pregabalin. Sedation may limit dose

drugs.name: Atropine
drugs.notes: For symptomatic ↓ HR from dysautonomia. Have at bedside for all GBS pts with autonomic instability. Consider external pacing if refractory

drugs.name: Nicardipine or Clevidipine
drugs.dose: Nicardipine 515 mg/h IV; Clevidipine 12 mg/h IV titrate
drugs.notes: Shortacting IV CCB for hypertensive episodes from dysautonomia. Avoid BB (↑ ↓ HR risk). Transition to PO CCB if sustained

drugs.name: Enoxaparin
drugs.dose: 40mg SC daily (or 30mg SC BID if high risk)
drugs.notes: DVT prophylaxis: immobile pts athigh VTE risk. Start early. Add SCDs

drugs.name: Neostigmine
drugs.dose: 2mg IV over 5 min (pretreat with glycopyrrolate 0.4mg IV)
drugs.notes: For acute colonic pseudoObst. (Ogilvie syndrome) from autonomic dysfunction. Use cautiously: worsens ↓ HR

pairs.key: ICU / immediate higher level of care if
pairs.value: persistent respiratory distress, high-flow or NIV need, hemodynamic instability, vasopressor need, severe arrhythmia, airway risk, neurologic decline, or a trajectory suggesting pt may crash on the floor.

sections.title: References & Resources
items: Hughes RA, Wijdicks EF, Barohn R, et al. Practice Parameter: Immunothetherapy for GuillainBarré Syndrome: Report of the Quality Standards Subcommittee of the American Academy of Neurology. Neurology. 2003. Guideline.

van Doorn PA, Van den Bergh PYK, Hadden RDM, et al. European Academy of Neurology/Peripheral Nerve Society Guideline on Diagnosis & Treatment of GuillainBarré Syndrome. European Journal of Neurology. 2023.

topics.title: Myasthenic Crisis (Myasthenia Gravis)
topics.subtitle: Ptosis, bulbar weakness, dyspnea, fatigability, hypercapnia

sections.title: Acute Management
items: Airway: check patency and protect. Breathing: check WOB, oxygenation, and ventilation. Circulation: check pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
Airway: follow FVC, NIF, capnography, secretion burden, cough strength, bulbar weakness, & WOB
Move to early intubation if numbers are falling or the pt cannot protect the airway: FVC <20 mL/kg (or <1 L), NIF weaker than 30 cmH2O, rising pCO2, poor secretion clearance, or major bulbar sx
If trying BiPAP, treat it as a short bridge with frequent reassessment, not a setandforget plan
Call neurology early while you look for the trigger
If intubated, hold pyridostigmine until closer to extubation so secretions do not worsen

sections.title: Chart Check / Hx.
items: Known MG? How long? AChR vs MuSK? Prior thymoma or thymectomy?
Current regimen: pyridostigmine dose, steroids, immunosuppressants, missed doses, abrupt taper, or recent med change
Common triggers: infection, aspiration, surgery/anesthesia, pregnancy/postpartum, heat, stress, thyroid disease, Ca/Phos/Mg issues

Review every med for MGworsening agents: aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones, macrolides, clindamycin, vancomycin, BB, procainamide, quinidine, lithium, phenytoin, antipsychotics, magnesium, iodinated contrast
Recent steroid start or dose increase can temporarily make weakness worse
Prior crisis, prior intubation, prior PLEX vs IVIG, & what response was seen
Bulbar sx: dysphagia, dysarthria, nasal voice, aspiration, weak cough, trouble clearing secretions
Crisis can be the first presentation of MG: keep it on the list in unexplained hypercarbic weakness

sections.title: Exam
items: Resp: tachypnea, shallow breathing, orthopnea, weak cough, pausing midsentence, poor secretion clearance
Eyes: ptosis (often asymmetric), diplopia, CN III/IV/VI weakness, fatigability on sustained upgaze
Bulbar/facial: dysarthria, dysphonia, dysphagia, nasal voice, weak palate elevation, pooling secretions
Motor: proximal > distal weakness, neck extensor weakness ("dropped head"), weakness worsens with repetition or later in the day
Reflexes, sensation, pupils, & autonomic exam are normal: if abnormal, reconsider the dx
Bedside tests: ice pack test, prolonged upgaze test, single breath count <20

pairs.value: Autoimmune NMJ disorder, most often from AChR antibodies. Weakness is fatigable: worse with repeated use, better after rest.
Severe MG flare causing respiratory failure or airway risk, often requiring intubation. Many crises happen early in the disease course.
Too much acetylcholinesterase inhibitor causing weakness plus muscarinic excess. Rare now, but still worth distinguishing from true crisis.
Intubation, with or without full ventilator support, qualifies as MGFA Class V.
Normal ≥60 mL/kg. <20 mL/kg or rapid decline = impending respiratory failure
Normal about 80 to 100 cmH₂O. Weaker than 30 cmH₂O = significant respiratory weakness

Infection is the leading trigger. Others: med changes, nonadherence, surgery/anesthesia, steroids, stress, heat, thyroid disease, pregnancy/postpartum, and electrolyte shifts. Sometimes no trigger is found.
Myasthenic crisis usually has normal pupils and lacks prominent muscarinic findings. Cholinergic crisis points more toward miosis, sweating, bradycardia, fasciculations, and heavy secretions.
MG often starts ocular/bulbar and gets worse with repetition; reflexes stay normal. LEMS usually starts in the legs, may briefly improve after use, has low reflexes, and often includes autonomic sx.
Think about botulism, GBS, ALS, organophosphate toxicity, tick paralysis, brainstem stroke, spinal cord disease, and periodic paralysis.
Do not miss ACS, arrhythmia, sepsis, hypoglycemia, anemia, dehydration, thyroid disease, electrolyte problems, or another cause of respiratory failure.

sections.title: Management
items: Watch the breathing trend, not a single number: serial FVC/NIF, capnography, secretion burden, bulbar weakness, cough, and WOB. Do not rely on SpO2 alone.
Use NIV only if the pt is protecting the airway and can clear secretions. If bulbar function is poor or the numbers are falling, choose elective intubation before things crash.
If intubating, try to avoid paralytics if feasible. If needed, use lowdose rocuronium 0.50.6 mg/kg; if succinylcholine is chosen, higher doses may be needed. Have sugammadex ready.
Find and treat the trigger at once: infection/aspiration, med exposure, steroid change, surgery/anesthesia, thyroid disease, electrolyte issues, or missed MG meds.

Stop drugs that worsen MG, avoid magnesium, and if the pt is intubated, hold pyridostigmine until extubation is closer. If not intubated, keep home pyridostigmine going unless cholinergic crisis is a real concern.
Start immune therapy early with neurology. PLEX is often chosen first in crisis because it works faster; IVIG is a reasonable alternative when PLEX is not practical.
Be cautious with steroids: keep the home dose if already taking them, but new steroid escalation can transiently worsen weakness, so timing should be deliberate.

pairs.value: FVC & NIF q2h or more often, plus capnography, secretion burden, & single breath count.
Hypercapnia (pCO2 >45) is concerning; do not rely on SpO2 alone.
CBC, BMP/CMP, Mg, Phos, CRP if infection suspected.
CXR, cultures if infection is suspected, thyroid studies, med review, aspiration evaluation.
AChR Ab, MuSK Ab, CT chest for thymoma. Do not delay crisis treatment while waiting.
Telemetry & pulse ox; airway/aspiration precautions.

drugs.name: IVIG
drugs.notes: Diseasemodifying: effect lasts 3045 days; onset slower than PLEX (days to weeks). Easier to give (peripheral IV ok)

drugs.name: Plasmapheresis (PLEX)
drugs.contraindications: Hemodynamic instability, limited access, major coagulopathy
drugs.notes: Fastest onset: improvement often by the 2nd3rd session. Generally preferred in crisis if feasible.

drugs.name: Pyridostigmine
drugs.dose: 3060 mg PO q46h (max 90 mg q4h); IV: 23 mg (1/30th PO dose)
drugs.notes: Symptomatic only. Stop if intubated to reduce secretions; restart near extubation. If not intubated, abrupt cessation can worsen weakness.

drugs.name: Dexamethasone / Prednisone
drugs.notes: If already taking steroids, continue. New initiation can transiently worsen weakness, especially in the first 5-10 days.

sections.title: Important Points
items: Reflexes, sensation, pupils, and autonomic findings should stay normal in MG: abnormalities push you toward another diagnosis.
SpO2 can stay normal until late: worsening ventilation often shows up first as rising CO2, weak cough, and poor secretion clearance.
PLEX and IVIG both work; PLEX is often favored in crisis because the response is quicker.
Avoid magnesium: it can noticeably worsen NMJ transmission and tip a borderline pt into respiratory failure.
Thymic disease is common in MG, but thymectomy is a longgame issue, not the priority in the acute crisis window.

pairs.key: ICU / immediate higher level of care if
pairs.value: persistent respiratory distress, high-flow or NIV need, hemodynamic instability, vasopressor need, severe arrhythmia, airway risk, neurologic decline, or a trajectory suggesting pt may crash on the floor.

sections.title: References & Resources
items: Sanders DB, Wolfe GI, Benatar M, et al. International Consensus Guidance for Management of Myasthenia Gravis: Executive Summary. Neurology. 2016;87(4):419425. https://doi.org/10.1212/WNL.0000000000002790

Narayanaswami P, Sanders DB, Wolfe G, et al. International Consensus Guidance for Management of Myasthenia Gravis: 2020 Update. Neurology. 2021;96(3):114122. https://doi.org/10.1212/WNL.0000000000011124

topics.title: Sepsis & Septic Shock
topics.subtitle: Fever, hypotension, tachycardia, elevated lactate, organ dysfunction

sections.title: Acute Management
items: Airway: check patency and protect. Breathing: check WOB, oxygenation, and ventilation. Circulation: check pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
How do they look? Toxic, mottled, febrile, tachypneic, altered, hypotensive, oliguric?
ABCs: protect airway if severe encephalopathy or respiratory failure; give supplemental O2 & escalate support as needed
Place on monitor, get IV access, & start immediate sepsis resuscitation if shock or probable sepsis is present
Get cultures & lactate promptly, butdon't meaningfully delay antibiotics in probable sepsis or septic shock
Give broad spectrum antibiotics urgently; septic shock is a timecritical emergency

sections.title: Chart Check / Hx.
items: Time course: new fever, rigors, hypotension, altered mentation, oliguria, or rising lactate
Likely source: lung, urine, abdomen, skin/soft tissue, line/device, CNS, biliary tree
Recent antibiotics, breakthrough fever, prior resistant organisms, immunosuppression, neutropenia, transplant, steroids, chemotherapy
Indwelling devices: central line, Foley, nephrostomy, dialysis access, drains, prosthetic joints/valves, VP shunt
Recent surgery, procedures, aspiration, diarrhea, wounds, travel, sick contacts, animal exposures
Baseline BP, renal function, mentation, & current code status

sections.title: Exam
items: Vitals & perfusion: fever or hypothermia, tachycardia, tachypnea, hypotension, narrow pulse pressure, mottling, delayed cap refill
Respiratory source: crackles, focal findings, hypoxemia, increased WOB
Abdominal source: distension, tenderness, peritoneal signs, RUQ pain, wound infection
GU source: CVA tenderness, suprapubic tenderness, cloudy urine, obstructed catheter or stent
Skin/device source: cellulitis, pressure injury, line erythema/purulence, necrotizing features
Neuro: encephalopathy, meningismus, focal deficits, agitation, severe lethargy

pairs.value: Lifethreatening organ dysfunction caused by a dysregulated host response to infection
Sepsis with persistent hypotension requiring vasopressors to maintain MAP >=65 &lactate >2 mmol/L despite adequate fluid resuscitation
Screening bedside prompt: RR >=22, altered mentation, or SBP <=100
Marker of illness severity & hypoperfusion; trend it, butdon't treat it in isolation
Medical emergency: fever with ANC <500/mm3 or expected decline to <500; start broad spectrum antibiotics urgently
Pneumonia, urinary tract infection, abdominal infection, skin/soft tissue infection, line/device infection, CNS infection
Hemorrhage, cardiogenic shock, pulmonary embolism, adrenal crisis, severe dehydration, anaphylaxis
Pancreatitis, autoimmune flare, drug reaction, ischemic bowel, thyroid storm, neuroleptic malignant syndrome
DVT/PE, postoperative inflammatory fever, hematoma, gout/pseudogout, transfusion reaction, drug fever
Abscess, empyema, infected line, obstructed pyelonephritis, bowel perforation, necrotizing infection

sections.title: Management
items: If hypotensive or hypoperfused, start balanced crystalloid & prepare early norepinephrine if MAP stays <65
Look for source control needs early: abscess, infected line, obstructed urinary system, ischemic bowel, necrotizing infection

Initial bundle: Measure lactate, get blood cultures before antibiotics when feasible, start broad spectrum antibiotics, begin balanced crystalloid for hypoperfusion, & start vasopressors early if MAP stays <65.
Fluids: Use balanced crystalloids not normal saline; traditional initial target is 30 mL/kg in septic shock or significant hypoperfusion, then recheck dynamically.
Vasopressors: Norepinephrine is first line & can started peripherally while central access is being arranged. Add vasopressin if norepinephrine requirements climb.
Adjuncts: Consider hydrocortisone 200 mg/day in vasopressordependent septic shock not improving despite fluids & vasopressors.
Antibiotics: Use broad spectrum thetherapy tailored to source, host factors, & resistance risk; deescalate once cultures & clinical data return.
Source control: Drain, debride, remove, or decompress infected sources early.

pairs.value: CBC, CMP/BMP, lactate, blood cultures x2 sets, VBG/ABG if ill, coags, glucose.
UA/urine culture, CXR, sputum culture if appropriate, stool testing if diarrheal source, wound or line cultures when relevant.
Continuous monitoring, strict I/O, repeat lactate when elevated, consider bedside ultrasound for shock phenotype & fluid responsiveness.
CT/US or focused imaging if abdominal, biliary, urinary, CNS, or occult source is suspected.
Check ANC if immunocompromised; avoid rectal temperature checks in neutropenic pts; check devices & remove infected lines when feasible.

drugs.name: Norepinephrine
drugs.notes: Firstline vasopressor. Target MAP >=65.

drugs.name: Vasopressin
drugs.notes: Addon pressor if norepinephrine requirements are rising.

drugs.name: Hydrocortisone
drugs.notes: Adjunct for refractory septic shock.

drugs.name: Fludrocortisone
drugs.notes: Optional adjunct with hydrocortisone in refractory septic shock.

drugs.name: Balanced crystalloid
drugs.dose: Initial bolus based on shock/hypoperfusion;30 mL/kg
drugs.notes: Preferred resuscitation fluid when feasible.

drugs.name: Empiric broad spectrum antibiotics
drugs.notes: Give urgently in probable sepsis or septic shock; tailor & deescalate as data return.

drugs.name: Vancomycin + piperacillintazobactam / cefepime / meropenem
drugs.notes: Reasonable broad empiric pattern for undifferentiated severe sepsis, depending on source & resistance risk.

drugs.name: Ceftriaxone + azithromycin
drugs.notes: Common empiric CAP regimen when pneumonia is the likely source.

pairs.key: ICU / immediate higher level of care if
pairs.value: persistent respiratory distress, high-flow or NIV need, hemodynamic instability, vasopressor need, severe arrhythmia, airway risk, neurologic decline, or a trajectory suggesting pt may crash on the floor.

sections.title: References & Resources
items: Surviving Sepsis Campaign: International Guidelines for Management of Sepsis & Septic Shock. Critical Care Medicine. 2025. https://journals.lww.com/ccmjournal/fulltext/9900/surviving_sepsis_campaign__international.786.aspx

topics.title: Meningitis / Encephalitis
topics.subtitle: Fever, headache, neck stiffness, AMS, seizures, CSF abnormalities

sections.title: Acute Management
items: Airway: check patency and protect. Breathing: check WOB, oxygenation, and ventilation. Circulation: check pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
If concern is high: get BCxs, then give empiric abx now. Do NOT wait for CT or LP if there will be delay
If encephalitis is possible: add acyclovir early
Give dexamethasone before or with first abx dose if bacterial meningitis is suspected
Stabilize ABCs, treat shock/seizures, & use droplet isolation if meningococcal disease is possible

sections.title: Chart Check / Hx.
items: Timing: hyperacute toxic presentation vs subacute progressive symptoms
Recent URI, otitis, sinusitis, mastoiditis, pneumonia, CSF leak, neurosurgery, VP shunt, or head trauma
Immune status: steroids, transplant, HIV, chemo, asplenia, alcohol use disorder
Age >50, pregnancy, malignancy, or immunocompromise: raises concern for Listeria
Travel, animal/tick/mosquito exposure, TB risk, HSV/VZV exposure, fungal risk factors
Ask about seizure, focal deficits, behavior change, hallucinations, or memory loss: more suggestive of encephalitis

sections.title: Exam
items: Vitals: fever, hypotension, tachycardia, hypoxia
Mentation: agitation, delirium, lethargy, coma
Meningismus: neck stiffness, Kernig, Brudzinski
Neuro exam: focal deficits, aphasia, cranial neuropathies, papilledema, seizure activity
Skin: petechiae/purpura suggests meningococcemia
Look for source: otitis, sinus tenderness, mastoiditis, endocarditis stigmata, trauma or neurosurgical wounds

pairs.value: Infection/inflammation of the meninges. Typical features: fever, HA, neck stiffness, AMS.
Brain parenchymal inflammation,viral. Think AMS, seizure, focal deficits, behavior change, or memory problems.
Mixed meningeal + parenchymal process. Treat for both until data narrow the cause.
Medical emergency. Delayed abx worsens mortality & neurologic outcomes.

sections.title: Recognition
items: Classic triad: fever, neck stiffness, AMS (present in only 44% —don't wait for all three)
Kernig's sign: pain/resistance extending knee with hip flexed at 90 degrees
Brudzinski's sign: passive neck flexion causes involuntary hip/knee flexion
Photophobia, phonophobia, severe headache
Petechial/purpuric rash suggests Neisseria meningitidis (meningococcus)

pairs.value: S. pneumoniae, N. meningitidis, Listeria, H. influenzae, GBS, nosocomial gramnegatives.
HSV1, VZV, enterovirus, arboviruses, influenza, HHV6.
Cryptococcus, coccidioides, TB: think subacute course or immunocompromise.
Autoimmune encephalitis, carcinomatous meningitis, drug induced aseptic meningitis.
SAH, brain abscess, sepsisassociated encephalopathy, toxicmetabolic encephalopathy, status epilepticus, cerebral venous sinus thrombosis.

sections.title: Management
items: Treat first if suspicion is high: BCxs, dexamethasone, empiric abx, & acyclovir if encephalitis is on the table
Decide on CT before LP only if focal deficit, papilledema, immunocompromised state, new seizure, or GCS <14; otherwise get LP quickly
Narrow thetherapy once CSF, PCR, & culture data return; continue seizure control, ICP management, fluids/vasopressors, & ICUlevel monitoring as needed

sections.title: Workup
items: BCxs x2 now
CT head BEFORE LP only if: focal neuro deficits, papilledema, immunocompromised, new seizure, or GCS <14
LP: opening pressure, cell count, glucose, protein, Gram stain, culture, HSV PCR
Do NOT delay antibiotics for CT or LP: treat first if any delay anticipated
Serum glucose, CBC, BMP, lactate, coags

pairs.value: Get now, ideally before abx if this does not delay treatment.
Only before LP if focal deficits, papilledema, immunocompromised, new seizure, or GCS <14.
Opening pressure, cell count/diff, protein, glucose, Gram stain, culture, HSV PCR; add extra tubes if TB/fungal workup is needed.
CBC, BMP/CMP, serum glucose, lactate, coags, HIV if relevant.
EEG if persistent AMS/seizure concern; MRI brain if encephalitis, abscess, or focal deficits are suspected.

drugs.name: Dexamethasone
drugs.notes: Give BEFORE or WITH first antibiotic dose. ↓ hearing loss & neurologic sequelae in bacterial meningitis.

drugs.name: Ceftriaxone
drugs.notes: Firstline for bacterial meningitis (S. pneumo, N. meningitidis).

drugs.name: Vancomycin
drugs.notes: Add for penicillinresistant S. pneumoniae coverage. Target trough 1520.

drugs.name: Ampicillin
drugs.notes: Add if age >50, immunocompromised, or pregnant: covers Listeria.

drugs.name: Acyclovir
drugs.notes: Start empirically for encephalitis until HSV PCR results. Renally dose adjusted.

pairs.key: ICU / immediate higher level of care if
pairs.value: persistent respiratory distress, high-flow or NIV need, hemodynamic instability, vasopressor need, severe arrhythmia, airway risk, neurologic decline, or a trajectory suggesting pt may crash on the floor.

sections.title: References & Resources
items: Tunkel AR, Hartman BJ, Kaplan SL, et al. Practice Guidelines for the Management of Bacterial Meningitis. Clinical Infectious Diseases. 2004;39(9):12671284. https://doi.org/10.1086/425368

van de Beek D, Cabellos C, Dzupova O, et al. ESCMID Guideline: Diagnosis and Treatment of Acute Bacterial Meningitis. Clinical Microbiology and Infection. 2016;22(Suppl 3):S37S62. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cmi.2016.01.007

Venkatesan A, Tunkel AR, Bloch KC, et al. Case Definitions, Diagnostic Algorithms, and Priorities in Encephalitis: Consensus Statement of the International Encephalitis Consortium. Clinical Infectious Diseases. 2013;57(8):11141128. https://doi.org/10.1093/cid/cit458

topics.title: Anaphylaxis
topics.subtitle: Hypotension, wheeze, urticaria, angioedema, GI symptoms after exposure

sections.title: Acute Management
items: Airway: check patency and protect. Breathing: check WOB, oxygenation, and ventilation. Circulation: check pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
IM EPINEPHRINE 0.30.5mg (1:1000) anterolateral thigh: FIRSTLINE, give now. Repeat q515min PRN
Remove trigger: stop infusion, remove stinger, d/c offending drug
ABCs: secure airway early if laryngeal edema (stridor, voice changes, tongue swelling). Do NOT delay intubation if airway progressing
IVF: NS 12L bolus: anaphylactic shock is distributive (vasodilation + capillary leak). May need aggressive volume
Supplemental O2, monitoring, 2 largebore IVs

sections.title: Chart Check / Hx.
items: Known allergies & prior anaphylaxis: severity of prior reactions
Trigger identification: new meds (antibiotics, NSAIDs, contrast, biologics), foods (nuts, shellfish), insect sting, latex, blood products, immunothetherapy
Timing: onset within min to 2h of exposure (most within 30 min)
Betablocker use: ↑ severity & epi resistance. May need glucagon
ACEI use: ↑ risk of severe angioedema
Mast cell disorder / mastocytosis (↑ anaphylaxis risk)
Exerciseinduced anaphylaxis (food + exercise trigger)
Epi autoinjector available? Used?

sections.title: Exam
items: Diagnostic criteria (NIAID): involvement of ≥2 organ systems after likely allergen exposure:
Skin/mucosal: urticaria, flushing, pruritus, angioedema ( 90% of cases have skin findings: but 10% do NOT)
Respiratory: stridor, wheezing, dyspnea, ↓ SpO2, tongue/lip/uvula swelling
Cardiovascular: ↓ BP, tachycardia, syncope, shock
GI: cramping, vomiting, diarrhea
Can present as isolated ↓ BP /shock without skin findings: esp perioperative anaphylaxis
check airway: voice changes, drooling, stridor: early intubation

pairs.value: Acute, potentially fatal systemic hypersensitivity reaction. IgEmed. (most) or nonIgE (anaphylactoid: radiocontrast, NSAIDs). Same tx for both
Recurrence of sx 172h after initial resolution (most within 812h). Occurs in~520% of cases. Reason for observation period
No response to ≥2 appropriate IM epi doses + IVF. Consider epi infusion, vasopressin, glucagon (if on BB)
Allergic/anaphylactic coronary spasm: ACS triggered by mast cell mediators. Chest pain + anaphylaxis: check troponin, EKG
Antibiotics (penicillins, cephalosporins), NSAIDs, foods (peanuts, tree nuts, shellfish, milk, eggs), insect stings (Hymenoptera), latex, contrast, blood products, biologics, anesthetics
Vasovagal syncope (↓ HR, no urticaria), asthma exacerbation, angioedema (ACEI: no urticaria/pruritus), panic attack, carcinoid, mastocytosis, vocal cord dysfunction, septic shock
Anaphylaxis = tachycardia + ↓ BP + skin findings. Vasovagal = ↓ HR + pallor. ACEI angioedema = no urticaria, no response to epi

sections.title: Management
items: Position pt flat with legs elevated if tolerated; if vomiting or in resp distress, use lateral or upright positioning as needed
Recheck after each IM epi dose. If still hypotensive after repeat IM epi + IVF: start epinephrine infusion 0.10.5 mcg/kg/min IV; consider vasopressin 0.04 U/min in refractory shock
Add adjuncts only after epi: diphenhydramine 2550mg IV, famotidine 20mg IV, methylprednisolone 125mg IV, albuterol neb for bronchospasm
If on betablocker & epiresistant: give glucagon 15mg IV bolus, then infusion if needed
Observe for recurrence/biphasic reaction & escalate to ICU if infusion, airway intervention, or persistent instability is needed

pairs.value: Draw within 12h of onset (peaks 6090min). Elevated confirms mast cell degranulation. nl. does NOT r/o anaphylaxis (esp food triggered)
If chest pain or hemodynamic instability: r/o Kounis syndrome (allergic ACS)
Baseline. Hemoconcentration from capillary leak
If wheezing, dyspnea: r/o pneumo, aspiration
telemetry, SpO2, frequent BP for ≥46h minimum (biphasic risk)

drugs.name: Epinephrine (IM)
drugs.notes: FIRST LINE: give now. Do NOT substitute with antihistamines or steroids. IM > SC (better absorption)

drugs.name: Epinephrine infusion
drugs.notes: For refractory anaphylaxis: persistent ↓ BP despite IM epi + IVF. Requires monitoring

drugs.name: Diphenhydramine
drugs.notes: H1 blocker: adjunct ONLY. Not substitute for epinephrine. Helps urticaria/pruritus

drugs.name: Famotidine
drugs.notes: H2 blocker: adjunct. Combined H1+H2 blockade may ↑ effectiveness

drugs.name: Methylprednisolone
drugs.notes: May ↓ biphasic reactions (oretical: not proven). Onset 46h. Does NOT treat acute anaphylaxis. Alt: dexamethasone 10mg IV

drugs.name: Glucagon
drugs.notes: For epiresistant anaphylaxis in pts on BB. Bypasses betareceptor blockade. Can cause vomiting: airway protection

drugs.name: Albuterol
drugs.notes: For bronchospasm component. Adjunct to epi, not replacement

pairs.key: ICU / immediate higher level of care if
pairs.value: persistent respiratory distress, high-flow or NIV need, hemodynamic instability, vasopressor need, severe arrhythmia, airway risk, neurologic decline, or a trajectory suggesting pt may crash on the floor.

sections.title: References & Resources
items: Cardona V, Ansotegui IJ, Ebisawa M, et al. World Allergy Organization Anaphylaxis Guidance 2020. World Allergy Organization Journal. 2020;13(10):100472. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.waojou.2020.100472

Shaker MS, Wallace DV, Golden DBK, et al. Anaphylaxis — A 2020 Practice Parameter Update, Systematic Review, and GRADE Analysis. Journal of Allergy & Clinical Immunology. 2020;145(4):10821123. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jaci.2020.01.017
Muraro A, Worm M, Alviani C, et al. EAACI Guidelines: Anaphylaxis (2021 Update). Allergy. 2022;77(2):357377. https://doi.org/10.1111/all.15032

topics.title: Necrotizing Fasciitis
topics.subtitle: Rapidly progressive softtissue infection with systemic toxicity, pain out of proportion, crepitus

sections.title: Acute Management
items: Airway: check patency and protect. Breathing: check WOB, oxygenation, and ventilation. Circulation: check pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
!!This is a surgical emergency!! — call surgery immediately if clinical suspicion is present
ABCs: IV access, fluids, vasopressors if septic shock
Draw labs & cultures but !!do NOT delay antibiotics or surgical consult for results!!
Start broad spectrum IV antibiotics immediately (see drug table)
Pain control — these pts often have severe, escalating pain
Mark wound margins with skin marker & note time — track rapid spread

sections.title: Chart Check / Hx.
items: Recent surgery, trauma, wound, abscess drainage, injection drug use
Immunosuppression: diabetes, obesity, chronic liver/kidney disease, cancer, steroids, HIV
History of PVD or chronic wounds
Recent NSAIDs or steroid use (may mask early signs)
Timing: when did pain/swelling start? How rapidly is it progressing?
Prior episodes, prior MRSA or resistant organisms

sections.title: Exam
items: Vital signs: fever (or hypothermia), tachycardia, hypotension — signs of sepsis
Skin: erythema, induration, edema, bullae, ecchymosis, gray/necrotic patches, crepitus
!!Pain out of proportion to exam!! — especially with relatively normalappearing skin early on
Probe wound if present: !!dishwatergray drainage!! or tissue that separates easily along fascial planes ("finger test")
Mark the leading edge of erythema with a skin marker & write the time
Check distal pulses if extremity involvement (compartment syndrome can coexist)

sections.title: Recognition
items: !!Pain out of proportion!! to visible skin findings is the hallmark early sign
Erythema, edema, warmth that spreads rapidly over hours
Skin changes: dusky/gray discoloration, hemorrhagic bullae, crepitus (late)
Systemic toxicity: fever, tachycardia, hypotension, AMS
!!Crepitus = subcutaneous gas = do not wait for imaging — call surgery NOW!!
Skin anesthesia or !!woodenhard!! induration of subcutaneous tissue
Often follows recent wound, surgery, injection, or minor skin break — but can occur spontaneously

pairs.value: Mixed aerobes + anaerobes. Usually postsurgical, diabetic, perineal (Fournier). Most common type overall
!!Group A Strep (S. pyogenes)!! most classic; also Staph aureus (including MRSA). Often in healthy pts after minor trauma
Clostridium perfringens or other Clostridial spp. Rapid gas production, myonecrosis. Often posttraumatic or contaminated wound
Saltwater/raw shellfish exposure + liver disease. Rapidly fatal — suspect in coastal pts with hemorrhagic bullae
Necrotizing fasciitis of the perineum/genitalia. Polymicrobial. !!Surgical emergency!! — high mortality

sections.title: Management
items: !!Emergent surgical debridement!! is the definitive treatment — antibiotics alone are not sufficient
Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation — these pts are often profoundly volume depleted
Start empiric broad spectrum antibiotics immediately (see drug table below)
Vasopressors for septic shock refractory to fluids (norepinephrine first line)
Repeat surgical debridement often needed at 24–48h — plan for reexploration
Wound management: negativepressure wound therapy (wound vac) after debridement when appropriate
!!If diagnosis is uncertain: take pt to the OR!! — operative exploration is both diagnostic and thetherapeutic
ICU admission for hemodynamic monitoring, serial wound checks, and ongoing resuscitation

pairs.value: CBC, BMP, lactate, CRP, CK, BCx × 2, wound culture (from OR), coags, type & screen
CT with contrast of affected area if diagnosis uncertain — look for gas, fascial thickening, fluid tracking. !!Do NOT delay surgery for imaging if clinical suspicion is high!!
Type & crossmatch — prepare for possible transfusion if extensive debridement
!!Surgery STAT!!. ICU. Infectious disease. Consider hyperbaric oxygen (controversial, do not delay debridement)
Serial wound margin checks (remark edges q2–4h), serial lactate, urine output, hemodynamics

drugs.name: Vancomycin
drugs.dose: 25–30 mg/kg IV load, then 15–20 mg/kg q8–12h
drugs.notes: MRSA coverage. Adjust by trough or AUC/MIC

drugs.name: PiperacillinTazobactam
drugs.notes: Broad gramnegative + anaerobic coverage. Alternative: meropenem 1 g IV q8h

drugs.name: Clindamycin
drugs.notes: !!Add for toxin suppression!! — inhibits GAS exotoxin & superantigen production. Key adjunct in Type II nec fasc

drugs.name: Meropenem
drugs.notes: Alternative broad spectrum option if PCN allergy or resistant organisms suspected

pairs.key: ICU / immediate higher level of care if
pairs.value: persistent respiratory distress, high-flow or NIV need, hemodynamic instability, vasopressor need, severe arrhythmia, airway risk, neurologic decline, or a trajectory suggesting pt may crash on the floor.

sections.title: References & Resources
items: Stevens DL, Bisno AL, Chambers HF, et al. Practice Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Management of Skin and Soft Tissue Infections: 2014 Update by the IDSA. Clinical Infectious Diseases. 2014;59(2):e10e52. https://doi.org/10.1093/cid/ciu296

Sarani B, Strong M, Pascual J, Schwab CW. Necrotizing Fasciitis: Current Concepts and Review of the Literature. Journal of the American College of Surgeons. 2009;208(2):279288. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jamcollsurg.2008.10.032

Wong CH, Khin LW, Heng KS, Tan KC, Low CO. The LRINEC (Laboratory Risk Indicator for Necrotizing Fasciitis) Score: A Tool for Distinguishing Necrotizing Fasciitis from Other Soft Tissue Infections. Critical Care Medicine. 2004;32(7):15351541.

topics.title: Massive GI Bleed
topics.subtitle: Hematemesis, melena, hematochezia, shock, falling hemoglobin

sections.title: Acute Management
items: Airway: if massive hematemesis, AMS, or inprotect airway: intubate early (blood in airway = aspiration risk)
Breathing: SpO2, supplemental O2; monitor for aspiration
Circulation: 2 largebore IVs (≥16G) or IO access
Activate massive transfusion protocol (MTP) if hemodynamically unstable or brisk hemorrhage
Transfuse pRBCs now: do NOT wait for type & cross if actively bleeding: use (O) or uncrossmatched
Reverse anticoagulation if needed
Call GI early for urgent endoscopy; call IR / surgery early if brisk hemorrhage or lower GI source is likely
Keep pt NPO

sections.title: Chart Check / Hx.
items: Ask if this is hematemesis, melena, hematochezia, or mixed: hematemesis + hematochezia together suggests a brisk upper GI source
Hx of cirrhosis, portal HTN, varices, prior GI bleed, peptic ulcer dz, aortic graft/surgery, anticoagulants, antiplatelets, NSAIDs, alcohol use
Red flags for massive bleed: syncope, shock, bright red output, for vasopressors, or failure to respond to 2 units pRBC
Find out if the pt is a candidate for endoscopy, IR, or surgery & call the right team early
If cirrhosis/varices are possible: plan for octreotide + ceftriaxone early

sections.title: Exam
items: Vitals: tachycardia, ↓ BP, narrowed pulse pressure, orthostasis, shock
Mentation: agitation, lethargy, syncope
Look for active hematemesis, melena, hematochezia, abdominal distention, or signs of chronic liver dz
Check perfusion: cool extremities, delayed cap refill, low urine output
In cirrhosis/portal HTN: ascites, jaundice, spider angiomata, splenomegaly

pairs.value: GI hemorrhage causing shock, brisk blood loss, rapid Hgb drop, or for blood/MTP.
Source proximal to the ligament of Treitz. Hematemesis or melena are common; brisk bleeds can also cause hematochezia.
Portal hypertensive hemorrhage,from esophageal or gastric varices. Treat early with octreotide, antibiotics, & urgent endoscopy.
Bloodfirst resuscitation for major hemorrhage: early MTP, minimal crystalloid, & correction of coagulopathy/fibrinogen/calcium.
Peptic ulcer, varices, gastritis/esophagitis, MalloryWeiss tear, Dieulafoy lesion, malignancy.
Diverticular bleed, angiodysplasia, ischemic colitis, malignancy, postpolypectomy bleed.
Aortoenteric fistula after prior aortic surgery, hemobilia.
Hemoptysis, posterior epistaxis, swallowed blood, severe coagulopathy without focal GI source, hemorrhagic shock from nonGI source.

sections.title: Management
items: Pillar 1: Hemodynamic resuscitation: activate MTP early, use bloodfirst resuscitation, target balanced products, minimize crystalloid, give calcium during large transfusion, & treat fibrinogen <150 mg/dL with cryoprecipitate. In cirrhosis/varices, avoid overtransfusion once perfusion is restored

Pillar 2: Airway management: avoid intubation unless needed for airway protection, severe hematemesis, or procedures. If intubating, resuscitate first, have suction ready, & start norepinephrine before induction if the pt is vasopressordependent or borderline hypotensive

Pillar 3: Temporizing measures: start octreotide if varices are possible, give IV PPI, reverse anticoagulation, & give ceftriaxone in cirrhosis. Use Blakemore only as a bridge for exsanguinating variceal bleed in an intubated pt

Get definitive source control: upper GI bleedgoes to urgent endoscopy; active lower GI bleed goes to CTA then IR if bleeding continues; surgery if endoscopy/IR fail or if aortoenteric fistula is suspected

Pillar 1: Resuscitation: Largebore access; consider CORDIS/Aline if MTP is running. If a vasopressordependent pt has a distended IVC, recheck whether this is truly hemorrhagic shock before committing to MTP.
Pillar 2: Airway: If endoscopy or intubation is likely, erythromycin 250mg IV 2090 min before can improve gastric emptying. If stomach is distended, NGT suction may help before induction.

Pillar 3: Temporizing: Do not reflexively give FFP for elevated INR alone in cirrhosis. CTA is only useful while actively bleeding. Order it STAT & call CT if lower GI or unclear active bleeding is suspected.
Source control: Variceal bleed: banding/sclerothetherapy, then TIPS if refractory. Posterior duodenal ulcer with active extravasation may go straight to IR if endoscopic control is unlikely.

pairs.value: CBC, CMP/BMP, INR/PTT, fibrinogen, lactate, VBG/ABG as needed, type & cross.
Call early. If bleeding is brisk, move quickly to uncrossmatched blood / MTP.
Use if avail to guide product replacement during massive hemorrhage.
Useful only while actively bleeding. Strongly consider for lower GI bleed or unclear source.
Continuous SpO2/telemetry, frequent BP, strict I&O, & consider arterial line if unstable.

drugs.name: Octreotide
drugs.notes: Start empirically for any possible variceal or portal hypertensive bleed. Continue for 35 days if variceal bleed confirmed.

drugs.name: Pantoprazole
drugs.notes: Intermittent dosing is acceptable. Continue after endoscopy for suspected peptic ulcer bleed.

drugs.name: Erythromycin
drugs.dose: 250mg IV 2090 min before endoscopy or intubation
drugs.notes: Prokinetic to clear the stomach before endoscopy/intubation.

drugs.name: Ceftriaxone
drugs.notes: Antibiotic prophylaxis in cirrhotic pts with GI bleed: ↓ bacterial translocation, SBP, & mortality.

drugs.name: Norepinephrine
drugs.notes: Start before intubation if vasopressordependent or borderline hypotensive.

drugs.name: (tramatic hemorrhage)Tranexamic acid (TXA)
drugs.notes: Not routine for nonvariceal GI bleed. Evidence is stronger in traumatic hemorrhage than GI bleeding.

drugs.name: Calcium gluconate
drugs.notes: Citrate in blood products chelates ionized calcium. Hypocalcemia worsens coagulopathy & cardiac function. Can Use 1g CaCl if central line present, but higher risk of extravasation injury.

pairs.key: ICU / immediate higher level of care if
pairs.value: persistent respiratory distress, high-flow or NIV need, hemodynamic instability, vasopressor need, severe arrhythmia, airway risk, neurologic decline, or a trajectory suggesting pt may crash on the floor.

sections.title: References & Resources
items: Laine L, Barkun AN, Saltzman JR, et al. ACG Clinical Guideline: Upper Gastrointestinal and Ulcer Bleeding. American Journal of Gastroenterology. 2021;116(5):899917. https://doi.org/10.14309/ajg.0000000000001245

Oakland K, Chadwick G, East JE, et al. Diagnosis and Management of Acute Lower Gastrointestinal Bleeding: Guidelines from the British Society of Gastroenterology. Gut. 2019;68(5):776789. https://doi.org/10.1136/gutjnl-2018-317807
Villanueva C, Colomo A, Bosch A, et al. Transfusion Strategies for Acute Upper Gastrointestinal Bleeding. New England Journal of Medicine. 2013;368(1):1121. https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1211801

topics.title: Acute Liver Failure
topics.subtitle: Jaundice, encephalopathy, coagulopathy, transaminitis, hypoglycemia

sections.title: Acute Management
items: ABCs: check airway (HE grade 3–4 may need intubation), breathing, circulation
Check fingerstick glucose: hypoglycemia is common: start D10 infusion if needed
For ACLF: early goaldirected thetherapy, intravascular volume resuscitation, broad spectrum abx within 1h of sepsis presentation

sections.title: Chart Check / Hx.
items: Known liver disease?: distinguishes ALF (no prior dz) from ACLF (known cirrhotic)
Acetaminophen use (intentional or unintentional), OTC meds, supplements, herbals
Recent drug changes: antibiotics, antiTB drugs, statins, immunothetherapy
Alcohol use history & pattern
Travel history (hepatitis A/E endemic areas)
Autoimmune disease history (SLE, thyroiditis)
Family hx of Wilson's disease or liver failure
Pregnancy status (HELLP, acute fatty liver of pregnancy)

sections.title: Exam
items: Hepatic encephalopathy grade: 1 (confusion/mood): 2 (drowsy/asterixis): 3 (somnolent but arousable): 4 (coma)
Jaundice, scleral icterus
Liver size: tender hepatomegaly (acute hepatitis) vs small/shrunken (fulminant necrosis)
Asterixis (flapping tremor)
Signs of chronic liver disease: spider nevi, palmar erythema, ascites, caput medusae (suggests ACLF, not pure ALF)
Right upper quadrant tenderness
check for cerebral edema: pupillary changes, posturing, Cushing response

pairs.value: Acute liver failure: coagulopathy (INR ≥1.5) + hepatic encephalopathy within <26 weeks of symptom onset in a pt without pre-existing liver disease
Acuteonchronic liver failure: acute hepatic decompensation in a known cirrhotic with ≥2 organ failures: use CLIFC ACLF score for grading
Encephalopathy within 7 days of jaundice (e.g., acetaminophen, ischemic): best transplantfree survival
Encephalopathy 8–28 days after jaundice
Encephalopathy 29 days–26 weeks after jaundice: worst transplantfree survival

ALFspecific: ammonia >200 µmol/L = 55% risk of intracranial HTN. Neuroprotective measures: HOB 30°, avoid fever/hypo/hyperglycemia, maintain Na 140–145 with hypertonic saline, normocapnia, highlevel sedation. Plasma exchange may help. Invasive ICP monitoring NOT routinely suggested

ACLF: lactulose 10–20g q2h until 2 soft BMs, then 2–4x daily. Grade 3–4: give via NG or enema (200g in 700 mL water, retain 30–60 min, q4–6h). PEG 3350 may superior for rapid resolution. Add rifaximin 550 mg BID (combined > lactulose alone). For ALF: lactulose & rifaximin have NOT demonstrated benefit: focus on treating precipitants, consider plasma exchange

Start vasoactive drugs now when suspected (octreotide 50 µg bolus: 50 µg/h gtt). PPI strongly suggested. High-risk pts (ChildPugh 10–13 or 8–9 with active bleed): preemptive TIPS improves mortality. If medical + endoscopic thetherapy fail: TIPS

Vasoconstrictor + albumin (1.5 g/kg day 1, 1 g/kg day 2). Terlipressin preferred (response = Cr <1.5 or within 0.3 of baseline, max 14 days). Norepinephrine (0.5–3 mg/h) is noninferior (~50% reversal). Alt: midodrine 5–15 mg q8h + octreotide 100–200 µg q8h (lower efficacy). All AKI pts: urgent transplant eval

Ceftriaxone 2g daily + IV albumin (1.5 g/kg day 1, 1 g/kg day 2): albumin ↓ mortality from 41% to 22% at 3 months. Broadspectrum abx within 1h if septic shock. After first episode: secondary ppx with TMP/SMX or ciprofloxacin

Despite ↑ INR, significant bleeding is uncommon (5–7%). INR does NOT predict bleeding risk (rebalanced hemostasis). Do NOT routinely correct with FFP/plts/cryo: may ↑ mortality. Use TEG/thromboelastometry for transfusion guidance. Correction may needed before high risk procedures (ICP monitoring)
MCC in US: intentional OD or unintentional (chronic suprathetherapeutic use). Check level & start NAC
Hep A, B (most common viral cause), E; rarely CMV, EBV, HSV, VZV
Shock liver: massive transaminase elevation (often >5,000) in setting of hemodynamic compromise
AntiTB drugs (INH, rifampin), antibiotics, anticonvulsants, statins, herbals/supplements, immunothetherapy
ANA, antismooth muscle Ab. Steroids controversial: may ↓ survival in highest MELD scores
Young pt, Coombsnegative hemolytic anemia, low ceruloplasmin, high urine copper, KF rings on slitlamp
BuddChiari syndrome, hepatic vein thrombosis, sinusoidal obstruction syndrome
HELLP syndrome, acute fatty liver of pregnancy (AFLP)

sections.title: Management
items: ICU referral early: all ALF pts need ICUlevel monitoring
Start NAC now if any suspicion for acetaminophen: also improves transplantfree survival in nonacetaminophen ALF
Hold all hepatotoxic medications & unnecessary drugs
Contact transplant center early: ~50% of ALF pts will require transplant (1yr survival 79–84%)
NAC: Start now for acetaminophen & consider for all ALF (improves transplantfree survival in nonacetaminophen causes)
Hemodynamics: Target MAP to maintain CPP 60–80 mmHg. Norepinephrine preferred vasopressor. Balanced crystalloids over NS. For ACLF: target MAP ≥60 mmHg

RRT / CRRT: CRRT preferred over intermittent HD (↓ cardiovascular instability & cerebral edema risk). Indications: acidbase disturbance, oliguria, volume overload, hyperammonemia independent of AKI. Early CRRT (within 4–12h) for hyperammonemia in ALF improves transplantfree survival. Highvolume hemofiltration (90 mL/kg/h) more effective for ammonia clearance
Infection: Empiric broad spectrum antibiotics if infection signs present. Systemic antifungal prophylaxis for high risk transplant recipients

Transplant evaluation: Urgent for all ALF. Status 1a: ICU + ventilator dependence, RRT, or INR ≥2 with encephalopathy within 8 wks. King’s College Criteria better for acetaminophen ALF; MELD more accurate for nonacetaminophen. ACLF grades 1–2: acceptable posttransplant outcomes; ACLF3: careful selection
ACLF3 futility criteria: Age ≥53, lactate ≥4 mmol/L, mechanical vent with P/F ≤200, WBC ≤10 g/L: ≥2 factors predict 8% 1yr survival

pairs.value: LFTs, T/D bili, albumin, INR/PT/PTT, CBC, BMP, ammonia, lactate, ABG, fibrinogen, lipase, blood glucose
Acetaminophen level, tox screen, hepatitis A IgM, HBsAg, HBcAb IgM, HCV RNA, HEV IgM, ANA, antismooth muscle Ab, ceruloplasmin, slitlamp exam (Wilson’s)
CT abdomen (BuddChiari, hepatic vein thrombosis), RUQ ultrasound with Doppler, CXR
Type & crossmatch, TEG/thromboelastometry, fibrinogen, coags
Continuous telemetry, SpO2, q1h glucose checks, q4–6h ammonia, strict I&Os, daily LFTs/INR

drugs.name: Nacetylcysteine (NAC)
drugs.dose: 150 mg/kg IV over 1h, then 50 mg/kg over 4h, then 100 mg/kg over 16h
drugs.notes: Start now for acetaminophen. Also improves transplantfree survival in nonacetaminophen ALF.

drugs.name: Lactulose
drugs.dose: 10–20 g PO/NG q2h until 2 soft BMs, then 2–4x daily
drugs.notes: For ACLF hepatic encephalopathy. Grade 3–4: give via NG or as enema (200g in 700 mL, retain 30–60 min). Not demonstrated beneficial in ALF.

drugs.name: Rifaximin
drugs.notes: Combined with lactulose > lactulose alone for ACLF encephalopathy (↓ hospital stay & mortality). Not demonstrated beneficial in ALF.

drugs.name: Octreotide
drugs.dose: 50 µg IV bolus, then 50 µg/h gtt
drugs.notes: Variceal hemorrhage: start now when suspected, before endoscopy.

drugs.name: Ceftriaxone
drugs.notes: SBP treatment. Give with IV albumin.

drugs.name: Albumin
drugs.notes: SBP: ↓ mortality from 41% to 22% at 3 months. HRSAKI: give with vasoconstrictor.

drugs.name: Norepinephrine
drugs.notes: Preferred vasopressor. Also used for HRSAKI (~50% reversal rate) when terlipressin unavailable.

drugs.name: Zinc sulfate
drugs.notes: Cofactor in urea cycle: ↓ ammonia in chronic hepatic encephalopathy.

pairs.key: ICU / immediate higher level of care if
pairs.value: persistent respiratory distress, high-flow or NIV need, hemodynamic instability, vasopressor need, severe arrhythmia, airway risk, neurologic decline, or a trajectory suggesting pt may crash on the floor.

sections.title: References & Resources
items: Nanchal R, Subramanian R, Alhazzani W, et al. Guidelines for the Management of Adult Acute & AcuteonChronic Liver Failure in ICU. Critical Care Medicine. 2023.
Maiwall R, Kulkarni AV, Arab JP, Piano S. Acute Liver Failure. Lancet. 2024.
Shingina A, Mukhtar N, WakimFleming J, et al. Acute Liver Failure Guidelines. The American Journal of Gastroenterology. 2023.

European Association for the Study of the Liver (EASL). EASL Clinical Practical Guidelines on the Management of Acute (Fulminant) Liver Failure. Journal of Hepatology. 2017;66(5):10471081. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jhep.2016.12.003
Lee WM, Stravitz RT, Larson AM. Introduction to the Revised AASLD Position Paper on Acute Liver Failure. Hepatology. 2012;55(3):965967. https://doi.org/10.1002/hep.25551
Bernal W, Wendon J. Acute Liver Failure. New England Journal of Medicine. 2013;369(26):25252534. https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMra1208937

topics.title: Acute Mesenteric Ischemia
topics.subtitle: Pain out of proportion, lactate rise, acidosis, atrial fibrillation

sections.title: Acute Management
items: Airway: check patency; if AMS from shock or severe sepsis: intubate
Breathing: SpO2, RR; tachypnea may compensatory for metabolic acidosis (lactic acidosis from ischemic bowel)
Circulation: 2 largebore IVs; aggressive IV crystalloid resuscitation: pts are profoundly volumedepleted
Emergent surgical consult: vascular surgery + general surgery + IR; do NOT delay for labs if clinical suspicion is high
NPO, NG tube if vomiting/distension, Foley for UOP monitoring
IV opioid analgesia: do NOT withhold pain control; fentanyl or morphine titrated to effect

sections.title: Chart Check / Hx.
items: Atrial fibrillation: #1 risk factor for SMA embolism; check if on anticoagulation
Cardiac hx: recent MI (mural thrombus), cardiomyopathy, prosthetic valves, endocarditis, ventricular aneurysm
Vascular hx: PAD, prior arterial thrombosis, aortic disease
Prior episodes of postprandial pain, food fear (sitophobia), weight loss: suggests chronic mesenteric ischemia (, then acuteonchronic thrombosis)
Hypercoagulable state: prior DVT/PE, Factor V Leiden, protein C/S deficiency, malignancy, OCP use
Recent surgery: esp cardiac bypass (NOMI risk)
ICU pt on vasopressors or with low cardiac output: think NOMI
Anticoagulant use: type, compliance, last dose
Cirrhosis / portal HTN: risk for mesenteric venous thrombosis

pairs.value: Pain out of proportion to physical exam: severe pain with benignappearing, soft, nontender abdomen (early)
Tachycardia (often earliest sign); ↓ BP (late: suggests necrosis/sepsis); tachypnea (metabolic acidosis compensation); fever (late)
Soft, minimal tenderness, nondistended: deceptively benign exam despite severe pain
Peritoneal signs (guarding, rigidity, rebound) = bowel necrosis has likely already occurred: ominous & late finding
May be hyperactive early: absent late (ileus from necrosis)
Hemepositive stool or frankly bloody ("currant jelly" stools = mucosal sloughing): present in ~25%
Rapid gut emptying (forceful diarrhea) early; AMS, mottled skin, cool extremities if in shock
Inadequate blood flow to intestines: ischemia: infarction: necrosis if untreated; mortality 6080% overall
Thrombus lodges in SMA (usually at branch points); most common type; classically from AFib or cardiac source
Progressive arosclerotic narrowing;hx of chronic mesenteric ischemia; highest mortality (70100%)
Nonocclusive mesenteric ischemia: low flow state (shock, CHF, vasopressors) without mechanical Obst.; occurs in critically ill pts
SMV thrombosis: most insidious onset; best prognosis (2050% mortality); associated with hypercoagulable states
Most common form of intestinal ischemia (8085%);watershed areas (splenic flexure, rectosigmoid);selflimited
AFib, recent MI, prosthetic valves, endocarditis, ventricular aneurysm, cardiomyopathy
Arosclerosis, chronic mesenteric ischemia, PAD, aortic disease/dissection
Hypercoagulable states, malignancy, cirrhosis/portal HTN, pancreatitis, recent surgery, OCP use
Shock (any type), CHF with ↓ CO, vasopressor use, cardiac bypass surgery, hemodialysis, sepsis
Bowel Obst. (SBO/LBO), perforated viscus, pancreatitis, ruptured AAA, volvulus, acute cholecystitis
Aortic dissection with mesenteric malperfusion, renal infarction, splenic infarction
Ischemic colitis (usually less severe, selflimited), C. diff colitis, IBD flare, acute diverticulitis

sections.title: Management & Pearls
items: Call surgery urgently and involve vascular or interventional radiology early if avail.
Place on monitor, give oxygen as needed, and get 2 large-bore IV lines.
Keep NPO and begin aggressive isotonic IV fluids unless contraindicated.
Send STAT labs including CBC, CMP or BMP, lactate, ABG or VBG, coagulation studies, and type and screen.
Start broad-spectrum IV antibiotics early.
Begin systemic anticoagulation with IV heparin unless active bleeding or another clear contraindication.
get emergent CTA abdomen or pelvis with IV contrast without delaying consultation.
Avoid delays from nonspecific tests and do not rely on a normal lactate to exclude disease.
Escalate immediately to operative or endovascular management if peritonitis, bowel infarction, or ongoing instability is present.

pairs.value: Surgical embolectomy (mainstay) or catheter directed tPA thrombolysis (IR); resect necrotic bowel; second look laparotomy at 2448 hrs
Angioplasty ± stenting (IR) or surgical bypass/endarterectomy; bowel resection if necrosis
Anticoagulation alone is sufficient in ~95%; catheter directed thrombolysis if worsening; surgery only for peritonitis/necrosis
Transcatheter vasodilator infusion (papaverine) via SMA catheter; treat underlying cause (optimize CO, d/c vasoconstrictors); bowel resection if necrosis
Planned reexploration at 2448 hrs: standard after revascularization to recheck bowel viability;don't skip
If peritonitis present: emergent laparotomy for bowel resection;don't delay for furr workup

sections.title: Orders
items: Lactate: ↑ as ischemia progresses; nl. lactate does NOT r/o AMI (can be nl. early)
CBC: leukocytosis with left shift (WBC>15,000)
BMP: metabolic acidosis (AG), ↑ BUN/Cr (prerenal from dehydration)
ABG/VBG: base deficit correlates with severity; lactic acidosis
Ddimer: elevated (sensitive but nonspecific; high negative predictive value)
Phosphate: hyperphosphatemia (released from ischemic bowel mucosa: relatively specific)
LDH: ↑ (nonspecific tissue necrosis marker)
Amylase: may ↑ (confuses with pancreatitis)
LFTs: AST/ALT may ↑ if portal/hepatic flow affected
Coags (PT/INR, aPTT): baseline before heparin; DIC screen
Type & screen: prepare for possible surgery
BCxs if septic
CTA abdomen/pelvis (arterial + venous phase): study of choice; sensitivity ~94%, specificity ~95%
Plain Abd XR —nl. early; late: thumbprinting, pneumatosis intestinalis, portal venous gas, free air

drugs.name: Heparin (UFH)
drugs.dose: 80 units/kg IV bolus (max 5,000 units), then 18 units/kg/hr infusion (max 1,000 units/hr)
drugs.notes: Start now once dx suspected: even if surgery planned, heparinize first. Target aPTT 1.52.5× nl. (6080 sec)

drugs.name: PiperacillinTazobactam
drugs.notes: Covers gramnegatives + anaerobes: bacterial translocation from ischemic gut. Start empirically

drugs.name: Meropenem
drugs.notes: For critically ill / septic pts or if high resistance risk; alternative to piptazo

drugs.name: Fentanyl
drugs.dose: 25-100 mcg IV q3060 min PRN; or infusion 25-100 mcg/hr
drugs.notes: For analgesia: do NOT withhold pain control; hemodynamically neutral (preferred over morphine in ↓ BP )

drugs.name: Papaverine (via IR catheter)
drugs.dose: 3060 mg/hr intraarterial infusion via SMA catheter
drugs.notes: Mesenteric vasodilator: used for arterial embolism & NOMI; administered via IR catheter directly into SMA

drugs.name: Norepinephrine (if shock)
drugs.dose: 0.10.5 mcg/kg/min IV; use lowest effective dose
drugs.contraindications: Worsens mesenteric ischemia: use only if necessary
drugs.notes: Vasopressors worsen mesenteric perfusion; minimize dose; avoid pure alphaagonists (phenylephrine)

pairs.key: ICU / immediate higher level of care if
pairs.value: persistent respiratory distress, high-flow or NIV need, hemodynamic instability, vasopressor need, severe arrhythmia, airway risk, neurologic decline, or a trajectory suggesting pt may crash on the floor.

sections.title: References & Resources
items: Bala M, Kashuk J, Moore EE, et al. Acute Mesenteric Ischemia: Guidelines of the World Society of Emergency Surgery. World Journal of Emergency Surgery. 2017;12:38. https://doi.org/10.1186/s13017-017-0150-5
Defined Approach to Acute Mesenteric Ischemia — AGA Clinical Practice Update. Clin Gastroenterol Hepatol. 2020;18(5):944953. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cgh.2019.11.029
Defined Approach: Defined Approach to diagnosis and treatment of mesenteric ischemia. European Journal of Vascular and Endovascular Surgery. 2017;53(4):4605-10.

topics.title: DKA / HHS
topics.subtitle: Hyperglycemia, ketoacidosis, hyperosmolarity, dehydration, AMS

sections.title: Acute Management
items: Airway: if GCS <8 or unable to protect airway: intubate; CAUTION: Kussmaul respirations are compensatory: if intubating, match high RR & tidal volume to avoid worsening acidosis
Breathing: SpO2, RR; Kussmaul breathing (deep, rapid) is expected compensatory mechanism: do NOT suppress with sedation
Circulation: 2 largebore IVs; NS 1 L in first hour (avg fluid deficit: DKA 5–9 L, HHS 100–220 mL/kg); recheck volume status then 250–500 mL/hr
Start insulin 1–2 hours AFTER initiating fluid resuscitation: early insulin before fluids may detrimental, especially in HHS

sections.title: Chart Check / Hx.
items: Known T1DM vs T2DM; baseline insulin regimen (basal/bolus, pump)
Home A1c, last glucose monitoring
SGLT2 inhibitor use: risk for euglycemic DKA (glucose may <200 mg/dL)
Recent illness, infection, surgery, trauma
Medication compliance: missed insulin doses, new meds (steroids, atypicals)
Alcohol use (ketoacidosis, reduced food intake)
Pregnancy status (higher risk for DKA)
Prior episodes of DKA/HHS, prior ICU admissions

sections.title: Exam
items: Volume status: skin turgor, dry mucous membranes, cap refill, tachycardia, hypotension
Kussmaul respirations (deep, rapid: compensatory for metabolic acidosis)
Fruity breath (acetone)
Abdominal tenderness (common in DKA: may mimic surgical abdomen)
Mentation: lethargy: obtundation: coma (HHSmore severe AMS than DKA)
Signs of precipitant: fever, focal infection, new murmur, focal neuro deficits

pairs.value: Glucose >250 mg/dL + pH <7.3 + bicarb <18 + anion gap >12 + positive serum/urine ketones. All criteria must be met. ~10% present as euglycemic DKA (glucose <200): associated with SGLT2i, reduced intake, pregnancy, alcohol, liver failure
Mild: pH 7.25–7.30, bicarb 15–18. Moderate: pH 7.00–7.24, bicarb 10–15. Severe: pH <7.00, bicarb <10
Glucose >600 mg/dL + serum osmolality >320 mOsm/kg + minimal/absent ketones + pH >7.3. Fluid deficit100–220 mL/kg. Higher mortality than DKA
Infection (MCC), new T1DM/T2DM, insulin noncompliance, MI, stroke, pregnancy, meds (steroids, SGLT2i, atypical antipsychotics)
DKA, lactic acidosis, renal failure (uremia), toxic ingestion (methanol, ethylene glycol, salicylates): MUDPILES mnemonic
DKA, HHS, stress hyperglycemia, steroidinduced, newonset DM, pancreatitis
Starvation ketosis, alcoholic ketoacidosis: glucose is normal or low
SGLT2 inhibitor use, pregnancy, reduced oral intake, alcohol use, liver failure: check ketones & AG even with normal glucose

sections.title: Management
items: Check K+ BEFORE starting insulin: if K+ <3.3, replete K+ FIRST (insulin drives K+ intracellularly: fatal hypokalemia)
Search for precipitant: infection (#1), insulin noncompliance, newonset DM, MI, stroke, pancreatitis, pregnancy, meds (steroids, SGLT2i)
DKA & HHSpresent concurrently: mixed presentations have higher mortality than either alone

pairs.value: NS 1 L in first hour. If shock: rapid boluses with attention to airway. Then 250–500 mL/hr based on volume status. Replace deficits over 24–48h. Switch to D5½NS when glucose <250 mg/dL

Start 1–2h after fluids. Regular insulin 0.1 units/kg/hr IV infusion. Target glucose reduction ~50 mg/dL/hr & bicarb ↑ ~3 mEq/L/hr. If not meeting goals: ↑ by 1 unit/hr. When glucose <250: ↓ to 0.05 units/kg/hr & add D5 to IVF. Minimum rate 1 unit/hr to allow acidosis resolution
SQ rapidacting insulin q1–2h may used in ED or stepdown (safer, more costeffective). No significant outcome difference vs IV insulin when combined with aggressive fluids
Check K+ before insulin. If K+ <3.3: hold insulin, replete K+ first. Add 20–40 mEq KCl/L to IVF if K+ <5.0. Target K+ 4.0–5.0. Monitor q2h (q1h if abnormal)
Continue home basal dose or start glargine 0.3 units/kg early alongside IV insulin: prevents rebound hyperglycemia & facilitates SQ transition
Not routinely suggested: studies show no difference in acidosis resolution or time to discharge. Consider only if pH <6.9 with ↓ cardiac function or severe hyperkalemia
Not routinely replaced. Consider if severe deficiency present
Clinical improvement + pH >7.3 + betahydroxybutyrate normalized + bicarb ≥18 + anion gap closed + glucose <200 mg/dL. Do NOT stop insulin based on glucose alone: gap must close
NS (0.9% NaCl) is principal fluid. Replace ~50% of estimated fluid loss within first 12h. Fluid replacement alone will ↓ glucose. Switch to 5% or 10% dextrose when glucose <252 mg/dL (14 mmol/L)

Withhold insulin until glucose is no longer falling with IV fluids alone (unless ketonaemia present). When starting: 0.05–0.1 units/kg/hr. ↑ by 1 unit/hr as needed. Early insulin before adequate fluids is harmful in HHS

Measure/calculate serum osmolality regularly. Target reduction 3–8 mOsm/kg/h: rapid correction risks central pontine myelinolysis. An initial ↑ in Na is expected & is NOT an indication for hypotonic fluids
10–15 mmol/L (180–270 mg/dL) in first 24h: avoid overcorrection
Higher mortality than DKA. Risk of MI, stroke, seizures, cerebral edema, central pontine myelinolysis, VTE, foot ulceration. Prevent VTE with prophylaxis. Involve diabetes specialist team early
Osmolality <300 mOsm/kg + hypovolemia corrected (UOP ≥0.5 mL/kg/h) + cognitive status returned to baseline + glucose <270 mg/dL
Give basal SQ insulin 2–4h BEFORE stopping IV insulin to prevent rebound hyperglycemia & recurrent ketoacidosis. Overlap IV + SQ for 30–60 min minimum
If wellcontrolled on home regimen: resume home doses. If new: 0.5–0.8 units/kg/day total, with half as basal (glargine/detemir) & half as prandial (lispro/aspart) with meals
Giving lowdose basal analog early during IV infusion prevents rebound hyperglycemia without ↑ hypoglycemia risk
BMP (glucose, K+, bicarb, BUN/Cr), VBG or ABG, serum ketones (betahydroxybutyrate preferred), CBC, lactate, serum osmolality, urinalysis
Acetaminophen level (r/o coingestion), lipase, troponin if MI suspected, blood cultures if febrile, CXR, ECG
POC glucose & betahydroxybutyrate q1h. BMP & pH q2–4h. K+ q2h (q1h if abnormal). Strict I&Os
Serum osmolality q2–4h (target ↓ 3–8 mOsm/kg/h). POC glucose q1h. BMP q2–4h. Strict I&Os

drugs.name: Normal saline (0.9% NaCl)
drugs.dose: 1 L in first hour, then 250–500 mL/hr
drugs.notes: Cornerstone of DKA & HHS tx. For HHS: replace ~50% deficit in first 12h.

drugs.name: Regular insulin
drugs.notes: Start 1–2h after fluids. ↓ to 0.05 units/kg/hr when glucose <250. Min rate 1 unit/hr for acidosis resolution. For HHS: may start at 0.05 units/kg/hr.

drugs.name: KCl replacement
drugs.notes: Start when K+ <5.0 & UOP confirmed. Target 4.0–5.0. Monitor q2h (q1h if abnormal).

drugs.name: D5W or D10W
drugs.dose: Add to IVF when glucose <250 (DKA) or <252 (HHS)
drugs.notes: Prevents hypoglycemia while insulin continues for acidosis/ketosis resolution.

drugs.name: Insulin glargine (basal)
drugs.notes: Give early alongside IV insulin for faster resolution & easier SQ transition. Give 2–4h before stopping IV insulin.

drugs.name: Sodium bicarb
drugs.notes: Not routinely suggested: no benefit shown in studies. Consider only if pH <6.9 with ↓ cardiac function or severe hyperkalemia.

pairs.key: ICU / immediate higher level of care if
pairs.value: persistent respiratory distress, high-flow or NIV need, hemodynamic instability, vasopressor need, severe arrhythmia, airway risk, neurologic decline, or a trajectory suggesting pt may crash on the floor.

sections.title: References & Resources
items: American Diabetes Association Professional Practice Committee. 16. Diabetes Care in the Hospital: Standards of Care in Diabetes—2026. Diabetes Care. 2026;49(Suppl 1):S339–S355.
Veauthier B, LevyGrau B. Diabetic Ketoacidosis: Evaluation & Treatment. American Family Physician. 2024.

Umpierrez GE, Davis GM, ElSayed NA, et al. ADA Consensus Report: Management of Hyperglycaemic Crises — Diabetic Ketoacidosis and Hyperglycaemic Hyperosmolar State. Diabetologia. 2024;67:14551479. https://doi.org/10.1007/s00125-024-06183-8
Joint British Diabetes Societies (JBDS). The Management of Diabetic Ketoacidosis in Adults. 2023. https://abcd.care/resource/current/jbds-02-management-diabetic-ketoacidosis-adults

topics.title: Hypoglycemia
topics.subtitle: Diaphoresis, tremor, confusion, seizure, glucose <70 mg/dL

sections.title: Acute Management
items: ABCs: check airway (protect if obtunded/seizing), breathing, circulation
Fingerstick glucose STAT: confirm hypoglycemia (<70 mg/dL)
If unconscious, seizing, or unable to swallow: D50 25 g (1 amp) IV push or glucagon 1 mg IM if no IV
If alert & can swallow: 15–20 g fastacting PO carbs (4 oz juice, glucose tabs): recheck in 15 min (Rule of 15)
Place on cardiac monitor: arrhythmia risk with severe hypoglycemia
IV access if not already established

sections.title: Chart Check / Hx.
items: Diabetes type & home regimen: insulin (type, dose, timing), sulfonylurea, meglitinide
Last meal, PO intake, recent illness, vomiting, diarrhea
Recent medication changes: new insulin dose, ↑ sulfonylurea, added potentiator (fluoroquinolone, TMPSMX)
EtOH use: inhibits gluconeogenesis; causes prolonged hypoglycemia
Renal function: CKD ↓ insulin & sulfonylurea clearance
Adrenal insufficiency, liver disease, sepsis, malignancy, bariatric surgery hx
Betablocker use: masks adrenergic sx (tremor, tachycardia) but NOT diaphoresis
Intentional OD or insulin misuse: consider if recurrent or unexplained

sections.title: Exam
items: Adrenergic signs: diaphoresis, tremor, tachycardia, pallor, anxiety
Neuroglycopenic signs: confusion, slurred speech, ataxia, seizure, focal deficits, coma
Vitals: tachycardia, hypo or hypertension; hypothermia in severe cases
Signs of underlying cause: jaundice (liver failure), Kussmaul breathing (DKA overshoot), Addisonian features (hyperpigmentation, ↓ BP)
Injection sites: lipohypertrophy, signs of surreptitious insulin use

pairs.value: Glucose <70 mg/dL. Clinically significant: <54 mg/dL. Severe: requires help from another person.
1) Sx consistent with hypoglycemia, 2) Low measured glucose, 3) Resolution of sx after glucose correction
Loss of adrenergic warning sx from recurrent episodes: common in T1DM & tightly controlled T2DM. ↑ risk of severe events.
Somogyi effect: overcorrection or counterregulatory hormone surge causing ↑ glucose after treatment
Exogenous insulin (most common in DM pts), sulfonylurea, meglitinide: check Cpeptide & insulin level
Prolonged hypoglycemia (up to 72h) from long halflife: requires octreotide & extended monitoring
Inhibits hepatic gluconeogenesis. May present hours after drinking. Glycogen stores depleted in malnourished pts.
↓ cortisol: impaired gluconeogenesis & glycogenolysis. Check random cortisol.
↑ glucose consumption, hepatic dysfunction, ↓ glycogen stores
↓ glycogen storage & gluconeogenesis capacity
Rare. Fasting hypoglycemia with inappropriately ↑ insulin & Cpeptide. 72h supervised fast is gold standard dx.
Fluoroquinolones, TMPSMX, pentamidine, quinine, BB (mask sx), ACEi (rare)

sections.title: Management
items: Mild (54–70, alert): 15–20 g PO carbs: recheck 15 min: repeat PRN (Rule of 15). Follow with complex carb snack if next meal >1h.
Moderate (40–54, symptomatic): D50 25 g IV or D10 250 mL IV if peripheral line. Recheck q15 min.
Severe (<40, AMS, seizure): D50 25 g IV push. If no IV: glucagon 1 mg IM. Recheck q15 min. Consider D10 infusion.
Sulfonylureainduced: Octreotide 50–100 mcg SQ/IV q6–12h: prevents rebound hypoglycemia. Monitor 24–72h (prolonged effect).
EtOH related: D50 + thiamine 100 mg IV BEFORE or WITH dextrose (prevent Wernicke). D10 infusion until eating.
After correction: identify & address root cause: adjust insulin dose, hold sulfonylurea, treat infection, r/o adrenal crisis
Recheck glucose q15–30 min during active treatment, then q1–2h until stable >100 for ≥2 consecutive checks
If recurrent despite treatment: start D10 infusion at 75–125 mL/h & investigate underlying cause

pairs.value: STAT, then q15 min during treatment, q1–2h once stable
K+, Cr, Na: check renal function & electrolytes
If liver disease suspected as contributor
Random cortisol if adrenal insufficiency suspected (draw before giving steroids)
If cause unclear: draw DURING hypoglycemic episode. ↑ insulin + ↑ Cpeptide = endogenous (insulinoma, sulfonylurea). ↑ insulin + ↓ Cpeptide = exogenous insulin.
If medication induced hypoglycemia suspected & pt denies use
If alcohol related hypoglycemia suspected
For glucose <54 or symptomatic: arrhythmia risk

drugs.name: Dextrose 50% (D50)
drugs.contraindications: Extravasation causes tissue necrosis: ensure good IV
drugs.notes: Firstline for severe hypoglycemia. Raises glucose ~75–100 mg/dL. Transient effect: follow with D10 or PO if needed.

drugs.name: Dextrose 10% (D10)
drugs.dose: 250 mL IV over 15–30 min or infusion at 75–125 mL/h
drugs.notes: Gentler on veins than D50. Preferred for maintenance infusion or peripheral IV only. Avoids rebound spikes.

drugs.name: Glucagon
drugs.contraindications: Ineffective if glycogen depleted (EtOH, starvation, adrenal insufficiency)
drugs.notes: When no IV access. Onset 10–15 min. May cause N/V. Nasal formulation also available (3 mg).

drugs.name: Octreotide
drugs.notes: Sulfonylureainduced hypoglycemia: suppresses insulin secretion, prevents rebound. Give for 24–72h.

drugs.name: Thiamine
drugs.notes: Give BEFORE or WITH dextrose in EtOH related or malnourished pts: prevents Wernicke encephalopathy.

drugs.name: Diazoxide
drugs.notes: For refractory hypoglycemia (insulinoma, nesidioblastosis): inhibits insulin release. Rarely used acutely.

pairs.key: ICU / immediate higher level of care if
pairs.value: persistent respiratory distress, high-flow or NIV need, hemodynamic instability, vasopressor need, severe arrhythmia, airway risk, neurologic decline, or a trajectory suggesting pt may crash on the floor.

sections.title: References & Resources
items: ADA Standards of Care 2026: Glycemic targets & hypoglycemia management in diabetes
Cryer PE. NEJM 2024: Evaluation & management of adult hypoglycemic disorders
Endocrine Society 2009: Evaluation & management of hypoglycemia in adults (guideline)
American Diabetes Association. Standards of Care in Diabetes — 2024: Glycemic Goals and Hypoglycemia. Diabetes Care. 2024;47(Suppl 1):S111S125. https://doi.org/10.2337/dc24-S006
