Cryer PE, Axelrod L, Grossman AB, et al. Evaluation and Management of Adult Hypoglycemic Disorders: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline. Journal of Clinical Endocrinology & Metabolism. 2009;94(3):709728. https://doi.org/10.1210/jc.2008-1410

topics.title: Adrenal Crisis
topics.subtitle: Hypotension, hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, hypoglycemia, shock

sections.title: Acute Management
items: Airway: check patency and protect. Breathing: check WOB, oxygenation, and ventilation. Circulation: check pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
Suspect adrenal crisis in shock refractory to fluids & vasopressors —don't wait for confirmatory testing
Aggressive isotonic fluid: nl. saline 13 L initially,thencontinue guided by hemodynamics, sodium, urine output, & comorbidities
If hypoglycemic: give dextrose now & continue glucose monitoring
Consult endocrinology early, especially if diagnosis is uncertain, shock persists, or transition planning is needed

sections.title: Chart Check / Hx.
items: Known primary, secondary, or tertiary adrenal insufficiency; prior adrenal crisis; usual glucocorticoid replacement dose
Recent GI illness, flulike illness, fever, infection, trauma, childbirth, surgery, extreme heat/cold exposure, or major emotional/physical stress
Abrupt cessation or underdosing of chronic steroids: prednisone ≥5 mg/day for 4 weeks or longer can suppress HPA axis
Look for nonoral steroid exposure: inhaled steroids, steroid creams, joint injections, or exogenous glucocorticoids
Medication review: levothyroxine initiation, phenytoin, phenobarbital, rifampin, carbamazepine, St John's wort, ketoconazole, etomidate, fluconazole, checkpoint inhibitors
History of autoimmune disease or polyglandular syndrome: type 1 diabetes, thyroid disease, vitiligo, pernicious anemia

sections.title: Exam
items: ↓ BP, orthostasis, tachycardia, signs of volume depletion
AMS ranging from lethargy/confusion to obtundation
Fever may present & can reflect trigger or cytokine driven stress response
Diffuse abdominal tenderness or pain may mimic an acute abdomen
Hyperpigmentation suggests primary adrenal insufficiency
Look for associated autoimmune findings or endocrine disease

pairs.value: ↓ BP that remains unexplained or resistant to standard fluid & vasopressor support
Weakness, fatigue, nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, back pain, anorexia, dizziness, fever, confusion, syncope
Can worsen from nonspecific symptoms to shock, metabolic encephalopathy, obtundation, & coma
Hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, volume depletion, salt craving, hyperpigmentation, higher risk than secondary adrenal insufficiency
Low cortisol with low or inappropriately nl. ACTH; aldosterone ispreserved, so hyperkalemia may absent
Gastrointestinal infection / illness is common precipitating event
Infection, trauma, surgery, pregnancy, childbirth, strenuous activity, severe emotional stress, or any major physiologic insult
Abrupt cessation of glucocorticoids, antiadrenal medications, checkpoint inhibitors, & drugs that increase cortisol metabolism
Known adrenal insufficiency, prior adrenal crisis, primary adrenal insufficiency, older adults, multiple comorbidities, autoimmune polyglandular disease, type 1 diabetes
Starting levothyroxine in an untreated adrenal insufficient pt can precipitate crisis by increasing metabolic demand & cortisol clearance
Septic, hypovolemic, cardiogenic, & obstructive shock can all overlap; refractory ↓ BP is major adrenal crisis clue
Thyroid storm, myxedema coma, DKA, HHS, pituitary disease
Gastroenteritis, bowel obstruction, appendicitis, diverticulitis, pancreatitis, or causes of acute abdomen
Checkpointinhibitor endocrinopathy, adrenal metastases, adrenal hemorrhage, steroid withdrawal, antiadrenal drugs

sections.title: Management
items: Suspect adrenal crisis in hypotension or shock with AMS, vomiting, abdominal pain, hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, or hypoglycemia.
Place pt on a monitor, give oxygen as needed, and get 2 IV lines.
Check point-of-care glucose immediately and give IV dextrose if low.
Draw BMP, glucose, and if helpful cortisol, ACTH, and cultures without delaying treatment.
Give hydrocortisone 100 mg IV immediately.
Start isotonic fluids with a 1 L normal saline bolus, then recheck and repeat as needed.
Continue hydrocortisone 50 mg IV q6h or 200 mg/day as an infusion.
Treat hyperkalemia, hypoglycemia, and other critical abnormalities per standard protocol.
Identify and treat the trigger, with early antibiotics if infection is suspected.
Escalate persistent hypotension to vasopressors and ICU transfer.

pairs.value: BMP/CMP, CBC, glucose, cortisol, ACTH, lactate, VBG/ABG, BCxs if infectious trigger suspected
Hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, ↓ Glucose, prerenal AKI, metabolic acidosis; hypercalcemia can also occur
If stable enough, draw cortisol & ACTH before steroids. ACTH stimulation testing can help later, but never delay acute therapy
Urinalysis, CXR, cultures, ECG, troponin, thyroid testing, or abdominal imaging depending on trigger & differential

drugs.name: Hydrocortisone
drugs.notes: Tx of choice.Don't delay treatment for labs. At doses >50mg/day, mineralocorticoid replacement isunnecessary acutely.

drugs.name: Dexamethasone
drugs.notes: Alternative if diagnostic cortisol testing is still needed. Least preferred emergency alternative. No mineralocorticoid activity.

drugs.name: Prednisolone
drugs.dose: 25mg IV bolus,then25mg x2 more doses in first 24h,then50mg daily
drugs.notes: Preferred alternative parenteral steroid if hydrocortisone is unavailable.

drugs.name: Methylprednisolone
drugs.notes: Alternative steroid option if hydrocortisone is unavailable.

drugs.name: nl. saline
drugs.notes: Cornerstone of initial resuscitation for hypovolemia & ↓ BP.

drugs.name: Dextrose 50%
drugs.notes: Treat ↓ Glucose now; continue glucose monitoring afterward.

drugs.name: Fludrocortisone
drugs.notes: Add after stabilization in primary adrenal insufficiency if needed. Not required acutely when high dose hydrocortisone is being used.

pairs.value: Patients with known adrenal insufficiencydouble or triple ir oral glucocorticoid dose during acute illness for 23 days depending on severity
Patients have emergency injectable hydrocortisone (or dexamethasone), saline, & syringes available
Encourage medical alert bracelet / necklace & steroid card with emergency dosing instructions
In undifferentiated shock, adrenal crisis ismissed b/c presenting symptoms are nonspecific: major bedside clue is persistent ↓ BP despite usual resuscitation

pairs.key: ICU / immediate higher level of care if
pairs.value: persistent respiratory distress, high-flow or NIV need, hemodynamic instability, vasopressor need, severe arrhythmia, airway risk, neurologic decline, or a trajectory suggesting pt may crash on the floor.

sections.title: References & Resources
items: Bornstein SR, Allolio B, Arlt W, et al. Diagnosis and Treatment of Primary Adrenal Insufficiency: An Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline. Journal of Clinical Endocrinology & Metabolism. 2016;101(2):364389. https://doi.org/10.1210/jc.2015-1710
Rushworth RL, Torpy DJ, Falhammar H. Adrenal Crisis. New England Journal of Medicine. 2019;381(9):852861. https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMra1807486

topics.title: Thyroid Storm
topics.subtitle: Fever, tachycardia, agitation, heart failure, GI symptoms

sections.title: Acute Management
items: Airway: check patency and protect. Breathing: check WOB, oxygenation, and ventilation. Circulation: check pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
ABCs: ICUlevel monitoring, IV access × 2, telemetry
Aggressive IV fluid: pts are volumedepleted from fever, diaphoresis, GI losses
Cooling measures: cooling blankets, cold IV fluids. Rectal acetaminophen if altered. Avoid aspirin (displaces T4 from binding proteins)

sections.title: Chart Check / Hx.
items: Known hyperthyroidism or Graves' disease
Recent discontinuation of antithyroid Meds (PTU, methimazole)
Recent iodine load: contrast dye, amiodarone
Recent surgery, anesthesia, trauma, or sepsis (precipitants)
Med list: levothyroxine dose, anticholinergics, adrenergic drugs
Hx. of prior thyroid storm or thyroidectomy

sections.title: Exam
items: High fever (often >40°C / 104°F), profuse diaphoresis
Tachycardia (often >140), irregular rhythm (AFib common)
Lid lag, proptosis, goiter, thyroid bruit
Hyperreflexia, fine tremor, proximal muscle weakness
AMS: agitation: confusion: obtundation: coma
Signs of CHF: JVD, crackles, peripheral edema

pairs.value: Lifethreatening exaggeration of thyrotoxicosis with multiorgan dysfunction. BurchWartofsky score ≥45.
Excess thyroid hormone from any cause: Graves', toxic nodule, thyroiditis, exogenous thyroid hormone
≥45 = thyroid storm, 25–44 = impending storm. Components: temp, HR, AFib, CHF, GI/hepatic, CNS effects
10–30% even with tx. Early recognition & multiagent therapy are critical
MCC. TSI antibodies. Proptosis, diffuse goiter, pretibial myxedema.
Nodular thyroid disease. Usually elderly pts.
Subacute, postpartum, or drug induced (amiodarone, checkpoint inhibitors)
Thyroid hormone OD (intentional or accidental)
Fever, tachycardia, AMS. Check TFTs in any unexplained multiorgan dysfunction.
Clonus/hyperreflexia, agitation, hyperthermia. Check Med list for serotonergic agents.
Paroxysmal HTN, tachycardia, diaphoresis, headache. Plasma/urine metanephrines.

sections.title: Management
items: **Step 1: Block new hormone synsis**: PTU 600–1000 mg load PO/NG: 200–250 mg q4–6h
**Step 2: Block sympathetic effects**: propranolol 1–2 mg IV q15min (max 10 mg) OR esmolol gtt 50–100 mcg/kg/min
**Step 3: Block T4, then T3 conversion**: hydrocortisone 100 mg IV q6–8h (also prevents adrenal crisis)
**Step 4: Block hormone release** (give ≥1h AFTER PTU): potassium iodide (SSKI) 5 drops PO/NG q6h
Consult endocrinology STAT
Continue PTU + betablocker + hydrocortisone + iodine regimen
Monitor HR, temp, mentation q1–2h: gauge tx response
Serial TFTs q12–24h initially
Transition PTU: methimazole 20 mg PO q6h once stable (less hepatotoxicity)
Taper hydrocortisone over 3–5 days as pt improves
Treat precipitant: antibiotics if sepsis, address Med noncompliance
Refractory: consult nephrology for plasmapheresis or cholestyramine 4g PO QID (binds thyroid hormone)

pairs.value: TSH, free T4, free T3 (TSH undetectable, elevated fT4/fT3)
CBC, BMP (hypercalcemia common), Mg, LFTs (hepatic dysfunction), ABG/VBG, lactate
AFib/flutter(common), sinus tachycardia, ventricular arrhythmias
CXR (pulmonary edema, cardiomegaly). Head CT if AMS to exclude or causes.
BCxs (r/o sepsis as precipitant), cortisol level

drugs.name: PTU
drugs.notes: Preferred over methimazole in storm: also blocks peripheral T4, then T3 conversion. Check LFTs.

drugs.name: Propranolol
drugs.notes: Controls tachycardia & adrenergic sx. Also blocks T4, then T3. PO: 60–80 mg q4–6h.

drugs.name: Esmolol
drugs.notes: Ultrashort acting. Preferred if concerned ~betablocker tolerance.

drugs.name: Hydrocortisone
drugs.notes: Blocks T4, then T3 conversion. Prevents relative adrenal insufficiency. Taper over days.

drugs.name: SSKI (Iodine)
drugs.notes: Give ≥1h AFTER PTU: otherwise feeds new hormone synsis (WolffChaikoff effect).

drugs.name: Acetaminophen
drugs.notes: For fever. Avoid aspirin: displaces T4 from TBG, worsening free T4 levels.

pairs.key: ICU / immediate higher level of care if
pairs.value: persistent respiratory distress, high-flow or NIV need, hemodynamic instability, vasopressor need, severe arrhythmia, airway risk, neurologic decline, or a trajectory suggesting pt may crash on the floor.

sections.title: References & Resources
items: Ross DS, Burch HB, Cooper DS, et al. 2016 ATA Guidelines for Diagnosis and Management of Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis. Thyroid. 2016;26(10):13431421. https://doi.org/10.1089/thy.2016.0229

Akamizu T, Satoh T, Isozaki O, et al. Diagnostic Criteria, Clinical Features, and Incidence of Thyroid Storm Based on Nationwide Surveys. Thyroid. 2012;22(7):661679. https://doi.org/10.1089/thy.2011.0334

topics.title: Myxedema Coma
topics.subtitle: Hypothermia, bradycardia, AMS, hypoventilation, hyponatremia

sections.title: Acute Management
items: ABCs: check airway (macroglossia can complicate intubation), breathing (hypoventilation/hypercapnia common), circulation (↓ HR, refractory ↓ BP )
cardiac monitoring, pulse ox, frequent BP, temp monitoring
IV liothyronine (T3): 520 mcg IV load,then2.5-10 mcg IV q8h: save for critically ill pts requiring hemodynamic/vent support. D/C after 48h or if T3 levels ↑
IV dextrose for ↓ Glucose: follow serial glucose
Intubate if resp failure, severe hypercapnia, or unable to protect airway. Anticipate difficult airway (macroglossia, edema)
Shock may vasopressorrefractory until thyroid hormone is administered: for refractory shock, consider T3

sections.title: Chart Check / Hx.
items: Known hypothyroidism: check compliance with levothyroxine. Recent dose changes or discontinuation
Prior thyroidectomy, radioactive iodine ablation, h/o Hashimoto thyroiditis
Precipitants (most common = infection): infection/sepsis, cold exposure, med nonadherence, surgery/trauma, MI, stroke, GI bleed
meds that worsen: amiodarone, lithium, sedatives, opioids, anesthetics, BB, diuretics, checkpoint inhibitors, TKIs
Recent sedation, surgery, or anesthesia: common inpt trigger
Baseline mentation: decompensationpresents as hypoactive delirium, not true coma
Comorbidities: CAD (thyroid replacement can unmask ischemia), adrenal insufficiency, pituitary dz (central hypothyroidism)
Prior TSH/T4 values, last labs

sections.title: Exam
items: Classic triad: AMS + hypothermia + precipitating event
AMS (~90%): confusion, lethargy, obtundation —hypoactive delirium, not true coma despite name
Hypothermia (~7090%): may severe (<32°C). Absence of fever despite infection is classic: hypothyroidism masks sepsis
↓ HR (~70%): sinus brady, may refractory to atropine
↓ BP:refractory to fluids & vasopressors until thyroid hormone given
Hypoventilation: shallow respirations, ↑ PaCO2
Skin: dry, coarse, cool, nonpitting edema (myxedema), periorbital edema
Delayed DTRs (hungup reflexes): slow relaxation phase
Macroglossia, hoarse voice
Ileus (↓ bowel sounds, distension), urinary retention
Hair loss (lateral eyebrows), brittle nails

pairs.value: Extreme decompensated hypothyroidism with endorgan dysfunction. Endocrine emergency. 3060% mortality even with tx. Most pts are NOT truly comatose (misnomer)
Thyroid gl& failure (Hashimoto, postthyroidectomy, postRAI). ↑↑ TSH, ↓↓ free T4
Pituitary/hypothalamic cause. TSH may lowith nl./mildly ↑ despite low free T4. Can coexist with adrenal insufficiency ( must give steroids first)
Infection (most common), cold exposure, med nonadherence, surgery, MI, CVA, sedatives, opioids, amiodarone, lithium
Hypothyroidism may coexist with adrenal insufficiency (esp central). Giving thyroid hormone ↑ cortisol clearance: can precipitate adrenal crisis if cortisol is already low

sections.title: Hypothermia & Rewarming
items: Passive rewarming ONLY: warm blankets, ↑ room temp, warm IV fluids
Do NOT use active external rewarming (forced air, heating blankets at high temp): causes peripheral vasodilation: ↓ SVR: cardiovascular collapse in already hypotensive pt
Core temp may severely low (<32°C / 89.6°F): standard rmometers may not register
Use esophageal or rectal probe for accurate core temp
Goal: gradual rewarming ~0.5°C/h
Absence of fever does NOT r/o infection: hypothyroidism blunts febrile response. Treat empirically if any suspicion

pairs.value: Hashimoto thyroiditis (autoimmune, most common), postthyroidectomy, postradioactive iodine, radiation to neck, drugs (amiodarone, lithium, checkpoint inhibitors, TKIs)
Pituitary adenoma, Sheehan syndrome, pituitary surgery/radiation, infiltrative dz, TBI. TSH unreliable: check free T4
Sepsis, adrenal crisis, hypothermia (primary exposure), ↓ Glucose, drug OD (opioids, sedatives), stroke, CO poisoning
Adrenal crisis (check cortisol), severe sepsis, betablocker/CCB OD, ↑K, complete heart block, PE
Adrenal insufficiency (Schmidt syndrome: autoimmune thyroid + adrenal), or autoimmune disease (vitiligo, pernicious anemia, T1DM), acquired von Willebrand syndrome

sections.title: Management
items: Give stressdose steroids BEFORE or concurrent with thyroid hormone: hydrocortisone 50mg IV q6h (or 100mg IV q8h). Giving T4 without steroids can precipitate adrenal crisis
IV levothyroxine (T4): loading dose 200400 mcg IV push (lower dose for elderly, low body weight, cardiac dz).then1.2 mcg/kg/day IV or 100mcg/day IV
IV route only: GI absorption unreliable in decompensated state (ileus, edema)
Passive rewarming ONLY: warm blankets, warm room. Avoid active external rewarming (can cause vasodilation: cardiovascular collapse)
IVF cautiously: pts may volumedepleted, but hyponatremia is common (dilutional from ↑ ADH). Avoid hypotonic fluids
Serial TSH, free T4, T3 q12 days: TSH ↓ ~50%/wk, free T4 normalize within 4 days
Vitals improvement expected 2448h. Mentation may take days to weeks to normalize
Identify & treat precipitant: infection is most common but may masked (no fever, no leukocytosis). Low threshold for broad spectrum abx
Aggressive bowel regimen: ↓ motility, ileus risk
Stress ulcer prophylaxis
Avoid sedatives, opioids: can worsen/trigger decompensation
Monitor for cardiac ischemia during thyroid replacement: esp in elderly/CAD pts. EKG changes, troponin
Transition IV T4: PO levothyroxine when GI function returns & pt improving
Taper hydrocortisone once adrenal insufficiency excluded (cortisol, ACTH stim test)
DVT prophylaxis: immobile pts
Endocrinology consult for all pts: long term thyroid management

pairs.value: Markedly ↑↑ in primary hypothyroidism. May be lowith nl. in central hypothyroidism: do NOT rely on TSH alone
Very low or undetectable. Key diagnostic test
Often low. Useful for monitoring T3 replacement therapy
Draw BEFORE giving steroids. r/o concomitant adrenal insufficiency. If low: supports giving stressdose steroids
Hyponatremia (~50%: dilutional from ↑ ADH). ↓ Glucose. ↑ Cr (↓ renal perfusion)
Anemia (often macrocytic). Infection may NOT cause leukocytosis in myxedema
Check even without apparent resp distress: hypoventilation/hypercapnia common. Respiratory acidosis
Often ↑ (hypothyroid myopathy, Rhabdo)
Markedly ↑ cholesterol/TG (chronic hypothyroidism)
r/o infection as precipitant: remember fever may absent
Sinus ↓ HR, low voltage, prolonged QT, ± pericardial effusion (low voltage + electrical alternans)
TSH, free T4, T3 q12 days. TSH ↓ ~50%/wk. Free T4 normalize within 4 days

drugs.name: Hydrocortisone
drugs.notes: Give BEFORE or concurrent with thyroid hormone. Can precipitate adrenal crisis if omitted. D/C once adrenal insufficiency r/o (nl. cortisol, ACTH stim test)

drugs.name: Levothyroxine (T4) IV
drugs.dose: Loading: 200400 mcg IV push. Maintenance: 100 mcg/day IV (or 1.2 mcg/kg/day)
drugs.contraindications: Acute MI (relative: use lower dose 100200mcg), severe CAD (risk of ischemia/arrhythmia)
drugs.notes: Lower loading dose (100200mcg) in elderly, low body weight, cardiac dz. IV only: GI absorption unreliable. Improvement in vitals expected 2448h

drugs.name: Liothyronine (T3) IV
drugs.dose: Loading: 520 mcg IV. Maintenance: 2.5-10 mcg IV q8h
drugs.contraindications: Acute MI, unstable angina (relative: ↑ myocardial O2 demand)
drugs.notes: save for critically ill (hemodynamic instability, vent dependence, no improvement with T4 alone). More rapid onset than T4. D/C after 48h or if T3 levels ↑. Higher arrhythmia risk

drugs.name: Thiamine
drugs.notes: Empiric: malnourished/chronically ill pts at Wernicke risk. Give before or with dextrose

drugs.name: Desmopressin (DDAVP)
drugs.notes: Consider if hemorrhage: acquired von Willebrand syndrome can occur in severe hypothyroidism

pairs.key: ICU / immediate higher level of care if
pairs.value: persistent respiratory distress, high-flow or NIV need, hemodynamic instability, vasopressor need, severe arrhythmia, airway risk, neurologic decline, or a trajectory suggesting pt may crash on the floor.

sections.title: References & Resources
items: Jonklaas J, Bianco AC, Bauer AJ, et al. Guidelines for the Treatment of Hypothyroidism: ATA/AACE Task Force. Thyroid. 2014;24(12):16701751. https://doi.org/10.1089/thy.2014.0028
Wall CR. Myxedema Coma: Diagnosis and Treatment. American Family Physician. 2000;62(11):24852490.
Wiersinga WM. Myxedema and Coma (Severe Hypothyroidism). Endotext [Internet]. 2018. https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK279007/

topics.title: Acute renal failure/ESRD
topics.subtitle: Rising creatinine, oliguria, volume issues, electrolyte derangements

sections.title: Acute Management
items: Airway: check patency; severe uremia or volume overload causes AMS or pulmonary edema: may need intubation
Breathing: SpO2, RR; if pulmonary edema (crackles, ↑ WOB): sit upright, O2, consider NIPPV or IV furosemide
Circulation: check volume status: hypovolemic (prerenal) vs euvolemic vs hypervolemic (cardiorenal)
Check K+: ↑K is most now life-threatening complication: ECG, treat if K+ >5.5 or EKG changes
If ↑K with EKG changes: calcium gluconate 3 g IV: insulin 10 units + D50: albuterol nebs
Foley catheter: r/o urinary obstruction (postrenal); check bladder volume with bladder scan or POCUS

pairs.value: Cr ↑ >0.3 mg/dL within 48h OR 1.51.9x baseline within 7 days OR UO <0.5 mL/kg/h for 612h
Cr 2.02.9x baseline OR UO <0.5 mL/kg/h for >12h
Cr >3x baseline OR Cr >4 mg/dL OR RRT initiation OR UO <0.3 mL/kg/h for >24h or anuria >12h
pH <7.1 refractory to medical management
↑K refractory to medical tx
Dialyzable toxins: lithium, methanol, ethylene glycol, ASA, metformin
Volume overload refractory to diuretics
Pericarditis, encephalopathy, bleeding, BUN >100 with sx
Volume depletion, heart failure, hepatorenal syndrome, renal artery stenosis. FENa <1%, BUN/Cr >20
Ischemia, nephrotoxins (contrast, aminoglycosides, NSAIDs, myoglobin). Muddy brown casts. FENa >2%
RBC casts, proteinuria, HTN. ANA, ANCA, antiGBM, complement levels
Drugs (PCNs, NSAIDs, PPIs), WBC casts, eosinophiluria, rash, fever
Obst.: check bladder scan, renal ultrasound. BPH, stones, pelvic malignancy.

sections.title: Management
items: If hypovolemic: IV crystalloid bolus; if hypervolemic: hold fluids, consider diuresis
Urgent dialysis indications (AEIOU): Acidosis (refractory), Electrolytes (refractory hyperK), Ingestion (toxic), Overload (refractory pulmonary edema), Uremia (encephalopathy, pericarditis, bleeding)
Hold nephrotoxins: NSAIDs, ACEi/ARBs, aminoglycosides, contrast; dose adjust renally cleared meds

sections.title: Workup
items: BMP, CBC, urinalysis with microscopy, urine electrolytes (Na, Cr for FENa)
Bladder scan: r/o Obst. first
Renal ultrasound: hydronephrosis, kidney size
Review meds: nephrotoxins, ACE/ARB, NSAIDs, contrast
Urine protein/creatinine ratio if nephrotic/nephritic syndrome suspected
ANA, ANCA, antiGBM, complement (C3/C4), SPEP if glomerulonephritis suspected

sections.title: References & Resources
items: Kidney Disease: Improving Global Outcomes (KDIGO). KDIGO Clinical Practice Guideline for Acute Kidney Injury. Kidney International Supplements. 2012;2(1):1138. https://kdigo.org/guidelines/acute-kidney-injury/
Bagshaw SM, Darmon M, Bhagwanjee S, et al. Clinical Review: Prevention of Acute Kidney Injury: AKICLINI Update. Critical Care. 2024;28:55.

Gaudry S, Hajage D, Schortgen F, et al. Initiation Strategies for RenalReplacement Therapy in the Intensive Care Unit (AKIKI trial). New England Journal of Medicine. 2016;375(2):122133. https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1603017

topics.title: ↑K
topics.subtitle: Weakness, peaked T waves, bradyarrhythmia, widened QRS, renal failure

sections.title: Acute Management
items: Airway: check patency and protect. Breathing: check WOB, oxygenation, and ventilation. Circulation: check pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
ABCs: telemetry, IV access, stat ECG, repeat BMP to confirm unless arresting or classic ECG changes
If ECG changes or K ≥6.5: give IV calcium now for membrane stabilization
Shift K intracellularly: regular insulin IV + dextrose, high dose albuterol, add sodium bicarb if significant metabolic acidosis
Remove K from body: loop diuretic if making urine, potassium binder for nonimmediate removal, hold all exogenous K
Call nephrology early for dialysis if refractory, recurrent, severe AKI/CKD, or anuric

sections.title: Chart Check / Hx.
items: Renal Hx.: AKI, CKD, dialysis dependence, missed dialysis, oliguria/anuria
Meds: ACEi/ARB, spironolactone/eplerenone, amiloride, trimethoprim, NSAIDs, heparin, tacrolimus/cyclosporine, BB, digoxin toxicity
Recent potassium intake: supplements, salt substitutes, tube feeds, TPN, large transfusion burden
Cell breakdown: Rhabdo, tumor lysis, hemolysis, crush injury, burns, seizure
Acidbase / endocrine: DKA, metabolic acidosis, adrenal insufficiency
Ask whether sample was hemolyzed or drawn from line running Kcontaining fluids: pseudohyperkalemia is common

sections.title: Exam
items: Cardiac: bradycardia, irregular rhythm, hypotension, poor perfusion
Neuro / muscular: weakness, paresthesias, ascending flaccid paralysis in severe cases
Volume status: overload (JVD, crackles, edema) vs dehydration / DKA
Signs of underlying cause: muscle tenderness / dark urine (Rhabdo), fistula / dialysis cath, hyperpigmentation or shock (adrenal crisis)
Remember: severity of ECG toxicity does not always correlate tightly with serum K

pairs.value: K >5.05.5 mEq/L depending on lab. Mild: 5.55.9. Moderate: 6.06.4. Severe: ≥6.5 or any ECG change
Falsely elevated K from hemolysis, thrombocytosis, severe leukocytosis, prolonged tourniquet time, fist clenching, or traumatic draw. Always confirm if story / ECGdon't fit
Peaked T waves: PR prolongation / Pwave flattening: QRS widening: sine wave: VT/VF / asystole
Calcium stabilizes the myocardium but does NOT lower K. Insulin / albuterol shift K temporarily. Definitive tx requires K removal or dialysis
AKI, CKD, hypoaldosteronism, adrenal insufficiency, type 4 RTA, obstructive uropathy
ACEi/ARB, potassiumsparing diuretics, NSAIDs, trimethoprim, calcineurin inhibitors, heparin
Metabolic acidosis, DKA, insulin deficiency, hyperosmolar states, betablocker effect, digoxin toxicity, succinylcholine
Rhabdo, tumor lysis syndrome, hemolysis, burns, crush injury, transfusions, excessive supplements / salt substitutes
Hemolyzed sample or severe thrombocytosis / leukocytosis: repeat BMP / plasma K before chasing if pt is stable
Opposite ECG pattern: U waves, flattened T waves, QT prolongation. Differentiate on BMP.

sections.title: Management
items: Membrane stabilization: calcium gluconate 23g IV peripheral or calcium chloride 1g IV central. Repeat in 5-10 min if QRS remains wide / ECG unchanged
Shift K: regular insulin 10 units IV + dextrose 25g IV. Consider dextrose even if glucose is normal; monitor glucose q1h for at least 46h
Add albuterol 1020mg neb: synergistic with insulin, but may less effective in pts on BB or ESRD
Give sodium bicarb if significant metabolic acidosis (best when acidemic; weak as sole thetherapy)
Remove K: furosemide if making urine, stop Kcontaining fluids / meds, use binder only if no ileus / bowel ischemia & not relying on immediate effect
Dialysis for refractory hyperK, recurrent rebound after temporizing measures, severe renal failure, or anuria / missed dialysis
Recheck K 1h after temporizing thetherapy, then q24h untilimproving
Repeat ECG after calcium / insulin if initial tracing was abnormal
Expect rebound hyperkalemia after insulin wears off if K has not been removed
Hold ACEi/ARB, spironolactone, trimethoprim, NSAIDs, K supplements, TPN K, & K in maintenance fluids
Treat underlying cause: dialysis, relieve obstruction, treat DKA / acidosis, manage Rhabdo or tumor lysis
Review chronic prevention plan before discharge: diet, dialysis adherence, med changes, outpt lab follow up

pairs.value: BMP / CMP, repeat K if hemolysis suspected, Mg, Phos, creatinine, glucose, ECG
ABG / VBG, lactate, cardiac monitoring
Review med list, check urine output, UA, CK if Rhabdo, uric acid / phosphate if tumor lysis, cortisol if adrenal insufficiency suspected
Fingerstick glucose q1h for 46h after IV insulin; BMP q24h during active tx
Missed dialysis + ECG changes / severe hyperK = call nephrology now,don't wait for binders to work

drugs.name: Calcium gluconate
drugs.notes: Preferred for peripheral administration. Membrane stabilization only. Onset 13 min, duration ~3060 min. Repeat if ECG changes persist.

drugs.name: Calcium chloride
drugs.notes: More elemental calcium than gluconate. Prefer central line or code situation.

drugs.name: Regular insulin
drugs.notes: Give with dextrose unless markedly hyperglycemic. Lowers K by ~0.61.0 mEq/L. Watch for delayed hypoglycemia, esp in CKD.

drugs.name: Dextrose 50%
drugs.notes: Give with insulin. Consider more dextrose infusion if baseline glucose <150 or recurrent hypoglycemia risk.

drugs.name: Albuterol
drugs.notes: High dose required for K shift. Lowers K by ~0.51.0 mEq/L. May cause tachycardia / tremor.

drugs.name: Sodium bicarbonate
drugs.notes: Best adjunct when metabolic acidosis is present. Less reliable as isolated hyperK thetherapy.

drugs.name: Furosemide
drugs.notes: Promotes renal K excretion if pt has urine output. Follow urine output & volume status.

drugs.name: Sodium zirconium cyclosilicate
drugs.notes: Potassium binder with onset in hours. Useful for control, not cardiac rescue.

drugs.name: Patiromer
drugs.notes: GI K binder. Onset hours: not for immediate emergencies. Separate from other oral meds.

pairs.key: ICU / immediate higher level of care if
pairs.value: persistent respiratory distress, high-flow or NIV need, hemodynamic instability, vasopressor need, severe arrhythmia, airway risk, neurologic decline, or a trajectory suggesting pt may crash on the floor.

sections.title: References & Resources
items: Clase CM, Carrero JJ, Ellison DH, et al. Potassium Homeostasis and Management of Dyskalemia in Kidney Diseases: Conclusions from a KDIGO Controversies Conference. Kidney International. 2020;97(1):4261. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.kint.2019.09.018
Palmer BF, Clegg DJ. Diagnosis and Treatment of Hyperkalemia. Cleveland Clinic Journal of Medicine. 2017;84(12):934942. https://doi.org/10.3949/ccjm.84a.17056
Long B, Warix JR, Koyfman A. Controversies in Management of Hyperkalemia. Journal of Emergency Medicine. 2018;55(2):192205. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jemermed.2018.04.004

topics.title: Hypokalemia
topics.subtitle: Weakness, ileus, PVCs, U waves, low serum potassium

sections.title: Acute Management
items: Airway: check patency and protect. Breathing: check WOB, oxygenation, and ventilation. Circulation: check pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
ABCs: Place on telemetry if K <3.0 or symptomatic
Stat ECG: check for U waves, QT prolongation, arrhythmia
**Severe (<2.5) or symptomatic:** KCl 20–40 mEq/h IV via central line + cardiac monitoring. Recheck BMP q2h.

sections.title: Chart Check / Hx.
items: Diuretic use: loops (furosemide), thiazides (most common Meds cause)
GI losses: vomiting, diarrhea, NG suction, laxative use
Insulin administration (transcellular shift)
Betaagonist use (albuterol nebulizers: shifts K intracellularly)
Digoxin use: hypokalemia potentiates digoxin toxicity
Renal Hx., dialysis status, acidbase disturbances

sections.title: Exam
items: Muscle weakness: esp proximal (difficulty standing from chair)
↓ deep tendon reflexes (hyporeflexia)
Abd distension, ↓ bowel sounds (ileus)
Cardiac rhythm: irregular rhythm if arrhythmia
Respiratory effort: severe hypoK causes respiratory muscle weakness

pairs.value: Often asymptomatic. Fatigue, muscle cramps, constipation
Muscle weakness, myalgia, ileus, ECG changes (U waves, flattened T waves)
Paralysis, Rhabdo, arrhythmias (VT/VF, torsades), respiratory failure
Hypokalemia is refractory if Mg is low: always check & replace Mg first
Vomiting, diarrhea, NG suction, laxative abuse, villous adenoma
Diuretics (loops, thiazides), RTA (type 1 & 2), hyperaldosteronism, Mg depletion
Insulin, betaagonists (albuterol), alkalosis, refeeding syndrome, hypothermia
Malnutrition, alcoholism, anorexia nervosa
Cushing's, Bartter/Gitelman syndrome, amphotericin B, cisplatin
Opposite presentation: peaked T waves, widened QRS. Differentiate on BMP.

sections.title: Management
items: Check & replace Mg first: give MgSO4 2g IV if Mg <2.0
**Mild (3.0–3.5):** KCl 40 mEq PO. Recheck in 4h.
**Moderate (2.5–3.0):** KCl 40 mEq PO + 10–20 mEq/h IV. Place on tele.
Max peripheral IV rate: 10 mEq/h (higher rates cause phlebitis: use central line)
Each 10 mEq IV KCl raises serum K by ~0.1 mEq/L
Continue replacement until K >4.0
Recheck BMP q2–4h during active replacement until K >3.5
Start PO replacement once K >3.0 & tolerating PO
Txt underlying cause: hold diuretic, treat GI losses
For pts on digoxin: maintain K >4.0 (hypoK potentiates dig toxicity)
Consider Ksparing agent (spironolactone, amiloride) if diuretic induced
Dietary counseling: potassiumrich foods (bananas, oranges, potatoes, spinach)

pairs.value: BMP (K, Cr), Mg (always check: hypoK refractory if Mg low), ECG
Flattened T waves: ST depression: prominent U waves: QT prolongation: torsades/VT/VF if K <2.5
Urine K (>20 mEq/L = renal loss, <20 = extrarenal), urine Cl, ABG/VBG, phos, calcium
Urine K/Cr ratio, plasma renin/aldosterone (hyperaldosteronism), TSH, cortisol

drugs.name: KCl (oral)
drugs.notes: Preferred for mildmoderate. Take with food to reduce GI upset.

drugs.name: KCl (IV)
drugs.dose: 10–20 mEq/h peripheral, up to 40 mEq/h central
drugs.notes: Max 10 mEq/h peripherally. Central line for higher rates. Always on tele.

drugs.name: MgSO4
drugs.notes: Replace Mg first: hypoK refractory if Mg low. Target Mg >2.0.

drugs.name: Spironolactone
drugs.notes: Ksparing diuretic. For chronic hypoK from diuretic use or hyperaldosteronism.

pairs.key: ICU / immediate higher level of care if
pairs.value: persistent respiratory distress, high-flow or NIV need, hemodynamic instability, vasopressor need, severe arrhythmia, airway risk, neurologic decline, or a trajectory suggesting pt may crash on the floor.

sections.title: References & Resources
items: Clase CM, Carrero JJ, Ellison DH, et al. Potassium Homeostasis and Management of Dyskalemia in Kidney Diseases: Conclusions from a KDIGO Controversies Conference. Kidney International. 2020;97(1):4261. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.kint.2019.09.018
Unwin RJ, Luft FC, Shirley DG. Pathophysiology and Management of Hypokalemia: A Clinical Perspective. Nature Reviews Nephrology. 2011;7(2):7584. https://doi.org/10.1038/nrneph.2010.175

topics.title: Hyponatremia
topics.subtitle: Confusion, seizures, nausea, edema or hypovolemia, low sodium

sections.title: Acute Management
items: Airway: check patency and protect. Breathing: check WOB, oxygenation, and ventilation. Circulation: check pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
check symptom severity first: sx guide urgency, not number alone
Mild/asymptomatic: start workup, treat underlying cause, no emergent hypertonic saline needed
If overcorrecting: stop saline, give D5W 610 mL/kg IV over 12h, give DDAVP 2mcg IV (clamp). Goal: re lower Na back to safe correction rate

sections.title: Chart Check / Hx.
items: Acuity: acute (<48h: marathon runners, psychogenic polydipsia, postop, ecstasy) vs chronic (>48h: most inpts). Acute can corrected faster; chronic = high ODS risk
Meds: thiazides (most common drug cause), SSRIs, carbamazepine, oxcarbazepine, NSAIDs, desmopressin, cyclophosphamide, sulfonylureas, bupropion, opioids
Volume losses: vomiting, diarrhea, diuretics, poor PO, NG suction, burns
Volume overload: CHF, cirrhosis, nephrotic syndrome, renal failure
Pulm/CNS dz: PNA, lung cancer (SIAD), brain tumor, SAH, meningitis, postop state
Endocrine: hypothyroidism, adrenal insufficiency ( must r/o: both cause hyponatremia & mimic SIAD)
Alcohol use, malnutrition, liver dz: ↑ ODS risk
Baseline Na trend: how fast did it drop? Prior Na values critical for determining chronicity
Recent IVF: hypotonic fluids (D5W, 0.45% NS) worsens hyponatremia

sections.title: Exam
items: Symptom severity determines urgency:
Severe: seizures, obtundation/coma (GCS <8), cardiorespiratory distress, vomiting
Moderate: confusion, headache, nausea without vomiting
Mild: gait disturbance, falls, concentration difficulty, cognitive deficits
Sxemerge below Na ~120 mEq/L, but depend on rate of decline
Volume status assessment Narrows DDx:
Hypovolemic: dry mucous membranes, ↓ skin turgor, tachycardia, orthostasis, flat JVP
Euvolemic: clinically nl. volume: think SIAD, hypothyroid, adrenal insufficiency
Hypervolemic: edema, JVD, crackles, ascites: think CHF, cirrhosis, nephrotic syndrome, renal failure
Neuro exam: mentation, focal deficits (r/o CNS cause), seizure activity

pairs.value: Na <135 mEq/L. Mild: 130134. Moderate: 125129. Severe: <125
Osm = 2×Na + BUN/2.8 + Glucose/18. nl.: 275295 mOsm/kg. Hypotonic (<275) = true hyponatremia
Isotonic (275295): lab artifact from hyperlipidemia or hyperproteinemia. Not true hyponatremia
Osm >295: hyperglycemia or mannitol. Correct Na: add 2.4 mEq/L per 100 mg/dL glucose >100
Euvolemic hypotonic hyponatremia with UOsm >100, UNa >2030, nl. renal/adrenal/thyroid function. Dx of exclusion
Central pontine myelinolysis. Overly rapid Na correction. Dysarthria, dysphagia, quadriparesis, lockedin syndrome. Irreversible. Risk ↑ with Na <120, alcoholism, malnutrition, hypoK, cirrhosis
DDAVP 2mcg IV q8h: prevents uncontrolled aquaresis by locking ADH 'on'. Allows controlled Na correction with concurrent 3% NaCl. Used when autocorrection risk is high

sections.title: Correction Rate & ODS Prevention
items: Max correction rates: ≤10 mEq/L in first 24h, ≤8 mEq/L per 24h reafter, until Na ≥130
K correction counts toward Na correction: every 1 mEq K repleted raises Na ~1 mEq/L. Factor this in
High ODS risk groups (aim for even slower correction, ≤68 mEq/24h): Na <105, chronic hyponatremia, alcoholism, malnutrition, hypokalemia, cirrhosis, burns
Autocorrection risk: when cause of hyponatremia resolves (e.g., hypovolemia corrected with IVF, drug stopped), ADH drops: sudden massive free water excretion: rapid Na rise
DDAVP clamp strategy: if autocorrection risk is high, start DDAVP 2mcg IV q8h proactively + restrict free water to <1L/day + controlled 3% NaCl if needed
Monitor Na q12h after intervention,thenq46h. Do NOT setandforget
If Na overcorrects: STOP all saline. Give D5W 610 mL/kg over 12h. Give DDAVP 2mcg IV. Recheck Na q12h. Goal: relower Na back to safe trajectory
ODS sx appear 26 days after overcorrection: dysarthria, dysphagia, quadriparesis, AMS, lockedin syndrome. MRI may delayed. May be irreversible
Give thiamine 100mg IV daily in pts with alcohol use disorder or malnutrition risk

pairs.value: Hypotonic (<275) = true hyponatremia: proceed. Isotonic (275295) = pseudohyponatremia. Hypertonic (>295) = dilutional (hyperglycemia, mannitol)
<100 mOsm = dilute urine, ADH suppressed (psychogenic polydipsia, beer potomania, low solute intake, reset osmostat). >100 mOsm = ADH present: step 3
<2030 mEq/L = RAAS activated: hypovolemic (GI losses, 3rdspacing) or hypervolemic (CHF, cirrhosis). >2030 mEq/L = SIAD, adrenal insufficiency, hypothyroidism, renal failure, diuretics
GI losses (vomiting, diarrhea), 3rdspacing (pancreatitis, burns), poor PO intake
Thiazides, cerebral salt wasting, RTA, mineralocorticoid deficiency, osmotic diuresis
SIAD (most common inpt cause), hypothyroidism, adrenal insufficiency, psychogenic polydipsia (UOsm <100), drugs (SSRIs, carbamazepine)
CHF, cirrhosis, nephrotic syndrome
Renal failure (CKD, AKI)
Adrenal insufficiency (random cortisol, ACTH stim) & hypothyroidism (TSH): both mimic SIAD but require specific tx

sections.title: Management
items: Severe sx (seizures, coma, resp distress): 3% NaCl 150mL IV bolus over 20 min: can repeat q20min up to 34 times. Each 100mL raises Na ~2 mEq/L. Goal: ↑ Na by 46 mEq/L to stop sx
Moderate sx (confusion, headache, nausea): 3% NaCl 150mL IV over 20 min x1. Recheck Na at 1h. May repeat if no improvement
Correction rate limits: ≤10 mEq/L in first 24h, ≤8 mEq/L per 24h reafter. Overcorrection: osmotic demyelination syndrome (ODS)
Check Na q12h after hypertonic saline bolus,thenq46h during correction
DDAVP clamp (2mcg IV q8h): consider if baseline Na <120 & cause likely to autocorrect (resolved hypovolemia, water intoxication, reversible SIAD). Prevents uncontrolled free water excretion
Identify & treat underlying cause: volume status, meds, infection, adrenal insufficiency, hypothyroidism

sections.title: SIAD (SIADH) Management
items: Dx of exclusion: hypotonic hyponatremia, UOsm >100, UNa >2030, euvolemic, nl. renal function (GFR >2025), adrenal & thyroid function nl.
Common causes: pulm dz (PNA, cancer, COPD), CNS dz (SAH, tumor, meningitis, stroke), drugs (SSRIs, carbamazepine, oxcarbazepine, opioids, cyclophosphamide, MDMA), pain, nausea, postop state
Treat underlying cause first: stop offending drug, treat infection, etc.
Fluid restriction: 5001000 mL/day (first line but compliance is poor, esp in inpts receiving IV meds/nutrition)
Oral urea 1560g/day: emerging as preferred tx. Osmotic diuresis. Mix with juice
Salt tabs 13g PO TID with meals: less potent alternative
Furosemide 20mg q6h + salt tabs/urea: ↑ free water excretion
Avoid NS in SIAD: if UOsm > NS osmolality (308 mOsm), NS worsens hyponatremia (kidney retains water, excretes Na)
Vaptans (tolvaptan): unpredictable aquaresis, overcorrection risk, hepatotoxicity. Not suggested in most ICU settings
SGLT2 inhibitors (empagliflozin 25mg/day): emerging option for chronic SIAD. Promotes osmotic diuresis

pairs.value: Na, K ( hypoK ↑ ODS risk: correct K simultaneously), glucose (correct Na for hyperglycemia), BUN/Cr
First step. Hypotonic (<275) = true hyponatremia. If not hypotonic: pseudohyponatremia or hypertonic
<100 = ADH suppressed (polydipsia, beer potomania). >100 = ADH active: check UNa
<2030 = effective volume depletion or edematous state. >2030 = SIAD, diuretics, adrenal insufficiency, renal dz
r/o hypothyroidism as cause
r/o adrenal insufficiency: critical before labeling as SIAD
Low in SIAD (↑ uric acid excretion). Elevated in hypovolemia
If cirrhosis suspected
Lung pathology causing SIAD (PNA, cancer, TB)
If CNS cause suspected (tumor, SAH, meningitis) or if AMS without clear cause
q12h after hypertonic saline,thenq46h during correction. Trend is everything

drugs.name: 3% NaCl (hypertonic saline)
drugs.dose: 150mL IV bolus over 20 min. May repeat q20min x34 for severe sx. Each 100mL ↑ Na ~2 mEq/L
drugs.route: IV (peripheral OK for boluses; central for prolonged infusion)
drugs.contraindications: Hypervolemic hyponatremia (relative: use cautiously with diuretics in CHF/cirrhosis)
drugs.notes: Only for symptomatic hyponatremia. Goal: ↑ Na 46 mEq/L to resolve acute sx. Do NOT target nl. Na. Recheck Na after each bolus

drugs.name: DDAVP (desmopressin)
drugs.notes: DDAVP clamp: prevents overcorrection by locking ADH on. Use if Na <120 & cause can autocorrect. Also use to relower Na if overcorrecting (with D5W)

drugs.name: D5W (5% dextrose in water)
drugs.notes: For overcorrection rescue: free water to relower Na. Use with DDAVP. Goal: bring Na back down to safe correction trajectory

drugs.name: Oral urea
drugs.dose: 1560 g/day PO (start ~30g daily, mix with juice to mask taste)
drugs.contraindications: Hepatic encephalopathy, severe hepatic failure, GI intolerance
drugs.notes: Emerging first line for chronic SIAD. Osmotic diuretic: promotes free water excretion. Better tolerated than extreme fluid restriction

drugs.name: nl. saline (0.9% NaCl)
drugs.notes: For hypovolemic hyponatremia only. Corrects volume depletion: turns off ADH: Na rises. Caution: worsens SIAD (Na in NS < UOsm: net free water retention)

drugs.name: Furosemide
drugs.notes: For hypervolemic hyponatremia (CHF, cirrhosis): promotes free water excretion. Can combine with salt tabs or oral urea in SIAD. Replace K aggressively

drugs.name: KCl (potassium chloride)
drugs.notes: Correct K simultaneously: K repletion raises Na (K is osmotically active). HypoK ↑ ODS risk. Every 1 mEq K given ≈ raises Na by ~1 mEq (counts toward correction limit)

pairs.key: ICU / immediate higher level of care if
pairs.value: persistent respiratory distress, high-flow or NIV need, hemodynamic instability, vasopressor need, severe arrhythmia, airway risk, neurologic decline, or a trajectory suggesting pt may crash on the floor.

sections.title: References & Resources
items: Spasovski G, Vanholder R, Allolio B, et al. Clinical Practice Guideline on Diagnosis and Treatment of Hyponatraemia. European Journal of Endocrinology. 2014;170(3):G1G47. https://doi.org/10.1530/EJE-13-1020

Verbalis JG, Goldsmith SR, Greenberg A, et al. Diagnosis, Evaluation, and Treatment of Hyponatremia: Expert Panel Recommendations. American Journal of Medicine. 2013;126(10 Suppl 1):S1S42. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.amjmed.2013.07.006
Sterns RH. Disorders of Plasma Sodium — Causes, Consequences, and Correction. New England Journal of Medicine. 2015;372(1):5565. https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMra1404489

topics.title: Hypercalcemia
topics.subtitle: AMS, constipation, polyuria, dehydration, shortened QT, elevated calcium

sections.title: Acute Management
items: Airway: check patency and protect. Breathing: check WOB, oxygenation, and ventilation. Circulation: check pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
Place on cardiac telemetry if Ca >14 mg/dL: risk of fatal arrhythmias
Monitor I&Os: watch for volume overload in pts with CHF or renal failure
Aggressive Fluids (NS): 200300 mL/h UOP (target 36L in first 24h)

sections.title: Chart Check / Hx.
items: Hx Cancer?: SCC, breast, RCC, MM, lymphoma (occurs in 2030% of cancer pts)
S/S: AMS, lethargy, polyuria, polydipsia, constipation, N/V
Bone pain (osteolytic metastases)
Meds: thiazides, lithium, Ca/Vit D supplements, antacids
Volume status: most pts are significantly dehydrated
Cardiac Hx: arrhythmia risk with severe hypercalcemia
Renal Fxn: affects bisphosphonate choice & dosing
Prior Ep. of hypercalcemia? Txt used?

sections.title: Exam
items: Mentation: AMS, lethargy, obtundation/coma (severe)
Volume assessment
Abd exam: distension (ileus)
EKG: short QTc
MSK: proximal weakness, hyporeflexia
Bone tenderness at metastatic sites
Psych: psychosis, depression, personality changes

pairs.value: Most common outpt cause. PTH inappropriately nl. or elevated; lownl. phosphorus. Usually adenoma, less commonly hyperplasia or MEN syndrome.
Most common inpt cause. SCC (lung, head/neck), renal cell, bladder, breast, ovarian cancers. PTH suppressed; PTHrP elevated.
Breast cancer, multiple myeloma, bony metastases: direct bone resorption with high calcium &bone pain.
Lymphoma & some granulomatous or hematologic processes increase 1,25OH vitamin D.
Vitamin D intoxication, excessive calcitriol, granulomatous disease (sarcoid, TB, fungal disease): increased GI calcium absorption.
Thiazides, lithium, calcium carbonate or vitamin A excess, Ca/Vit D supplements, teriparatide; review OTCs & antacids.
Hyperthyroidism, adrenal insufficiency, pheochromocytoma;milder than malignancy or primary hyperPTH.
Immobilization, Paget disease, rhabdo recovery, advanced CKD/transplantrelated hypercalcemia, milkalkali syndrome.
Lifelong mild hypercalcemia with low urine calcium; benign &mistaken for primary hyperparathyroidism.
High albumin or dehydration can raise total calcium; confirm with corrected calcium or ionized calcium.

sections.title: Management
items: Start aggressive NS and track UOP; use loop diuretic only after volume is restored
Give calcitonin 4 units/kg SC or IV q12h for rapid but shortterm Ca lowering
Add antiresorptive txt: zoledronic acid 4mg IV preferred; use denosumab if bisphosphonaterefractory or renal impairment
If calcitriolmed. cause is likely, add prednisone; correct Mg/K and stop Ca/vit D, thiazides, and lithium
Treat the cause in parallel: malignancy, hyperPTH, granulomatous dz, or meds
Aggressive IV hydration: titrate to UOP 200300 mL/h (watch for retention; overload)
Repeat iCal q68h until trending down & <12 mg/dL
Continue calcitonin q12h for first 48h (stop if Ca returns to normal)(bridge to bisphosphonate effect)
Recheck Cal 24 days after bisphosphonate: peak effect at 47 days
Correct hypomagnesemia (Mg <2.0) & hypokalemia as needed
Monitor renal function daily: saline infusion + bisphosphonates can affect kidneys
Definitive tx: tumordirected therapy (chemo, radiation, surgery)
Hypercalcemia of malignancy suggests poor prognosis: median survival 36 months Discuss goals of care

pairs.value: Most accurate: not affected by albumin. >5.6 mg/dL confirms hypercalcemia
Should be suppressed (<20 pg/mL) in malignancy. If elevated: primary hyperparathyroidism
Elevated in hypercalcemia of malignancy
check renal function, coelectrolyte abnormalities
Shortened QT, ST changes, Osborn waves (severe). Telemetry if Ca >14
Elevated 1,25OH in calcitriolmed. (lymphoma)

drugs.name: nl. saline
drugs.notes: Firstline tx. Restores intravascular volume & promotes renal calcium excretion. Target 36L in 24h.

drugs.name: Calcitonin (salmon)
drugs.notes: Fastest onset (24h). Lowers Ca by 12 mg/dL. Tachyphylaxis develops in 4872h: bridge to bisphosphonates.

drugs.name: Zoledronic acid
drugs.notes: Preferred bisphosphonate. Onset 24 days, duration 24 weeks. More effective & faster than pamidronate.

drugs.name: Pamidronate
drugs.notes: Alternative bisphosphonate. 90mg dose for Ca >13.5. Slower infusion needed for renal protection.

drugs.name: Denosumab
drugs.notes: For bisphosphonaterefractory hypercalcemia or renal impairment. Onset 410 days.

drugs.name: Prednisone
drugs.notes: Effective for calcitriolmed. hypercalcemia: lymphoma, myeloma, granulomatous disease.

drugs.name: Furosemide
drugs.notes: Only AFTER adequate volume resuscitation. Promotes calciuresis. Do NOT use before hydration: worsens dehydration.

pairs.key: ICU / immediate higher level of care if
pairs.value: persistent respiratory distress, high-flow or NIV need, hemodynamic instability, vasopressor need, severe arrhythmia, airway risk, neurologic decline, or a trajectory suggesting pt may crash on the floor.

sections.title: References & Resources
items: Bilezikian JP, Khan AA, Potts JT Jr, et al. Guidelines for the Management of Asymptomatic Primary Hyperparathyroidism: Summary Statement from the Fourth International Workshop. Journal of Clinical Endocrinology & Metabolism. 2014;99(10):35613569. https://doi.org/10.1210/jc.2014-1413

Rosner MH, Dalkin AC. OncoNephrology: The Pathophysiology and Treatment of MalignancyAssociated Hypercalcemia. Clinical Journal of the American Society of Nephrology. 2012;7(10):17221729. https://doi.org/10.2215/CJN.02470312

topics.title: Massive Hemorrhage / MTP
topics.subtitle: Shock, active bleeding, falling hemoglobin, coagulopathy, transfusion need

sections.title: Acute Management
items: Airway: if AMS, massive hematemesis, or hemodynamic collapse: intubate early; RSI with hemodynamically neutral agents (ketamine, avoid propofol in shock)
Breathing: SpO2, supplemental O2; monitor for aspiration if upper GI source
Circulation: 2 largebore IVs (≥16G), IO, or rapid infuser access; start crystalloid wide open while blood products prepared
Activate MTP now: do NOT wait for labs to confirm hemorrhage if clinical picture is clear
Transfuse Oneg or uncrossmatched pRBCs while type & cross pending

sections.title: PostMTP assessment
items: Repeat MTP labs: CBC, coags, fibrinogen, iCa, ABG
Check pt temp: treat hypothermia
POCUS to check volume status: flat IVC may indicate continued hemorrhage vs overresuscitation
Correct electrolyte abnormalities (calcium, potassium, magnesium)
Platelet & coagulation factor targets depend on hemostasis security: if definitive hemostasis achieved (IR embolization), repletion less critical; if tenuous, target more robust coag parameters
Fibrinogen >150200, INR <1.5, platelets >50k (>100k for CNS or ocular bleeding), iCa >1.1, temp >35, pH >7.2

sections.title: When to Activate MTP
items: >5 units PBCs within 4 hours
Active hemorrhage with hemodynamic instability (SBP <90, HR >120)
on pressors
ruptured AAA, massive GIB, Ob bleed
Do NOT use hemoglobin to trigger MTP: Hgb takes hours to fall after hemorrhage
If doubt exists whether the pt is in hemorrhagic shock: bedside echo to confirm hypovolemia

pairs.value: 1:1:1: pRBC: FFP: Platelets.
Consider 1:1:1:1 adding cryoprecipitate for prolonged MTP. Give 10 units cryo if fibrinogen <150200 mg/dL. inc ↑fibrinogen ~75 mg/dL / 10 units.
Saline & LR dilute clotting factors & do nothing to stop hemorrhage. Blood products only for hemorrhagic shock resuscitation.
Target MAP 5060 mmHg until hemostasis achieved. Overresuscitation dislodges clots & worsens venous bleeding (esp variceal). Exception: TBI: target higher MAP.
Multiple 1618G peripheral IVs: adequate with Belmont pressure infuser (300500 mL/min).
Rapid Infusion Cater: 7Fr placed via Seldinger through existing PIV. Equivalent to 13.3G. Best option for largebore peripheral access. NOT pressureinjectable (no CT contrast).
Ultrasoundguided access above medial malleolus. Large,pristine vein. Distend with tourniquet + Trendelenburg. Good option when arm veins are collapsed or damaged.
Acceptable but lower flow rates. Remove line cap before attaching pressure infuser. Use largest lumen. Most are pressureinjectable for CT.
Introducer sheaths: high failure rate, kink without catheter in place. Hemodialysis catheter NOT pressureinjectable.
Place early for BP monitoring. Femoral artery + femoral vein on same side (dirty double) is fastest way to get both simultaneously.

sections.title: MTP Labs (q3060 min)
items: CBC: trending Hgb & platelets
Coags: INR, PTT, fibrinogen
AntiXa level if on heparin or oral Xa inhibitor
TEG (if avail): guides fibrinogen, antifibrinolytic, & platelet therapy in real time
Electrolytes + ionized calcium (iCa): follow closely during MTP
ABG/VBG: monitor pH, avoid acidosis
Type & crossmatch: already be done before MTP activation

sections.title: Management
items: Goal 1:1:1 ratio (pRBCs: FFP: platelets); give TXA 1 g IV over 10 min within first 3 hrs of hemorrhage
check & treat lethal triad: hypothermia (warm fluids, blankets), acidosis (resuscitate, limit crystalloid), coagulopathy (blood products, TXA, factor replacement)
Permissive ↓ BP: target SBP 8090 mmHg or MAP ~5060 until source control achieved. Exception: TBI, spinal cord ischemia, or pregnancy may require higher targets
Use blood products, not liters of crystalloid, as the primary resuscitation fluid once hemorrhagic shock is recognized
Replace fibrinogen early: cryoprecipitate if fibrinogen <150200 mg/dL or if prolonged MTP suggests depletion
Replace calcium aggressively during MTP; citrate load from products causes hypocalcemia & worsens coagulopathy
Reverse pre-existing anticoagulation / coagulopathy: PCC + vitamin K for warfarin, specific DOAC reversal when indicated, protamine for heparin
Identify source & achieve source control: surgical consult, GI for endoscopy, IR for embolization, OB for postpartum hemorrhage
Do not chase a normal hemoglobin early: hemodynamics, transfusion requirement, bedside exam, & response to products matter more in the first hours

pairs.value: Tranexamic acid (TXA) 1g over 10 minthen1g over 8h: give within 3h of injury. Indications: trauma, obstetric hemorrhage, TEG LY30 >3%, cirrhosis with hyperfibrinolysis.
Cryoprecipitate if fibrinogen <150200 mg/dL. Fibrinogen is first factor to reach critically low levels in massive hemorrhage.
12g CaCl2 OR 36g calcium gluconate per MTP round (every 6 units pRBC). Target iCa 13 mM. Citrate in blood products chelates calcium: hypocalcemia causes coagulopathy.
0.3 mcg/kg IV (max 21 mcg). Consider if: renal failure, thrombocytopenia, or antiplatelet drugs.
MTP will not correct pre-existing coagulopathy. Reverse warfarin (PCC + Vit K), DOACs (&exanet/idarucizumab), heparin (protamine) independently.
Target nl. pH. Even mild acidosis (pH 7.20) substantially impairs coagulation cascade. Correct with bicarb if needed.
Use warmed blood products (Level1 or Belmont infusers with inline warming). Forcedair warming blanket. Monitor core temp ly.
Prioritize fastest strategy: IR embolization or surgical packing over definitive repair.Don't delay source control for resuscitation optimization.

drugs.name: Tranexamic acid (TXA)
drugs.notes: Give within 3h of injury. Trauma & obstetric hemorrhage. Aminocaproic acid 45g loadthen1g/hr is an alternative.

drugs.name: Calcium chloride (CaCl2)
drugs.dose: 12g IV per MTP round (6 units pRBC)
drugs.notes: Preferred if central access available. Peripheral extravasation causes tissue necrosis: use calcium gluconate for peripheral lines.

drugs.name: Calcium gluconate
drugs.notes: Safe for peripheral administration. Target iCa 13 mM. Replace routinely —don't wait for sx.

drugs.name: Cryoprecipitate
drugs.notes: Give if fibrinogen <150200 mg/dL. Consider empiric 1:1:1:1 ratio (adding cryo) for prolonged MTP.

drugs.name: DDAVP (desmopressin)
drugs.notes: Promotes platelet function. Use if renal failure, thrombocytopenia, or on antiplatelet agents.

drugs.name: 4factor PCC (Kcentra)
drugs.notes: Warfarin reversal: faster than FFP. Always give with Vitamin K 10mg IV to prevent rebound.

drugs.name: Sodium bicarbonate
drugs.notes: Correct acidosis: pH <7.2 substantially impairs coagulation. Target nl. pH during MTP.

sections.title: References & Resources
items: Spahn DR, Bouillon B, Cerny V, et al. The European Guideline on Management of Major Bleeding and Coagulopathy Following Trauma: 6th Edition. Critical Care. 2023;27:80. https://doi.org/10.1186/s13054-023-04327-7

Holcomb JB, Tilley BC, Baraniuk S, et al. Transfusion of Plasma, Platelets, and Red Blood Cells in a 1:1:1 vs a 1:1:2 Ratio (PROPPR trial). JAMA. 2015;313(5):471482. https://doi.org/10.1001/jama.2015.12

Cannon JW, Khan MA, Raja AS, et al. Damage Control Resuscitation in Patients with Severe Traumatic Hemorrhage: A Practice Management Guideline. Journal of Trauma and Acute Care Surgery. 2017;82(3):605617.

topics.title: Anticoagulation Reversal
topics.subtitle: Major bleeding, suprathetherapeutic anticoagulation, urgent procedure, intracranial hemorrhage

sections.title: Acute Management
items: Airway: check patency and protect. Breathing: check WOB, oxygenation, and ventilation. Circulation: check pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
Do NOT delay reversal for labs if life-threatening hemorrhage: give empiric reversal based on known agent

sections.title: Chart Check / Hx.
items: IDanticoagulant, dose, indication, & time of last dose: this determines if reversal is still useful
Clarify bleeding site & severity: ICH, GI bleed, retroperitoneal, airway, pericardial, intraocular, postop, traumatic
Ask whether the issue is major bleeding, clinically relevant nonmajor bleeding, or urgent surgery / procedure
Renal & hepatic function matter: DOAC clearance is prolonged in CKD / liver disease
Review concomitant agents: aspirin, clopidogrel, ticagrelor, NSAIDs, SSRIs, thrombolytics
For warfarin: check baseline INR trend, diet changes, antibiotics, amiodarone, liver disease, alcohol use
For heparin / LMWH: determine when it was started, infusion rate, bolus timing, & recent procedures
Look for thrombotic indication & risk if held: mechanical valve, recent VTE, LV thrombus, AFib with prior stroke

sections.title: Exam
items: Airway & neuro status first: headache, vomiting, focal deficit, declining GCS, neck swelling, stridor
Hemodynamics: tachycardia, hypotension, perfusion, orthostasis, shock
Look for occult bleeding: flank / back ecchymosis, abd distension, melena, hematuria, large hematomas, joint swelling
Skin / access sites: expanding IV site hematoma, surgical drains, catheter sites, ecchymoses
Remember: anticoagulant level / INR DOESN'T predict bleeding severity: treat pt & source

sections.title: Management
items: Identify anticoagulant: warfarin, heparin, enoxaparin, rivaroxaban, apixaban, dabigatran, etc.
Hold ALL anticoagulants & antiplatelets now
Control source: endoscopy, IR embolization, surgery, neurosurgery, ENT, bronchoscopy, compression / packing
Transfuse blood products as clinically indicated; reversal agents don't replace standard hemorrhage resuscitation
For warfarin: 4factor PCC (Kcentra) 2550 units/kg IV + vitamin K 10 mg IV (PCC works in minutes; vitamin K takes 624 hrs)
For dabigatran: idarucizumab (Praxbind) 5 g IV
For rivaroxaban/apixaban: &exanet alfa (&exxa) or 4factor PCC 50 units/kg if &exanet unavailable
For heparin: protamine sulfate 1 mg per 100 units heparin given in last 23 hrs (max 50 mg)
If bleeding is not life-threatening & the last dose was remote, supportive care + temporary drug hold may safer than full reversal
Repeat reassessment after reversal: vitals, bleeding exam, Hgmb trend, & whether source control has been achieved
Recheck coag parameters after intervention: INR after warfarin reversal, aPTT / antiXa, fibrinogen if massively bleeding
Watch for rebound anticoagulation or continued bleeding: especially after vitamin K is omitted, PCC wears off, or dabigatran redistributes
Monitor for thrombosis after PCC / &exanet / factor products, particularly in recent VTE, ACS, stroke, or mechanical valve pts
Develop a reanticoagulation plan post hemostasis. thrombosis risk returns quickly in some pts

pairs.value: 4factor PCC (Kcentra) for urgent reversal + Vitamin K 10mg IV. FFP if PCC unavailable.
Protamine sulfate 1mg per 100 units heparin given in last 23h. Max 50mg.
Protamine 1mg per 1mg enoxaparin if <8h since last dose. Partial reversal if >8h.
4factor PCC 50 units/kg.
Idarucizumab (Praxbind) 5g IV: complete reversal. Dialyzable as backup.
No specific antidote. Supportive care; consider PCC / aPCC in life-threatening bleeding after specialist discussion
No specific antidote. Stop infusion; short halflife means effect falls quickly, but may prolonged in organ dysfunction
Reverse only for true major bleeding / critical procedures. Restart planning matters early: discuss with cardiology / hematology
CBC, PT/INR, aPTT, fibrinogen, CMP / Cr, type & screen, lactate if unstable
AntiXa level if avail for apixaban / rivaroxaban or LMWH; thrombin time for dabigatran; heparin antiXa / aPTT for UFH
CT head for neuro sx or trauma, CTA / endoscopy / bronchoscopy / IR imaging based on suspected source
Serial Hgb / Hct q46h in active bleed, telemetry if unstable, strict I&Os, neuro checks if CNS bleed suspected
Pharmacy, hematology, IR / surgery / GI / neurosurgery depending on bleed site; cardiology if mechanical valve or extreme thrombotic risk

drugs.name: 4factor PCC (Kcentra)
drugs.notes: Fastest warfarin reversal. Always give with Vitamin K to prevent rebound.

drugs.name: Vitamin K (phytonadione)
drugs.notes: Sustained warfarin reversal (effect in 624h). Always give with PCC for urgent reversal.

drugs.name: Protamine sulfate
drugs.notes: Give slowly over 10 min: rapid infusion causes ↓ BP. Monitor for anaphylaxis.

drugs.name: Idarucizumab (Praxbind)
drugs.notes: Dabigatran reversal. Immediate reversal.

drugs.name: &andexanet alfa
drugs.dose: Low dose: 400mg bolus + 480mg over 2h; High dose: 800mg bolus + 960mg over 2h
drugs.notes: Reverses rivaroxaban, apixaban. High dose for rivaroxaban >10mg or apixaban >5mg taken <8h prior.

drugs.name: FFP
drugs.notes: Use if PCC unavailable for warfarin reversal or if broader factor replacement is needed. Slower & largervolume than PCC.

drugs.name: Tranexamic acid (TXA)
drugs.dose: 1g IV over 10 min, then 1g over 8h when appropriate
drugs.contraindications: Active intravascular clotting; caution in hematuria / upper urinary tract bleeding
drugs.notes: Adjunctive hemostatic thetherapy for selected trauma, postop, mucosal, or postpartum bleeding. Not an anticoagulant antidote.

pairs.key: ICU / immediate higher level of care if
pairs.value: persistent respiratory distress, high-flow or NIV need, hemodynamic instability, vasopressor need, severe arrhythmia, airway risk, neurologic decline, or a trajectory suggesting pt may crash on the floor.

sections.title: References & Resources
items: Tomaselli GF, Mahaffey KW, Cuker A, et al. 2020 ACC Expert Consensus Decision Pathway on Management of Bleeding in Patients on Oral Anticoagulants. Journal of the American College of Cardiology. 2020;76(5):594622. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jacc.2020.04.053
Frontera JA, Lewin JJ III, Rabinstein AA, et al. Guideline for Reversal of Antithrombotics in Intracranial Hemorrhage. Neurocritical Care. 2016;24(1):646. https://doi.org/10.1007/s12028-015-0222-x

Pollack CV Jr, Reilly PA, van Ryn J, et al. Idarucizumab for Dabigatran Reversal — Full Cohort Analysis (REVERSE AD trial). New England Journal of Medicine. 2017;377(5):431441. https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1707278

topics.title: Bloodless Medicine / Jehovah's Witness
topics.subtitle: Severe anemia, bleeding risk, transfusion refusal, hypoperfusion signs

sections.title: Acute Management
items: Airway: check patency and protect. Breathing: check WOB, oxygenation, and ventilation. Circulation: check pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
Control bleeding now: source control, procedural hemostasis, surgery, IR, OB/GI intervention, & local/topical hemostatic measures.
Minimize iatrogenic blood loss: pediatric tubes, bundle labs, point of care testing, & bloodconservation sampling devices when available.
Support oxygen delivery: supplemental oxygen, optimize preload & perfusion, treat fever, treat shock, & avoid unnecessary hemodilution.

sections.title: Chart Check / Hx.
items: What is specifically refused: packed RBCs, plasma, platelets, cryoprecipitate, albumin, cell salvage, acute normovolemic hemodilution, clotting factor concentrates, PCC, recombinant factor VIIa, etc.
Baseline hemoglobin, trajectory, active bleeding source, hemodynamic stability, & signs of endorgan ischemia.
Current anticoagulants / antiplatelets & last dose.
Iron deficiency, CKD, malignancy, infection, or nutritional deficiency that may limit erythropoiesis.
Prior documentation, advance directives, blood refusal card, durable power of attorney, or prior bloodless medicine plan.

sections.title: Exam
items: Vitals/perfusion: tachycardia, ↓ BP, cool extremities, delayed cap refill, shock
Look for active blood loss: melena, hematemesis, hematuria, vaginal bleeding, drain output, expanding hematoma
Signs of critical anemia: chest pain, dyspnea, syncope, AMS, ischemic EKG changes, low UOP, rising lactate
check volume status: severe anemia may coexist with hemorrhagic shock or dilution from excess IVF
Document neuro, cardiopulm, & airway findings if the bleed location could worsen quickly

pairs.value: Care strategy that respects transfusion refusal while maximizing hemostasis, oxygen delivery, & erythropoiesis.
Acceptance of fractions or bloodconservation techniques is individualized. Ask & document exactly what is acceptable.
No single cutoff, but risk rises sharply as Hgb approaches <56 g/dL, especially with bleeding, shock, sepsis, or ischemia.
Whole blood & primary blood components such as RBCs, plasma, platelets, & WBCs are commonly refused.

Some pts may accept minor fractions or procedures that maintain continuity with body, such as clotting factors, PCC, recombinant factor VIIa, albumin, cell salvage, or acute normovolemic hemodilution. Individual preference matters.
Never assume acceptance or refusal of fractionated product or bloodconservation technique without asking pt.
Mortality rises sharply as hemoglobin falls into <56 g/dL range, especially with bleeding, shock, ischemia, or sepsis.
Stop bleeding, reduce oxygen consumption, optimize perfusion, & stimulate erythropoiesis early.
Hemoglobinbased oxygen carriers may considered in extreme, life-threatening anemia where available, but access is limited &requires emergency regulatory processes.

sections.title: Blood Conservation Strategies
items: Use pediatric / lowvolume phlebotomy tubes for all draws.
Order only essential labs & avoid repetitive "routine" blood work.
Use point of care testing when it decreases total blood loss.
If pt accepts it, consider cell salvage or autologous techniques for surgical/procedural blood loss.
Avoid unnecessary IV fluids once initial resuscitation is complete: excessive crystalloid worsens hemodilution.
Treat fever, agitation, pain, & shivering to reduce oxygen demand.

drugs.name: Epoetin alfa
drugs.dose: 20,00030,000 units IV daily to every or day OR 40,000 units daily until Hgb >7 g/dL,thenweekly
drugs.contraindications: Uncontrolled severe HTN, history of ESA hypersensitivity; use caution with thrombosis risk
drugs.notes: Stimulates erythropoiesis. Effect is not immediate; reticulocyte response begins after several days. Requires adequate iron support.

drugs.name: Iron sucrose
drugs.dose: 100200 mg IV daily for several days (commonly 310 days depending on severity & iron status)
drugs.contraindications: Prior iron hypersensitivity; caution with active infection
drugs.notes: Useful when iron deficiency is present or suspected, or when rapid support of erythropoiesis is needed. Oral iron is slower.

drugs.name: Folate
drugs.notes: Supportive vitamin supplementation to maximize erythropoiesis.

drugs.name: Vitamin B12
drugs.dose: Per deficiency protocol or daily supplementation if deficiency status is unclear
drugs.notes: Correct deficiency or empirically supplement when erythropoietic support is being pursued.

drugs.name: Vitamin C
drugs.notes: Often used as an adjunct with iron therapy in bloodless anemia protocols.

drugs.name: Tranexamic acid
drugs.dose: 1 g IV over 10 min,then1 g over 8 h for major bleeding when appropriate
drugs.contraindications: Active intravascular clotting; use indicationspecific caution
drugs.notes: Antifibrinolytic adjunct for trauma, procedural bleeding, postpartum hemorrhage, & selected mucosal bleeding states.

drugs.name: 4factor PCC (Kcentra)
drugs.dose: 2550 units/kg IV depending on indication / INR
drugs.notes: Often acceptable to some Jehovah's Witness pts b/c it is blood fraction, but acceptance is individual. Useful for urgent warfarin or factorbased reversal.

drugs.name: Recombinant factor VIIa
drugs.notes: May be considered as rescue hemostatic adjunct in selected life-threatening bleeding cases if acceptable to pt.

pairs.value: Trauma, GI bleed, postpartum hemorrhage, operative/procedural bleeding, ruptured vascular source.
Iron deficiency, CKD, marrow suppression, malignancy, B12/folate deficiency, inflammation.
Autoimmune hemolysis, microangiopathy, drug induced hemolysis, mechanical hemolysis.
Repeated phlebotomy, excess crystalloid causing hemodilution, anticoagulation/antiplatelet effect.
Do not assume anemia alone: also consider sepsis, PE, ACS, tamponade, tension physiology, or occult hemorrhage.

sections.title: Management
items: Clarify goals & exactly what pt will or will not accept early. Document discussionin chart.
If pt has decisionmaking capacity, discuss blood products privately & directly with pt not relying only on family input.
Stop anticoagulants & antiplatelet agents when bleeding riskoutweighs benefit; reverse coagulopathy with acceptable agents.
Consult hematology, anesthesia, surgery/procedure team, pharmacy, ethics, & bloodless medicine services early if avail.

sections.title: Workup & Monitoring
items: CBC trend, reticulocyte count, ferritin, iron studies, B12, folate, CMP, hemolysis labs if needed.
Coags & fibrinogen when bleeding or coagulopathy is suspected.
Track perfusion & oxygen delivery: lactate, telemetry, chest pain symptoms, neuro status, urine output.
Recheck hemoglobin trend after limiting phlebotomy b/c act of monitoring worsens anemia.

pairs.value: CBC trend, retic, ferritin/iron studies, B12, folate, CMP, coags/fibrinogen, hemolysis labs if needed.
Telemetry, serial vitals, lactate, urine output, ischemic symptoms, & minimize repeat phlebotomy.
Sourcespecific imaging/endoscopy/procedural evaluation if active blood loss is suspected.
Record accepted and refused products, fractions, & procedures in a visible part of the chart.
Early heme, surgery/procedural team, anesthesia, pharmacy, ethics, & bloodless medicine service if avail.
In severe hemorrhage, definitive bleeding control matters more than any anemia adjunct.
Erythropoietin helps, but it is not an immediate rescue therapy: plan for lag of days before meaningful hemoglobin response.
IV iron is preferred over oral iron in severe inpt anemia or when rapid marrow support is needed.
Document accepted & refused therapies precisely so consultants, overnight teams, & procedural servicesdon't make assumptions.

pairs.key: ICU / immediate higher level of care if
pairs.value: persistent respiratory distress, high-flow or NIV need, hemodynamic instability, vasopressor need, severe arrhythmia, airway risk, neurologic decline, or a trajectory suggesting pt may crash on the floor.

sections.title: References & Resources
items: [ASH review: Bloodless medicine: what to do when you can't transfuse](https://www.hopkinsmedicine.org/-/media/bloodless-medicine-surgery/documents/bloodless-papers/bloodless-ash-paper-2015.pdf)
[WikEM: Severe anemia in Jehovah's Witness pts](https://wikem.org/wiki/Severe_anemia_in_Jehovah%27s_Witness_patients)

topics.title: Coagulopathy Reversal
topics.subtitle: Elevated INR, low fibrinogen, bleeding, liver disease, DIC

sections.title: Acute Management
items: Airway: check for expanding neck/oropharyngeal bleed; intubate early if airway compromise is possible
Breathing: monitor SpO2 & WOB; treat hemothorax or pulmonary hemorrhage
Circulation: 2 largebore IVs, type & screen, CBC, coags, fibrinogen; activate MTP if life-threatening hemorrhage
Do NOT delay treatment while waiting for confirmatory factor levels if known or strongly suspected inherited bleeding disorder

sections.title: Chart Check / Hx.
items: Known diagnosis & type: Hemophilia A, Hemophilia B, vWD type 1, 2A, 2B, 2M, 2N, or 3
Baseline severity & home regimen: prophylactic factor, emicizumab use, prior DDAVP response, HumateP/Wilate/Vonvendi availability
Inhibitor history: prior factor VIII or IX inhibitor, bypassing agent use (rFVIIa or FEIBA)
Bleeding phenotype: hemarthrosis, deep muscle bleed, mucosal bleeding, epistaxis, menorrhagia, postpartum hemorrhage, procedurerelated bleeding
Timing & mechanism: spontaneous vs trauma vs postop/dental; head strike always matters
Medication review: aspirin, NSAIDs, anticoagulants, SSRIs, herbal supplements, recent DDAVP
Family history & prior abnl. procedures/extractions
Renal disease, CAD, pregnancy/postpartum status, & hyponatremia risk: se affect DDAVP safety

sections.title: Exam
items: Inspect for expanding hematoma, ecchymosis, hemarthrosis, compartment syndrome, & occult blood loss
Neuro exam if headache, trauma, or AMS: ICH can occult
Joint exam: warmth, swelling, ↓ ROM suggest hemarthrosis
Muscle groups/back/abdomen: deep bleeds can present with pain, neuropathy, or ↓ BP
Mucosal exam: epistaxis, gingival bleeding, oral cavity bleeding, menorrhagia severity
Airway/neck exam: tongue, retropharyngeal, or cervical hematoma

pairs.value: Factor VIII deficiency. Xlinked. Severity by factor level: severe <1%, moderate 15%, mild 540%.
Factor IX deficiency. Xlinked. Clinical bleeding pattern similar to Hemophilia A.
Partial quantitative vWF deficiency. Most common &mild;DDAVP responsive.
Qualitative vWF defect. 2A = loss of highmolecularweight multimers, 2B = increased platelet binding, 2M = defective platelet binding, 2N = low FVIII binding & may mimic mild Hemophilia A.
Nearcomplete vWF deficiency. Severe disease with mucosal & deep bleeding; DDAVPineffective.
Deep tissue bleeding, hemarthrosis, prolonged aPTT, low factor VIII. Consider acquired factor VIII inhibitor if no family history & severe unexplained bleeding.
Deep tissue bleeding, hemarthrosis, prolonged aPTT, low factor IX.
Mucosal bleeding, easy bruising, menorrhagia, procedurerelated bleeding; partial quantitative vWF deficiency.
Qualitative vWF disorders. 2N can look like mild Hemophilia A b/c factor VIII is low; multimer analysis & vWF studies distinguish m.
Enhanced platelet binding with thrombocytopenia risk; avoid DDAVP if suspected or known b/c it worsens thrombocytopenia
Severe inherited vWF deficiency; major mucosal & surgical bleeding, sometimes hemarthrosis b/c factor VIII canlow.
Mucosal bleeding with nl. PT/PTT may reflect platelet dysfunction, uremia, antiplatelet drugs, or thrombocytopenia.
Abnl. PT & PTT, low fibrinogen, thrombocytopenia, schistocytes, critical illness context.
Consider with new bleeding later in life, autoimmune disease, LVAD/aortic stenosis, lymphoproliferative disease, or isolated prolonged aPTT that does not correct.

sections.title: Management: Hemophilia A
items: Give recombinant factor VIII concentrate now for major bleeding; typical target FVIII activity 80100% for life-threatening/CNS/neck/GI bleeding & 4060% for less severe bleeds
If mild Hemophilia A & known DDAVP responder, DDAVP can used for minor bleeding or procedures; do NOT rely on DDAVP alone for major bleeding
If inhibitor history or poor response to factor VIII, use bypassing therapy (rFVIIa or FEIBA per hematology) instead of repeated ineffective FVIII dosing
Pts on emicizumab can still have breakthrough bleeding: coordinate with hematology before giving bypassing agents, especially FEIBA
Hemarthrosis or muscle bleed: factor first,thenrest/ice/compression/elevation & pain control; avoid NSAIDs

sections.title: Management: Hemophilia B
items: Give recombinant factor IX concentrate now for major bleeding; typical target FIX activity 60100% depending on bleed severity & site
DDAVP is not useful in Hemophilia B
Because factor IX has longer halflife, repeat dosing unless frequent than factor VIII but still guided by bleed site, severity, & hematology
If known factor IX inhibitor or inadequate response, involve hematology urgently for bypassing therapy
Deep muscle, retroperitoneal, CNS, airway, & abdominal bleeds require admission & factor support even if initial symptoms improve

sections.title: Management: von Willebrand Disease
items: Type 1: DDAVP isfirst line if prior response is known or likely & bleeding is not life-threatening; monitor sodium & restrict free water
Type 2A & 2M: some pts respond to DDAVP, but many need vWFcontaining concentrate for significant bleeding or procedures
Type 2B: avoid DDAVP b/c it worsens thrombocytopenia; use vWFcontaining concentrate instead
Type 2N: may mimic mild Hemophilia A b/c FVIII is low; treat major bleeding with vWFcontaining concentrate not assuming isolated FVIII deficiency
Type 3: severe disease: DDAVP is ineffective; use vWFcontaining concentrate for major bleeding, surgery, & serious trauma

For all vWD types, add tranexamic acid for mucosal/oral/gynecologic bleeding when not contraindicated; escalate to vWFcontaining concentrate for major bleeding, failed DDAVP, pregnancy/postpartum hemorrhage, or invasive procedures

sections.title: Ongoing Management
items: Trend bleeding, hemoglobin, & product response; repeat factor levels if guided by hematology
Immobilize & ice hemarthrosis initially; avoid joint aspiration unless directed by specialists
Monitor sodium after DDAVP & restrict free water for 24h
For GI/intracranial/retroperitoneal bleeding or surgery, continue factor support beyond first dose based on site & severity
Documentproduct, dose, time, & response: this matters for repeat dosing & transfer
Before discharge, confirm access to home factor/vWF product, hematology follow up, & return precautions

pairs.value: CBC, type & screen, PT/INR, aPTT, fibrinogen, CMP;PT/PTT can nl. in mild vWD.
