Factor VIII, factor IX, vWF antigen, vWF activity (ristocetin or GPIbbased), ± factor XI if needed.
Mixing study & Besda inhibitor assay.Don't wait for result before treating major bleed.
CT head for trauma/headache; CTA/CT as needed for neck, retroperitoneal, GI, or intramuscular bleeding.
Type & cross, serial hemoglobin, joint circumference/compartment checks, strict I&O if giving DDAVP.

drugs.name: Recombinant factor VIII
drugs.dose: Dose per hematology/product guide; typical major bleed target FVIII 80100%
drugs.notes: Firstline for Hemophilia A major bleeding. If inhibitor present, may need bypassing agent instead.

drugs.name: Recombinant factor IX
drugs.dose: Dose per hematology/product guide; typical major bleed target FIX 60100%
drugs.notes: Firstline for Hemophilia B major bleeding. Longer halflife than FVIII products.

drugs.name: vWFcontaining concentrate
drugs.dose: Per product guide (HumateP, Wilate, Vonvendibased strategy)
drugs.notes: Preferred for major vWD bleeding, type 3 disease, DDAVP nonresponse, or when DDAVP is contraindicated.

drugs.name: DDAVP (desmopressin)
drugs.dose: 0.3 mcg/kg IV over 2030 min or 150300 mcg IN depending on weight
drugs.contraindications: Type 2B vWD, significant hyponatremia risk, unstable CAD
drugs.notes: Useful for mild Hemophilia A & many type 1 vWD pts if known responder. Restrict free water & monitor sodium.

drugs.name: Tranexamic acid
drugs.dose: 10 mg/kg IV q8h or 1300 mg PO TID
drugs.contraindications: Gross hematuria, high risk urinary tract clot retention
drugs.notes: Excellent adjunct for mucosal, oral, epistaxis, gynecologic, & dental bleeding.

drugs.name: rFVIIa (NovoSeven)
drugs.notes: Bypassing agent for hemophilia with inhibitors when factor replacement is ineffective.

drugs.name: FEIBA
drugs.notes: Alternative bypassing agent for inhibitor pts; thrombosis risk rises with emicizumab.

pairs.key: ICU / immediate higher level of care if
pairs.value: persistent respiratory distress, high-flow or NIV need, hemodynamic instability, vasopressor need, severe arrhythmia, airway risk, neurologic decline, or a trajectory suggesting pt may crash on the floor.

sections.title: References & Resources
items: Pabinger I, Fries D, Schöchl H, et al. Tranexamic Acid for Treatment and Prophylaxis of Bleeding and Hyperfibrinolysis. Wiener Klinische Wochenschrift. 2017;129(910):303316. https://doi.org/10.1007/s00508-017-1194-y
Levy JH, Dutton RP, Hemphill JC III, et al. Multidisciplinary Approach to the Challenge of Hemostasis. Anesthesia & Analgesia. 2010;110(2):354364. https://doi.org/10.1213/ANE.0b013e3181c84ba5

Levi M, Toh CH, Thachil J, Watson HG. Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Management of Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation. British Journal of Haematology. 2009;145(1):2433. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1365-2141.2009.07600.x

topics.title: Transfusion Reactions
topics.subtitle: Fever, dyspnea, hypotension, hemolysis, rash during transfusion

sections.title: Acute Management
items: Airway: check patency and protect. Breathing: check WOB, oxygenation, and ventilation. Circulation: check pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
STOP transfusion now: clamp tubing, keep IV access open with NS
ABCs: check airway, breathing, hemodynamics. monitoring
Notify blood bank: save blood product bag & tubing for analysis
Draw: type & screen (new), DAT (Coombs), CBC, haptoglobin, LDH, bilirubin, free Hgb, fibrinogen
If anaphylaxis: IM epinephrine 0.30.5mg, IV fluids, Stop blood product
If TRALI: supportive care, O2, may need intubation & mechanical ventilation. Diuretics NOT helpful (noncardiogenic edema)
If TACO: sit upright, O2, furosemide 2040mg IV, consider NIPPV. Volume overload: not permeability
If acute hemolytic: aggressive IVF (NS 200300 mL/h) to maintain UOP >1 mL/kg/h, prevent renal failure from free Hgb. Send type & screen, DAT, hemolysis labs

sections.title: Chart Check / Hx.
items: Transfusion hx: prior reactions (type, severity), prior antibodies
Blood product type & volume being transfused, rate, how long into transfusion
Pretransfusion vitals vs current vitals: ↑ temp, ↑ HR, ↓/↑ BP, ↓ SpO2
Volume status: cardiac hx ( CHF: TACO risk), renal failure, recent fluid balance
PreMeds given? (acetaminophen, diphenhydramine: no longer routinely suggested but still common)
Reason for transfusion, Hgb pretransfusion, how many units already given
IgA deficiency ( anaphylaxis risk: need IgAdeficient products)
Multiparous female or prior pregnancies ( ↑ TRALI risk from donor antibodies)

sections.title: Exam
items: Vitals: temp (fever: FNHTR, hemolytic, sepsis), BP ( ↓ in hemolytic, TRALI, anaphylaxis; ↑ in TACO), HR, SpO2, RR
Respiratory: crackles (TACO), bilateral infiltrates on CXR (TRALI vs TACO), wheezing (anaphylaxis), dyspnea
Skin: urticaria (allergic), flushing, angioedema (anaphylaxis), jaundice (hemolysis)
Flank/back pain, dark urine ( hemolytic)
Rigors, chills (FNHTR, hemolytic, sepsis)
JVD, peripheral edema (TACO)
Mentation changes (hemolytic, TRALI, anaphylaxis)

pairs.value: Febrile nonhemolytic transfusion reaction: most common (1:300 pRBC, 1:10 plt). Temp ↑ ≥1°C during/after transfusion. Cytokinemed.. Dx of exclusion: must r/o hemolytic rxn & sepsis first
Transfusionassociated circulatory overload (1:700). Volume overload: pulm edema. HTN, ↑ JVP, ↑ BNP. Responds to diuretics. MCC of transfusionrelated death
Transfusionrelated acute lung injury (1:5,000). Noncardiogenic pulm edema within 6h of transfusion. ↓ BP, fever, bilateral infiltrates, nl BNP. Diuretics do NOT help: supportive care only
ABO incompatibility: medical emergency. Fever, flank pain, hemoglobinuria, DIC, renal failure, shock. Positive DAT. Mortality 5-10% if recognized early
Minor allergic (urticaria) = 1:100. Anaphylaxis = 1:40,000. IgAdeficient pts at highest risk of anaphylaxis. Treat with epi
Contaminated product: esp platelets (stored at room temp). High fever, rigors, ↓ BP, shock. Gram stain & culture of product
TRALI: acute, within 6h. TACO: gradual, during/shortly after transfusion
TRALI: ↓ BP . TACO: HTN
TRALI: febrile. TACO: nl.
TRALI: nl.. TACO: elevated
TRALI: nl. TACO: elevated
Both: bilateral infiltrates / pulm edema
TRALI: net even/negative. TACO: net positive (volume overload)
TRALI: no: noncardiogenic. TACO: yes: volume overload
TRALI: supportive (O2, MV if needed). TACO: upright positioning, diuretics, O2, slow future transfusions

sections.title: Management
items: Stop the transfusion immediately, keep the IV open with normal saline, recheck pt and product identification, and notify the blood bank.
Send the remaining blood product plus post-reaction blood and urine samples for repeat type and screen, DAT, hemolysis evaluation, and culture when sepsis is a concern.
Acute hemolytic reaction: ICU plus renal support, aggressive isotonic fluids to maintain urine output, treat hyperkalemia, shock, and DIC, and send repeat ABO or crossmatch with full hemolysis labs.
FNHTR: exclude hemolysis and sepsis first, give acetaminophen or meperidine for rigors, and only resume transfusion if evaluation is negative and symptoms fully resolve.

Allergic reaction: pause for urticaria and give diphenhydramine; for bronchospasm, hypotension, or airway edema, stop permanently and treat as anaphylaxis with IM epinephrine plus oxygen, IV fluids, bronchodilator, antihistamines, and steroids.
TACO: stop transfusion, sit upright, give oxygen, and diurese; think hypertension, positive fluid balance, elevated BNP or CVP, and pulmonary edema on chest x-ray.

TRALI: stop transfusion and give supportive respiratory care with oxygen or lung-protective ventilation; avoid diuretics and distinguish from TACO by normal or low BNP or CVP and more frequent hypotension.

Delayed hemolytic reaction: confirm with DAT or antibody testing and hemolysis labs; avoid further transfusion if possible, use extended antigen-matched blood if transfusion is unavoidable, and treat severe hyperhemolysis with hematology-guided steroids plus IVIG and possible complement blockade.
Septic transfusion reaction: stop transfusion, start broad-spectrum antibiotics immediately, get pt and product cultures, and manage as sepsis with fluids and vasopressors if needed.

pairs.value: Stop transfusion, save product & tubing, notify blood bank, new type & screen
Positive = antibodycoated RBCs (acute hemolytic rxn)
↑ LDH, ↑ indirect bilirubin, ↓ haptoglobin (often undetectable), ↑ free Hgb, ↑ reticulocytes
Hemoglobinuria (pink/red urine, dip + for blood, no RBCs on micro)
↓ Hgb (if hemolytic), ↓ plt (DIC), check vs pretransfusion
DIC screen if hemolytic rxn suspected (↑ PT/INR, ↑ PTT, ↓ fibrinogen, ↑ Ddimer)
TACO vs TRALI differentiation. ↑ = TACO
If septic transfusion rxn suspected
Bilateral infiltrates (TRALI, TACO). Cardiomegaly (TACO)
If anaphylaxis suspected: send within 12h

drugs.name: Acetaminophen
drugs.notes: For FNHTR (fever/rigors). Only after hemolytic rxn r/o

drugs.name: Diphenhydramine
drugs.notes: For mild allergic rxn (urticaria). May cautiously restart transfusion after sx resolve

drugs.name: Epinephrine (IM)
drugs.notes: Firstline for anaphylactic transfusion rxn. Do NOT restart product

drugs.name: Furosemide
drugs.notes: For TACO only: diuresis for volume overload. Does NOT work for TRALI

drugs.name: NS
drugs.dose: 200300 mL/h IV, titrate to UOP >1 mL/kg/h
drugs.notes: For acute hemolytic rxn: aggressive IVF to maintain renal perfusion & flush free Hgb. Prevent AKI

drugs.name: Meperidine
drugs.notes: For severe rigors unresponsive to acetaminophen. Rarely needed

pairs.key: ICU / immediate higher level of care if
pairs.value: persistent respiratory distress, high-flow or NIV need, hemodynamic instability, vasopressor need, severe arrhythmia, airway risk, neurologic decline, or a trajectory suggesting pt may crash on the floor.

items: Delaney M, Wendel S, Bercovitz RS, et al. Transfusion Reactions: Prevention, Diagnosis, and Treatment. The Lancet. 2016;388(10061):28252836. https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(15)01313-6
AABB. Standards for Blood Banks and Transfusion Services, 33rd Edition. 2022.
Panch SR, MontemayorGarcia C, Klein HG. Hemolytic Transfusion Reactions. New England Journal of Medicine. 2019;381(2):150162. https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMra1802338

topics.title: Acute Chest Syndrome
topics.subtitle: Chest pain, fever, hypoxemia, infiltrate, sickle cell disease

sections.title: Acute Management
items: Airway: check patency and protect. Breathing: check WOB, oxygenation, and ventilation. Circulation: check pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
ABCs: O2 to maintain SpO2 ≥95%. monitoring. Largebore IV access
Simple transfusion: target Hgb 10 g/dL (do NOT exceed: ↑ viscosity). For moderate ACS without rapid decline
Exchange transfusion (automated erythrocytapheresis): for severe ACS (SpO2 <90% despite O2, multilobar infiltrates, rapid deterioration, HgbS >30%). Target HgbS <30%, Hgb ~10
Bronchodilators (albuterol neb q46h): if wheezing or reactive airway component
Avoid excessive O2 (rebound sickling when removed), excessive IVF, transfusion to Hgb >10

sections.title: Chart Check / Hx.
items: Sickle cell genotype: HbSS (most severe), HbSC, HbSbeta thal
Baseline Hgb & HgbS% (for transfusion targets)
Triggers: VOC (vasoocclusive crisis): ACS is most common complication of hospitalized VOC pts. Also: infection, fat embolism from bone marrow necrosis, PE, postop (esp Abd/chest surgery)
Hydroxyurea use & compliance (↓ ACS episodes)
Prior ACS episodes: frequency, severity, prior exchange transfusions
Alloimmunization status: sickle pts develop alloantibodies from frequent transfusions: need extended phenotypematched blood
Iron overload status (chronic transfusions)
Recent surgery, anesthesia (↑ ACS risk postop)

sections.title: Exam
items: Fever + new pulmonary infiltrate + respiratory sx in sickle cell pt = ACS until proven otherwise
Respiratory: tachypnea, ↓ SpO2, crackles, wheezing, ↓ breath sounds, pleuritic chest pain
Fever (common: does not differentiate infection from infarction)
Pain: chest wall, rib, sternal: distinguish from VOC pain vs new ACS
Abd: splinting from pain, hepatomegaly, RUQ tenderness (hepatic sequestration)
Signs of worsening anemia: pallor, tachycardia, fatigue, jaundice (hemolysis)
Neuro: AMS, focal deficits (concurrent stroke: can cooccur with ACS)

pairs.value: New pulmonary infiltrate on CXR + ≥1 of: fever (>38.5°C), chest pain, respiratory sx (cough, dyspnea, tachypnea, ↓ SpO2) in sickle cell pt
Vasoocclusion in pulmonary vasculature + infection + fat embolism from bone marrow necrosis: V/Q mismatch, atelectasis, infarction: cycle of sickling
ACS is #1 cause of death in sickle cell dz & #1 cause of ICU admission
Automated erythrocytapheresis: removes HgbS cells & replaces with HgbA. Target HgbS <30%, Hgb ~10. Avoids hyperviscosity
Most common identifiable cause (~30%). Atypicals (Mycoplasma, Chlamydia) + typical bacteria (S. pneumo, H. flu)
From bone marrow necrosis during VOC. Leads to lung injury & ACS
In situ thrombosis / vasoocclusion in pulm vasculature
Rib/chest wall pain from VOC: splinting: atelectasis: ACS. Why IS q2h is critical
PNA (overlap), PE, VOC without ACS (no infiltrate), ARDS, heart failure, fat embolism syndrome, pulm hemorrhage

sections.title: Management
items: Start empiric abx: ceftriaxone 2g IV + azithromycin 500mg IV/PO to cover typical + atypical triggers
Control pain aggressively & use incentive spirometry q2h while awake: pain/splinting drives atelectasis & worsens ACS
Give judicious IVF only; avoid overhydration. Add albuterol if wheezing or reactive airway component is present
Escalate transfusion based on severity: simple transfusion for moderate ACS, exchange transfusion for severe hypoxemia, multilobar disease, or rapid decline
Add supportive care: DVT prophylaxis, repeat CXR/oxygen assessment, & ICUlevel monitoring if worsening

pairs.value: New infiltrate required for dx. May lag behind clinical sx by 2448h. Repeat if initially negative but clinical concern
Hgb (↓ from baseline: transfusion threshold), reticulocyte count, WBC
HgbS%: guides exchange transfusion (target <30%)
Extended phenotype match: sickle pts have high alloimmunization rates
Hemolysis markers, renal function
If febrile: r/o bacteremia
Oxygenation, ventilation, A gradient
If PE suspected (can coexist). Incidental finding of in situ thrombosis common in SCD

drugs.name: Ceftriaxone
drugs.notes: Empiric coverage for typical bacteria (S. pneumo, H. flu). Combine with azithromycin

drugs.name: Azithromycin
drugs.notes: Atypical coverage (Mycoplasma, Chlamydia): common ACS triggers

drugs.name: Morphine / Hydromorphone
drugs.dose: Morphine 24mg IV q24h PRN or hydromorphone 0.51mg IV q34h PRN. Consider PCA
drugs.contraindications: Resp depression (monitor closely), severe hepatic failure
drugs.notes: Adequate pain control is critical: splinting: atelectasis: worsens ACS. Avoid meperidine (seizure risk in SCD). Avoid oversedation

drugs.name: Albuterol
drugs.notes: If wheezing or reactive airway component. ~30% of SCD pts have airway hyperreactivity

pairs.key: ICU / immediate higher level of care if
pairs.value: persistent respiratory distress, high-flow or NIV need, hemodynamic instability, vasopressor need, severe arrhythmia, airway risk, neurologic decline, or a trajectory suggesting pt may crash on the floor.

topics.title: Immune Thrombocytopenia (ITP)
topics.subtitle: Petechiae, mucosal bleeding, severe thrombocytopenia, otherwise normal coagulation

sections.title: Acute Management
items: Airway: check patency and protect. Breathing: check WOB, oxygenation, and ventilation. Circulation: check pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
check for life-threatening bleeding: ICH, GI hemorrhage, mucosal hemorrhage, hemodynamic instability
Type & screen, crossmatch if surgical intervention possible
If critical bleeding or plt <10,000 with active bleeding: platelet transfusion (even though consumed rapidly) + IVIG 1g/kg IV + methylprednisolone 1g IV daily x3 days + consider TXA

sections.title: Chart Check / Hx.
items: New vs chronic ITP (new: <3 months, persistent: 312 months, chronic: >12 months)
Meds: heparin ( r/o HIT), quinine, sulfonamides, vancomycin, GPIIb/IIIa inhibitors, vaccines
Recent infections: viral URI, HIV, HCV, H. pylori, CMV, EBV
Autoimmune dz: SLE (lupus), antiphospholipid syndrome, CLL, lymphoma
Pregnancy (gestational thrombocytopenia vs ITP vs HELLP vs TTP)
Prior plt counts: sudden ↓ vs chronic low baseline
Bleeding hx: mucocutaneous (petechiae, purpura, gingival bleeding, menorrhagia, epistaxis), GI, hematuria, ICH
Prior treatments for ITP: steroids, IVIG, antiD, splenectomy, TPO agonists
Family hx of bleeding disorders

sections.title: Exam
items: Mucocutaneous bleeding pattern: petechiae, purpura, ecchymoses (nonpalpable: palpable purpura: think vasculitis/TTP)
Wet purpura: blood blisters in oral mucosa: marker of severe disease & ↑ ICH risk
Gingival bleeding, epistaxis, menorrhagia
Splenomegaly: absence expected in ITP: if spleen palpable, consider or dx (lymphoma, liver dz, splenic sequestration)
Signs of underlying cause: lymphadenopathy (lymphoma), malar rash (SLE), hepatomegaly (liver dz)
Neuro exam: headache, AMS, focal deficits: r/o ICH (CT head now)
No joint hemarthroses or deep tissue bleeds (that's coagulation factor deficiency, not platelet disorder)

pairs.value: Immunemed. platelet destruction: autoantibodies (usually antiGPIIb/IIIa) target platelets for splenic destruction. Dx of exclusion: r/o or causes of thrombocytopenia
<10,000: spontaneous bleeding risk (esp ICH). <20,000: tx indicated. <30,000: tx if bleeding or procedure. >30,000 + no bleeding: may observe
Blood blisters in oral mucosa. Marker of severe ITP with ↑ risk of ICH. Warrants aggressive tx
Primary: isolated thrombocytopenia, no identifiable cause. Secondary: from underlying condition (SLE, HIV, HCV, drugs, lymphoma)

TTP/HUS (MAHA + thrombocytopenia + AKI/neuro sx), HIT (plt ↓ 5-10d after heparin), DIC (↑ PT/INR, ↓ fibrinogen), drug induced, bone marrow failure, splenic sequestration, pseudothrombocytopenia (EDTA artifact: check citrated tube)
SLE, antiphospholipid syndrome, HIV, HCV, H. pylori, CLL, lymphoma, common variable immunodeficiency, postvaccine
↓ Production: bone marrow failure, chemo, radiation, B12/folate deficiency, MDS. ↑ Destruction: ITP, TTP/HUS, HIT, DIC. Sequestration: hypersplenism, cirrhosis. Dilutional: massive transfusion
ITP: isolated plt ↓, nl. smear (or ↑ megakaryocytes). TTP: MAHA (schistocytes), ↑ LDH, ↓ haptoglobin. DIC: ↑ PT, ↓ fibrinogen. HIT: 5-10d postheparin

sections.title: Management
items: First decide if this is critical bleeding, noncritical bleeding, or isolated thrombocytopenia
If critical bleeding: give platelets + IVIG + high dose steroids now, hold anticoagulants/antiplatelets, & control the bleed source
If plt <30,000 with significant but nonlife-threatening bleeding: start dexamethasone 40mg PO/IV daily x4 days preferred, or prednisone 1 mg/kg/day
If plt <20,00030,000 without bleeding: treat with corticosteroids; if plt >30,000 & no bleeding or planned procedure: observation may reasonable
Avoid IM injections, rectal exams, & unnecessary procedures while platelets arelow

pairs.value: Isolated thrombocytopenia with nl. WBC & Hgb (unless bleeding). Smear: large platelets (young), NO schistocytes (schistocytes: TTP/DIC)
nl. (unless bleeding)
nl. in ITP: if abnl., consider DIC, liver dz, or coagulopathy
Mandatory: r/o schistocytes (TTP), blasts (leukemia), pseudothrombocytopenia (clumps)
r/o hemolysis (TTP/HUS/Evans syndrome)
If anemia present: Evans syndrome = ITP + AIHA
Screen all new ITP pts: secondary causes that change management
If SLE or APLS suspected
Eradication can improve plt count
Immediately if headache, AMS, or focal neuro deficit: r/o ICH

drugs.name: Dexamethasone
drugs.notes: Preferred first line over prednisone: faster plt response, shorter course. Plt rise in 24 days. Can repeat cycles

drugs.name: Prednisone
drugs.notes: Alternative to dexamethasone. Slower response, longer course, more side effects from prolonged use

drugs.name: IVIG
drugs.notes: For life-threatening bleeding or preprocedure. Rapid plt rise in 2448h (faster than steroids). Temporary effect (24 wks). Combine with steroids

drugs.name: Platelet transfusion
drugs.contraindications: Avoid if not actively bleeding (consumed rapidly, rarely effective alone)
drugs.notes: Only for life-threatening bleeding in ITP. Consumedbut provides transient hemostasis. Always combine with IVIG + steroids

drugs.name: Tranexamic acid (TXA)
drugs.contraindications: Active intravascular clotting, hematuria from upper urinary tract
drugs.notes: Adjunct for mucosal bleeding (epistaxis, menorrhagia, GI). Antifibrinolytic

drugs.name: Aminocaproic acid
drugs.notes: Alternative antifibrinolytic to TXA for mucosal bleeding

pairs.key: ICU / immediate higher level of care if
pairs.value: persistent respiratory distress, high-flow or NIV need, hemodynamic instability, vasopressor need, severe arrhythmia, airway risk, neurologic decline, or a trajectory suggesting pt may crash on the floor.

sections.title: References & Resources
items: Neunert C, Terrell DR, Arnold DM, et al. ASH 2019 Guidelines for Immune Thrombocytopenia. Blood Advances. 2019;3(23):38293866. https://doi.org/10.1182/bloodadvances.2019000966

Provan D, Arnold DM, Bussel JB, et al. Updated International Consensus Report on the Investigation and Management of Primary Immune Thrombocytopenia. Blood Advances. 2019;3(22):37803817. https://doi.org/10.1182/bloodadvances.2019000812

topics.title: TMA / TTP / HUS
topics.subtitle: MAHA, thrombocytopenia, AKI, neuro changes, hemolysis labs

sections.title: Acute Management
items: Airway: check patency and protect. Breathing: check WOB, oxygenation, and ventilation. Circulation: check pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
Plasma exchange (PLEX): definitive tx. Contact blood bank/apheresis team IMMEDIATELY.Don't wait for ADAMTS13 results
Do NOT transfuse platelets: worsens microvascular thrombosis & organ damage. Exception: life-threatening bleeding only
IVF, monitor UOP, check Cr q612h (renal involvement)

sections.title: Chart Check / Hx.
items: Acute onset: AMS, petechiae/purpura, fatigue, dark urine, ↓ UOP, Abd pain
Preceding diarrhea (bloody: Shiga toxin HUS: E. coli O157:H7, Shigella)
meds: quinine, calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus, cyclosporine), gemcitabine, bevacizumab, ticlopidine, clopidogrel
Pregnancy / postpartum (TTP, HELLP, preeclampsia overlap)
HIV, autoimmune dz (SLE: secondary TMA)
Transplant: BMT or solid organ (calcineurin inhibitor toxicity)
Recurrent TTP episodes (relapsing acquired TTP: may need rituximab)
Family hx (congenital TTP: UpshawSchulman syndrome, hereditary ADAMTS13 deficiency)

sections.title: Exam
items: Classic TTP pentad (FAT RN): Fever, Anemia (MAHA), Thrombocytopenia, Renal dysfunction, Neurologic sx. All 5 rarely present: do NOT wait for full pentad
Neuro: AMS, headache, seizures, focal deficits, visual changes, coma ( present in ~60%)
Skin: petechiae, purpura, jaundice (hemolysis)
Renal: ↓ UOP, edema, HTN (more prominent in HUS)
Abd: pain, N/V, bloody diarrhea (Shigatoxin HUS)
Cardiac: chest pain, arrhythmia (myocardial microthrombi: underrecognized cause of death)
Pallor, tachycardia, fatigue (severe anemia from MAHA)

pairs.value: Thrombotic microangiopathy: umbrella term for MAHA + thrombocytopenia + organ damage from microvascular thrombosis. Includes TTP, HUS, & or causes
Acquired autoimmune (antiADAMTS13 antibodies) or congenital (UpshawSchulman). ADAMTS13 <10% = TTP. Deficiency: ultralarge vWF multimers: platelet microthrombi
Microangiopathic hemolytic anemia: schistocytes on smear (fragmented RBCs), ↑ LDH, ↓ haptoglobin, ↑ indirect bilirubin, negative DAT
Typical HUS: bloody diarrhea: E. coli O157:H7 or Shigella. Mainly pediatric. Supportive care only: PLEX & abx NOT beneficial. Avoid antidiarrheals
Complementmed. TMA. Primarily renal. Tx: eculizumab (antiC5 monoclonal ab). PLEX is bridge only
Predicts ADAMTS13 <10%. Score <5 = effectively r/o TTP (99% NPV). Factors: plt <30K, hemolysis, no cancer, no transplant, MCV <90, INR <1.5, Cr <2.0
Acquired autoimmune (most common adult TMA), congenital ADAMTS13 deficiency (UpshawSchulman), pregnancyassociated
Shigatoxin (E. coli O157, Shigella), complementmed. (aHUS), drug induced
Calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus, cyclosporine), quinine, gemcitabine, bevacizumab, mitomycin C, ticlopidine
TTP, HUS, DIC (↑ PT, ↓ fibrinogen), HELLP syndrome, malignant HTN, scleroderma renal crisis, catastrophic APLS, prosthetic valve hemolysis, disseminated cancer
TTP: nl. PT/INR & fibrinogen. DIC: ↑ PT/INR, ↓ fibrinogen, ↑ Ddimer. Both have MAHA & thrombocytopenia
TTP: neuro predominant, ADAMTS13 <10%. HUS: renal predominant, ± bloody diarrhea, ADAMTS13 nl.

sections.title: General TMA Management
items: MAHA + thrombocytopenia = TTP until proven otherwise while you send confirmatory studies
Stop potential triggers: quinine, calcineurin inhibitors, gemcitabine, bevacizumab, clopidogrel / ticlopidine, offending chemo or transplant meds
Support endorgan perfusion: isotonic IVF if not overloaded, strict I&Os, telemetry, serial CBC / LDH / Cr
RBC transfusion is appropriate for symptomatic anemia; avoid platelet transfusion unless life-threatening hemorrhage or urgent procedure
Involve hematology early for all suspected TMAs, plus nephrology if AKI / dialysis risk & blood bank / apheresis if TTP remains on table

sections.title: TTP Management
items: If TTP suspected: treat empirically: mortality >90% untreatedDon't wait for ADAMTS13 result
Start PLEX now & continue daily until plt >150K for 2 days & hemolysis resolves
Give steroids: methylprednisolone 1g IV daily x3 days, then prednisone 1mg/kg/day
Add caplacizumab 11mg IV before first PLEX, then 11mg SC daily after each PLEX & continue 30 days after PLEX completion
If PLEX is delayed, give FFP 1530 mL/kg as bridge after drawing ADAMTS13 & inhibitor studies
Consider rituximab early for refractory, relapsing, autoimmune, or severe disease with slow platelet recovery

sections.title: Shigatoxin HUS Management
items: Supportive care is definitive tx. Careful isotonic IVF early, monitor UOP, daily weights, electrolytes, CBC, LDH, & Cr
Avoid antibiotics & antimotility agents in suspected enterohemorrhagic E. coli: may increase toxin exposure & worsen HUS
Manage complications: transfuse RBCs for symptomatic anemia, treat severe HTN, correct electrolytes, & start dialysis if needed
Do NOT use routine PLEX or eculizumab for typical Shigatoxin HUS unless another TMA phenotype is emerging
Escalate level of care for worsening AKI, encephalopathy, severe colitis, fluid overload, or dialysis need

sections.title: Atypical HUS Management
items: Suspect aHUS when renalpredominant TMA has nl. / nearnl. ADAMTS13 & no convincing Shigatoxin trigger
Start eculizumab (or ravulizumab if your center has it per local protocol) as definitive complement blockade once meningococcal prophylaxis plan is addressed
PLEX is not definitive for aHUS, but may used only as a temporary bridge while diagnosis is clarified & complement inhibition is arranged
Involve nephrology early: many pts need dialysis support & close renal recovery monitoring
Screen for complementmed. / secondary triggers: pregnancy, transplant, infection, autoimmune disease, malignant HTN, & offending meds

pairs.value: <10% = TTP. 1020% = equivocal. >20% = not TTP. Draw BEFORE starting PLEX. Also send ADAMTS13 inhibitor/antibody
Calculate now: plt <30K, hemolysis markers, no active cancer, no transplant, MCV <90, INR <1.5, Cr <2.0. Score <5 effectively r/o TTP
Schistocytes (fragmented RBCs): hallmark of MAHA. Also: polychromasia, ↓ plt
↑↑ LDH (often >1000), ↓↓ haptoglobin (often undetectable), ↑ indirect bilirubin, ↑ reticulocytes, negative DAT
PT/INR & fibrinogen nl. in TTP. If abnl.: think DIC
Renal function: AKI more prominent in HUS
Shiga toxin PCR / E. coli O157 culture if bloody diarrhea
C3, C4, Factor H, Factor I: if aHUS suspected
Cardiac microthrombi: underrecognized cause of mortality

drugs.name: Plasma exchange (PLEX)
drugs.dose: 11.5 plasma volumes daily until plt >150K x2 days + LDH returning to normal
drugs.contraindications: None absolute in TTP (lifesaving). Hemodynamic instability (relative)
drugs.notes: Definitive tx for TTP. Removes antiADAMTS13 antibodies & ultralarge vWF multimers. Replaces with donor ADAMTS13. Start ASAP —don't wait for ADAMTS13 result

drugs.name: Methylprednisolone (pulse)
drugs.notes: Suppresses autoantibody production. Start concurrent with PLEX

drugs.name: Caplacizumab
drugs.dose: 11mg IV bolus (before first PLEX),then11mg SC daily (after each PLEX) + 30 days after PLEX series ends
drugs.notes: AntivWF nanobody: blocks plateletvWF interaction. ↓ Thrombotic events, ↓ exacerbations, ↓ time to plt recovery. ↑ Bleeding risk: monitor closely

drugs.name: Rituximab
drugs.notes: For refractory or relapsing TTP. Depletes antiADAMTS13 antibodyproducing B cells. Takes 12 wks for effect

drugs.name: FFP (fresh frozen plasma)
drugs.notes: Bridge only until PLEX available. Provides ADAMTS13 enzyme. Not substitute for PLEX

drugs.name: Eculizumab
drugs.contraindications: Unvaccinated for meningococcus (must vaccinate + prophylactic abx)
drugs.notes: For atypical HUS (complementmed.). AntiC5 monoclonal antibody. Not for TTP or Shigatoxin HUS

pairs.key: ICU / immediate higher level of care if
pairs.value: persistent respiratory distress, high-flow or NIV need, hemodynamic instability, vasopressor need, severe arrhythmia, airway risk, neurologic decline, or a trajectory suggesting pt may crash on the floor.

sections.title: References & Resources
items: Zheng XL, Vesely SK, Cataland SR, et al. ISTH Guidelines for Treatment of Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura. Journal of Thrombosis and Haemostasis. 2020;18(10):24962502. https://doi.org/10.1111/jth.15010

Scully M, Cataland S, Coppo P, et al. Consensus on the Standardization of Terminology in Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura and Related Thrombotic Microangiopathies. Journal of Thrombosis and Haemostasis. 2017;15(2):312322. https://doi.org/10.1111/jth.13571
Coppo P, Cuker A, George JN. Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura: Toward Targeted Therapy and Precision Medicine. Research and Practice in Thrombosis and Haemostasis. 2019;3(1):2637.

topics.title: HLH
topics.subtitle: Fever, cytopenias, hepatosplenomegaly, hyperferritinemia, multiorgan failure

sections.title: Acute Management
items: Airway: check patency and protect. Breathing: check WOB, oxygenation, and ventilation. Circulation: check pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
ABCs: many pts need ICU (MV/pressors in ~70% of ICU admissions)
Stat labs: CBC, CMP, ferritin, fibrinogen, triglycerides, LDH, sCD25 (sIL2R), Ddimer
Lifethreatening hyperinflammatory emergency: high mortality if untreated

sections.title: Chart Check / Hx.
items: Known malignancy (esp lymphoma: 76% of malignant triggers), recent chemo/immunothetherapy
Autoimmune dz (SLE = 39% of autoimmune triggered HLH; sJIA, AOSD)
Recent infections: EBV (worst viral prognosis, 5yr survival 25%), CMV, HIV
Immunodeficiency, transplant history, immunosuppressant use
Family hx: consanguinity, infant deaths, primary HLH (median onset 3–6 months)
CART or bispecific Ab thetherapy (CRS: HLH overlap)

sections.title: Exam
items: Persistent fever (≥38.5°C) unresponsive to abx
Hepatosplenomegaly (common; unusual in sepsis: distinguishing feature)
Lymphadenopathy
Petechiae, purpura, maculopapular rash
Jaundice, RUQ tenderness (hepatic involvement in ~60% adults)
Neuro: AMS, seizures, ataxia, meningism (~1/3 at dx; ↑ mortality)
Signs of multiorgan failure: pulmonary, renal, GI

pairs.value: Hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis: overwhelming immune activation with uncontrolled macrophage/Tcell proliferation: cytokine storm: multiorgan failure
Inherited defects in cytotoxic lymphocyte function (PRF1, UNC13D, STX11, STXBP2). Mainly infants. Requires HSCT for cure
Acquired; triggered by infection, malignancy, or autoimmune dz. Adults: mean age ~50, M:F 1.7:1
Macrophage activation syndrome: form of secondary HLH in autoimmune dz (SLE, sJIA, AOSD)

Need ≥5/8: fever ≥38.5°C, splenomegaly, bicytopenia (Hb <90, plt <100, ANC <1.0), hypertriglyceridemia (≥3.0 mmol/L) or hypofibrinogenemia (≤1.5 g/L), hemophagocytosis on biopsy, ferritin ≥500, ↓/absent NK activity, sCD25 ≥2400 U/mL. 4 criteria = 95% sensitivity, 94% specificity
Weighted scoring (0–337) using 9 variables. Score ≥168 = 100% sensitivity, 94% specificity. Better validated for adults
EBV (most common viral), CMV, HIV, adenovirus, bacterial sepsis. EBVHLH: 5yr survival only 25%
Lymphoma (76% of malignant triggers), esp T/NKcell, AITL, intravascular DLBCL. sCD25:ferritin ratio ≥2.0 = 85% PPV for lymphomaHLH. Worst prognosis (20–30% 2yr survival)
SLE (39%), sJIA, AOSD. AOSD:high CRP, IL18 >18,550 pg/mL (90% Sn, 94% Sp vs HLH)
Shares fever, cytopenias, MOF. HLH distinguished by: hepatosplenomegaly, hypofibrinogenemia, hypertriglyceridemia, markedly ↑ sCD25, CD38²⁰/HLADR+ >7% of CD8+ Tcells
Spiking fever, rash, arthralgia, ↑↑ ferritin. CRP >130 + sCD25 <3900 = 91% Sn/93% Sp for AOSD vs HLH
DIC, TTP, MODS, histiocytic malignancies, inborn errors of metabolism (infants)

sections.title: Management
items: Check ferritin early: it is almost always elevated; >500 supports HLH and >10,000 is more specific in adults.
Apply HLH-2004 criteria or an adult HScore; send sCD25, NK-cell activity, and perforin or CD107a flow cytometry when appropriate.
Identify the trigger immediately: infection, malignancy, rheumatologic disease or MAS, or familial or genetic HLH.
Give ICU-level supportive care when organ dysfunction, respiratory failure, or shock is present; start empiric antimicrobials if infection is possible and support cytopenias or coagulopathy.
If disease is severe or progressive, start immunomodulation while the workup continues: high-dose methylprednisolone 10-30 mg/kg/day (max 1 g) or dexamethasone 10 mg/m²/day.
Add anakinra 2-10 mg/kg/day early when suspicion remains high; IVIG 1-2 g/kg is another adjunct when appropriate.
Primary or familial HLH usually needs an HLH-2004-type regimen with etoposide, dexamethasone, and cyclosporine as a bridge to HSCT.
Secondary HLH may respond to glucocorticoids with or without IVIG or anakinra, but severe, progressive, or CNS disease should prompt etoposide-based therapy.
check for CNS involvement and do not delay systemic treatment for MRI or LP; intrathecal methotrexate plus corticosteroids may be needed when CNS disease is confirmed.
Trend clinical status daily and ferritin, CBC, LFTs, coagulation studies, and triglycerides at least twice weekly; worsening ferritin, cytopenias, hepatitis, or DIC means recheck and escalate.
For refractory disease, discuss emapalumab, ruxolitinib, or alemtuzumab with hematology.
Consult hematology or oncology urgently and involve rheumatology, infectious disease, critical care, and HSCT teams early.

pairs.value: CBC with diff, CMP, LDH, ferritin, fibrinogen, triglycerides, Ddimer, PT/PTT, CRP, lactate
sCD25 (sIL2R), CXCL9, NK cell activity, flow cytometry (perforin, CD107a degranulation). IL18 if AOSD suspected
EBV PCR, CMV PCR, adenovirus PCR, HIV, blood cultures, fungal markers (if immunosuppressed)
Aspirate preferred over biopsy (84% vs 64% sensitivity for hemophagocytosis). R/o malignancy. May be absent early
CT C/A/P (r/o malignancy, occult infection). FDGPET if lymphoma suspected (may present without discrete lesions on CT)
Brain MRI (multifocal WM lesions, cerebellar involvement) + CSF analysis (cell count, protein, glucose) if neuro sx, age <1 yr, or known genetic dz
NGS panel or WES early in pediatric cases or young adults. Flow for perforin/CD107a for rapid screening

drugs.name: Methylprednisolone
drugs.notes: Firstline per EULAR/ACR 2022. Alt: dexamethasone 10 mg/m²/d (better CNS penetration).

drugs.name: Anakinra
drugs.notes: IL1RA. Early immunomodulation with minimal immunosuppression. Favorable safety at high doses.

drugs.name: Etoposide
drugs.notes: For severe/refractory dz or CNS involvement. Ageadjusted dosing. Core of HLH94/2004 protocols.

drugs.name: Cyclosporine
drugs.notes: Part of HLH2004 protocol for primary HLH. Monitor levels, renal function.

drugs.name: Emapalumab
drugs.notes: AntiIFNγ mAb. FDAapproved for primary HLH. 57% preHSCT survival in refractory dz.

drugs.name: IVIG
drugs.notes: Immunomodulatory adjunct. May be used early alongside steroids.

pairs.key: ICU / immediate higher level of care if
pairs.value: persistent respiratory distress, high-flow or NIV need, hemodynamic instability, vasopressor need, severe arrhythmia, airway risk, neurologic decline, or a trajectory suggesting pt may crash on the floor.

sections.title: References & Resources
items: Hsu JI, Nikiforow S, Berliner N. Hemophagocytic Lymphohistiocytosis in Adults. Blood. 2025.
Henter JI. Hemophagocytic Lymphohistiocytosis.thenEngl J Med. 2025.
Shakoory B, et al. 2022 EULAR/ACR Points to Consider for HLH/MAS. Arthritis Rheumatol. 2023.
RamosCasals M, et al. Adult Haemophagocytic Syndrome. Lancet. 2014.
La Rosée P, Horne A, Hines M, et al. Recommendations for the Management of Hemophagocytic Lymphohistiocytosis in Adults. Blood. 2019;133(23):24652477. https://doi.org/10.1182/blood.2018874828

Jordan MB, Allen CE, Greenberg J, et al. Challenges in the Diagnosis of Hemophagocytic Lymphohistiocytosis: Recommendations from the North American Consortium for Histiocytosis (NACHO). Pediatric Blood & Cancer. 2019;66(11):e27929. https://doi.org/10.1002/pbc.27929

Henter JI, Horne A, Aricó M, et al. HLH2004: Diagnostic and Thetherapeutic Guidelines for Hemophagocytic Lymphohistiocytosis. Pediatric Blood & Cancer. 2007;48(2):124131. https://doi.org/10.1002/pbc.21039

topics.title: Malignant Spinal Cord Compression
topics.subtitle: Back pain, weakness, sensory loss, urinary retention, cancer history

sections.title: Acute Management
items: Airway: check patency and protect. Breathing: check WOB, oxygenation, and ventilation. Circulation: check pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
Immediately start dexamethasone 10mg IV: do NOT delay for imaging
Get emergent MRI of entire spine (whole spine: multifocal in 1038%)
Consult radiation oncology STAT for emergent radiothetherapy

sections.title: Chart Check / Hx.
items: Known cancer dx & type (breast, lung, prostate, renal, thyroid most common)
Back pain Hx.: 90% present with back pain, worse when supine (unlike degenerative pain)
Motor weakness (75%), sensory changes, bowel/bladder dysfunction
Ambulatory status before onset: strongest predictor of outcome
Prior spinal radiation or surgery
Current oncologic tx regimen
Hx. of DVT/PE

sections.title: Exam
items: Point tenderness over spine at level of compression
Motor exam: grade strength in all extremities, note level
Sensory level: pinprick & light touch: identify dermatomal level
Rectal exam: ↓ rectal tone = cauda equina involvement
Reflexes: upper motor neuron signs (hyperreflexia, Babinski) above conus; lower motor neuron signs (areflexia) in cauda equina
Gait assessment if safe: ambulatory status is key prognostic factor
Bladder scan for post void residual (>200mL suggests retention)

pairs.value: Compression of spinal cord or cauda equina by direct tumor extension or vertebral body collapse in setting of metastatic or primary spinal malignancy
5-10% of cancer pts; most common in breast, lung, prostate, renal cell, & thyroid cancers
Thoracic spine 70%, lumbosacral 20%, cervical 10%
Neurological outcome depends on pretx function: once paraplegia is established, recovery is rare
MCC: tumor extends from vertebral body into epidural space
Vertebral body collapse compresses cord: lytic lesions from myeloma, breast, lung, renal, thyroid
Paraspinal tumors (lymphoma, neuroblastoma) growing through neural foramen
Fever, elevated WBC/ESR, IV drug use Hx.
Anticoagulation, recent procedure
Inflammatory, MRI shows cord signal without epidural mass
Mechanical back pain, radiculopathy distribution

sections.title: Management
items: Immediate stabilization: keep spinal precautions, continue dexamethasone 4mg IV/PO q6h after the 10mg IV load, & treat pain without delaying serial neuro exams

Definitive planning: get MRI whole spine & call radiation oncology plus neurosurgery / spine surgery now. Surgery is favored for instability, singlelevel disease, retropulsed bone, radioresistant tumor, or worsening deficits; otherwise move quickly to radiation
Supportive care: Foley if retention, keep NPO if surgery possible, give DVT prophylaxis if safe, & document serial motor / sensory / bowel bladder exams
Continue dexamethasone 4mg IV/PO q6h: begin taper after radiation starts
Radiothetherapy:30 Gy in 10 fractions or 20 Gy in 5 fractions
Surgery (decompressive laminectomy ± stabilization) if: single level compression, radioresistant tumor, spinal instability, neurological deterioration during radiation
Maintain DVT prophylaxis throughout hospitalization
Physical therapy & rehabilitation consultation
Monitor neurological exam q46h during acute phase
GI prophylaxis (PPI) while on high dose steroids
Glucose monitoring q6h: steroidinduced hyperglycemia common

pairs.value: Gold standard: identifies level, extent, & multifocal disease (1038%)
Alternative if MRI contraindicated (pacemaker, etc.)
Baseline labs, check renal function for contrast
Elevated in infection: helps differentiate epidural abscess
check for urinary retention
Low sensitivity (~60%): do NOT use to r/o MSCC

drugs.name: Dexamethasone
drugs.notes: Give now if MSCC suspected: before imaging. ↓ vasogenic edema. Taper over 2 weeks after definitive tx.

drugs.name: Dexamethasone (high dose)
drugs.dose: 96mg IV bolus,then24mg IV q6h x 3 days
drugs.notes: Consider for progressive deficits or nearcomplete block on MRI. Higher complication rate.

drugs.name: Enoxaparin
drugs.notes: DVT prophylaxis: immobilized oncology pts are extremely risk for VTE.

pairs.value: persistent respiratory distress, high-flow or NIV need, hemodynamic instability, vasopressor need, severe arrhythmia, airway risk, neurologic decline, or a trajectory suggesting pt may crash on the floor.

topics.title: SVC Syndrome
topics.subtitle: Facial swelling, venous distention, dyspnea, upperextremity edema

sections.title: Acute Management
items: Airway: check patency and protect. Breathing: check WOB, oxygenation, and ventilation. Circulation: check pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
check & secure airway: may difficult from upper airway edema; prepare for difficult airway
Elevate head of bed to 3045°: ↓ venous pressure & facial edema
Supplemental O2 as needed: monitor for respiratory decompensation
Emergent CT chest with IV contrast (via lower extremity IV) to define Obst.
Get tissue dx if not previously established: do not delay tx for biopsy if critically ill

sections.title: Chart Check / Hx.
items: Known malignancy: lung cancer, lymphoma, mediastinal tumors most common
Central venous catheters, ports, pacemaker/defibrillator leads
Symptom onset & progression: gradual (weeks) vs acute (days)
Dyspnea, facial/neck swelling worse when leaning forward or lying flat
Headache, visual changes, confusion (signs of cerebral edema)
Dysphagia, hoarseness, stridor
Prior radiation to chest
Anticoagulation status

sections.title: Exam
items: Facial plethora & edema: classic finding, worse in morning
Neck & upper extremity venous distension: nonpulsatile
Upper extremity & h& edema (b/l)
Pemberton sign: facial flushing/venous distension when arms raised above head
Chest wall collateral veins (dilated superficial veins)
Stridor or respiratory distress: suggests airway compromise
Mentation changes: suggests cerebral edema
Papilledema on fundoscopic exam

pairs.value: Obst. of blood flow through superior vena cava causing facial/upper extremity edema, dyspnea, & venous distension
Malignancy (6085%): lung cancer (most common), lymphoma, mediastinal tumors. Thrombosis (1040%): central lines, pacemaker leads
External compression, intraluminal thrombus, or direct tumor invasion of SVC
Airway compromise & cerebral edema are life-threatening: manage airway FIRST
Most common malignant cause (50%+), esp rightsided tumors
Second most common malignancy causing SVC syndrome
Mediastinal masses in younger pts
Increasing incidence with central lines, ports, device leads
Acute facial/lip/tongue swelling: check for ACEinhibitor use, C4 level
JVD & dyspnea, but with pulsus paradoxus & diffuse edema
Chronic venous congestion, pericardial calcification

sections.title: Management
items: Keep HOB up, give O2 PRN, & use LE IV access when able
Call IR early for SVC stent if severe sx or airway/CNS compromise
Call onc/RT for tumordirected tx; if thrombus is present, start AC if safe
If stridor, cerebral edema, or HD instability: ICU + urgent airway / definitive intervention
SVC stenting (interventional radiology): Fastest sx relief —within hours, >90% success rate
Radiation therapy: for radiosensitive tumors (lymphoma, small cell lung cancer)
Chemothetherapy: for chemosensitive tumors (lymphoma, germ cell, small cell)
Anticoagulation: continue for thrombusrelated SVC syndrome; duration depends on etiology
Monitor for cerebral edema: neurological checks q4h
Maintain head of bed elevation
Continue all meds & fluids via lower extremity access
Treat underlying malignancy per oncology

pairs.value: Via lower extremity IV: defines level/extent of Obst. & guides stenting
May be added to define thrombus extent
Baseline labs; LDH may suggest lymphoma
If thrombosis suspected
Essential for undiagnosed malignancy: coordinate with interventional radiology or surgery
Mediastinal widening, pleural effusion: but CT is definitive

drugs.name: Dexamethasone
drugs.notes: Most effective for lymphoma & thymoma. Questionable benefit for or solid tumors: may delay tissue dx.

drugs.name: Furosemide
drugs.notes: May provide temporary sx relief. Questionable efficacy: use cautiously as pts may already be volumedepleted.

drugs.name: Heparin (UFH)
drugs.notes: If thrombus identified on CT. Goal aPTT 6080 seconds.

drugs.name: Enoxaparin
drugs.notes: Alternative to UFH for SVC thrombosis if hemodynamically stable.

pairs.value: persistent respiratory distress, high-flow or NIV need, hemodynamic instability, vasopressor need, severe arrhythmia, airway risk, neurologic decline, or a trajectory suggesting pt may crash on the floor.

topics.title: Tumor Lysis Syndrome
topics.subtitle: AKI, hyperkalemia, hyperphosphatemia, hypocalcemia, high uric acid

sections.title: Acute Management
items: Airway: check patency and protect. Breathing: check WOB, oxygenation, and ventilation. Circulation: check pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
Monitor electrolytes (K, Ca, PO4, uric acid) q46h
cardiac telemetry: ↑K causes fatal arrhythmias
Treat ↑K emergently if K >6 or ECG changes (see ↑K topic)

sections.title: Chart Check / Hx.
items: Cancer type & tumor burden: Burkitt, ALL, AML (high WBC), bulky NHL highest risk
Recent chemotherapy initiation (1272h prior), including targeted therapies & immunotherapies
Spontaneous TLS possible in highburden tumors without tx
Baseline GFR: pre-existing CKD ↑ risk dramatically
meds: allopurinol prophylaxis? NSAIDs? ACEinhibitors?
Volume status & recent UOP
G6PD status: IDEALLY check before giving rasburicase
Symptoms: nausea, vomiting, muscle cramps, tetany, seizures, palpitations

sections.title: Exam
items: Volume status: JVD, lung crackles (overload), or dry mucous membranes (dehydration)
Cardiac: irregular rhythm, tachycardia (↑K)
Neuromuscular: Chvostek sign, Trousseau sign (hypocalcemia), muscle cramps, weakness
Neurologic: AMS, seizures (severe hypocalcemia or uremia)
Abd: flank pain (uric acid nephropathy), ↓ UOP
Skin: petechiae, bruising (DIC in severe cases)

pairs.value: rapid release of intracellular contents (K, PO4, uric acid, nucleic acids) from tumor cell death
CairoBishop criteria: ≥2 of: uric acid ≥8, K ≥6, PO4 ≥4.5, Ca ≤7 (or 25% change from baseline)
Laboratory TLS + renal failure, cardiac arrhythmia, seizure, or death
Burkitt lymphoma, ALL, AML with high WBC, bulky NHL, CLL with alemtuzumab
Typically 1272h after initiation of chemotherapy: can occur spontaneously in hightumorburden malignancies
Most common: 1272h after starting tx for hematologic malignancies
Hightumorburden malignancies without tx; Burkitt lymphoma, ALL with high WBC
Venetoclax, obinutuzumab, lenalidomide: risk may underappreciated
Similar electrolyte pattern (hyperK, hyperPO4, hyperuricemia): check CK
Contrast nephropathy, Obst., sepsis: does not have classic electrolyte pattern
↑K, ↓ BP: but with hyponatremia (not seen in TLS)

sections.title: Management
items: Start aggressive NS: ~200250 mL/h, goal UOP ≥2 mL/kg/h; strict I&O, Foley if needed
Give rasburicase for high risk or established TLS; avoid K/phos in IVF & diet
Check/treat lytes serially: Ca for ECG changes, insulin/D50 for ↑K, phosphate binders PRN
Call nephrology early for rising Cr, oliguria, refractory lyte issues, or for RRT
aggressive IVF: recheck volume status frequently
Repeat electrolytes q46h until stable & trending toward nl. for 24h
Rasburicase: recheck uric acid at 4h: repeat dose if still elevated (on ice!)

Emergent dialysis indications: K >6 refractory to medical management, Cr >10, uric acid >10 with oliguria, PO4 >10, symptomatic volume overload, consulting Nephrology service, esp if calciumphosphorus product continues to rise > 60 mg2/dL
Avoid calcium supplementation unless symptomatic: calciumphosphate precipitation causes renal damage
Hold chemotherapy until electrolytes & renal function stabilize
Transition to allopurinol prophylaxis once acute episode resolves
Restart chemotherapy with lower intensity once TLS resolved (discuss with oncology)

pairs.value: K, Ca, PO4, BUN/Cr: critical electrolytes to trend
≥8 mg/dL meets CairoBishop criteria. Send on ice if rasburicase given
Markedly elevated: reflects tumor cell death. Useful for trending
↑K changes: peaked T waves, widened QRS, sine wave. Prolonged QT from hypocalcemia
Check BEFORE rasburicase: causes fatal hemolysis in G6PDdeficient pts
Required for all TLS pts: fatal arrhythmias from ↑K
Target UOP ≥2 mL/kg/h

drugs.name: Rasburicase (Elitek)
drugs.notes: Recombinant urate oxidase —converts uric acid to allantoin. Must send uric acid on ice (enzyme continues working in tube at room temp, giving falsely low results)Repeat single dose of rasburicase for uric acid rise to ≥ 7.5 mg/dL.

drugs.name: Allopurinol
drugs.notes: Prevention/mild TLS: blocks xanthine oxidase to prevent uric acid formation. Does NOT lower existing uric acid. Start 2448h before chemo.

drugs.name: nl. saline
drugs.notes: Cornerstone of TLS management. Target UOP ≥3L/24h. Promotes renal excretion of uric acid, K, & PO4.

drugs.name: Calcium gluconate
drugs.notes: Only for symptomatic hypocalcemia (tetany, seizures, QT prolongation). Avoid routine supplementation: may precipitate calciumphosphate crystals in kidneys.

drugs.name: Sevelamer
drugs.notes: Phosphate binder for hyperphosphatemia. Use with aggressive hydration.

pairs.key: ICU / immediate higher level of care if
pairs.value: persistent respiratory distress, high-flow or NIV need, hemodynamic instability, vasopressor need, severe arrhythmia, airway risk, neurologic decline, or a trajectory suggesting pt may crash on the floor.
