topics.title: Hyperviscosity Syndrome
topics.subtitle: Headache, vision changes, bleeding, AMS, mucosal congestion

sections.title: Acute Management
items: Airway: check patency and protect. Breathing: check WOB, oxygenation, and ventilation. Circulation: check pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
Emergent plasmapheresis: definitive tx for immunoglobulinmed. hyperviscosity
Protect airway if AMS
Avoid packed RBC transfusion until viscosity lowered: ↑ viscosity & can precipitate crisis

sections.title: Chart Check / Hx.
items: Known hematologic malignancy: Waldenström, myeloma, polycymia vera, leukemia
CNS sx: headache, confusion, dizziness, strokelike sx, coma
Visual changes: blurred vision, diplopia, visual loss
Bleeding: mucosal (epistaxis, gingival), GI, skin
Constitutional sx: fatigue, weight loss, night sweats
Known paraprotein type & level (IgM MCC)
Prior plasmapheresis txs

sections.title: Exam
items: Neurologic: AMS, focal deficits, nystagmus, ataxia
Fundoscopic: sausagelink retinal veins (pathognomonic), flame hemorrhages, papilledema
Mucosal bleeding: epistaxis, gingival bleeding, oral mucosal petechiae
Skin: purpura, ecchymoses
Lymphadenopathy, hepatosplenomegaly (underlying hematologic malignancy)
Heart failure signs: highoutput failure from ↑ blood viscosity
Raynaud phenomenon, livedo reticularis (cryoglobulinemia)

pairs.value: Symptomatic ↑ in blood viscosity causing impaired microcirculatory blood flow: hematologic emergency
CNS disturbances + bleeding + visual changes
Most common: Waldenström macroglobulinemia (IgM paraprotein). Also: multiple myeloma (IgA > IgG), polycymia vera, leukostasis (AML/CML blast crisis)
Symptomsoccur when serum viscosity >45 cP (nl. 1.41.8 cP)
IgM paraprotein: MCC of symptomatic hyperviscosity (1030% of WM pts)
IgA > IgG3 > IgG1: polymerizes & ↑ viscosity. Less common than WM
Elevated hematocrit causes hyperviscosity: phlebotomy is tx
WBC >100k (AML, CML blast crisis): sludging in microcirculation, esp lungs & brain
Similar neurologic presentation: check serum viscosity & protein electrophoresis
Bleeding + neurologic changes: but low fibrinogen, elevated Ddimer
MAHA, thrombocytopenia, neuro changes: check schistocytes, ADAMTS13

sections.title: Management
items: Start IV NS & avoid diuresis unless the pt is volume overloaded
Definitive tx depends on cause: plasmapheresis for paraproteinemia, leukapheresis for leukostasis, phlebotomy for PV
Call heme/onc STAT for urgent apheresis / cytoreduction planning
Avoid antiplatelets; treat bleeding supportively, including plt transfusion if clinically needed
Plasmapheresis:daily until sx resolve & viscosity returns to normal
Definitive tx: chemotherapy for underlying malignancy (rituximab, BTK inhibitors for WM; bortezomib based for myeloma)
Avoid rituximab initially in WM: causes IgM flare & worsen hyperviscosity
Continue IV hydration between plasmapheresis sessions
Monitor viscosity daily (or surrogate: total protein/IgM level)
Serial neurologic & fundoscopic exams
DVT prophylaxis once bleeding controlled

pairs.value: nl. 1.41.8 cP. Symptomatic>45 cP. >6 cP = emergency
Identifies paraprotein type & amount
WBC >100k suggests leukostasis; elevated Hct suggests polycymia
PT/INR may elevated from paraprotein interference
Spurious hypoxemia from paraprotein interference: check ABG if SpO2 discordant with clinical status
check renal function & tumor burden
r/o ICH if focal neurologic deficits

drugs.name: nl. saline
drugs.notes: Temporizing measure until plasmapheresis available. ↓ viscosity by dilution.

drugs.name: Hydroxyurea
drugs.notes: For leukostasis —↓ WBC count. Used as bridge to definitive chemotherapy.

drugs.name: Dexamethasone
drugs.notes: May be part of initial myeloma/Waldenström tx. Discuss with hematology.

pairs.key: ICU / immediate higher level of care if
pairs.value: persistent respiratory distress, high-flow or NIV need, hemodynamic instability, vasopressor need, severe arrhythmia, airway risk, neurologic decline, or a trajectory suggesting pt may crash on the floor.

sections.title: References & Resources
items: Stone MJ, Bogen SA. EvidenceBased Focused Review of Management of Hyperviscosity Syndrome. Blood. 2012;119(10):22052208. https://doi.org/10.1182/blood-2011-04-347690

Dimopoulos MA, Kastritis E, Owen RG, et al. Treatment Recommendations for Patients with Waldenström Macroglobulinemia (WM) and Related Disorders: IWWM7 Consensus. Blood. 2014;124(9):14041411. https://doi.org/10.1182/blood-2014-03-565135

topics.title: Cytokine Release Syndrome
topics.subtitle: Fever, hypotension, hypoxemia, capillary leak after immunothetherapy

sections.title: Acute Management
items: Airway: check patency and protect. Breathing: check WOB, oxygenation, and ventilation. Circulation: check pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
Grade 1 (fever only): supportive care, acetaminophen, monitor closely
Grade 2 (↓ BP /hypoxia): IV fluid bolus, tocilizumab 8mg/kg IV (max 800mg), consider ICU transfer
Monitor for ICANS (immune effector cell associated neurotoxicity syndrome): altered consciousness, aphasia, seizures

sections.title: Chart Check / Hx.
items: Type & date of immunothetherapy: CART (which product: axicel, tisacel, lisocel, etc.), bispecific antibody, checkpoint inhibitor
Day postinfusion: CART CRSday 114
Tumor burden: higher burden = higher risk & severity of CRS
Baseline organ function: cardiac, hepatic, renal
Prior CRS episodes & txs used
Contact information for treating oncology/CART team
Institutional CRS management protocol (varies by center)
Concurrent infections or neutropenia

sections.title: Exam
items: Fever: hallmark of CRS,high (>38.0°C required for dx)
Hemodynamics: ↓ BP, tachycardia: may require vasopressors
Respiratory: tachypnea, hypoxia, crackles (capillary leak: pulmonary edema)
Neurologic ( screen for ICANS): orientation, naming, writing, attention: use ICE (Immune Effector CellAssociated Encephalopathy) scoring tool
Skin: rash, capillary leak with peripheral edema
check for coagulopathy: petechiae, bleeding (DIC common in severe CRS)
Hepatomegaly, jaundice (hepatic involvement in severe cases)

pairs.value: Systemic inflammatory response from massive cytokine release following immunothetherapy: particularly CART cell therapy & bispecific antibodies
Activated immune effector cells release IL6, IFNγ, TNFα: systemic inflammation mimicking sepsis
CART:114 days after infusion (median day 23). Bispecific antibodies: within 2472h of first dose
Grade 1: fever only | Grade 2: ↓ BP responsive to fluids, O2 <40% | Grade 3: vasopressors required, O2 ≥40% | Grade 4: multiple vasopressors, ICUlevel support
Most common & severe cause: all commercial CART products carry CRS risk
Blinatumomab, teclistamab, mosunetuzumab: CRSmilder than CART
Rare CRS; more common to cause immunerelated adverse events (irAEs)
Clinically identical: always treat empirically for both until cultures negative
Can occur with or independently of CRS: neurologic sx predominate
Massive ferritin elevation, cytopenias, hepatosplenomegaly: CRS can trigger secondary HLH
Fever, organ dysfunction: imaging may needed to differentiate

sections.title: Management
items: r/o sepsis: send BCxs/labs & start empiric broad spectrum abx
Grade severity & call the CART / oncology team now
Grade 1: supportive care; Grade 2+: tocilizumab; add dex if worsening or higher grade
Grade 34: ICU, pressors/O2 support, tocilizumab + dex 10mg IV q6h
Trend CRP q612h: best objective marker of CRS trajectory
Recheck CRS grade q46h: escalate tx if worsening
Continue antibiotics until cultures negative at 4872h & clinical improvement
Tocilizumab: can repeat x1 in 8h if inadequate response (max 3 doses/24h, 4 doses total)
If refractory to tocilizumab + steroids: consider siltuximab (antiIL6), anakinra, or ruxolitinib
Monitor for delayed ICANS (can present days after CRS resolves)
Corticosteroid taper over 37 days once CRS controlled
Coordinate closely with CART/oncology team throughout

pairs.value: Best realtime marker of CRS activity: correlates with severity, dropsafter tocilizumab
Markedly elevated in severe CRS (>10,000 suggests HLH overlap)
Elevated but rises paradoxically after tocilizumab (blocked receptor, unbound IL6 accumulates) —don't use to judge tx response
Mandatory: cannot distinguish CRS from sepsis clinically
check cytopenias, organ function, DIC
Fibrinogen, Ddimer, PT/INR: DIC common in Grade 34 CRS
Cardiac arrhythmias in severe CRS
standardized ICANS grading: orientation, naming, writing, attention, commands

drugs.name: Tocilizumab (Actemra)
drugs.contraindications: Active severe infections (relative: still give if CRS suspected)
drugs.notes: Firstline for ≥Grade 2 CRS. IL6 receptor antagonist. Max 3 doses in 24h. CRP will dropafter: useful marker.

drugs.name: Dexamethasone
drugs.notes: Add to tocilizumab for Grade 34 CRS or refractory Grade 2. May impair CART cell efficacy: discuss with oncology before starting.

drugs.name: Methylprednisolone
drugs.notes: Alternative corticosteroid. Highdose (1g IV daily) for Grade 4 or steroidrefractory CRS.

drugs.name: Anakinra
drugs.notes: IL1 receptor antagonist: for tocilizumab/steroidrefractory CRS. Also effective for ICANS.

drugs.name: Broadspectrum antibiotics
drugs.notes: Give empirically: CRS is indistinguishable from sepsis. Typical: vancomycin + piperacillintazobactam or meropenem.

pairs.key: ICU / immediate higher level of care if
pairs.value: persistent respiratory distress, high-flow or NIV need, hemodynamic instability, vasopressor need, severe arrhythmia, airway risk, neurologic decline, or a trajectory suggesting pt may crash on the floor.

sections.title: References & Resources
items: Lee DW, Santomasso BD, Locke FL, et al. ASTCT Consensus Grading for Cytokine Release Syndrome and Neurologic Toxicity Associated with Immune Effector Cells. Biology of Blood and Marrow Transplantation. 2019;25(4):625638. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bbmt.2018.12.758

Neelapu SS, Tummala S, Kebriaei P, et al. Chimeric Antigen Receptor TCell Therapy — assessment and Management of Toxicities. Nature Reviews Clinical Oncology. 2018;15(1):4762. https://doi.org/10.1038/nrclinonc.2017.148
NCCN Guidelines: Management of ImmunotherapyRelated Toxicities, Version 2.2024. National Comprehensive Cancer Network. 2024. https://www.nccn.org/guidelines/guidelines-detail?category=3&id=1486

topics.title: Differentiation Syndrome
topics.subtitle: Fever, edema, pulmonary infiltrates, hypotension during ATRA or arsenic

sections.title: Acute Management
items: Airway: check patency and protect. Breathing: check WOB, oxygenation, and ventilation. Circulation: check pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
Start dexamethasone 10mg IV q12h now upon clinical suspicion: do NOT wait for confirmatory testing
Do NOT stop ATRA/ATO for mildmoderate DS: continue differentiation therapy while treating DS
For severe DS (respiratory distress requiring vent, renal failure, ICUlevel care): temporarily hold ATRA/ATO until resolution,thenrestart at lower dose
Aggressive supportive care: supplemental O2, diuresis for volume overload, vasopressors if needed
Monitor for DIC: common in APL at baseline & may worsen

sections.title: Chart Check / Hx.
items: Confirmed APL (acute promyelocytic leukemia): must be on ATRA &/or arsenic trioxide
Day of ATRA/ATO therapy: DS247 days after start (median day 712)
Initial WBC count: WBC >10k at presentation is major risk factor for DS
Rising WBC during tx: leukocytosis during ATRA suggests differentiation (& DS risk)
Weight gain: key early sign (>5kg suggests significant capillary leak)
Fever: lowgrade, persistent
Dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain
DIC status at baseline (nearly all APL pts have some DIC)

sections.title: Exam
items: Respiratory: dyspnea, tachypnea, hypoxia: pulmonary infiltrates & pleural effusions
Volume status: peripheral edema, weight gain >5kg, JVD, crackles
Fever: lowgrade, persistent (but can highgrade)
Cardiac: pericardial effusion (check for distant heart sounds, tachycardia)
Musculoskeletal: diffuse bone pain
Renal: ↓ UOP, rising creatinine
Skin: look for DICrelated petechiae/purpura (common in APL)
Neurologic: headache, AMS (rare but serious)

pairs.value: Lifethreatening complication of differentiation therapy in acute promyelocytic leukemia (APL): caused by ATRA (alltrans retinoic acid) or arsenic trioxide
Rapid differentiation of malignant promyelocytes: cytokine release, capillary leak, & tissue infiltration by differentiating cells
~25% of APL pts on ATRA/ATO; can occur 247 days after starting therapy (median 712 days)
5-10% overall; up to 40% if mechanical ventilation required: early recognition is critical
Retinoic acid syndrome (RAS): renamed b/c arsenic trioxide causes identical syndrome
MCC: used in induction therapy for APL
Same syndrome: occurs with ATO alone or in combination with ATRA
Ivosidenib, enasidenib for IDHmutant AML: similar mechanism, emerging recognition
Most important DDx: APL pts are immunocompromised. BCxs, empiric antibiotics mandatory
DIC in APL: alveolar hemorrhage. CT chest may differentiate
WBC >100k with pulmonary/CNS sx: different mechanism, treated with leukapheresis
From aggressive transfusion support in APL: no fever, no weight gain trajectory

sections.title: Management
items: Start dexamethasone now & hold ATRA/arsenic only if severe
Give O2, cautious diuresis if overloaded, & Foley/strict I&O if needed
Call heme/onc now; restart differentiating tx once sx improve
Do NOT use leukapheresis: this is not leukostasis
Continue dexamethasone 10mg IV q12h until complete sx resolution (minimum 3 days)
Taper dexamethasone slowly over 12 weeks: premature discontinuation causes relapse
For mildmoderate DS: continue ATRA/ATO throughout tx with dexamethasone
For severe DS: hold ATRA/ATO until resolution,thenrestart at ↓ dose (ATRA 25mg/m²: consider 12.5mg/m²)
Aggressive DIC management: cryoprecipitate for fibrinogen <150, platelets for plt <3050k
Daily weights & strict I&O: diuresis for volume overload
Prophylactic dexamethasone: some protocols start dexa prophylactically with ATRA if WBC >5-10k
DS can recur: monitor for recurrent sx after steroid taper or ATRA restart

pairs.value: Rising WBC during ATRA is risk factor: WBC >10k associated with higher DS incidence
B/l infiltrates, pleural effusions, pericardial effusion
Renal function (AKI from capillary leak), hepatic involvement
DIC monitoring: fibrinogen <100 requires cryoprecipitate
Weight gain >5kg is diagnostic clue: track trend daily
Cannot differentiate DS from infection clinically: cultures mandatory
Elevated in DS; helps track response to tx

drugs.name: Dexamethasone
drugs.notes: Cornerstone of tx. Start at first suspicion. Continue until complete resolution of sx (minimum 3 days,12 weeks). Taper slowly.

drugs.name: Furosemide
drugs.notes: For pulmonary edema & volume overload from capillary leak. Monitor electrolytes closely.

drugs.name: Hydroxyurea
drugs.notes: If WBC >10k during ATRA tx: hyperleukocytosis worsens DS. Some protocols add prophylactically.

pairs.key: ICU / immediate higher level of care if
pairs.value: persistent respiratory distress, high-flow or NIV need, hemodynamic instability, vasopressor need, severe arrhythmia, airway risk, neurologic decline, or a trajectory suggesting pt may crash on the floor.

sections.title: References & Resources
items: Montesinos P, Bergua JM, Vellenga E, et al. Differentiation Syndrome in Patients with Acute Promyelocytic Leukemia Treated with AllTrans Retinoic Acid and Anthracycline Chemotherapy. Blood. 2009;113(4):775783. https://doi.org/10.1182/blood-2008-07-168617

Sanz MA, Fenaux P, Tallman MS, et al. Management of Acute Promyelocytic Leukemia: Updated Recommendations from an Expert Panel of the European LeukemiaNet. Blood. 2019;133(15):16301643. https://doi.org/10.1182/blood-2019-01-894980
NCCN Guidelines: Acute Myeloid Leukemia, Version 3.2024. National Comprehensive Cancer Network. 2024. https://www.nccn.org/guidelines/guidelines-detail?category=1&id=1411

topics.title: Baclofen Toxicity & Withdrawal
topics.subtitle: Somnolence or agitation, rigidity, seizures, autonomic instability

sections.title: Acute Management
items: Airway: check patency and protect. Breathing: check WOB, oxygenation, and ventilation. Circulation: check pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
Secure airway: intubation for respiratory depression or GCS ≤8. Start enteral nutrition early
Hemodynamic support: IV fluids, vasopressors for ↓ BP; rewarm if hypothermic
Determine: Is this TOXICITY (OD) or WITHDRAWAL (cessation)?: management is opposite

sections.title: Chart Check / Hx.
items: Oral vs intracal baclofen: route determines severity of withdrawal
Intracal pump: manufacturer, last refill date, dose, alarm Hx., recent pump procedures
Renal function: GFR <30 is strong contraindication for oral baclofen; accumulation causes toxicity
Current baclofen dose & recent changes: abrupt dose ↑ or renal decline causes toxicity
Concurrent CNS depressants: opioids, benzodiazepines, alcohol: synergistic respiratory depression
Underlying spasticity etiology: spinal cord injury, MS, cerebral palsy, TBI
Hx. of prior withdrawal episodes or toxicity
Recent surgery, trauma, or illness that may have interrupted baclofen administration

sections.title: Exam
items: ── TOXICITY findings ──
CNS depression: somnolence: coma. With large intoxications, brainstem reflexes may lost (mimics brain death)
Hypothermia, ↓ HR, ↓ BP (lowdose). Paradoxical HTN/tachycardia with >200mg
Hypotonia, flaccid paralysis, absent reflexes
Respiratory depression: rate, depth, SpO2
Excessive salivation, nystagmus, myoclonus
── WITHDRAWAL findings ──
Rebound spasticity: rigidity, hyperreflexia, clonus, hypertonia
Autonomic instability: tachycardia, HTN or ↓ BP, hyperthermia
Agitation, hallucinations, delirium, disorientation, seizures
Diaphoresis, goosebumps, pruritus
Resembles NMS: rigidity + fever + tachycardia + AMS + autonomic instability
Pump site: inspect for infection, swelling, catheter displacement

pairs.value: GABAB receptor agonist used for spasticity (oral or intracal pump). Narrow therapeutic window, esp with renal impairment
OD or accumulation causing CNS depression, respiratory failure, seizures, & hemodynamic instability. Ingestion >200400mg associated with coma, seizure, or intubation
Lifethreatening cessation of IT baclofen from pump malfunction, catheter fracture, battery/reservoir depletion, or dosing error. More severe than oral withdrawal
Agitated delirium, seizures, autonomic instability within 14 days of stopping. Slower onset than IT withdrawal
Peak plasma 23h postingestion. Halflife 45h (nl. renal function). CSF penetration is slow: clinical effects begin 34 days, peak 5-10 days
GFR <30 is strong contraindication. GFR 5080: reduce dose 33%. GFR 3050: reduce dose 50%. Accumulation causes toxicity
Intentional or accidental. Risk factors: renal impairment, concurrent CNS depressants, dose escalation
Pump delivers excessive bolus: ascending flaccid paralysis, respiratory arrest
AKI or CKD progression without dose adjustment. Check GFR
Battery depletion, reservoir empty, catheter fracture/kink, dosing error
Fever, AMS, hemodynamic instability: always check for infection, esp infected pump
Rigidity, fever, AMS, autonomic instability: check antipsychotic exposure, CK
Clonus, hyperreflexia, agitation, diaphoresis: check serotonergic meds
Rigidity + hyperthermia postanesthesia: dantrolene is definitive tx
In spinal cord injury pts: HTN, ↓ HR, flushing above lesion
Tremor, agitation, seizures, tachycardia: Hx. of alcohol or benzo use

sections.title: Management
items: ─── BACLOFEN TOXICITY (OD) ───
Stop baclofen now: for pump pts, consult pump specialist & stop for 48h
EEG monitoring: nonconvulsive status epilepticus can occur in comatose pts
Hemodialysis: consider if severe renal failure + critical intoxication; NOT suggested with preserved renal function. Multiple sessionsneeded
Must eventually restart baclofen at lower dose to prevent withdrawal
─── INTRACAL BACLOFEN WITHDRAWAL ───
Resume intracal baclofen asas possible: this is only definitive tx
Consult neurosurgery/anesthesia for pump revision or temporary lumbar drain
Single IT bolus via lumbar puncture as temporizing bridge
If IT access unavailable: multimodal oral therapy (see meds below)
Aggressive supportive care: IV fluids, cooling for hyperthermia, Rhabdo management
─── ORAL BACLOFEN WITHDRAWAL ───
Resume oral baclofen: definitive therapy, but resolution delayed up to 4 days (slow CNS penetration)
Benzodiazepines + dexmedetomidine for agitation & autonomic instability
── TOXICITY ──
Monitor respiratory & neurological status closely: toxicity can last days with renal impairment
Baclofen must be restarted at lower dose once toxicity resolving: abrupt discontinuation causes withdrawal
If hemodialysis performed: multiple sessionsneeded. Goal is reduce baclofen enough for extubation: avoid excessive removal triggering withdrawal
Serial CK if Rhabdo suspected; aggressive IV fluids to prevent renal injury
── WITHDRAWAL ──
Multimodal therapy is essential: no single agent adequately treats IT withdrawal. Use several antispasmodics at low doses for synergistic effect
Preferred combination: oral baclofen + benzodiazepine + dexmedetomidine + tizanidine
Strongest indication for intubation: profound hyperthermia: chemical paralysis eliminates skeletal muscle heat generation
Aggressive cooling: target normothermia. Rhabdo management: IV NS, target UOP >200 mL/h
Definitive: restore intracal delivery via pump revision or temporary lumbar drain
Monitor CK q612h, renal function daily, telemetry

pairs.value: Critical: renal function determines baclofen clearance & toxicity risk
Rhabdo from severe spasticity/rigidity in withdrawal: causes AKI
r/o sepsis: infected pump is an important ddx
Baseline liver Fxn; dantrolene is hepatotoxic
Respiratory status in toxicity; acidosis in Rhabdo
For comatose pts with toxicity: nonconvulsive status epilepticus occurs
Consult pump specialist: check reservoir volume, battery, alarm Hx., programmed dose
Check catheter: kinking, migration, disconnection, fracture
If radiographs inconclusive: catheter tip position, CSF flow study

drugs.name: Oral baclofen (for withdrawal)
drugs.notes: Cannot fully treat IT withdrawal with oral baclofen: downregulated spinal GABAB receptors limit effectiveness. Slow onset (34 days). Use as part of multimodal approach.

drugs.name: Lorazepam (for withdrawal)
drugs.notes: Stimulates spinal GABAA receptors: may circumvent GABAB deficiency. ↓ seizure risk. Titrate IV while observing response; hold if somnolence.

drugs.name: Midazolam (for withdrawal)
drugs.notes: Alternative benzodiazepine. Shorter acting than lorazepam. No optimal dosing data: titrate to effect.

drugs.name: Diazepam (for withdrawal)
drugs.notes: Long halflife (2050h): accumulates. May be useful for sustained coverage but monitor for oversedation.

drugs.name: Dexmedetomidine (for withdrawal)
drugs.notes: Preferred for agitation: highly titratable, does NOT cause respiratory suppression. Suppresses sympathetic activation (tachycardia, HTN).

drugs.name: Tizanidine (for withdrawal)
drugs.notes: Oral alpha2 agonist with greater antispasmodic activity & less sedation than dexmedetomidine. Adjunct for spasticity.

drugs.name: Cyproheptadine (for withdrawal)
drugs.notes: Antihistamine with antiserotonergic & antispasmodic properties. Sedative. Reasonable adjunct in multimodal approach.

drugs.name: Dantrolene (secondline for withdrawal)
drugs.notes: Ryanodine receptor antagonist: acts directly on skeletal muscle. Role unclear; save for refractory spasticity.

drugs.name: Propofol (rescue for withdrawal)
drugs.notes: Rescue thetherapy: for refractory rigidity or extreme hyperthermia when other agents fail. Requires intubation. Chemical paralysis may needed.

pairs.key: ICU / immediate higher level of care if
pairs.value: persistent respiratory distress, high-flow or NIV need, hemodynamic instability, vasopressor need, severe arrhythmia, airway risk, neurologic decline, or a trajectory suggesting pt may crash on the floor.

sections.title: References & Resources
items: Norris BM, Clark MS. A Review of Baclofen Overdoses in Australia: Calls to a Poisons Information Centre and a Case Series. Alcohol and Alcoholism. 2019;54(1):73-78. https://doi.org/10.1093/alcalc/agy082
Ross JC, Cook AM, Stewart GL, Fahy BG. Acute Intrathecal Baclofen Withdrawal: A Brief Review of Treatment Options. Neurocritical Care. 2011;14(1):103108. https://doi.org/10.1007/s12028-010-9422-6

topics.title: Malignant Hyperthermia
topics.subtitle: Hypercarbia, rigidity, hyperthermia, acidosis, rhabdomyolysis after anesthetic trigger

sections.title: Acute Management
items: Airway: check patency and protect. Breathing: check WOB, oxygenation, and ventilation. Circulation: check pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
Stop ALL triggering agents (volatile anesthetics, succinylcholine)
100% O2 at max LPM: hyperventilate to blow off CO2
Call MHAUS Hotline: 18006449737 (24/7 physician support)
AVOID CCBs: may cause ↑K or cardiac arrest in presence of dantrolene
Actively cool pt: ice packs, cold IV fluids, cooling blanket. STOP cooling when temp reaches 38°C (100.4°F)

sections.title: Chart Check / Hx.
items: Recent exposure to volatile anesthetics (halothane, sevoflurane, desflurane, isoflurane) or succinylcholine
Timing relative to induction, maintenance, or early postanesthesia period: lateonset MH is uncommon but can begin shortly after anesthesia stops
Family or personal history of malignant hyperthermia, unexplained anesthetic death, or known RYR1/CACNA1S mutation
Masseter spasm after succinylcholine, prior perianesthetic hypercarbia, or unexplained rhabdomyolysis history
Concurrent illness, trauma, or procedure context; identify available dantrolene formulation & location now

sections.title: Exam
items: Earliest signs: rising EtCO2, tachycardia, unexplained hypercarbia despite increased ventilation
Muscle rigidity, masseter spasm, generalized contracture,rising core temperature
Dark urine or myoglobinuria, signs of rhabdomyolysis, metabolic instability
Dysrhythmias, ↓ BP or hemodynamic collapse late in course
Hyperthermia may later sign —don't wait for high temperature to treat

pairs.value: Pharmacogenetic hypermetabolic crisis of skeletal muscle triggered by volatile anesthetics or succinylcholine
Rising EtCO2 / hypercarbia is first detectable sign
Muscle rigidity +rising temperature + tachycardia shortly after anesthesia induction
Recurrence after initial txt occurs in ~25% of cases: watch for 2448h
Volatile anesthetics & succinylcholine are classic triggers
CK >20,000 c/w MH; +30 min delay in treatment increases complication risk
Hours to days after dopamine blockade, leadpipe rigidity, not tied to anesthesia exposure
Clonus & hyperreflexia with serotonergic exposure not anesthetic triggered rigidity

pairs.key: DDx: Sepsis / thyroid storm / heat injury
pairs.value: Can cause hyperthermia & tachycardia, but does not explain the classic anesthesia triggered hypercarbia & rigidity pattern

sections.title: Management
items: Call anesthesia / OR team & give dantrolene 2.5 mg/kg IV; repeat q5 min until controlled,>10 mg/kg total
Hyperventilate with 100% O2 & stop all triggering agents
Treat complications: bicarb for acidosis, standard txt for ↑K, & treat dysrhythmias by correcting acidosis/↑K first
Cool aggressively with ice, cooling blanket, & cold IV fluids; stop active cooling once temp reaches 38°C
Monitor for Rhabdo / recrudescence & continue ICUlevel care for 2448h
Core temp: monitoring, target <38°C
EtCO2 & arterial blood gas: check CO2 clearance & acidbase status
CK every 46 hours: peak may not occur until 1224h after episode
Urine output & color: target UO >1 mL/kg/hr; dark urine = myoglobinuria
Electrolytes (K, Ca, Mg): ↑K is common
Coagulation studies: DIC can develop
Glucose q1h if insulin was given for ↑K
check for recrudescence (25% rate) for 2448 hours

pairs.value: check hypercarbia & metabolic acidosis; trend response to hyperventilation & resuscitation
check K, Ca, Mg: hyperkalemia is common & dangerous
Check every 46 hours; peak may not occur until 1224h after event
Target UO >1 mL/kg/hr; check for dark urine & myoglobinuria
Coagulation abnormalities & DIC can develop in severe cases
check q1h if insulin was used for hyperkalemia treatment

drugs.name: Dantrolene
drugs.dose: 2.5 mg/kg IV push, repeat q5min until resolved
drugs.notes: **Firstline & only effective tx for MH.** May need >10 mg/kg acutely. Follow with 1 mg/kg q46h for 2448h. Call MHAUS: 18006449737.

drugs.name: Sodium bicarbonate
drugs.notes: For metabolic acidosis temporization if blood gas not yet available. Titrate to ABG.

drugs.name: Calcium gluconate
drugs.notes: For ↑K: cardiac toxicity. **AVOID CCBs** (verapamil, diltiazem): cardiac arrest risk with dantrolene.

drugs.name: Regular insulin + Dextrose
drugs.notes: ↑K tx. check glucose q1h after administration.

drugs.name: Cold IV fluids
drugs.dose: Cold NS or LR wide open
drugs.notes: Active cooling. Target temp <38°C (100.4°F). Stop active cooling at 38°C to avoid overshoot hypothermia.

pairs.key: ICU / immediate higher level of care if
pairs.value: persistent respiratory distress, high-flow or NIV need, hemodynamic instability, vasopressor need, severe arrhythmia, airway risk, neurologic decline, or a trajectory suggesting pt may crash on the floor.

sections.title: References & Resources
items: [ORCC Malignant Hyperthermia Resource](https://orcc.ariadnelabs.net/malignant_hyperthermia)
Malignant Hyperthermia Association of the United States (MHAUS). Emergency Therapy for Malignant Hyperthermia. 2024. https://www.mhaus.org/healthcare-professionals/managing-a-crisis/
Rosenberg H, Pollock N, Schiemann A, et al. Malignant Hyperthermia: A Review. Orphanet Journal of Rare Diseases. 2015;10:93. https://doi.org/10.1186/s13023-015-0310-1

Hopkins PM, Rüffert H, Snoeck MM, et al. European Malignant Hyperthermia Group Guidelines for Investigation of Malignant Hyperthermia Susceptibility. British Journal of Anaesthesia. 2015;115(4):531539. https://doi.org/10.1093/bja/aev225

topics.title: Cholinergic Toxidrome
topics.subtitle: Salivation, lacrimation, diarrhea, bronchorrhea, bradycardia, miosis

sections.title: Acute Management
items: Airway: check patency and protect. Breathing: check WOB, oxygenation, and ventilation. Circulation: check pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
Secure airway early: bronchorrhea & bronchospasm are primary killers
Suction copious secretions; prepare for intubation if resp distress
Avoid succinylcholine: causes prolonged paralysis (depressed pseudocholinesterase)

sections.title: Chart Check / Hx.
items: Occupation: farming, pesticide application, industrial chemical exposure
Exposure type: organophosphate insecticide, carbamate, nerve agent
Route: dermal, ingestion, inhalation
Time since exposure: determines likelihood of enzyme aging
Intentional vs accidental: suicide attempt common in developing countries
Specific agent if known: aging time varies (soman: minutes; malathion: ~3.7 hrs; diethyl compounds: >30 hrs)
Prehospital tx: was atropine given? How much?
Coexposures: solvents, or pesticides

pairs.value: HR variable (↓ HR from muscarinic, tachycardia from nicotinic); BP ↑ or variable; RR ↑; temp nl. or ↓
Miosis (pinpoint): classic muscarinic finding
Bronchorrhea (copious secretions), bronchospasm, wheezing, ↑ WOB: primary cause of death
Diaphoresis: profuse sweating; may see chemical odor (garliclike with organophosphates)
Hyperactive bowel sounds, cramping, diarrhea, emesis, salivation
Fasciculations (nicotinic), weakness, AMS, seizures
Urinary incontinence
Lacrimation, miosis, blurred vision
**S**alivation, **L**acrimation, **U**rination, **D**iarrhea, **G**I cramping, **E**mesis, **M**iosis: muscarinic effects mnemonic
**D**iarrhea/**D**iaphoresis, **U**rination, **M**iosis, **B**radycardia/**B**ronchorrhea/**B**ronchospasm, **E**mesis, **L**acrimation, **S**alivation
Mydriasis, tachycardia, weakness, fasciculations, HTN: can mask classic muscarinic presentation
Irreversible binding of organophosphate to acetylcholinesterase: once aged, 2PAM is ineffective; timing varies by agent
Proximal muscle weakness & respiratory failure occurring 2496 hrs postexposure; monitor 4896 hrs after severe exposure
Malathion, parathion, chlorpyrifos, diazinon: agricultural insecticides
Sarin, soman, tabun, VX, Novichok: chemical warfare agents
Aldicarb, carbaryl: similar presentation but reversible (no aging); 2PAM still indicated
Neostigmine, pyridostigmine, edrophonium, echothiophate: cholinesterase inhibitors
Inocybe, Clitocybe species: muscarinic sx without nicotinic effects
Opioid toxicity (miosis but no secretions/fasciculations), nicotine toxicity, serotonin syndrome (more diaphoresis/clonus)

sections.title: Management & Pearls
items: Determine exposure route & decontaminate ASAP; use full PPE & remove contaminated clothing
Give atropine 2 mg IV & double q35 min until pulmonary secretions dry
Add pralidoxime (2PAM) 12 g IV over 1530 min, then infusion if needed; give early before enzyme aging
Treat seizures/agitation with benzos & support airway/ventilation as needed
Nicotinic effects (tachycardia, HTN, mydriasis) can mask classic muscarinic presentation —don't exclude dx based on absence of miosis alone
Atropine drip: once secretions controlled, start infusion at 1020% of total loading dose per hour
Monitor for intermediate syndrome: proximal weakness & resp failure at 2496 hrs
Lipophilic agents (fenthion, chlorfenthion) may cause delayed or prolonged toxicity: observe 24+ hrs
Avoid succinylcholine for RSI: depressed pseudocholinesterase causes prolonged paralysis; use rocuronium
Decontaminate early: doubleglove providers; remove & bag all pt clothing
2PAM is most effective within first 2448 hrs but may still help later —don't withhold based on time alone

sections.title: Orders
items: POC glucose
CBC: leukocytosis common
BMP: monitor K+, renal function
VBG/ABG: resp failure, acidosis assessment
Lipase: pancreatitis reported
ECG: QTc prolongation correlates with severity; dysrhythmias
CXR: pulmonary edema in severe cases
RBC acetylcholinesterase (confirmatory but results delayed)
Plasma butyrylcholinesterase / pseudocholinesterase (faster but less specific)
cardiac monitoring, pulse oximetry

drugs.name: Atropine
drugs.dose: 2 mg IV; double dose q35 min until secretions dry; may need 10100+ mg/24h
drugs.notes: Titrate to drying of pulmonary secretions: NOT pupil size. Start infusion once stabilized (1020% of loading dose/hr)

drugs.name: Pralidoxime (2PAM)
drugs.dose: 12 g IV over 1530 min, then 500 mg/hr infusion; peds: 2050 mg/kg, then 5-10 mg/kg/hr
drugs.notes: Give ASAP: must give before aging occurs. Reverses nicotinic effects (weakness, fasciculations). Continue infusion ≥24 hrs

drugs.name: Diazepam
drugs.notes: For seizures: organophosphate seizures are cholinergic, not epileptic; benzos are first line

drugs.name: Glycopyrrolate
drugs.notes: Alternative to atropine for peripheral muscarinic sx only; does NOT treat CNS effects or seizures

pairs.key: ICU / immediate higher level of care if
pairs.value: persistent respiratory distress, high-flow or NIV need, hemodynamic instability, vasopressor need, severe arrhythmia, airway risk, neurologic decline, or a trajectory suggesting pt may crash on the floor.

sections.title: References & Resources
items: Eddleston M, Buckley NA, Eyer P, Dawson AH. Management of Acute Organophosphorus Pesticide Poisoning. The Lancet. 2008;371(9612):597607. https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(07)61202-1
King AM, Aaron CK. Organophosphate and Carbamate Poisoning. Emergency Medicine Clinics of North America. 2015;33(1):133151. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.emc.2014.09.010

topics.title: Anticholinergic Toxidrome
topics.subtitle: Agitation, mydriasis, dry skin, urinary retention, hyperthermia

sections.title: Acute Management
items: Airway: check patency and protect. Breathing: check WOB, oxygenation, and ventilation. Circulation: check pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
Immediate ECG: QRS widening suggests TCA ingestion: changes entire management
Monitor on cardiac telemetry
Activated charcoal 1 g/kg PO if within 12 hrs of ingestion & airway protected

sections.title: Chart Check / Hx.
items: Meds list: antihistamines, TCAs, antipsychotics, muscle relaxants, bladder antispasmodics
OTC meds: diphenhydramine (Benadryl) is MCC
Intentional vs accidental ingestion
Amount ingested & time since ingestion
Psychiatric hx: suicidal ideation, prior OD attempts
Plant/mushroom exposure: jimson weed (Datura), deadly nightshade
Eye drop use: atropine, cyclopentolate, tropicamide
Coingestants: esp acetaminophen, aspirin

pairs.value: HR ↑↑ (120160 typical); BP ↑; RR nl. or ↓; temp ↑ (anhydrotic hyperthermia)
Mydriasis: dilated, nonreactive; may delayed 1224 hrs
Hot, dry, flushed: "red as beet, dry as bone, hot as hare"
Delirium, agitation, hallucinations: "mad as hatter"; dysarthria, mumbling, picking at air
Decreased/absent bowel sounds, ileus
Urinary retention: "full as flask"
Sinus tachycardia; if QRS wide: suspect TCA
Anticholinergic = DRY skin vs sympathomimetic = WET (diaphoretic). Both have mydriasis + tachycardia
"Blind as bat" (mydriasis), "Mad as hatter" (delirium), "Red as beet" (flushed), "Hot as hare" (hyperthermia), "Dry as bone" (anhidrosis), "Full as flask" (urinary retention)
Blockade of muscarinic acetylcholine receptors: loss of parasympathetic tone: sympathetic dominance
Reversible acetylcholinesterase inhibitor: crosses BBB: reverses both central (delirium) & peripheral (tachycardia, mydriasis) effects
TCAs block fast sodium channels: QRS widening, right axis deviation, Brugada pattern: treat with sodium bicarbonate
Diphenhydramine (most common), hydroxyzine, promethazine, doxylamine
Amitriptyline, nortriptyline, imipramine, desipramine: most dangerous (sodium channel blockade + seizures)
Chlorpromazine, quetiapine, olanzapine
Cyclobenzaprine (structurally similar to TCA), orphenadrine
Oxybutynin, tolterodine, hyoscyamine
Benztropine, trihexyphenidyl
Jimson weed (Datura stramonium), deadly nightshade (Atropa belladonna)
Sympathomimetic (wet vs dry skin), serotonin syndrome (clonus, hyperreflexia), NMS (rigidity, ↑ CK), thyroid storm, heat stroke

sections.title: Management & Pearls
items: Check ECG first: if QRS >100 ms, give sodium bicarbonate 2 mEq/kg IV & treat as possible TCA toxicity
Use benzos first line for agitation & start active cooling for hyperthermia
Treat retention/supportive issues: Foley for urinary retention, IVF PRN, serial temp/ECG monitoring
Consider physostigmine 0.51 mg IV slow only if no C/F TCA, no wide QRS, no seizure hx, & no brady/AV block
Anticholinergic = DRY; sympathomimetic = WET: check axillae for moisture (key distinguishing exam finding)
If physostigmine causes seizures: benzos will be INEFFECTIVE: use phenobarbital or propofol
Avoid Class IA/IC antiarrhythmics (procainamide, flecainide) if TCA suspected
Plant ingestions (jimson weed) may havedelayed absorption from ↓ GI motility: observe 2448 hrs
Mydriasis may persist 1224 hrs after or sx resolve —don't use pupil size to judge resolution
Diphenhydramine is dose dependent: mild anticholinergic at low doses: QRS widening, seizures, Rhabdo at high doses
Always check ECG before giving physostigmine: QRS >100 ms = contraindication

sections.title: Orders
items: ECG: sinus tach; QRS >100 ms: TCA; QTc prolongation
POC glucose
Acetaminophen & salicylate levels ( mandatory coingestion screen)
BMP
CPK if prolonged agitation (Rhabdo)
Urinalysis
UDS (limited utility: detects many anticholinergic agents poorly)
cardiac monitoring

drugs.name: Lorazepam
drugs.dose: 24 mg IV q515 min PRN agitation
drugs.notes: Firstline for agitation: controls muscular heat generation, prevents hyperthermia

drugs.name: Physostigmine
drugs.dose: 0.51 mg IV slowly over 5 min; may repeat q20 min up to 2 mg total in first hr
drugs.contraindications: TCA ingestion, QRS >100 ms, ↓ HR, AV block, seizure hx, unknown OD
drugs.notes: Reverses central & peripheral effects. If needing >3 doses in 6 hrs, consider infusion 1 mg/hr. Duration shorter than most ingested agents: rebound risk

drugs.name: Sodium bicarbonate
drugs.dose: 2 mEq/kg IV bolus, then infusion 150 mEq in 1L D5W at 250 mL/hr
drugs.notes: For QRS >100 ms (TCA sodium channel blockade): goal QRS <120 ms. Can give serial boluses

drugs.name: Activated charcoal
drugs.notes: Most effective within 12 hrs of ingestion; anticholinergics delay gastric emptying: may still be effective later

pairs.key: ICU / immediate higher level of care if
pairs.value: persistent respiratory distress, high-flow or NIV need, hemodynamic instability, vasopressor need, severe arrhythmia, airway risk, neurologic decline, or a trajectory suggesting pt may crash on the floor.

sections.title: References & Resources
items: Burns MJ, Linden CH, Graudins A, Brown RM, Fletcher KE. A Comparison of Physostigmine and Benzodiazepines for the Treatment of Anticholinergic Poisoning. Annals of Emergency Medicine. 2000;35(4):374381.
Defined Approach: Goldfrank's Toxicologic Emergencies, 11th Edition. McGrawHill. 2019. Chapter: Anticholinergic Toxicity.

topics.title: Sympathomimetic Toxidrome
topics.subtitle: Agitation, tachycardia, hypertension, diaphoresis, hyperthermia, mydriasis

sections.title: Acute Management
items: Airway: check patency and protect. Breathing: check WOB, oxygenation, and ventilation. Circulation: check pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
Active cooling: ice packs to axillae/groin, evaporative cooling, cooling blankets
If temp >41°C & refractory to benzos: intubate + paralyze (rocuronium) to stop heat generation
Benzodiazepines are first line for EVERYTHING: agitation, HTN, tachycardia, hyperthermia, seizures

sections.title: Chart Check / Hx.
items: Substance used: cocaine, methamphetamine, MDMA, bath salts, Adderall, ephedrine
Route: smoked, snorted, IV, oral
Time of last use & amount
Coingestants: alcohol, opioids, benzodiazepines
Chest pain? (acute coronary syndrome, aortic dissection risk with cocaine)
Headache? (ICH risk)
Prior substance use: chronic vs naive (tolerance affects presentation)
Psychiatric hx: stimulantinduced psychosis, prior OD

pairs.value: HR ↑↑; BP ↑↑; RR ↑; temp ↑ (can be severely elevated)
Mydriasis (dilated)
Diaphoretic (WET): key distinction from anticholinergic (dry)
Agitation, psychosis, paranoia, tremor, hyperreflexia, clonus, seizures
Tachycardia, HTN; chest pain: r/o ACS, aortic dissection
Muscle rigidity, Rhabdo
Sympathomimetic = WET (diaphoretic) + agitated + mydriatic; Anticholinergic = DRY + delirious + mydriatic
Smoked/snorted/IV: short acting (3060 min); risk of ACS, aortic dissection, ICH
Methamphetamine, dextroamphetamine, lisdexamfetamine: longer duration (hours)
Also causes hyponatremia (SIADH) & serotonin syndrome overlap
"Bath salts" (mephedrone, MDPV): extreme agitation, Rhabdo (63% incidence)
Ephedrine, pseudoephedrine, caffeine (massive OD), ophylline, PCP (mixed)
Serotonin syndrome (clonus/hyperreflexia prominent), thyroid storm, pheochromocytoma, anticholinergic (dry vs wet), NMS, heat stroke

sections.title: Management & Pearls
items: Give benzos first line: lorazepam 24 mg IV or diazepam 5-10 mg IV; repeat aggressively as needed
Check core temp & cool early; severe hyperthermia needs aggressive external cooling +/ intubation/paralysis
Give IV crystalloids & treat refractory HTN only after adequate benzos, e.g. nicardipine or phentolamine
Avoid BB, avoid haloperidol as first line, & avoid antipyretics
No BB: labetalol, metoprolol, propranolol all contraindicated with cocaine (unopposed alpha: HTN crisis, coronary vasospasm)
No succinylcholine: ↑K risk from Rhabdo
No antipyretics: hyperthermia is from muscular heat generation, not febrile response
Rhabdo is VERY common: check CPK on all sympathomimetic presentations
Cocaine chest pain: benzos + NTG + aspirin; avoid BB; cath lab if STEMI
MDMA causes severe hyponatremia (SIADH + excessive free water intake): seizures
Bath salts / syntic cathinones cause most severe agitation & highest Rhabdo rates
Body packers (swallowed cocaine): wholebowel irrigation; surgical consult if packet rupture suspected

sections.title: Orders
items: POC glucose
BMP: Cr, lytes (hyponatremia with MDMA)
CPK: Rhabdo screen (bath salts 63%, amphetamines 40%, cocaine 33%)
UA with myoglobin
ECG: QTc prolongation, QRS widening, ischemic changes, dysrhythmias
Troponin if chest pain or ECG changes
VBG/ABG
Coags/DIC panel if severe hyperthermia
UDS (limited: may miss syntic agents)
Acetaminophen & salicylate levels
Head CT if focal neuro findings, severe HA, or AMS (hemorrhage, stroke)

drugs.name: Lorazepam
drugs.dose: 24 mg IV q5-10 min; may needlarge cumulative doses (50100+ mg)
drugs.contraindications: Resp depression at high doses (airway management)
drugs.notes: Firstline for everything: agitation, HTN, tachycardia, seizures, hyperthermia. Treats the underlying cause

drugs.name: Nicardipine
drugs.notes: For refractory HTN after adequate benzodiazepines. Safe with cocaine (no betablockade)

drugs.name: Phentolamine
drugs.dose: 2.55 mg IV bolus; may repeat q515 min
drugs.notes: Alphablocker: for refractory HTN esp cocaine associated. Addresses unopposed alpha stimulation

drugs.name: Nitroglycerin
drugs.dose: 0.4 mg SL or 5200 mcg/min IV infusion
drugs.notes: For cocaine associated ACS: coronary vasodilator

drugs.name: IV Crystalloid
drugs.dose: 12 L bolus, then target UOP 200300 mL/hr if Rhabdo
drugs.notes: Aggressive hydration for Rhabdo prevention + volume depletion from diaphoresis

pairs.key: ICU / immediate higher level of care if

topics.title: Sympatholytic Toxidrome
topics.subtitle: Bradycardia, hypotension, sedation, miosis, hypoglycemia

sections.title: Acute Management
items: Airway: check patency and protect. Breathing: check WOB, oxygenation, and ventilation. Circulation: check pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
IV access, cardiac monitoring, EKG
IV crystalloid bolus 20 mL/kg for ↓ BP
For CCB OD: high dose insulin (HDI) 1 unit/kg bolus + D50: 0.51 unit/kg/hr infusion (start early: 3060 min to full effect)
Vasopressors: norepinephrine or epinephrine if fluidrefractory ↓ BP
For clonidine: trial of naloxone 0.42 mg IV (may require high doses up to 10 mg)
Contact ECMO center early for massive BB/CCB ingestions with refractory shock

sections.title: Chart Check / Hx.
items: Which agent?: specific drug, dose, formulation (immediaterelease vs sustainedrelease)
Time of ingestion: sustainedrelease CCBs have delayed, prolonged toxicity
Amount ingested: single pill can kill in pediatric clonidine ingestion
Intentional vs accidental
Concurrent meds: digoxin (synergistic ↓ HR), or antihypertensives
Cardiac hx: baseline conduction disease, CHF, pacemaker
Renal/hepatic function: affects drug clearance
Prior hx of similar ingestion

pairs.value: HR ↓ (↓ HR); BP ↓ (↓ BP ); RR ↓ (esp clonidine); temp nl. or ↓
nl. or miosis (esp clonidine: mimics opioid OD)
↓ LOC, sedation, lethargy: coma (esp clonidine); clonidine can closely mimic opioid OD
↓ HR, ↓ BP, AV blocks (verapamil/diltiazem), wide QRS (propranolol)
Dry, cool, pale
↓ RR, periodic apnea, CheyneStokes (clonidine)
↓ Glucose = betablocker; hyperglycemia = CCB: critical distinguishing feature when agent unknown
Agents that ↓ sympathetic nervous system activity: ↓ HR, ↓ BP, ↓ consciousness
Euglycemic therapy: insulin ↑ inotropy independent of betareceptors; most effective tx for CCB OD: start early as 3060 min to full effect
Bypasses betareceptor blockade via Gs protein activation: ↑ cAMP: ↑ inotropy & chronotropy; first line for betablocker OD
Amlodipine, nifedipine: primarily vasodilation: ↓ BP with reflex tachycardia (may NOT have ↓ HR)
Verapamil, diltiazem: negative chronotropy + inotropy: ↓ HR, AV block, ↓ cardiac output
Clonidine, guanfacine, dexmedetomidine, tizanidine, methyldopa: mimic opioid OD (miosis + CNS depression + resp depression)
Propranolol (most dangerous: lipophilic, Na channel blockade: QRS widening, seizures); sotalol (QT prolongation, TdP); metoprolol, atenolol, carvedilol
Verapamil (most dangerous CCB in OD); diltiazem; amlodipine, nifedipine (dihydropyridines)
Opioid OD (responds to naloxone), digoxin toxicity (bidirectional VT, ↑K), cholinergic toxidrome (SLUDGE), hypothyroidism, ↑K, sepsis

sections.title: Management & Pearls
items: Identify the agent class: clonidine vs BB vs CCB, b/c treatment differs
Support ABCs & treat symptomatic bradycardia/hypotension; atropine 0.51 mg IV may help butfails in severe BB/CCB OD
For BB OD: glucagon 5 mg IV bolus, then infusion if responsive
For CCB OD: IV calcium chloride 13 g or calcium gluconate 39 g, then escalate per shock severity
Glucose is key distinguishing clue: ↓ Glucose = BB; hyperglycemia = CCB; hyperglycemia severity correlates with CCB severity
Clonidine mimics opioid OD: miosis + CNS depression + resp depression; consider clonidine in pts who don't fully respond to naloxone
Start HDI early for CCB OD: takes 3060 min to full effect; don't wait for or therapies to fail
Sustainedrelease CCBs (amlodipine, ER verapamil/diltiazem): delayed & prolonged toxicity: admit ALL SR ingestions even if asymptomatic
Propranolol is most dangerous BB: lipophilic (crosses BBB: seizures) + Na channel blockade (, then QRS widening)
Sotalol is unique: K channel blockade: QT prolongation: TdP; give Mg 2 g IV; delayed toxicity
Consider wholebowel irrigation for sustainedrelease preparations
VAECMO is definitive rescue therapy for refractory cardiogenic shock: contact ECMO center early

sections.title: Orders
items: ECG: ↓ HR, AV blocks, QRS widening (propranolol), QT prolongation (sotalol)
POC glucose: ↓ Glucose: betablocker; hyperglycemia: CCB ( key distinguishing feature)
BMP: electrolytes, renal function, calcium
Serum lactate: tissue hypoperfusion marker
Digoxin level (if applicable —coprescribed)
Acetaminophen & salicylate levels
VBG: check perfusion, acidbase status
cardiac monitoring ( mandatory)
pulse oximetry

drugs.name: Glucagon
drugs.dose: 5 mg IV bolus over 1 min, then 25 mg/hr infusion; peds: 50 mcg/kg bolus, then 70 mcg/kg/hr
drugs.notes: Firstline for betablocker OD: bypasses betareceptor. Give antiemetic (ondansetron) with bolus. Tachyphylaxis occurs; less effective for CCBs

drugs.name: Highdose insulin (regular)
drugs.dose: 1 unit/kg IV bolus + D50 (0.5 g/kg), then infusion 0.51 unit/kg/hr (titrate up to 10 units/kg/hr)
drugs.contraindications: Monitor glucose q15 min; maintain K+ >3.0
drugs.notes: Most effective tx for CCB OD: takes 3060 min for full effect: start early. ↑ inotropy independent of betareceptors. Maintain euglycemia with dextrose infusion

drugs.name: Calcium chloride 10%
drugs.dose: 13 g IV (2040 mg/kg) via central line; may repeat
drugs.notes: For CCB OD: ↑ extracellular calcium to overcome channel blockade. CaCl has 3x more elemental calcium than gluconate. Target Ca 1518 mg/dL

drugs.name: Atropine
drugs.dose: 0.51 mg IV q35 min, max 3 mg
drugs.notes: For symptomatic ↓ HR —ineffective in severe BB/CCB OD

drugs.name: Naloxone
drugs.dose: 0.42 mg IV; may repeat up to 10 mg; may need infusion
drugs.contraindications: May precipitate withdrawal in opioiddependent pts
drugs.notes: For clonidine toxicity: high doses required; partial response suggests clonidine vs opioid

drugs.name: Lipid emulsion 20%
drugs.dose: 1.5 mL/kg IV bolus (repeat 12x if pulseless), then 0.25 mL/kg/min × 3060 min; max 12.5 mL/kg
drugs.notes: For lipophilic betablocker OD (propranolol) refractory to or tx; consider for severe CCB OD

drugs.name: Sodium bicarbonate
drugs.notes: For QRS widening from propranolol (sodium channel blockade)

topics.title: SedativeHypnotic Toxidrome
topics.subtitle: Somnolence, hypoventilation, slurred speech, hypotension, coma

sections.title: Acute Management
items: Airway: check patency and protect. Breathing: check WOB, oxygenation, and ventilation. Circulation: check pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
Airway is paramount: respiratory depression is primary cause of death
check GCS, gag reflex, respiratory effort: intubate for GCS <8, absent gag, or resp failure
Supplemental O2, pulse oximetry, capnography
IV access, POC glucose (↓ Glucose common with ethanol)
IV crystalloid for ↓ BP
Activated charcoal 1 g/kg if within 12 hrs & airway protected

sections.title: Chart Check / Hx.
items: Specific agent: benzodiazepine, barbiturate, GHB, Zdrug, ethanol, baclofen
Chronic vs acute use: chronic benzo use = flumazenil contraindicated
Amount & time of ingestion
Coingestants: opioids + benzos = synergistic resp depression
Intentional vs recreational vs accidental
Psychiatric hx: suicidal ideation, prior attempts
Prescription Hx.: which benzos, how long, what dose
Alcohol use hx: crosstolerance with barbiturates/benzos

pairs.value: HR nl. or ↓; BP nl. or ↓; RR ↓ (resp depression: primary lethal mechanism); temp nl. or ↓
Variable —midpoint, nl. reactive (unlike opioids which are pinpoint)
Somnolence: stupor: coma; slurred speech, ataxia, hyporeflexia, nystagmus
Variable; barbiturates may cause bullous skin lesions ("barb blisters")
↓ RR, ↓ tidal volume, apnea: primary mechanism of death
Rapid cycling between coma & agitation; abrupt awakening;young adults at raves/parties
May mimic brain death: loss of brainstem reflexes, fixed dilated pupils possible; do NOT withdraw care prematurely
Alprazolam, lorazepam, diazepam, clonazepam, midazolam: most common sedativehypnotic OD
Phenobarbital, pentobarbital: more profound coma, longer duration, higher mortality
Zolpidem (Ambien), zaleplon, eszopiclone —less severe than benzos
Rapid onset/offset; NOT detected on standard UDS;recover 26 hrs
Ethanol, chloral hydrate, meprobamate/carisoprodol (Soma), baclofen, propofol
Opioid OD (miosis, responds to naloxone), sympatholytic (↓ HR + ↓ BP ), hepatic encephalopathy, ↓ Glucose, hypothyroidism, stroke, postictal state

sections.title: Management & Pearls
items: Support airway/ventilation first; many pts need only time & supportive care
Rewarm if hypothermic & monitor for recurrent sedation or apnea
Flumazenil is not routine; reserve for clear iatrogenic benzo oversedation in the right pt
Always check for coingestants, especially acetaminophen & salicylates
Airway mgmt. is #1 intervention: sedativehypnotic deaths are from respiratory failure
Flumazenil is almost never indicated: risk of precipitating withdrawal seizures far outweighs benefit in most scenarios
If flumazenil causes seizures, benzos will NOT work (blocked by flumazenil): use phenobarbital or propofol
GHB: ptsrecover in 26 hrs; coma (>6 hrs) + alternative dx: workup
GHB: rapid cycling between coma & agitation: anticipate sudden awakening; restrain before intubation attempt
Barbiturate OD can mimic brain death: do NOT make withdrawalofcare decisions acutely
Standard UDS misses many benzos (lorazepam, alprazolam, clonazepam, midazolam) & all GHB
Phenobarbital haslong halflife (80120 hrs): prolonged monitoring required
Opioid + benzo coingestion = synergistic resp depression common lethal combination

sections.title: Orders
items: POC glucose ( ↓ Glucose common with ethanol)
Acetaminophen & salicylate levels: mandatory in any OD presentation
Serum ethanol level
BMP
CBC
ECG
VBG/ABG: vent status assessment
UDS ( Pearl: UDS may miss lorazepam, alprazolam, clonazepam, midazolam; GHB NOT detected)
Serum Osm if toxic alcohol suspected
LFTs if chronic ethanol use

drugs.name: Flumazenil
drugs.dose: 0.2 mg IV; repeat q1 min up to max 3 mg total
drugs.contraindications: Chronic benzo use/dependence, suspected TCA, seizure hx, unknown OD, coingestion of seizurethresholdlowering agents
drugs.notes: Very limited indications: primarily iatrogenic oversedation in benzonaive pts. Duration 4590 min (shorter than most benzos: rebound sedation). If causes seizures: benzos will be INEFFECTIVE: phenobarbital or propofol

drugs.name: Activated charcoal
drugs.notes: Most effective within 12 hrs of ingestion. Protect airway first. Consider multidose charcoal for phenobarbital

drugs.name: Phenobarbital
drugs.notes: For flumazenil induced seizures (benzos will be blocked by flumazenil). Also for severe barbiturate withdrawal

drugs.name: Sodium bicarbonate (urinary alkalinization)
drugs.dose: 150 mEq in 1L D5W at 150200 mL/hr; target urine pH 7.58.0
drugs.notes: For phenobarbital OD: ↑ renal clearance via ion trapping. Monitor urine pH, serum K+

topics.title: CCB & Beta-Blocker Overdose
topics.subtitle: Bradycardia, hypotension, shock, conduction abnormalities, cardiogenic collapse

sections.title: Acute Management
items: Airway: check patency and protect. Breathing: check WOB, oxygenation, and ventilation. Circulation: check pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
IV access (establish central access early for severe cases), continuous cardiac monitoring, 12-lead ECG
IV crystalloid bolus for hypotension; avoid over-resuscitation (volume overload is common in severe intoxications)
Vasopressors: epinephrine for bradycardia + hypotension; norepinephrine for vasodilatory shock (dihydropyridine CCBs, labetalol, carvedilol)
IV calcium: calcium chloride 1 g IV (central) or calcium gluconate 3 g IV (peripheral) over 5 min; repeat q10-20 min up to 3 g CaCl or 9 g Ca gluconate. Target ionized Ca ~2 mM

Hyperinsulinemic euglycemia (HIE): insulin 1 unit/kg IV bolus then 1 unit/kg/hr infusion. Start EARLY (takes 15-60 min to work). Give D50W 1-2 amps + D50W infusion ~1 mL/kg/hr via central line. Monitor glucose q15-30 min, target 125-250 mg/dL. Monitor K+, Mg, Phos

Glucagon (primarily for BB OD): 5 mg IV bolus over 5 min; if no response after 10 mg total, stop. If effective, infuse at response dose (1-10 mg/hr). Pre-treat with ondansetron (causes emesis). Caution: unprotected airway
Atropine 1 mg IV q5 min x 1-3 doses (max 3 mg) for symptomatic bradycardia (often ineffective in severe OD)
Decontamination: activated charcoal if within 1-2 hr of ingestion; consider whole bowel irrigation for sustained-release CCBs or amlodipine (must be given before onset of shock/ileus)
For refractory shock: contact ECMO center early for VA-ECMO (best for cardiogenic shock); consider methylene blue for refractory vasodilatory shock from CCBs
For QRS widening (propranolol, carvedilol): IV sodium bicarbonate 1-2 mEq/kg bolus
For QTc prolongation (sotalol, acebutolol): IV magnesium 2 g bolus; treat torsade de pointes with Mg infusion + overdrive pacing

sections.title: Chart Check / Hx.
items: Which agent: specific drug, dose, formulation (immediate-release vs sustained-release)
Time of ingestion: immediate-release causes deterioration within ~6-8 hr; sustained-release or sotalol may be delayed up to 24 hr
Amount ingested & intentional vs accidental
Coingestants: check acetaminophen & salicylate levels; TCA or antipsychotic coingestion increases mortality
Concurrent meds: digoxin (synergistic bradycardia), other antihypertensives, other QT-prolonging drugs
Cardiac history: baseline conduction disease, CHF, pacemaker
Renal/hepatic function: affects drug clearance (atenolol, nadolol, sotalol are renally cleared)
Prior episodes of overdose or toxicity

pairs.value: ↓ HR, ↓ BP, shock. Dihydropyridine CCBs at lower doses may cause reflex tachycardia with ↓ BP
Hyperglycemia suggests CCB toxicity; hypoglycemia suggests BB toxicity. Critical distinguishing feature when agent is unknown
Delirium, seizure, coma (from hypoperfusion or lipophilic BBs like propranolol crossing BBB). Propranolol is especially CNS toxic
Bradycardia, AV blocks, wide QRS (propranolol/carvedilol Na channel blockade), prolonged QTc (sotalol/acebutolol K channel blockade), Brugada pattern
Nausea, vomiting
Cool, pale, diaphoretic (poor perfusion)
Verapamil, diltiazem: cause myocardial suppression > vasodilation. Early ↓ HR + ↓ BP. Verapamil is most dangerous CCB in overdose
Amlodipine, nifedipine, felodipine: primarily vasodilatory at lower doses (↓ BP with reflex tachycardia). Higher doses lose selectivity and cause bradycardia + vasodilation
Most dangerous BB in OD. Highly lipophilic (crosses BBB: seizures, coma). Na channel blockade: QRS widening, monomorphic VT, Brugada pattern. Severe hypotension
K channel blockade: QTc prolongation + torsade de pointes risk. Bradycardia + QT prolongation synergistically promote torsade. Delayed & prolonged toxicity. Dialyzable
Acebutolol, betaxolol, carvedilol, oxprenolol, pindolol, propranolol: cause QRS widening, monomorphic VT, Brugada pattern. Treat with IV sodium bicarbonate
Betaxolol, carvedilol, labetalol, nebivolol: cause hypotension partly from peripheral vasodilation. May need norepinephrine
Insulin ↑ myocardial inotropy independent of beta-receptors. Most effective for cardiogenic shock from CCB/BB OD. Takes 15-60 min to work: start early, do not wait for other therapies to fail
Increases myocardial cAMP bypassing beta-receptor. Positive inotropic + chronotropic effects. Most useful for BB OD with cardiogenic shock. Short half-life (5-7 min): requires continuous infusion
Also ↑ cAMP bypassing beta-receptor. Consider if glucagon supply is limited. Risk of vasodilation worsening ↓ BP. Renally cleared with long half-life
Verapamil (most dangerous), diltiazem, amlodipine, nifedipine, felodipine, isradipine, nimodipine. Hyperglycemia distinguishes from BB
Propranolol (most dangerous), sotalol (unique QT risk), metoprolol, atenolol, carvedilol, labetalol, nadolol, acebutolol. Hypoglycemia distinguishes from CCB

Digoxin toxicity (bidirectional VT, ↑ K), clonidine/alpha-2 agonist OD (miosis, responds to naloxone), cholinergic toxidrome (SLUDGE), hypothyroidism/myxedema, hyperkalemia, inferior MI, sick sinus syndrome, Lyme disease, sepsis

sections.title: Tier 1 Management
items: Decontamination: activated charcoal if early (<1-2 hr); whole bowel irrigation for sustained-release CCBs/amlodipine (must be before shock/ileus). Glucagon may not be compatible with bowel irrigation (relaxes GI smooth muscle)
Airway: secure early if deteriorating. Try to achieve hemodynamic stability before intubation to avoid post-intubation hypotension (volume load + epinephrine infusion first)
Vascular access: central access for severe cases (needed for D50W, calcium, vasopressors). Consider arterial line for hemodynamic monitoring
Volume: IV fluids if hypovolemic, but most pts are NOT hypovolemic. Avoid over-resuscitation (volume overload is a major problem in severe cases)

Vasopressors: epinephrine for bradycardia + shock; norepinephrine for vasodilatory shock. Patients may need unusually high doses. No maximum dose of catecholamines. Consider early HIE rather than escalating to extreme vasopressor doses

Glucagon: 5 mg IV over 5 min (may repeat x1). If no improvement after 10 mg: stop. If effective: infuse at effective dose 1-10 mg/hr. Pre-treat with ondansetron. Caution: emesis risk with unprotected airway. Hospital supply may be limited: consider milrinone instead
IV calcium: CaCl 1 g (central) or Ca gluconate 3 g (peripheral) slow push over ≥5 min. Repeat q10-20 min. Target ionized Ca ~2 mM. May improve BP & contractility more than heart rate
Atropine: 1 mg IV q5 min x 1-3 doses (max 3 mg). Unlikely to work in severe CCB OD

HIE: insulin 1 unit/kg bolus then 1 unit/kg/hr. Up-titrate q10-15 min to 1-10 units/kg/hr (>5 units/kg/hr may be excessive). Give D50W 1-2 amps unless glucose >250. Start D50W infusion ~1 mL/kg/hr via central line. Monitor glucose q15-30 min (target 125-250 mg/dL), K+ >3 mM, Mg, Phos

sections.title: Tier 2 Management
items: Hemodialysis: consider for hydrophilic BBs (nadolol, sotalol, acebutolol, atenolol, timolol). CRRT for renal failure to avoid volume overload
Methylene blue: for refractory vasodilatory shock from CCBs (CCBs ↑ nitric oxide synthesis). Not for cardiogenic shock
VA-ECMO: definitive rescue for refractory cardiogenic shock. Supports pt until drug metabolism occurs. Limited by availability. Contact ECMO center early

Transvenous pacemaker: may help refractory bradycardia with preserved contractility, but results often disappointing. Capturing the myocardium is difficult (myocardial problem, not conduction problem). Loss of atrial kick and uncoordinated contraction may negate benefit of ↑ HR

Lipid emulsion (20%): falling out of favor. Concern that lipid may ↑ drug absorption from intestine. Reasonable for cardiac arrest/periarrest. 1.5 mL/kg bolus, then 0.25 mL/kg/min x 30-60 min. Unlikely to work with hydrophilic BBs. Risks: pancreatitis, ARDS, lab interference, ECMO circuit clotting

pairs.value: Following clinical improvement. In CCB OD, emergence of hypoglycemia may signal recovery
Insulin accumulates & persists hours to days after stopping (especially with renal dysfunction). Can wean rapidly as it auto-tapers. Resume if hemodynamic deterioration
Continue dextrose infusion and adjust based on glucose levels. Do NOT stop dextrose just because insulin was stopped
Watch for hypoglycemia and hyperkalemia/hypokalemia even after insulin is off (rebound hyperkalemia can occur)
Primarily a BB issue (interferes with gluconeogenesis & glycogenolysis). Monitor glucose closely; replete with D50W as needed
BBs: propranolol, acebutolol, betaxolol, carvedilol, oxprenolol, pindolol. Causes QRS widening, monomorphic VT, Brugada pattern. Treat: IV hypertonic bicarbonate 1-2 mEq/kg (same as TCA OD)
BBs: acebutolol, sotalol. Causes QTc prolongation, torsade de pointes. Treat: IV magnesium 2 g bolus; Mg infusion for torsade. Consider overdrive pacing

sections.title: Orders
items: ECG: bradycardia, AV blocks, QRS widening (propranolol), QTc prolongation (sotalol), Brugada pattern. get serial ECGs
POC glucose: hypoglycemia = BB; hyperglycemia = CCB (severity correlates with CCB severity)
BMP: electrolytes, renal function, calcium, phosphorus, magnesium
Ionized calcium level (target ~2 mM if giving IV calcium)
Serum lactate: tissue hypoperfusion marker
VBG: acid-base status, perfusion
Digoxin level if taking digoxin
Acetaminophen & salicylate levels if coingestion possible
Consider TSH, Lyme/Tick serology
POCUS: check vasodilatory vs pump failure, check volume status & EF
Continuous cardiac monitoring (mandatory)
Pulse oximetry
Arterial line if hemodynamically unstable

drugs.name: Epinephrine
drugs.dose: Start 1 mcg/min, titrate to MAP ≥60; no maximum dose
drugs.notes: Good first-line vasopressor: helps both bradycardia and hypotension. High doses may be needed

drugs.name: Norepinephrine
drugs.notes: Preferred for vasodilatory shock (dihydropyridine CCBs, vasodilatory BBs like labetalol, carvedilol)

drugs.name: Calcium chloride 10%
drugs.dose: 1 g IV over ≥5 min via central line; repeat q10-20 min up to 3 g total
drugs.contraindications: Digoxin toxicity (calcium worsens dig toxicity). Causes tissue necrosis if extravasates
drugs.notes: 3x more elemental Ca than gluconate. Target ionized Ca ~2 mM. May need repeat doses q1-2 hr

drugs.name: Calcium gluconate 10%
drugs.dose: 3 g IV over ≥5 min; repeat q10-20 min up to 9 g total
drugs.notes: Preferred without central access. Target ionized Ca ~2 mM

drugs.name: Regular insulin (HIE)
drugs.dose: 1 unit/kg IV bolus, then 1 unit/kg/hr infusion. Up-titrate q10-15 min to max 10 units/kg/hr
drugs.contraindications: Monitor glucose q15-30 min; maintain K+ >3.0 mM
drugs.notes: Most effective therapy for CCB/BB cardiogenic shock. Takes 15-60 min for full effect: START EARLY. Give D50W bolus + infusion. Wean dextrose based on glucose, not insulin

drugs.name: Dextrose 50% (D50W)
drugs.dose: 1-2 amps (50-100 mL) bolus when starting HIE (unless glucose >250). Infusion: ~1 mL/kg/hr via central line
drugs.notes: Essential with HIE. D50W via central preferred to avoid volume overload. Target glucose 125-250 mg/dL

drugs.name: Glucagon
drugs.dose: 5 mg IV bolus over 5 min; may repeat x1 (max test dose 10 mg). Infusion: 1-10 mg/hr
drugs.contraindications: Pheochromocytoma, insulinoma. Caution: unprotected airway (causes emesis)
drugs.notes: Primarily for BB OD. Pre-treat with ondansetron. Short t1/2 (5-7 min): needs infusion. May exhaust pharmacy supply. If glucagon works but supply limited: switch to milrinone

drugs.name: Atropine
drugs.dose: 1 mg IV q5 min, max 3 mg cumulative
drugs.notes: Trial is reasonable for bradycardia but unlikely to work in severe CCB/BB OD

drugs.name: Sodium bicarbonate
drugs.notes: For QRS widening from Na channel blocking BBs (propranolol, carvedilol). Same approach as TCA OD

drugs.name: Magnesium sulfate
drugs.dose: 2 g IV bolus over 15-20 min; infusion 1-2 g/hr for active torsade
drugs.notes: For QTc prolongation (sotalol, acebutolol). Treat active torsade with Mg infusion + overdrive pacing

drugs.name: Methylene blue
drugs.dose: 1-2 mg/kg IV over 5-30 min; may repeat
drugs.contraindications: G6PD deficiency, serotonergic drugs (serotonin syndrome risk)
drugs.notes: Tier-2 therapy for refractory vasodilatory shock from CCBs. CCBs ↑ nitric oxide synthesis. Not for cardiogenic shock

drugs.name: Lipid emulsion 20%
drugs.dose: 1.5 mL/kg IV bolus (repeat 1-2x if pulseless), then 0.25 mL/kg/min x 30-60 min; max 12.5 mL/kg
drugs.contraindications: Pancreatitis, avoid if ECMO planned (clots circuits)
drugs.notes: Falling out of favor. May worsen absorption. Reasonable in cardiac arrest/periarrest only. Ineffective for hydrophilic BBs

pairs.value: All symptomatic pts. All sotalol ingestions (delayed/prolonged toxicity). All sustained-release CCB ingestions even if initially asymptomatic. Any pt requiring vasopressors, HIE, glucagon infusion, or ECMO
Asymptomatic immediate-release ingestions: observe ≥6 hr with continuous cardiac monitoring. Discharge if asymptomatic with normal vitals, ECG, and glucose after observation period
All sustained-release formulations, sotalol, and amlodipine ingestions require ≥24 hr monitoring even if initially asymptomatic due to delayed toxicity
Repeat vitals, confirm monitoring plan, verify telemetry, communicate re-escalation triggers, ensure serial ECGs and glucose checks are ordered, and document disposition rationale

topics.title: Compartment Syndrome
topics.subtitle: Severe pain, tense compartment, pain with passive stretch, paresthesias

sections.title: Acute Management
items: Airway: check patency and protect. Breathing: check WOB, oxygenation, and ventilation. Circulation: check pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
Place limb at heart level (elevation: ↓ arterial perfusion; dependency ↑ edema: both worsen compartment syndrome)
Call surgery pager!
If clinical dx is clear: STAT fasciotomy: do NOT delay for pressure measurements
Fasciotomy indicated if: absolute pressure >30 mmHg OR delta pressure (diastolic BP − compartment pressure) <30 mmHg
Monitor UOP: goal >11.5 mL/kg/h to prevent AKI

sections.title: Chart Check / History
items: MOA: fracture (esp tibial shaft: MCC), crush injury, prolonged limb compression, reperfusion after vascular repair
Recent surgery: postop positioning, tourniquet time, vascular procedures
Burns: circumferential burns: escharotomy may needed not fasciotomy
Anticoagulation status: ↑ risk of bleed into compartment
Massive IVF (>10L crystalloid in 24h: risk of Abd compartment syndrome)
Hx of vascular injury or repair, arterial embolism, DVT with phlegmasia
For Abd: massive transfusion, damage control surgery, pancreatitis, bowel obstruction, ascites, peritonitis, Abd packing
Baseline Cr: Rhabdo risk
Altered sensorium (intubated, sedated, obtunded): may not report pain, ↑ risk of delayed dx

sections.title: Exam
items: Classic 6 P's (late findings: do NOT wait for all): Pain, Pressure, Paressia, Paralysis, Pallor, Pulselessness
Pain out of proportion to injury: earliest & most sensitive finding. ↑ with passive stretch of involved muscles
Tense, swollen compartment on palpation: compare to contralateral limb
↓ Sensation / paressias: suggests nerve ischemia (peroneal n. in leg: ↓ 1st web space sensation)
Weakness or paralysis of muscles in affected compartment: late & ominous
Pulses are PRESERVED untillate: presence of pulse does NOT r/o compartment syndrome
Cap refill: may nl. early
For Abd compartment syndrome: tense distended abdomen, ↑ peak airway pressures, ↓ UOP, ↑ CVP, ↓ BP refractory to fluids
Measure bladder pressure (via Foley transducer) if suspecting Abd compartment syndrome: IAP >20 mmHg with organ dysfunction = Abd compartment syndrome

pairs.value: ↑ Pressure within closed fascial space: compromised perfusion: tissue ischemia & necrosis. Surgical emergency
08 mmHg
Absolute pressure >30 mmHg OR delta pressure (DBP − compartment pressure)<30 mmHg
68 hours: muscle necrosis begins. After 8h, outcomes significantly worse
Sustained IAP >20 mmHg with new organ dysfunction. IntraAbd HTN = IAP >12 mmHg. Grades: I (1215), II (1620), III (2125), IV (>25 mmHg)
IntraAbd pathology: hemorrhage, peritonitis, packing, bowel edema, pancreatitis
ExtraAbd cause: massive resuscitation, burns, sepsis: visceral & retroperitoneal edema
Anterior compartment of leg (tibial fractures). Also: forearm (supracondylar fx in kids), thigh, foot, h&, gluteal
Late sequela of missed forearm compartment syndrome: irreversible flexion contracture

sections.title: Abdominal Compartment Syndrome
items: Suspect in: massive resus. (>10L/24h), damage control surgery, Abd packing, pancreatitis, bowel Obst., burns >30% TBSA
Measure bladder pressure (gold standard): via Foley, supine, endexpiration, give 25mL NS. IAP = 57 mmHg
IntraAbd HTN: sustained IAP ≥12 mmHg. Grade I: 1215, Grade II: 1620, Grade III: 2125, Grade IV: >25 mmHg
ACS = IAP >20 mmHg + new organ dys. (renal failure, resp failure, shock)
Supportive txt: (for grades IIII without organ failure): NG decompression, rectal tube, sedation, neuromuscular blockade, minimize IVF, diuresis if euvolemic, paracentesis if ascites, HOB ≤20°
Abd perfusion pressure (APP) = MAP − IAP. Target APP ≥60 mmHg; low APP supports poor visceral perfusion
Recheck IAP q46h if IAH is present or the pt is worsening
Do not rely on IVC collapsibility to estimate volume status once intraAbd pressure is high
Decompressive laparotomy if: IAP >20 mmHg with refractory organ failure after nonoperative measures, OR ↑ IAP with clinical deterioration
Postdecompression: temporary Abd closure, ICU monitoring, plan for sequential Abd closure
Beware reperfusion syndrome after decompression: ↑ K, ↓ BP, acidosis, cardiac arrest. Have calcium, bicarb, vasopressors ready
Serial bladder pressure q46h in all atrisk ICU pts

pairs.value: MCC: tibial shaft, forearm, supracondylar humerus in children
Found down, intraop positioning, constrictive dressings/casts, drug/ETOH OD with prolonged immobility
Post vascular repair (embolectomy, bypass), restored flow after prolonged ischemia
Anticoagulation, coagulopathy, vascular injury
Circumferential burns: escharotomy (not fasciotomy) as first step
Snake bites: local swelling, ↑ compartment pressure
Not an acute emergency: recurrent exertional sx, resolves with rest
DVT, cellulitis, necrotizing fasciitis, fracture without compartment syndrome, Rhabdo, arterial insufficiency, neuropathy
Massive resuscitation (>10L/24h), damage control surgery, Abd packing, pancreatitis, bowel Obst., ascites, peritonitis, burns

sections.title: Management
items: Remove all constricting wraps, casts, dressings, or splints & keep the limb at heart level
If the clinical dx is clear, call surgery & move toward STAT fasciotomy; if uncertain, measure compartment pressures without delaying care
Start aggressive IVF & monitor for Rhabdo, ↑K, acidosis, & AKI
Do serial neurovascular exams q12h until decompressed or improving
For Abd compartment syndrome: decompress stomach/bowel, sedate, minimize IVF, & do decompressive laparotomy if IAP >20 mmHg with new organ failure
Postfasciotomy: wounds left open, VAC dressing or wettodry. Delayed primary closure or skin grafting in 57 days
Monitor CK q612h until trending down: peak2472h postinjury
Continue aggressive IVF until CK <5,000 & trending down, UOP maintained
Monitor for reperfusion syndrome: ↑K, acidosis, myoglobinuria, arrhythmias: esp after fasciotomy or decompression
Serial BMP q68h: watch K, Cr, Ca, phos
Renal replacement therapy if refractory ↑K, severe acidosis, fluid overload, or oliguric AKI despite IVF
For Abd compartment syndrome postlaparotomy: temporary Abd closure (Bogota bag, ABra VAC), serial IAP monitoring, plan return to OR in 2448h
Pain mgmt: avoid compartmentcompressing positions. Do NOT elevate above heart level
DVT prophylaxis when surgical bleeding risk permits
PT/OT early for extremity compartment syndrome: ROM exercises once stable

pairs.value: Stryker needle, arterial line transducer, or commercial device. Measure all compartments in affected limb (leg has 4: anterior, lateral, superficial posterior, deep posterior)
↑ in Rhabdo —>5x ULN. May lag behind clinical presentation
↑K (released from necrotic muscle), ↑ Cr, ↑ BUN, metabolic acidosis, ↑ phosphate, ↓ calcium
Serum & urine: myoglobinuria (dark teacolored urine). Urine dip + for blood but no RBCs on micro
Baseline Hgb (hemorrhage), WBC (infection)
INR/PTT: esp if on anticoagulation or coagulopathy suspected
May be ↑ from tissue ischemia
If surgical intervention anticipated
For Abd compartment syndrome: measure via Foley catheter transducer, supine, endexpiration, 25mL instilled
Acidosis assessment: esp with Rhabdo or shock

drugs.name: NS or LR
drugs.dose: 12 L/h initially, then titrate to UOP >11.5 mL/kg/h
drugs.notes: Aggressive IVF is critical to prevent myoglobininduced AKI. NS historically preferred for rhabdo (alkaline pH goal) but LR acceptable

drugs.name: Sodium bicarbonate
drugs.dose: 150 mEq in 1L D5W, infuse at 150-200 mL/h
drugs.notes: Urine alkalinization (goal pH >6.5) to ↓ myoglobin precipitation in renal tubules. Controversial: some centers use only if acidotic

drugs.name: Mannitol
drugs.notes: Osmotic diuresis to ↑ renal flow & ↓ myoglobin cast formation. May also ↓ muscle edema (oretical). Use with aggressive IVF

drugs.name: Calcium gluconate
drugs.notes: For ↑K with EKG changes: membrane stabilization. Avoid routine calcium repletion in rhabdo (may deposit in damaged muscle)

drugs.name: Insulin + dextrose
drugs.notes: For ↑K mgmt. Shifts K intracellularly. Monitor glucose q1530min

drugs.name: Cisatracurium or rocuronium
drugs.dose: Cisatracurium 0.15 mg/kg IV bolusthen13 mcg/kg/min; Rocuronium 0.61.2 mg/kg
drugs.notes: Neuromuscular blockade for Abd compartment syndrome: ↓ Abd wall compliance, ↓ IAP by 5-10 mmHg. Temporizing measure

pairs.key: ICU / immediate higher level of care if
pairs.value: persistent respiratory distress, high-flow or NIV need, hemodynamic instability, vasopressor need, severe arrhythmia, airway risk, neurologic decline, or a trajectory suggesting pt may crash on the floor.
